Original_Search_Title: Jigawa to Set Up Cassava Processing Firm
Headline: Nigeria: Jigawa to Set Up Cassava Processing Firm
Body_Text:
Dutse — Jigawa state government has announced its plan to set up a Cassava processing company in Gwaram Local Government Area of the state in order to improve the trade and industry base of the state. This was reavealed by the state Commissioner for Information, Youth, Culture and Sports, Alhaji Aminu Mohammed.
Briefing newsmen on the outcome of the state Executive Council meeting held at Government House Dutse, the commissioner said the feasibility studies has already been conducted, adding that it gave the government the strength to give the approval for its establishment and that very soon work will commence at the selected side.
Mohammed explained that it will cost about N23 million to establish the company and it is expected to start immediately. He stressed that when fully operational, the company will serve as a revenue earner and a "means of employment to the teeming citizens of the state as well as provide employment to the teeming populace. "The company will no doubt complement other means of employment to the teeming citizens of the state".

Original_Search_Title: The Plight of Vendors And Their Customers
Headline: Gambia: The Plight of Vendors And Their Customers
Body_Text:
Nyima a vendor, was sitting under the hot sun with her twelve months baby boy to struggle for the fish money of her family by selling few items. Musu was also seated at the same market.
According to her, she used to buy cassava, pepper, onion and other condiments to re-sell. She was quick to explain to this reporter that since the morning she has sold only eight dalasis worth of condiments and had to pay five dalasis as duty. She was left with only three dalasis which could not even provide her a cup of rice for the family.She explained that everybody is selling and few have money to buy the goods. Alhagie a kola nut seller says that he normally buys a bag which weighs 60kg for D6000 and the 48kg one for D4200. The old man told me that since morning he sold only 20 dalasi worth of Kola nuts. He said people buy Kola nuts mainly for ceremonies. He said that the sum of money he derives from the sales cannot be relied on each day to even feed himself not to mention his family. He added that things are not easy now because of the decline in purchasing power.
On his part Mbakeh, a carpenter says that there are trying just to manage to get their fish monies but even that is becoming difficult .He told me that they normally buy materials at high prices which reduces their over all income. He gave me an example of a contract for which they charge the customers D2000. He indicated that one would have to spend almost D1600 for the materials needed. One would have to feed those who assist him and provide them with pocket money, which means that the balance for the work will be less than D400.00 while the work takes days or weeks.
He added that to prevent his workshop from closing down they often borrow money to produce items in advance and put it out for sale. He went as far as to show this reporter a well designed box bed (master bed) which he says was there for more than two months without any customers appearing to buy them. He said that the government and all the business sectors should come together to see how to improve the economic environment so that those with skills will be able to find customers for their products. He added that they don't want to sit at home doing nothing but lamented that going to work and not able to have money to pay rent and feed one's family is worse than sitting at home. This reporter spoke to Khaddy, a customer just to find out what the consumers are saying. She explained that she bought the cheapest fish (Chalo) three for 15 dalasis.She calculated the cost of oil, cups of rice, firewood and other condiments and it added up to more than 100 dalasis. She indicated that this is twice the sum they receive for their daily consumption needs.

Original_Search_Title: Funding Key to Economic Empowerment for Women - Permanent Secretary
Headline: Nigeria: Funding Key to Economic Empowerment for Women - Permanent Secretary
Body_Text:
Abuja — The establishment of two funding programmes,  Women Fund for Economic Empowerment, WOFEE, and Business Development Fund for Women, BUDFOW, has been identified as part of efforts to ensure equal access of women to basic opportunities.
This was stated by the Permanent Secretary, Women Affairs and Social Development, Dr. Benard Ibe, at a two-day interactive meeting with directors of women affairs in the 36 states.He stressed that the meeting was coming at a time when the federal ministry of women affairs and social development was undergoing tremendous to transformation to ensure the protection of the rights of women, children, the aged and persons with disability.
Delivering the keynote address, Dr Ibe said, the establishment of the two programmes would help women to access soft loans without having to undergo the usual bureaucracies involved in borrowing from commercial banks.
"The interest rates are as low as 10% as against 18-25% charged by the banks," he said. "With these two funding programmes the barrier of lack of access to credit facility will be broken."
He further added that the ministry had established vegetable oil cottage industry in Damaturu, a pottery cottage in Kwali and a salt cottage in Okpoama in Bayelsa State and that a cassava cottage would commence in Abia and Ekiti states as well as a rice cottage in Sokoto.
Six zonal women political empowerment offices located in Gusau, Zamfara State(North-West), Gombe State(North-East) Lafia, Nasarawa (North-Central), Osogbo, Osun State(South- West), Owerri, Imo State (South-east) and Port Harcourt, Rivers State(South-south) are being empowered to assist and build capacity of women within their respective zones.In her address, the Director of Women Affairs, Mrs E.O Adeyemi, said that it was desirable to engage the untapped energies and abilities of majority of the poor, especially women, if lasting  progress was to be made in sustainable development.
"I also wish to use this medium to solicit the development assistance providers to recognise the pervasive additional obstacles that poor women face and give serious attention to those impediments not only to women but also for effective national development", she said.

Original_Search_Title: Memories of Dattijo
Headline: Nigeria: Memories of Dattijo
Body_Text:
Through the pain and grief following my senior brother Ibrahim Jega's death early last week, memories flooded in of him growing up in Jega and Sokoto, before he moved on in life.
From our earliest days, we heard our parents calling him Dattijo, or venerable old man. He was named after our great grandfather Illon Abdu, who died in 1932. At first Ibrahim wasn't fond of the name, but he later came to like it. One day in 1980, he suddenly paused from reading a novel and told me that our [then 6-year old] younger brother was impolite because he was calling him Ibrahim, instead of Dattijo.
I don't know how our parents programmed it, but as Ibrahim grew up, he began to look every bit like the Dattijo. For one, he was very neat and always passed the spot checks at primary school to determine if a pupil had taken bath that morning.
With nearly 20 of us going to primary school from his house in the late 1960s and early 1970s, our grandfather, the late Magatakarda Malam Kakale, had a tough task controlling us. Being an old schoolmaster who went to Katsina College in the 1920s, Magatakarda set for us many stringent rules. He banned sliding in the mud [bube], banned long-line angling [dogon labe], diving into the river from cliffs [alkahura], bare-handed pond fishing [lalabe], shooting pigeons, gang-like attacks on other kids, playing football within the house, riding donkeys, smoking the locally-made cigarette [huke], making a pass at girls, playing games in pitch darkness, and catching scorpions under the influence of charms we bought at the market. Though the punishment was severe, most of us regularly broke Magatakarda's rules, except Dattijo, who was a total conformist.
The early signs of a long and successful banking career were there in Ibrahim from the beginning. In 1968, when we were being sent from our father's house in Sokoto to school in Jega, each one of us was given 7 shillings, six pence as pocket money. I quickly squandered my own on sweets, sugar cane, boiled cassava and hired bicycles, but Ibrahim still had money many weeks later. When we begged him for money and he refused to give, we used to say that he was a miser.
Every morning before we went off to school, Magatakarda gave each one of us one and a half pence for breakfast. I quickly spent my own money, but Ibrahim opened an asusu in a milk tin and saved alot of money, which he opened when we were going to Sokoto for holidays.
In 1972, he left for Koko Secondary School, then based at Birnin Kebbi. Our father was so happy with Ibrahim's performance there, which he attributed to the stern Principal Mr. Peter Steward [a Briton], that he later stopped our brother Abbas from going to an FGC and sent him to Koko. Years later, he also pulled Maikano out of Government College, Sokoto and sent him to Koko.
In 1974, soon after the Udoji salary arrears were paid, our father bought a magnificent Raleigh Chopper bicycle for Ibrahim. This started Ibrahim's long love affair with bicycles and motorcycles. In 1976, when he entered SBS Zaria, he took his first student "bulga" and bought a Yamaha 100 motorcycle for N180. The following year, when I won a state-wide essay competition, our father gave me the Yamaha and gave Ibrahim N200, which he added to another bulga and bought a Honda 185 Roadmaster for N650.
Most motorcyclists in those days did only third-party insurance, but Ibrahim paid N50 for a comprehensive insurance. This turned out to be prescient because 21 days later, the motorcycle was stolen at ABU Zaria's Kongo Campus. After much delay, the insurance firm repaid the N650, minus what they called 21% depreciation. With that and other money, Ibrahim bought a powerful Yamaha 200 motorcycle with double silencer and double speedometer.
Dattijo was always very calm and very studious. When we lived at Abuja Road in Sokoto in the 1970s and early 1980s, we lived in one large guest room with many brothers and cousins. Every day, Ibrahim would sit on an armchair near the door for many hours and read a novel, glancing at his watch every now and then to see if it was prayer time.[He retained this habit to the end; hours before he died last week, he was still glancing at his watch]. When it was time for prayer, he would quietly get up, perform ablution, pray, and quickly resume reading.
He read very large books within a day or two, and generously recommended them to others to read. During the long holidays of 1978 alone, he asked me to read The Autobiography of Malcolm X, Alex Hailey's Roots, Shall We Tell The President, The R Document, Papillon, and many James Hadley Chase and Nick Carter novels.
In later years, I completely dropped the allegation that he was a miser. One day in 1982, I stopped him on the road and begged for some money. I was hoping to get N5, but he dipped his hand in his pocket and gave me N30. I was paralysed with gratitude. Later that year, I told him I was going to Zaria, and he gave me N50. Still later that year, our Mum asked him if he could go to Maiduguri and get some magic water for our Grandma who was losing her eyesight. He said he couldn't go due to his work, but when I agreed to go, he gave me his brand-new Fiat 132 Climatizzata and N100 to buy fuel.
Ibrahim also gave me my first car. In 1985, when he was transferred to Lagos, he left his Fiat with me so that I could take his wife to the university. A year later, when she graduated, I took the car back to him, and he said I should keep it. Over the next five years, I had serious trouble maintaining the Fiat, which consumed most of my N600 monthly salary, so anytime he came from Lagos, he gave me some money to help maintain it.
He worked for the Nigeria Bank of Commerce and Industry [NBCI] in Sokoto, Lagos, Katsina and Kano, then moved to Afribank and served in Kano and Kaduna, before he went to Standard Trust Bank in 2004, which later merged with UBA. He got rapid promotion at STB and UBA, ending up as Executive Director at UBA Group. He often spoke fondly of UBA's GMD Tony Elumelu and his exacting standards. He said one night, the GMD phoned him at 1am with some instructions, then called again at 2am, and again at 3am. His wife suggested to him to shut down the phone, but he merely smiled and said, "I can't do that."
On top of it, Ibrahim had a very large circle of friends, which he actively maintained over the years. Now he is gone, leaving behind a wife and five children. May Allah grant him Aljannat.

Original_Search_Title: Inmates Explain Ordeal At Gbarnga Central Prison
Headline: Liberia: Inmates Explain Ordeal At Gbarnga Central Prison
Body_Text:
Gbarnga — Inmates detained at the Gbarnga Central Prison have expressed complete frustration and indignation over their prolong confinement without facing court trial.
The prisoners, who have been detained for different crimes, ranging from murder to simple assault, lamented that some of them have been in detention for more than three years without having their days in court of competent jurisdiction to exonerate themselves from charges leveled against them.
A motorcyclist Big-Boy Mulbah, explaining his ordeal to our correspondent, say he has been in detention for the past three months on charges of 'misapplication of entrusted property', and the opportunity has not been created to give his side of the story through court proceedings.
His trouble ensued when a helmet belonging to one of his friends went missing from him, and he promised a replacement. While in the process of sourcing funding to do the replacement, he was taken to the Police at which time he was charged for 'misapplication of entrusted property', and forwarded to court for prosecution. Big-Boy said since his detention, trial has not commenced, making the prison compound to be his new home.
Another detainee Momo Willie, very disappointed, informed this Paper that he has also been in jail for four months without court trial with the charge of 'simple assault'. His plight is similar to Big-Boy.
Data obtained from Gbarnga Central prison established that there are seven sentenced prisoners while 62 detained inmates are awaiting court trial.
Among the 62 awaiting prosecution, 12 are facing trial for murder, seven for rape, eight for statutory rape, two for armed robbery, 13 for theft of property and eight for alleged burglaries.
Apart from the prolonged detention, inmates and convicts are said to be faced with serious food shortage.
They are being fed once a day with bulgur wheat provided by the World Food Program (WFP), sometimes without oil. They claimed that their food are poorly prepared, and not served on time.
They alleged that their alternative food source is dry gari (farina, made from cassava), normally sold to them by officers.
When contacted to comment on the situation, authorities at the Gbarnga Central Prison admitted that some of the prisoners have been jailed beyond the required statue as specified by law.
Though the Superintendent of the center was not available to comment, a Correction Offices, Bindu Kollie, told this Paper that as a result of the prolonged detention of prisoners at the Central Prison, authorities at the center have been faced with threats of jailbreak by hardened criminals there.
She maintained that on numerous occasions the Ministry of Justice as well as the Circuit Court in the county has been informed on the protracted incarceration of inmates.
She confirmed that the inmates are fed with bulgur wheat twice a day, and denied that they live on dry gari, as alleged by the inmates.
"They at time asked us to buy gari for them, but this is not their regular diet," one prison officer told our reporter.
Meanwhile, the Gbarnga Central Prison is in dire need of a generator that would provide electricity on the compound, Madam Kollie said.
She said as a result of the lack of electricity on the compound, correction officers are finding it tremendously difficult to exercise the normal cell inspection of criminals at night. Contact: jefferson.massah@yahoo.com/ +2316828622

Original_Search_Title: Strange Disease Hits Busia
Headline: Uganda: Strange Disease Hits Busia
Body_Text:
Kampala — EXPERTS from the health ministry are in Busia town to investigate a strange disease that has hit the area. The disease, which has symptoms similar to those of cholera, brok out in Soloa, Arubaine, Marachi A and B and Mugungu villages.
Seven people, including two children, died after suffering from severe diarrhoea and vomiting. Over 20 others were admitted at Samia-Bugwe North Health Centre IV.
Dr. George Oundo Bwire, the district health officer, said some of the patients had been discharged. "I cannot, therefore, say there is a cholera outbreak," Oundo said. The Arubaine and Marachi leaders blamed the disease on the Busia-Kenya sewage system, which they said had overflowed into the village wells.
They said residents started falling sick when the sewage started flooding. Oundo said an isolation centre had been created at the health centre, adding that some patients who were at the health centre before the outbreak, discharged themselves fearing that they would contract the disease.
He appealed to residents to report any suspected cases. However, some of the patients said they had eaten cassava and chapattis from Arubaine market.
James Bagaga, a Marachi B resident, said he developed the symptoms eight hours after he drunk water packed in kaveera (polythen bag).

Original_Search_Title: Four in Hospital After Beans Meal
Headline: Nigeria: Four in Hospital After Beans Meal
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Four persons have been reportedly admitted at one of the private hospitals in Ilorin, Kwara State capital, weekend, after eating beans suspected to be poisonous.
The incident, according to THISDAY checks, happened at Taiwo- Isale area of the ancient city.Sources close to the family told newsmen in Ilorin, yesterday, that after eating the beans, they complained of stomach ache, which later resulted to blood gushing out of their mouths.
Also yesterday, the state Ministry of Health said it had taken the cassava flour that killed six persons two weeks ago, for laboratory test.
Commissioner for Health, Mrs Ayinke Saka, said the development would assist the ministry to know the extent of the chemical component of the poisonous flour.
It would be recalled that, two families at Baruten Local Government Council area were at logger heads over a parcel of land, which led one of them poisoning the cassava flour spread in front of the house of the other family, killing six members of the family after a meal.

Original_Search_Title: Pallisa Passes Sh19 Billion Budget
Headline: Uganda: Pallisa Passes Sh19 Billion Budget
Body_Text:
Kampala — PALLISA district council has passed a sh19.9b budget for the 2009/2010 financial year. Priority was given to construction of more health centres to improve public access to health care.
Presenting the budget proposals to the council at Pallisa Red Cross Hall on Friday, the finance, planning and administration secretary, Emmanuel Odelle, said sh18.8b (94% of the budget) would be central government grants.
He said sh907.7m (5%) would be from development partners and sh220m (1%) would be from local revenue.
Odelle told the council presided over by the speaker, Fred Madautu, that sh517m had been earmarked for construction of classrooms, pit-latrines and provision of desks and scholastic materials in primary schools.
The district is also to procure disease-resistant cassava cuttings to reduce food shortages caused by the brown streak virus that has cut down cassava production in the area.
Odelle said sh580m would be used to improve the road network by opening new roads and routinely maintaining both district and community roads.

Original_Search_Title: Country, DR Congo Share Many Similitude
Headline: Liberia: Country, DR Congo Share Many Similitude
Body_Text:
The Charge d'Affaires en Pied at the Embassy of the Democratic Republic of Congo, Madam Nena Marie Helene Okitundu Ayaki has said that the people of Liberia and DR Congo have many things in common such as names of persons and places as well as their eating habits.
"Names such as Kollie, Kiazulu, Mobutu and Tamba, and places such as Congo Town, Mamba and Matadi are used in both countries where the two peoples also eat cassava, cassava leaves, potato greens, etc alike. We Congolese therefore feel at home in Liberia," she said.
Noting that that DR Congo and Liberia have had secular and fraternal relations existing long since the abolition of the slave trade, the Congolese envoy added that both countries also share a recent history characterized by civil wars and today they find themselves under United Nations peacekeeping operations and both needing reconstruction.
"All these common points must bring us together in order to exchange our experiences and it will help enrich us mutually," she observed.
Amb. Okitundu Ayaki spoke on June 30 at Mamba Point Hotel in Monrovia at a reception in observance of the 49th Independence Anniversary of her country, the Democratic Republic of Congo. He told the agathering that the DR Congo was declared the third republic three years ago and by promulgating a new constitution President Joseph Kabila Kabange launched a socio-economic project within the national reconstruction program that outlined five priorities including electricity and water; education; housing; infrastructure; and, health.
"The signing of a mining contract by the Government of the DR Congo with the Government of the People's Republic of China has been wrongly understood by the Breton Woods institutions while there is space for all foreign investors in the DR Congo," she noted.
"Another major factor that has undermined the Government's intervention program is the conflict in the east of the DR Congo which has taken a heavy toll in human lives, and displaced the population massively. We cannot stop to deplore the flagrant violation of humanitarian law and human rights, and the violence perpetuated against women and children by the different armed factions."
She said her mission was monitoring ongoing reforms in Liberia with interest and welcomed the reconstruction efforts by the leadership of President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf. "We extend our congratulations for her dedicated and tenacity in rebuilding Liberia," she noted.
"As we celebrate today June 30, the 49th Independence Anniversary of our Country, the greatest hope we express is that peace and security reign forever in DR Congo and Liberia," she concluded.
Also making remarks on behalf of the Government of Liberia, Vice President Joseph N. Boakai concurred with the Congolese envoy applauding the long cordial and fraternal relations that has persisted between DR Congo and Liberia.
"In fact late Congolese President Mobutu was an honorary citizen of Liberia and, Kakata, the capital city of Margibi County was named after a Congolese called Kaka," the VP said.
Vice President Boakai said Congolese residing in Liberia are law abiding and said the Liberian Government would continue to provide protection for them and all other foreign residents in Liberia, and urged them to continue to conduct their normal activities freely in Liberia within the confines of the laws of the country.
On the conflict in DR Congo, Vice President Boakai said the Government of Liberia supports the policy of dialogue. While the Congolese envoy earlier called for observance of one minute silence in memories of Congolese national heroes including the first Prime Minister Patrice Emery Lumumba, the Vice President of Liberia, on his part, called for equal ritual in memory of the distinguished Liberian woman, D. Musuleng Cooper, who passed away.
The event was attended by members of the Liberian Legislature, Cabinet, diplomatic corps, Congolese community and friends of DR Congo, among them the Special Representative of the Secretary General and Head of the United Nations Mission in Liberia Margret Loj, Foreign Minister Obubanke King-Akerele, Education Minister Dr. Joseph Korto, Nimba County Senator Aldophus Dolo, Maryland County Senator Gloria Scott.

Original_Search_Title: Six Die After Cassava Flour Meal
Headline: Nigeria: Six Die After Cassava Flour Meal
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Six people have been confirmed dead in Nimgurumi town in the Baruten Local Government Area of Kwara State after taking cassava flour suspected to be poisonous.
Also, four people are now receiving medical attention at a private hospital located in the town due to the incident.
THISDAY checks revealed that the incident occurred following the crisis between the two families over a parcel of land in the town.
It was gathered that one of the families was said to have spread the cassava flour in front of their house so as to get it dry for consumption but unknown to them, one of the neighbours was alleged to have put a substance suspected to be poison into the cassava flour.
Sources close to the town said that the unsuspected family was reported to have prepared a meal with the cassava flour which resulted in the food being poisoned, consequently leading to the death of some members of the family
Contacted yesterday, the state police command confirmed the death of six persons as a result of food poison , saying the command had commenced full investigation into the incident.
The state police command Public Relations Officer, Mr. Dabo Exekiel, who spoke with newsmen in Ilorin yesterday said that as soon as the detailed report on the incident is ready, the command will make it available to the public.

Original_Search_Title: Six Family Members Die After Eating 'Amala'
Headline: Nigeria: Six Family Members Die After Eating 'Amala'
Body_Text:
Ilorin — The animosity between two families over land dispute in Nimgurumi town in Baruten local government area of the state has led to the death of six out of the ten members of a family after consuming bowls of cassava flour popularly called 'Amala' suspected to have been poisoned by the rival family on Monday night
The other four members of the family were however said to be responding to treatment at an undisclosed hospital in the area.
The mother of the victims according to Vanguard findings was said to have been spreading the cassava flour on the bare floor in the process of drying it to make it suitable for pounding but unknown to her that it has been allegedly poisoned in the process of drying it before it was consumed Monday night.
Vanguard learnt that tragedy struck in the night after the family have consumed bowls the prepared "Amala" for dinner and they started developing stomach complications one after the other and died before they could be rushed to the hospital for attention.
Commissioner for Health Mrs Ayinke Saka while briefing journalists yesterday over the development confirmed that the feud between the two families over land might have led to the suspected cassava flour poisoning.
Ayinke said the incident has been reported at the state police headquarters for investigations.
She however warned residents in the state to desisit from exposing their food items so as to discourage this kind of untoward development.
When contacted, the state police command through its spokesman , Mr. Dabo Ezekiel confirmed the incident and assured that the detail report on the incident will will be made available to the public by the command.

Original_Search_Title: Steve Biko Academy to Clash With Row Squad in Lamin Noran Final
Headline: Gambia: Steve Biko Academy to Clash With Row Squad in Lamin Noran Final
Body_Text:
Biko Academy from Bakau will meet with Row Squad in the 2009 Lamin Noran final which is to be decided at a later date by the organizing committee.
Row Squad almost had their first leg 2-1 lead cancelled on Saturday when they lost to Cassava-Peace 1-0, thanks to Ebrima Touray's 76th minute strike. But despite their lone goal lost to Cassava Peace in the second leg match on Saturday, Row Squad progressed to the final based on their two away goals' advantage.
In the second fixture played on Sunday the Academy Lads from Bakau denied Jatta's Memorial Academy to the final when they pegged them to a goal less draw. It was a tough encounter, hard fought by both determined teams.
In the first leg match, Steven Biko Academy stunned Jatta's Memorial Academy 1-0 for any possibility to play in the final. Jatta's Memorial wants to turn over this score line, but Steve Biko Academy came all out to defend their slender lead which they succeeded.

Original_Search_Title: Who Goes to the Final?
Headline: Gambia: Who Goes to the Final?
Body_Text:
Row Squad, Cassava Peace, Biko Academy and Jatta's Memorial Football Academy will this weekend battle out for a place in the final of the 12 teams Noran Football Tournament that kicked off in early April at Sanna Jatta Memorial Football field.
In the Saturday's fixture, Cassava Peace will be out to overturn Row Squad 1st leg 2-1 goal deficit in their account. Whilst for Row Squad they only need to fortify their 2-1 lead to progress to the final.
In the same vein, Biko Academy with a lone goal wins over Jatta's Memorial Football Academy in the 1st leg, will also have to fight harder to consolidate their narrow lead.
While Jatta's Memorial Academy will have to neutralize the lone goal add another goal if they are to qualify to the final.
Yaya Manneh an executive member of the committee disclosed that they have been organizing Noran tournament for the past six years but sponsorship has still been their constraint. He revealed that their tournament has been self sponsored (committee) and therefore called on companies, business entrepreneurs and all able men to come and support their efforts.

Original_Search_Title: Obioma Imoke And the Power Project
Headline: Nigeria: Obioma Imoke And the Power Project
Body_Text:
Lagos — Of all the first ladies who have played complementary role in the governance of Cross River State with their husbands as Chief Executive, none can be equated under any development indices with Barrister Obioma Liyel Imoke.
This, in the first place, is due to the most relevant pet project of our time embarked upon by Her Excellency about one a half years ago with its various positive spin-off components which have become part and parcel of the State projects and programmes of action.
Strategically and technically called Partnership Opportunity for Women Empowerment Realisation, (POWER), this phenomenal pet project has actually made Her Excellency, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke to continue to tower well over her colleagues in the Nigerian Federation in terms of focus, initiative and national policy project relevance.
Formally launched by the First Lady of the Federation, Hadjiya Turai Yar'Adua on November 7, 2007 in Calabar, POWER, indeed marked the birth of a vision of Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke who sees Cross River State as a place where her women will be provided opportunities to attain great heights and maximize their potentials.
Against such a backdrop, POWER in its one and a half years of existence has recorded tremendous achievements including operation of a mini transportation system in Abi Local Government area, refurbishing and commissioning of a garri processing facility also in Abi and the acquisition of an ultra-modern cassava processing centre located at Okpudu in Yala Local Government Area.
Yet future projects of POWER are very captivating in economy of scale for the State. These include the establishment of ethanol refinery in collaboration with some State Ministries, installation of cassava processing centres in all the Local Government Areas in the State and the establishment oil palm mills and rice mills also in all Local Government Areas of the State.
Indeed, today the POWER project for Her Excellency, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke is competing nationally in market products like POWER TM Cassava Flour, POWER TM Starch, and POWER TM Garri, a development that has continued to empower the women folk in the Sate. Yet, there is the other high yielding programme component of POWER dividends known as A State, fit for Child "ASFAC, which was also launched by Her Excellency Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke in May, 2008.
The programme aims at promoting academic excellence among school children in Cross River State, and that was well demonstrated by her Excellency last year in an Essay Competition during the 2008 Carnival organized by ASFAC in conjunction with the Cross River Carnival Commission.
There is also the Mother Against Child Abandoment (MACA) as a major component of POWER. Some of the objectives of this laudable programme are to save the life of vulnerable mothers and children, and reduce the incidence of child abandonment and abortions. As if these programme components of POWER are not enough, there is also the micro-credit scheme between POWER ad the Central Bank of Nigeria, a programme that has empowered many rural women in the State
Finally, there is the REFUGE programme, another POWER component initiative of Her Excellency which is geared towards providing shelter for abandoned babies and succour to their mothers who are mainly single adolescents and rejected by society. The programme has rehabilitated many of such categories of people.
With all these strings of initiatives and achievements, there is no doubt as to why Her Excellency, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke, has continued to receive prestigious awards from all nooks and crannies of the country.
These wards include the "Standard Organization of Nigeria (SON) Prestigious Certificate and Seal for Her Excellency's Cassava Flour product; and Best Support First Lady for Women Empowerment (Micro Credit) for 2008 from the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN)".
These awards amply justify the fact that the First Lady in the State, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke has become the first women leader to sensitize and a removed the inertia of rural women in the State to be alive to their obligations in helping to implement national and state policies, especially those seeking to better their living conditions.
To that extent, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke today represents an ideal woman of virtue, a pride of womanhood and an epitome of beauty plus elegance.
She is, indeed, a great model, mentor, a lady with an enduring visionary drive, a motivational compass, a shepherding force and above all a champion of the Nigerian Women.
These qualities have no doubt been responsible for the command of followership which Her Excellency, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke, enjoys from the mass of Cross Riverians including women, men and children from the State and beyond.
In all, one highly commends, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke for her mastery in social engineering, a complementary role which she has continued to demonstrate in the most excellence governance of Cross River State with her tireless and pragmatic, dynamic, innovative and prudent husband, Governor Liyel Imoke.
And finally one joins Theodore Roethke to echoe his maxim that "What we need are more people who specialize in the impossible". For that is exactly what Her Excellency, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke has amply demonstrated in the past one and a half years through her pet project, POWER, a project that should be nationalized because of its national value contents.
Dr. Okpo Ojah is a Research Fellow at the Institute of Public Policy and Administration of the University of Calabar

Original_Search_Title: Halfway Scorecard - Rise And Fall of the Black Gold
Headline: Nigeria: Halfway Scorecard - Rise And Fall of the Black Gold
Body_Text:
Just emerging from yet another round of fuel crisis, the country is neck deep in a bigger crisis whereby its oil output has been cut drastically because of activities of militants in the Niger Delta. A report last year said government might have lost $20.7 billion to the Niger Delta uprising.
At the same time, revenues have nosedived, thanks also to the crash in oil prices that was further accentuated by the global economic meltdown.
Midway into the President Umaru Yar'adua government, Nigeria's earnings have been down by half. What it means is that the country's ability to carry out its developmental projects may suffer setbacks.
Last year, government's coffers were buoyed by high oil prices that peaked at $147 a barrel.
The prices of crude oil per drum at the international market have since kissed the dust. The prcies are now rallying though.
The prices, which now dance around $50 and $60 a drum, are not far off from the country's 2009 budget benchmark of $45 a barrel.
Nigeria is the most oil dependent economy in the world. The new government is yet to break this jinx while its over 140 million people that need 30 million litres of petrol a day are grossly undersupplied.
The recent fuel scarcity was attributed to the inability of the Petroleum Equalization Fund and the NNPC to offset the debt subsidy owed marketers. This brought about suspension of importation and bridging as the banks were not loaning marketers again.
But Minister of State for Petroleum Odein Ajumogobia explained that the fuel shortage was caused by the stoppage of supply by the major oil marketers, as a result of a disagreement over the template on how the prices of petroleum products should be fixed. The NNPC is responsible for 47 per cent supply of oil in the country. Last year alone government was said to have paid about N700 billion in subsidising petroleum products imported into the country.
While this was going on, experts say the bombshell is that Nigeria will cease to be on the map of oil producing nations and thus may be delisted from OPEC in some three decades.
But the country has made enormous fortune from oil since it was discovered at Oloibiri in Bayelsa State on June 10, 1956. According to oil players, Nigeria might have raked in up to $300 billion from oil in the past 30 years.
Oil has not helped Nigeria in developing its infrastructures and the refineries.
One of the major steps taken by the Yar'adua administration is the reversal of the sale of refineries. But experts say even if the refineries were working at full capacity, they would only be able to refine 336,000 barrel of oil per day.
A remarkable achievement of the NNPC during the period under review, some would say, is that the Warri Refinery and PetroChemicals are now working at virtually full capacity; the Kaduna refinery has also bounced back although it is undergoing turn around maintenance; while the Port Harcourt refinery is operating at less than full capacity.
One other feature of the Yar'adua oil credential is that the quantity of oil the country produces is not quite clear. The figures keep changing because of cuts caused by militants' strikes on oil facilities. Another major feature of this administration is the controversial deregulation of the oil sector.
But Venezuela, an oil producing state, has warned Nigeria against the dangers of deregulation, saying it is counterproductive at this moment. The South American nation's Deputy Minister of Petroleum Dr. Bernard Mommer said: "Oil is too important that you cannot afford to leave it to market economy. You have to intervene when activities of foreign companies are having adverse effect on your economy. In our country, many of the multinational companies at one time had the majority stake and were making excess profit.
Our national oil company could not have the majority stake. The effect was that Venezuela collapsed." Industry players say the solution to Nigeria's oil problem is to develop private refineries. Out of the 12 private refineries owners who secured licences to operate refineries in Nigeria, only two are said to be doing something with their licences.
But the Chief Executive Officer of Oando Plc Mr. Wale Tinubu said price capping appeared to be the reason why petroleum marketers in the country are giving private refinery ownership a wide gap.
He said: "If one is involved in developing refineries, one would be selling the products at the minimum price because the price is capped, and somebody must pay the difference, and that somebody must be the government, because it is the one who capped the price."
One equally important major stride in the administration of President Yar'adua is the planned unbundling of the the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC). To this end, an executive bill has been sent to the National Assembly to overhaul the oil sector. The bill seeks to implement the recommendations of the Oil Sector Implementation Committee (OSIC) to restructure the oil and gas sector.
The bill seeks to transform NNPC into a commercially viable oil giant that will compete with Shell, ExxonMobil, Total, Chevron and other multi-national oil companies in oil block bidding, exploration, development and production, both locally and internationally.
The new NNPC, according to the bill, will be called the Nigerian National Petroleum Company (NNPC) Limited and is expected to operate like Brazil's Petrobras, Malaysia's Petronas, Saudi Arabia's Aramco and other government-owned but commercially viable national oil companies that scout for oil blocks anywhere in the world, without relying on their home government for funding.
The bill will also set up the Nigerian Petroleum Inspectorate (NPI) to replace the current Department of Petroleum Resources (DPR) and will be responsible for the technical regulation of all upstream and downstream activities. Another provision of the bill is the setting up of the National Petroleum Assets Management Agency (NAPAM) to regulate the cost and commercial operations of the upstream sector.
In the downstream sector, the bill seeks to create a Petroleum Products Regulatory Agency (PPRA) as the downstream commercial regulator. PPRA will among other things, issue, renew, suspend or cancel permits or licences and also ensure that quality service is provided by the operators.
For the new bill to become an effective law, all the existing laws regulating the oil and gas sector are to be repealed. Some of the laws to be repealed include the Petroleum Act 1969 and Amendments, Petroleum Profit Tax Law and Amendments, NNPC Act 1977, PPPRA Act and the Oil Pipelines Act. Others are the Associated Gas Re-injection Act and Regulations, Petroleum Equalisation Fund (PEF) Act, Petroleum Technology Development Fund (PTDF) Act and other outdated laws in the oil sector.
Threats of strikes by tanker drivers--and the actual strike actions--have also become a recurring decimal during the past two years of the Yar'adua administration. Nigeria is also blessed with over 187 trillion cubic feet of natural gas reserves; the 7th in the world. The Yar'adua government is partnering with Russian gas company, Gazprom to develop the nation's gas deposits, which includes those in the North.
A memorandum of Understanding has been signed between New Nigerian Development Company (NNDC) and Gazprom over the exploration of oil in the North.
While the country oil sector suffers locally and internationally, and the fact that the Nigeria's oil is fast depleting, there seems to be a gradual effort at diversifying the country's source of income. One of which is the real sector and the other is the development of bio-fuel. Both have been rocked by controversies. On the bio-fuel and biodiesel, the government has been encouraging local and international investors.
To this end, the NNPC has completed two bankable feasibility studies for oil palm-bio-diesel projects in two sites in Cross River State. It has also identified potential investors. The development of bio-fuel through sugarcane and cassava are ongoing. Has the Yar'adua government recoreded any acheivemnets in the oil and gas sector in the past two years? Many experts say even if the governmnet has done anything in this respect, it is yet to manifest in the lives of Nigerians.

Original_Search_Title: How I Won Ekiti Re-Run Poll - Gov. Oni
Headline: Nigeria: How I Won Ekiti Re-Run Poll - Gov. Oni
Body_Text:
Penultimate week, the inconclusive rerun election in Oye Local Government, Ekiti State took place. The poll which was supposed to be the decider turned out to be an anti climax. Out of the estimated 18,000 votes, less than 3000 people actually turned out. Rather Oye, however, it was Ido Osi that decided the winner. In this interview with Vanguard, less than forty-eight hours after he was sworn in, Gov Segun Oni explained how the controversial election was won and lost. Excerpts:
What experience would say you've gained, between the time you were away in the last three months and now that you are back?
Well, I want to say that the first experience is to believe that God is not on leave. He continues to preside over the affairs of man, and that no matter what we think we have in capabilities and resources, ultimately God will take His own decision and His decision is supreme. We have been in government, out of government and now that we are back in government, I think the most important thing is to acknowledge the Hands of the Almighty God.
Talking of the Hands of Almighty God, when God does a thing He completes it. Do you think this battle is over, especially at present?
Well, as far as I'm concerned, I can't declare it over. What I know is that no matter what they try to do, God is still God. They will employ all sorts of devices, falsehood especially, and we are not underestimating them to declare the battle over but we know they will always try and they know also that they will always fail.
AC has said it wil go to court, are you ready for that?
You see, that was what I expected them to do first rather than the kind of charade we see on the television on the day the result was declared. I would have expected that they recognize that the law courts are there and I'm convinced that is where we'll meet next.
Probably they thought that going to court would take a long time and they felt that their expectations may not be met?
Well, going to court may take a long process but it is the only reasonable process. And I don't think any other option is available for them other than to go to court. Option of civil disobedience or whatever they were trying to do is not a feasible option.
Oye rerun election came up May 5 and it was supposed to be the decider but as it turned out, it was an anti-climax of a sort that produced less than 3,000 votes even though the place is said to have over 18, 000 registered voters?
You should recognize the fact that there were so much violence in the place and these people were scared away. The first time we were to have the election, the turn-out was good but they prevented the elections from holding. People were tired, so, the turnout was very low. But generally, turnout was low in this election and the reason was that people couldn't take unnecessary risk because of what they have seen.
They saw thugs from all over the country virtually converging on Ekiti to do mercenary work. And at times, at night, they(thugs) will just be shooting into the air as if that gives them some kicks. So, people were generally scared and that made turnout to be very low. The only place where turnout couldn't be low, no matter the reason, was in my home town. The entire place said if they bring nuclear devices, they would rather get killed on the queues than get scared away from voting and they did. But I can understand. Where there is not much at stake rather than defending what you believe is right, p
eople will be scared and that was what happened in Oye. People were really scared.
But Oye was supposed to be the stronghold of the PDP as the party had earlier said that AC will not get a single vote there?
Well, I must say that I'm even surprised that they got as much votes because our projections was that they would hardly get more than what they have. And I don't want to believe that our people were more scared than theirs. But I'm still happy that at least we've shown that the terrain belongs to us.
Don't you think Oye was over policed, that even in some polling units there were more policemen than voters.This probably scared the people rather than the action of thugs?
No, in fact, that saved the situation, I must tell you. The people who said they don't want the military were saying that because they had an agenda to bring in thugs and they blackmailed government to hands off the issue of military. Soldiers came to Oye but they were not involved in voting like you noticed.
If we had brought the military earlier to secure the entry points, probably we would have had a much more peaceful election than we had. And the election would have been concluded on the first day. But I think that is the way God wants it.
But that there were more than enough policemen, yes, the police couldn't take chances because even at that, the opposition still tried to bring in some fake mobile policemen.
They were intercepted and arrested in Ire but somehow got released before proper documentations were made of who they are, but we have some photographs to show who they are anyway. So, they still tried but police presence was so intimidating for them to be able to breakthrough with their thugs. The lesson is that evil will always succumb to the fire power of good.
How do you rate Oye election in terms of conduct even though you said you did not expect AC to get as much vote as they got?
I think it was very free and fair election and I don't think anybody on both sides are complaining about Oye election. It was free and fair. The only problem that we had was voter turnout that was very low, much lower than anybody could project.
Probably you were expecting a larger turnout?
Yes, we were expecting a much larger turnout.
Probably that was the vote PDP was counting on to cancel whatever edge AC had?
No, we were looking at everywhere. We didn't have a large turn out there and we canceled whatever edge. We were banking on everywhere and if you go and study the voting pattern and characteristics, you will know that we won much local governments than they did. So, we were not looking at a particular place, we were looking at everywhere and we believed our vote will come from everywhere.
Some people believe that it was the large presence of policemen in Oye that actually prevented PDP from rigging and made the turnout that low and you're telling us that...?
(Cuts in) You see, I believe that people should first get out of this stereotype. The people who rig election are mostly AC. In the last election Ishon, which is a quarter of Oye in size and population, turned out 3000 plus for AC and 34 votes for PDP and at the end of the day, they went and destroyed about 16 houses. I understand they burnt some houses again in Ishon. You people don't even ask questions.
So, anything they do is right because they have brainwashed everybody to believe that the other people are always going to be the ones guilty. One of those making noise at the collation centre, this gentleman you are seeing here, Akinsegun, (pointing at him) won election fair and square in his constituency.
Today Segun Babalola is not in the house and they said he was defeated with a majority of seven votes only. They counted only four wards out of six. The two wards of Ire where we had a majority of more than 5, 000 votes over the AC, were not counted. They snatched the ballot box and tore the papers, may be in connivance with some people, otherwise, they would have called for duplicates. But they went and declared the result in only four wards, a majority of seven votes, where there were two wards outstanding.
It is a coincidence we have this man (Akinsegun Babalola) here. People are not asking questions and you could know how pained this gentleman will be. He won election fair and square, somebody said hoodlums tore election results and because of that they declared another person winner. And yet they will be the ones to say somebody rigged. If the PDP had done that, I'm sure they probably would have called for UN sanction against Ekiti State.
You people should try and find out where the facts are. In the 2007 elections we had no results from ten wards in Efon local government. Who caused it? Also there were no results in Ilejemeje local government, two wards in Idun and in Eda Oniye two wards. Who caused it? In his local government, Ire, which was torn, both the House of Assembly and the governorship ele
ctions were supposed to be hijacked. In Oke Mesi, there were no result in two wards.
And, you see, they were the ones responsible so it didn't form part of their petition and winners don't petition otherwise, we would have also petitioned that they were responsible for such things and therefore they are not people of character. So, when they come round and say things and people choreograph it, as far as I'm concerned, most of these things are of very little value to me because I know the truth.
And I know that it is mere propaganda. So, when I read it, it is like trash to me. Where rigging occurred, we know who is responsible. You see, we should understand that Nigeria is approaching a situation where you have falsehood virtually taking over the terrain that should have been guided more by conscience and these guys are paying to arrest the conscience of most people. So, you people(press)who should be in the vanguard of truth are now apostles of falsehood. They will say things they didn't see.
They will say tings they didn't know and they will choreograph songs they don't understand just because they want to satisfy the whims of some pay masters. And nobody even ask questions: where are they getting the money? If somebody else is said to have stolen ten naira, I'm sure the press would be filled with talks about that.
When they sermonise and talk about Obama's election and I always challenged them: would their grand children and my own grand children or great grand children be president in America with the image that some of them have given to Nigeria as ? If Kenya has somebody who has Kenyan blood ultimately becoming president in the United States of America, part of it is the fact that the image of the average Kenyan is not that bad.
They may not be rich but they are people of dignity. And being poor does not bar anybody form attaining the ultimate, but being crooked does. And if you have a country where people who attained the heights that some of them attained and are still involved in the kind of things the are doing, you want the world to take us serious? They are destroying the fabric on which all of us are supposed to be standing, looking at the future with brightness.
Out of the ten local governments where the rerun took place, which one is the largest?
I think, eleven wards in Ido-Osi; if it is local government by local government, I will think Ikole, with twelve wards. But it is not all the twelve wards in Ikole that contested the rerun election. So, I would think Ijero or so, with twelve wards, but again, not in all the twelve wards that election took place. Ido-Osi is the only place where you have eleven wards contesting the election and the largest number of votes in the rerun election came in from Ido-Osi.
The contention is that Ido Osi is not the largest local government, how come it produced the largest number of votes?
That is a very stupid contention. It has the largest number of wards contesting. There are local governments that has eight wards. In Oye local government, for example, only two wards were involved. Were they going to say that Oye is probably larger then the two wards should produce a larger result? That's why I said it is a stupid contention. If they are ready to talk about Ido Osi, we're ready to engage them.
First Ishon, Ilafo, Elemesho, three towns make a ward. They produced 2000 plus votes for Fayemi out of a total of about 10, 000 votes all together that the AC ended up getting in a local government of twelve wards. So, one ward produced about a quarter or more than a quarter of what of what they got in twelve wards. That does not sound obtuse for people. And let me tell you, I'm not ready to address this yet in a comprehensive manner but I will take you back to history. Everywhere people contested elections on a large expanse of land, they will always fall back on the loyalty and the interest of their people.
When Papa Ajasin contested election, Owo people were very solid. When Olumilua contested election, he had more votes in Ikere than the population of Ikere, I'm not saying that is good, and these are the people who are pointing fingers now, to tell you the kind of home advantage that they enjoyed. Niyi Adebayo should show us how many votes other people got in Inyi, when he contested election. Are these not also rational people?
Or were they not rational votes? And that's the way it goes. Fayose should tell us how many votes he got in his home town when he contested elections. Or will it just become different because it is now Segun Oni? Yes, I enjoy the confidence of my people. Apart from that, Ido Osi has always been a PDP stronghold. When Niyi Adebayo won election in 1999, he did not win in Ido Osi.
The AD has never won elections in Ido Osi. So, when it became AC also, we expected that it will not win an election in the council area.This place has been what you can call the traditional home of the PDP. So,why will it now be different when an Ido Osi man is contesting, if we used our (Ido-Osi) votes to support a Fayose, for example?
Let me tell you the story of the Fayose election. He is alive and can deny or confirm it. In 2003, I personally told the PDP, (because I didn't quite like his character) 'look, this is our party, so, he is our baby. Whether he is a man of character or not, our party has chosen him, he will win elections'. I said, 'leave me to concentrate on Ifaki, two wards. Don't bother to bring anything; don't bring any resource, including paying party agents and all whatever.
Don't even campaign there. Just wait for the result of the election in Ifaki'. And I delivered results of the two wards to them and took it to the collation centre myself. If people could trust me and voted for somebody for somebody like him you think they won't trust me enough to vote for me, even on my own reputation and character? Why will I suddenly lose all that goodwill, because I'm governor, and not a governor that did not perform? If there was anything, it should be stronger.
If there was anything at all, my goodwill had widened. Ido Osi has 151 or so pooling units. Every polling unit is an independent entity. What happened in one unit may not bear resemblance of what happened in the others. If there was fight in one pooling unit, it does not mean that it will extend to the others. Why haven't they complained about pooling unit 'A' or polling unit 'B'? Why are they talking of Ido Osi as if everybody in Ido Osi lined up in one particular location and cast vote in a particular location? It is eleven wards, why didn't they talk about specifics.
They said, 'oh, we don't want Ido Osi votes'. What reasons? Did they over vote? Where they stuffing ballot boxes? Did they fight? Look, people who want to be taken seriously must be able to adduce very tactical reason and it is a surprise that people who are supposed to be knowledgeable also jump on that bandwagon without asking questions. What is the problem with Ido Osi votes?
They claimed there was violence?
Where was the violence? I want to tell you; maybe they are not saying it. PDP did not win all the wards in Ido Osi. Would that not be a surprise to you? And if it was an overrun, why didn't we overrun everywhere? And if they talk of violence, how many places was violence reported to the extent that it disrupted election? Or did they bring violence from the sky and dropped it in Ido Osi? I did not move out of my town during the election. And I didn't see any violence. Why was it not reported in the dailies the following day?
They have a lot of media people who were there to work for them. Why didn't they say, 'massive violence in unit two' or 'exceptional violence in unit three'. Now, violence is becoming an issue but it was not reported that violence marred Ido Osi election the day after. These people rely on falsehood. Let them come up with specific charges and we will answer them.
I want you to put the record straight, the Resident Electoral Commissioner (REC) at a point refused to go further with the announcement of results. She claimed she resigned because she was being put under pressure. Would you want to tell Nigerians exactly what went wrong at that time?
I will not be surprised, from what I saw, on the final day, because those guys were desperate, they could have put pressure on her because even right there in the glare of television and so on, they wanted to intimidate her to surrender. So, are you now surprised that such people could not have sent her threat messages or tried to intimidate her in whatever form. And if she believes that it will put her life in danger, she will definitely be afraid, because from the desperation they exhibited that day, the way they were shouting and howling, with great outbursts and so on.
One said he was going to die there. What will his death do? Will his death rewrite what has been written? And this is the same guy who tore somebody's result; a guy who should be in jail under normal condition for stealing election result and tearing it. He deliberately through falsehood prevented an innocent man from enjoying the fruit of his labour.
The impression many people have is that pressure was being pilled on Mrs Adebayo to announce a specific result which she said was against her conscience, and perhaps, what people are pointing at was the same Ido-Osi result?
Why would Ido-Osi result be against anybody's conscience? The electoral process is very simple. You vote at the polling booth, the results are counted there, and when they are counted there, it is recorded and everybody will sign, including officials and that is recorded in form EC8A.
But AC said its agents did not sign the result?
No, the Court has said that an agent not signing does not invalidate a result because they know that an agent may also be unhappy if his side loses. There were results from the polling units, EC8A, then at ward levels, all the EC8As are added together to make EC8B.
The EC8Bs were also duly signed and collated. So, who will announce any result that will not conform to EC8As and EC8Bs? Since we have been talking, unless they go out to manufacture, they have not come up with alternative to say that there was something wrong with EC8As that we all have.
I mean, everybody gets copies. Why haven't they come out to say, 'look, Ido-Osi, they said it is 15, 000 plus, it would have been 5, 000 plus, somebody added one in front' or something like that to now prove it using the form EC8A. Nobody has been able to do that and all they do is to shout about Ido-Osi result. My own thinking is that if they wanted her to do anything different from what form EC8As and EC8Bs will not support, as a woman of conscience she will not go for that because that will be the only way to announce a fake result, a result that will not be supported by EC8As and EC8Bs.
They did not say that there was violence in so and so place or there was no election in a particular place. Everybody was there; observers, both genuine observers and those who were their paid agents, local government chairmen, some unsuccessful candidates of the party and so on who masqueraded all over the place as observers. All of them could not say that they saw falsification in a particular unit or genuine problem in unit so, so and so.
I'm very happy that when they started their singing and choreographing, the REC made also an addendum to what you said that the pressure was coming firm both sides. But I'm sure that the pressure on our own side will not be for anything because we don't need any false report. The genuine results were enough to vindicate us.
On the day of collation of results at Christ school, were you surprised that rather than the REC announcing the result on local government by local government, she called on each electoral officer to come and do that?
I think that was a very good way because they were the ones who conducted the election and I think it was accountability in the full glare of everybody, 'come and announce what you brought' and 'it is your conscience, it is your work, so, it is not anybody being held responsible'. And you could see them. I had been a student of psychology from age 14 and you could psycho-analyse people from their confidence, because when people tell the truth, there is every confidence that go with it. When people are telling lies, there is always uncertainty that goes with it.
But there are some professional liars?
Well, like I said before, falsehood always stands on unstable equilibrium, you only need to apply a little force and it will tumble. I don't think also that everybody will go into school of lying because he wants to be involved in a normal exercise like collating election result. I don't think it will warrant that they should go to lying schools. What will they gain? I can see that asking them individually to present their results makes a lot of sense.
But people are saying that it was a kind of distancing herself from the results, like saying, 'at least you are the one that announced the result, not me'?
No, she announced the ultimate...
But the ultimate that gave rise to the...?
(Cuts in) But then, you see, result must emanate from somewhere and she cannot pretend that she was the one who was present everywhere. The person who was in charge to present the result. But she accepted it as the over-all boss and that's what she did. You see, when we were younger in the business school, one of the first things they told us was that a manager works through other people. The manager is not the hands or man at every desk. But she is the custodian of the over-all result because all those people would have worked for him and their result becomes his result. That's the way it should be. And we should not stand logic on its head.
As soon as the ultimate was announced at about 9: 05 p.m. and even before then there was tension in the city and immediately the result was announced the whole place went on fire in Ado-Ekiti. This my colleague and I were nearly lynched that night and we had to run for dear lives ?
Well, they brought thugs for that purpose. We have always been saying it. The thugs were even supposed to start violence simultaneously everywhere but they know that people were also waiting for them. They knew they were going to lose and they brought their thugs from all over the place to come and create problems. I'm not going to accept the fact that because people bring their own thugs and asked them to create problems will mean that it is the mood of the people. If that was the mood of the people, they would'nt have gone back home to rest even if you scare them away. But because that was not their mood, it became very easy to handle.
You mean thugs will come and barricade and make bonfires on major roads in Ado-Ekiti, people who don't know the terrain very well to the extent that I slept in a students' hostel because I couldn't go to my hotel?
What does it cost for thugs to operate? They were prepared for that because that was what they were paid to do. As far as they were concerned, they had told them, this is what you should do and they went on to do it. And they did it in few places. Why were they doing it on those major roads? If it was a people related issue, you will stand up wherever you are. You won't come to the main road, the dual carriage way to come and vent your anger; anger that has been in you from home? So, you should understand. Once you psycho-analyse the situation, you will just understand what I'm taking about that they were paid by their masters.
Now that you are back on a fresh four year mandate, what will be the relationship between the executive and legislature?
The relationship between the executive and the legislature has always been good, I must say, except for one incident that was created by the AC legislators when we were presenting the budget, otherwise I would say it has been very good. The legislature that we have, when I was there, was the most productive in Nigeria.
They passed more bills than any other House and the business of the legislature is to make laws and if the output was that high, it must mean that there was nothing seriously amiss because if there was anything wrong, the output would not have been that high. Now that we're back, I believe that it will even be better because those who have been trying to ensure that AC members of the House will not see anything good in government were doing that because they thought it will improve their chances. Now that they have seen that their chances did not improve, I'm sure they will also be tired. And I'm sure AC legislators themselves will look at the interest of Ekiti State and would not allow anybody to engage then in perpetual obstruction of the progress of the state.
There was this allegation by the former acting governor and the current Speaker, that the executive had perfected plot to remove him but the same week the Court of Appeal judgment came. How true is that?
Let me tell you, why will I want to remove somebody for the other in the House? If I had anybody in mind earlier, I would have canvassed for that person to be elected Speaker. I've never shied to tell people this is the position I stand. And during the little crisis they had amongst themselves on leadership, when the AC people first came to me to say, 'look, give us somebody but we want to change our Speaker' and I said, 'look.
I don't have any interest in who becomes your Speaker. I have interest in things running well'. If I had anybody in mind, then it would have been temptation for me to now mention names. I have the kind of background that makes me able to work with anybody and in any circumstance. I had worked as a manager outside the shores of this country. Even where you could say that the average Nigeria does not have the kind of reputation to make people trust him, I ran a multinational business that was incredibly successful. And I had to work through people. People first had a bias, 'ah, Nigerian?
What will a Nigerian be doing here as an expatriate?' I can work with anybody and that is me. I don't have to install a stooge anywhere to get things done. So, I will never have a hand in removing anybody whether at legislative or judiciary because I believe that they should take care of their own businesses. And that is why you should know that the judiciary and the legislature here enjoys financial independence.
If I want control, I'll keep the money and doll it out as favours. And it is not that I'm not smart enough to know that. It is because I'm confident enough to believe that I don't need that. Under Niyi Adebayo administration, nobody enjoyed financial independence. Even during Fayose administration, nobody did. It was not as if I met it like that but I introduced it, both for the judiciary and the legislature. I'm confident enough to believe that I'll be able to add value doing that.
If actually all is well between you and the House, how come the former acting governor said that you left a deficit account?
Well, I'm also going to believe that you also did not read the account of the House. Fortunately, the public account committee of the House spoke the day before and they said the balance was N4.182 billion. There can always be mistakes and that is how I see it. And since the house had addressed the press a day before with the correct figures, I didn't think it was necessary I should say anything. I believe that anybody who was sufficiently interested in knowing the truth will ask why the same body was saying different things and in the process, will know the truth. If the House was saying the same thing, of course, I would have been compelled to say something.
Now that you are back, will you recall the former commissioners and what will Ekiti people expect from you this time around?
We have a new government, therefore we're going to approach the fundamentals just like we should. We will nominate commissioners to the House and the process of getting commissioners will be done afresh so that it does not preclude anybody and it does not impose anybody. But I believe that it is very likely that some of the faces will be back and it is not also completely impossible that none of the faces will be back. We're going to approach the issue with a clean sheet of paper and we're going to consult with every stakeholder that should have a hand in it. And whatever comes out is the position of the new government.
What the people should expect?
The people should expect faster development, faster than what we had in first 21 months because a lot of gestations that we had to contend with are no longer necessary. For example, to award road contract then, we had to wait in some places to carry out a survey, conclude the design and in some cases we had to redesign. We had to advertise and analyse the tenders and eventually award the contract before we could get one kilometre of road done. Now, we can get 50 kilometres of roads done simultaneously because the contractors are already on sites in these places.
We have building projects going on at the Secretariat. I've been there to see the building projects that are going on within the state house premises. We don't need gestations to achieve that. We have awarded contracts for about a dozen water schemes. It took time to get the design and the whole process on. So, now we are not waiting for any design because the period you will say you will work within the substructure is over. And you know when you are working on the substructure, the result don't show.
Some chief executives have said that because an area did not vote for them that they will not extend developmental projects to them. What will be the fate of the areas that did not vote for the PDP in this election?
We're not running government for the first time and everybody knew that we extended development everywhere. For me, everywhere is Ekiti and I have a responsibility to develop this terrain. Somebody asked a question that I considered very unfortunate. He said most of the roads are inside the bush.
I said how there you call parts of Ekiti bush? Where we are putting a road from Odo Ero to Tapaju to Iyemero, cassava used to sell for N3, 000.00 per ton, but it is now selling for N42, 000 per ton because of access road and you want to say it is bush, where people have their lives tied to the economy of the productive measures they engaged in?
So, we are going to develop everywhere. And we're doing that, including a lot of places that have been virtually untouched by government since the inception of governance in this part of the world. We are now touching them because they are part of Ekiti and we cannot say because they are in remote areas, they should not enjoy the good life. Our basis for distribution of ameniies is not who voted and who did not vote. It is first on a need basis.
But that does not mean also that as human beings we will not have a softer spot for people who voted for us because it's largely on their goodwill that we are here. So, if there is any icing we need to put on their cake, we'll do it. And we'll do it without any apology to anybody. But we're not going to count anybody out because he did not vote. But we'll count some people doubly in because they voted.

Original_Search_Title: Minister of Industry Pleased With Sotecma Expansion
Headline: Angola: Minister of Industry Pleased With Sotecma Expansion
Body_Text:
Luanda — The Angolan minister of Industry, Joaquim David, Friday here recognized the investment made by Nova Sotecma company.
The information comes in a press note released Saturday to Angop, which states the acknowledgement of the minister during the visit he paid to the factory on Friday.
Nova Sotecma has production lines and manufacture of mill hammers for maize, cassava, palm oil, assembly up of motor-pumps and other activities.

Original_Search_Title: Mosquito Can't Order Arrest of Ex-Speaker
Headline: Ghana: Mosquito Can't Order Arrest of Ex-Speaker
Body_Text:
The Minority Leader, Mr. Osei Kyei-Mensah-Bonsu has questioned the basis for the call made by the General Secretary of the National Democratic Congress (NDC), Mr. Johnson Asiedu Nketiah, aka 'General Mosquito,' that the former Speaker of Parliament, Mr. Ebenezer Begyina Sekyi Hughes be arrested for theft.
According to the Minority Leader the Graphic report, which Asiedu Nketia relied on to demand the arrest of the ex-Speaker was incorrect, because Mr. Sekyi Hughes had not been ordered to refund any GH¢1.6 million for the items he took away from his official bungalow.
Speaking in an interview with The Chronicle yesterday, the Minority Leader said, "The Graphic story was a perpetration of falsehood, so if the basis of the General Secretary's statement was falsehood, then God save us."
Mr. Kyei-Mensah-Bonsu further quizzed, "Who has made him the Interior Minister or the Inspector General of Police? When did the General Secretary of a party become the IGP or the Interior Minister?"
The Daily Graphic newspaper in its yesterday edition reported that an ad hoc committee, chaired by the Minority Leader in Parliament, had recommended that the former Speaker should refund GH¢1.6 million, which was the value of items he took away from his official bungalow, and a further refund of GH¢1 million for some of the items, and keep the rest.
Mr. Kyei-Mensah-Bonsu said the statement from General Mosquito seemed to suggest that the former Speaker was involved in thievery, adding that the position of the Speaker of Parliament was a reputable one which must not be tainted with defamatory statements.
He continued that the Parliamentary Service Board was still investigating the matter therefore it was premature for one to make a conclusive statement. "We ourselves do not know how much was involved," he indicated.
A statement signed by the General Secretary of the NDC yesterday, called on the President to order the immediate arrest and prosecution of Mr. Sekyi Hughes. The statement said, "This call is based on the party's anti-corruption agenda captured on page 24 of the 2008 manifesto of the party, which states in parts, 'an NDC government will not make any excuses for corrupt ministers, officials and office holders generally. We shall promptly investigate allegations of corruption, and allow the law to take its course,' simply put, the NDC will make corruption a high risk activity for political and public office holders."
It stressed that the party strongly disagreed with any suggestion that the former Speaker be allowed to go free after returning some of the items and paying for others, since this would amount to setting a bad precedent in our anti-corruption crusade.
"As a social Democratic Party, we maintain that all citizens of Ghana must be treated equally before the law, stealing is stealing, whether the item involved is a cock, cassava, or state furniture," the statement concluded.

Original_Search_Title: Kondengui - Admission Round the Clock
Headline: Cameroon: Kondengui - Admission Round the Clock
Body_Text:
