It is not a secret that Cameroon has seven State Universities. Though some of them have singled out themselves through brilliant academic results, their popularity is at stake with the emergence of the "University of Kodengui". Compared to other Universities, the University of Kodengui has two specific conditions for admission and award two types of certificates. The first for one to be admitted into this highly solicited institution is that, one must have committed an offence.
The least crime can be the stealing of someone's cocoyam, cassava or assault. Usually those admitted at this level graduate with a First Degree after about one to twenty-five years of study. The second category of students are those who graduate with a Master's Degree.
To be admitted for this Programme, one must have held at least a high profile position, must embezzle State funds worth hundred of millions and must go through an interview conducted by board members of "Epervier".
The duration of studies for the Master's Programme ranges from twenty-five years to Though in the past some students have appealed and the number of years reduced Who said we lack professional institutions in Cameroon Watch out, admission is all-round the year.

Original_Search_Title: Shocking Visit to Yola Prison Where Inmates Are 'Condemned' Before Trial
Headline: Nigeria: Shocking Visit to Yola Prison Where Inmates Are 'Condemned' Before Trial
Body_Text:
When visitors approach the Jimeta and Yola Central Prisons in Adamawa State, the overpowering stench of urine and refuse dumps around the prison area is the first thing which welcomes them.
In addition to that, armed guards staying by the gate bark "get back" at visitors in the attempt to cut short the visits and prohibit any further interaction with the detainees.
Investigations by Weekly Trust have revealed that a good number of the inmates who are both awaiting trial or who are convicts are HIV-positive and their cases are very pathetic. The State Comptroller of Prisons, Alhaji Ado Muhammad, had confirmed that their cases, especially those awaiting trial, were compounded by the lack of visits to the state prisons by the jail delivery committee of Adamawa State, which is headed by the Chief Judge, who's supposed to attend to certain legal matters that affect the liberty of the citizens.
The situation prompted a request from the prison and police authorities for the committee to visit the prisons so that they can assess the problem of congestion in the prisons by themselves.
"It's very unfortunate that the committee has not been performing its function and duty for more than a year now", said the Comptroller of Prisons.
Weekly Trust also gathered that a letter dated March 3, 2009 and addressed to the Chief Judge of the state was written from the office of the state Comptroller of Nigerian Prisons Service, stressing the need to intensify the speedy trial of the inmates who tested positive to HIV.
The letter signed by DCP Pivs B. B. Nokah said the environment in which the affected inmates are living is not conducive for people with HIV, and as a result, they should be given speedy trial or conditional release as had been done in some states to enable them to have access to proper medical attention.
Similarly, a letter dated February 24, 2009 and signed by an Assistant Commissioner of Police, ACP H. U. Makama, for the State Police Command, was written. The police expressed concern over the condition of the affected inmates with a view to coming to their aid. The jail delivery was established so that members could visit prisons and attend to certain important legal matters which affect the liberty of the citizens. The law that gave powers to the committee to go round the prisons and decongest them is titled: "Criminal Justice Release from Custody Provision Act of the Federal Government Laws of the Federation of Nigeria 1990 CAP 79".
While speaking on the plight of prison inmates who tested positive to HIV, the state Comptroller of Prisons, Ado Muhammad, had confirmed thus: "In Jimeta and Yola Prisons, there are more than 20 HIV-positive inmates, and these include those awaiting trial and the convicts". However, he was quick to point out, "The prison officials have been assisting them by taking them to health centres for counselling".
He also noted with concern that the committee members have for sometime not gone round the prisons in the state like other states in the federation, a situation which is now causing a lot of concern and problems on the decongestion of prisons.
Weekly Trust learned that the committee could not go round because it is financially constrained to embark on the visitation of prisons. Efforts to draw words out of the committee chairman who is also the Chief Judge of Adamawa State, Bamari Bansi, were not successful. Our reporter was also unable to reach the Permanent Secretary on Security who is the secretary of the committee, as they were all said to have been out of the office on special engagements.
During a visit to some prisons in the state, Weekly Trust gathered that most of the inmates are alone and hardly ever receive visitors; their families live too far away and have abandoned them for fear of being associated with their crime and some simply cannot pay the visiting rights fees charged by some of the wardens.
There's also the complaint of lack of adequate food. If the conditions for death row inmates are harsh, they are hardly any better for other prisoners. For the sick and weak, incarceration can be tantamount to a death sentence. "The two main problems in our prisons are congestion and lack of food," an Inspector who pleaded anonymity said. The State Comptroller, however, denied it, saying "We are trying our best to meet their demands".
The risk of death in the prisons remains high, particularly because of lack of food. The daily ration generally consists of a bowl of beans in the morning and cassava (garri) in the afternoon and evening. Weekly Trust further gathered in some of the prisons visited that lack of food more than any other thing aggravates the already poor hygiene conditions. Malnutrition makes prisoners highly vulnerable to infectious diseases such as tuberculosis and skin diseases. The situation is the same for female inmates who sleep on iron beds piled one atop the other, while the bathrooms have long been without running water.
"We are devoured by mosquitoes, we all suffer from malaria and don't have enough beds", said Maryam (not real name), 33, who has been incarcerated since last year. "There is nothing. We even have to share one sanitary towel between two women when we're menstruating".
The prison conditions are weighing heavily on the detainees, which often cause depression and other psychological problems, according to one prison welfare officer who didn't want his name mentioned. To survive in their environment, some prisoners have taken the law into their hands, as they have created a veritable 'government.' One prisoner is president, another is police chief and another is head of justice", a source added.
In some states, the jail delivery committee has the power to release prisoners who include those who have spent many years awaiting trial by reviewing their cases. Also eligible are the elderly and those that have stayed longer than the prospective sentence of their crime. The Prisons Comptroller in Adamawa, Ado Muhammad, emphasised the need for stakeholders in the judiciary to put hands on deck toward achieving prisons reforms in Nigeria.
A study carried out by an NGO, which specialises in sexual activities going on in prisons, found that there were homosexuals and lesbians in some prisons in Nigeria and inmates are not provided with condoms and there's very little AIDS education taking place.

Original_Search_Title: I Write Sad Stories Because I Am Too Happy - Toni Kan
Headline: Nigeria: I Write Sad Stories Because I Am Too Happy - Toni Kan
Body_Text:
Toni Kan is the author of a wonderful new collection of short stories called Nights of the Creaking Bed set in Lagos about diverse characters in affecting dramas and a brand new poetry collection, Songs of Absence and Despair which is described as "elegies to the times we live in; a dirge to those who we mourn though they are not dead and a lament for those who fled and can no longer return."
In this interview, Toni Kan reveals why he felt the need to write such a gritty, sexually charged collection of short stories as well as a sad and almost elegiac collection of poems at this time all published by Cassava Republic.
CAN I start by asking what you're reading at the moment, and do you have anything you'd like to recommend to our readers?
I just finished ready Aravind Adiga's Booker winner, The White Tiger which is an impressive debut. I like the way the book casts a harsh and ferocious light on the underbelly of India. Using irony and cynicism as twin props, he weaves a yarn that is unsparing and critical of the political and monied class.
And because it was Valentine, I returned to Pablo Neruda's "20 love poems and a song of despair" which contains some of the best lines I have read in love poems: "...you are sad, all at once, like a voyage" and "you gather things to you like an old road" and "I want to do with you what spring does with the cherry trees."
Neruda makes April look good unlike Eliot who wrote "April is the cruellest month." Pablo Neruda's book acted as a muse for me while completing my new book of poems, Songs of Absence and Despair. You can even see the echo of Neruda in the title.
How would you describe Nights of the Creaking Bed to someone who has not read any of your writing?
I dont like to describe my works. I would say go ahead and read it yourself. But because you asked, I'd say its 14 stories about life and death set mostly in Lagos even when the setting is not clearly alluded to. They are stories about our daily lives as we live it in an uncaring world.
The collection is funny in places but some of the stories do go to some really dark places. Some one has asked me why the stories are so sad and whether it has anything to do with my life. It doesn't.
Tragic twist
People who know me know that I am generally happy and I have a good life. I am not depressive. Maybe I am too happy I need some sad stories to ground me and make me see the reality.
Are you working on anything right now?
Yes. I am working on a novel. It's set in Lagos and it's basically a love story with a tragic twist. Writing it, I am thinking of Ben Okri's Flowers and Shadows, you know with the tension and dynamics between father and son, and a fledgling romance between two of the major characters. The working title is Silent Rage and its coming on the Cassava Republic imprint.
How do you manage writing poetry and fiction - do you write the different forms at different times?
The easy answer is to say that I write prose with my right and poetry with my left. Just kidding. Anyway, I generally do not make a conscious decision to write a poem or short story. Most times I get an idea and in a way, the idea insists on the form in which it will be presented. I have written poems that ended up as short stories and I have written a short story half way then turned it into a poem. It's never a conscious decision that one makes.
Running through all the stories are themes of sexuality and violence. Are you trying to say there is a connection in some weird Freudian way?
You know as a writer and a critic, I find these sorts of questions quite amusing and also food for thought. First, you know that you don't make a list as you start a story with headings like put Freudian slip here, add Oedipus complex, insert the Electra complex or whatever. The stories tell themselves in certain ways.
The best way to put this is that they are like rolling stones that gather moss as they trundle along. Things just attach themselves to a story. What I do know is that my stories take shape only after I have determined a particular character's back story. Something like; she is a teacher and has just quarreled with her boyfriend after making love.
Now what made this happen? Was she raped as teenager? Did she catch her boyfriend with her sister of friend?
You know once you get the psychology of the character right, every thing else falls into place Freud or not.
Are there any authors (living or dead) that you would name as influences?
Ben Okri of the early books: Flowers and Shadows, The Landscapes Within (Dangerous Love) and The Famished Road is a great influence because he is the major reason why I am a writer today. Then I like to read Toni Morrison for her superb handling of characterisation and story; J.M Coetzee for his spare and austere style; Salman Rushdie whom I love for his way with words; Sefi Atta for her peculiar and impressive way of writing about Lagos as well as the humour that permeates her works.
I love Jim Crace for his very peculiar style and how he can handle any subject with easy aplomb. There are a lot of them and you know making lists always leads one into trouble because some one always gets left out.
What was the book that most influenced your life - and why?
On the literary turf, I'd say the Holy Bible which I never tire to read, first as a Christian and then, as a writer because the Bible is actually as DH Lawrence said, "the big book of life" because it's got everything - crime, deception, murder, rape and incest, love and sacrifice and some of the best love poems ever written.
But as a writer credit goes to Ben Okri's Flowers and Shadows which I read as an eleven year old and made up my mind to become a writer. I liked the way Okri handled the change in fortunes of the Okwe household, the psychological depth he brought to bear.
I admire the way he treated Jeffia's mum's descent into depression, the choking and suffocating melancholy. Finally, I was moved by his portrayal of a young boy caught in the middle of a crisis and his incipient but flawed love with the young nurse. I hope I can achieve half of that in my forthcoming novel.
What are your 10 favorite books -and why?
Can't say why for all of them, so I will just give you a list: The Holy Bible because it's the Holy Bible.
Flowers and Shadows, The Landscapes Within (Dangerous Love), The Famished Road all by Ben Okri; Sula and Beloved by Toni Morrison; So Long A letter by Mariama Ba; An Equal Music by Vikram Seth, All the books by Jim Crace, The Road by Corman McCarthy, Half of a Yellow Sun by Chimamanda, Blink by Malcolm Gladwell because its kick-arse smart, Freakonomics by Stephen Levitt because its also kick-arse smart and then Everything Good Will Come by Sefi Atta because everything good is coming my way right now.

Original_Search_Title: Bauchi Crisis Victims Reject Govt's Aid
Headline: Nigeria: Bauchi Crisis Victims Reject Govt's Aid
Body_Text:
Bauchi — As the residents of Bauchi still live in fear over the crisis that erupted in some parts of the metropolis, the displaced persons in different camps scattered across the state capital have rejected the relief materials brought to them by the state government.
Lamenting bitterly, the displaced people said the state government has failed them for not putting necessary measures in place to salvage the situation. They were shouting "we don't need the food. Government should go with their food. We want to see the governor, Yuguda, who has not visited us since Saturday nor send any government official. We voted him into power but he has rejected us because we are Christians."
It was gathered that one of the displaced person at Shadawaka barracks was killed at Railway area yesterday when he left the barracks to pick some of his clothes at home and all efforts to reach the Police headquarters, Bauchi to confirm the situation proved abortive.
One of the displaced persons at Shadawanka barracks who lived in Railway area said she lost everything to the crisis. Alice Azuzu, a mother of three children also said her family lost everything they had to the crisis
According to her, "On that fateful morning, my children were still sleeping when my husband and I heard the noises and before we knew it, they entered our house. We had to run out through the backyard to save our lives. As I am talking to you now, they have burnt our house. We have nothing left.
"The most annoying thing," she said, "is that since we came to this camp, they have not been giving us food and the governor has not come to see us. Today (yesterday) makes it the fifth day that we got to this camp. They are now bringing us food. We don't want it. They should take it back and tell the governor we want to see him.
"One of our brothers that we were in this barracks together left yesterday morning to the house due the government's announcement on the radio and television that everything has come back to normal, that we should go back to our houses. He was killed. We are bitter," she stated
The food items brought to the displaced persons included one trailer of rice, one trailer of beans, a trailer of cassava flour, red oil, vegetable oil, milk, sugar, spaghetti, tomato, tubers of yam and a trailer of millet.
According to the state Commissioner of Information, Mohammed Abdulahi, the state government is making all efforts to see that the displaced persons are comfortable adding that, "we all know it is not easy to leave your house and stay in such environment. That is why we are making all efforts to see that they are comfortable."
He said, "The governor ordered the authority in charge to take food to displaced persons yesterday night when he heard they were complaining of food. You can not expect him to know everything immediately, because some of the people in charge might not brief him on the situation immediately."
The Secretary of the state Red Cross, Adamu Abubakar disclosed that over 5,000 displaced persons are taking refuge in different camps across the state.
... as group blames crisis on failed govt
Meanwhile, in Jos, a human rights group, the League for Human Rights has condemned the recent violence in Bauchi State, describing the frequent destruction of lives and properties in parts of the country as a failure of governance at various levels of government.
"The North cannot progress politically, socially and economically when there is no peace and when the region has become an abattoir where people's lives and peace are constantly destroyed," the group said in a statement.
"The major responsibility of any government the world over is the protection of lives and property of all persons in the state or nation irrespective of gender, religion, ethnic group, political affiliation, etc.
"Therefore, each time an individual is killed or property burnt in the name of political or religious crises, one is at a loss as to where government and all its security apparatus was that tax payers' money is used to fund and maintain," it added.
The body called on the Federal and Bauchi State governments to assist those affected by the crisis with relief materials and other measures that would ease their pain.
It sympathised with the victims and appealed to the people of the state to be more tolerant of each other and learn to live in peace and harmony.

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Set Up Software Testing Centre
Headline: Nigeria: Govt to Set Up Software Testing Centre
Body_Text:
The Kano State Government is collaborating with a U S-based computer company, Microsoft Corporation, to establish a software testing centre in the state.
The Commissioner for Science and Technology, Dr Bashir Galadanci, said this during a study tour of a counterpart ministry in Kaduna State.
The commissioner said the centre would assist the state to ensure a smooth take-off of its ICT Park.
Galadanci said the park was established to provide services that would launch the state into a technology-based state
" ICT would play a major role in transforming the economy into a private sector-driven one.
" Kano State is making ICT an economic venture that would provide services to neighbouring states and institutions.
" This is to enable the state earn more revenue and also create more jobs for the teaming jobless youths," he said.
Galadanci said the state had earmarked more than N1 billion to put his new ministry on the right footing.
According to him, the ministry would follow the foot steps of countries like India where ICT provides services and generates revenue.
" Most developed countries have turned to ICT as an economic sector.
" We in Kano, are working to ensure we follow such foot steps and develop ICT as an economic sector, not just as service provider.
" By doing that, we shall improve our revenue base and also educate our teeming population through science and technology to fast track development," Galadanci said.
The team is understudying the Kaduna State ethanol pilot scheme where cassava, sugar cane and maize are being used to produce ethanol fuel.
It is also understudying the Skills Acquisition Scheme where more than 5,000 youths are being trained in 20 centres across the state. (NAN)

Original_Search_Title: 40 Deaths Recorded Over Non-Payment of Staff Salaries
Headline: Nigeria: 40 Deaths Recorded Over Non-Payment of Staff Salaries
Body_Text:
Abuja — As the sad neglect of the Ajaokuta Steel complex continues, at least 40 of the complex's personnel, both serving and retired, have lost their lives, due to non-payment of their legitimate wages by the authorities.
Leadership SUNDAY reports that not less than 40 of various staff members of the company are no longer alive, as many of them could not afford the cost of medical care or decent meals.
An investigation by leadership Sunday indicates that within the first few weeks of this year alone, 19 member of staff and pensioners of the company have been dispatched to the great beyond.
Leadership SUNDAY
gathered that most of these deaths resulted from hunger and aliments, as both staff and pensioners could not afford the cost of medication and feeding.
Leadership SUNDAY
had, in July last year, reported some of the deaths, especially that of the family of one Mr J.J Daniel of Flat 23A, Geregu Camp, who reportedly died after consuming unprocessed cassava.
Our correspondent, who was in Ajaokuta, also learnt that the staff now had to carry out protests before being paid their legitimate wages, which have never been fully paid at any time.
Leadership Sunday
also learnt that after several struggles, part of the outstanding salaries and allowances for last year were paid, leaving salary arrears for October, November and December.
This is apart from the several allowances such as leave and many others due the staff for the whole of last year.
When contacted, chairman, Iron and Steel Senior Staff Association of Nigeria, Ajaokuta chapter, Mr Abdukareem Jimoh, confirmed the various deaths resulting from ill-health and hunger.
He attributed it to the non-payment of salaries and other allowances.
He also told Leadership Sunday that most of the staff can no longer send their children and wards to school due to financial crunch.
Mr Jimoh, expressed fears that if nothing was done urgently to save the staff and pensioners in this year's budget, more of his colleagues may be sent to their early graves.
But Leadership SUNDAY's investigation reveals that the salary and other allowances are not reflected in this year's budget, instead, one billion naira was budgeted for technical audit of the company.
It was also gathered that the staff union executives were at the National Assembly between Thursday and Friday to seek the inclusion of their salaries and allowances in this year's budget but were not successful.
Similarly, their efforts to have audience with the minister for power and steel, Mrs Deziani Alison Madueke, were also fruitless as she was said to have travelled out of the country for an official assignment.

Original_Search_Title: Igbo's Vanishing Values
Headline: Nigeria: Igbo's Vanishing Values
Body_Text:
EVERY year, the Igbo people are wont to gather at any convenient place in any city in Igbo land for one symbolic show of ethnic solidarity or the other. When it is not the new yam festival, it is the annual Ahiajoku lecture. When it is not South East Economic Summit (as a later day sub socio-economic agenda, it is South East People's parliament (this one also a sub politico agenda. There are also obvious Pan Igbo gatherings - in and out of the country - the Ohanaeze annual meet, the world Igbo congress meet, etc. of course the just concluded Ozuruimo cultural festival (this one in the sub-socio-cultural agenda) was one of them. It hold in Owerri, capital city of the hosting state.
This year's Ozuruimo lecture like all such gatherings provided platforms for some Igbo elite and local folks present to ventilate and lament as usual the obvious retardation of prime Igbo cultures and norms for which the Igbo man is respected in Lagos, Abuja and all over the world.
If ever there is any remnant or regard of Igbo culture beyond wrestling, dancing and drinking of 'palmy', it is only observed in parsimonious histrionics, reminiscences and as tools for up cast political posturing and jockeying by those elite who know how to play the ethnic card (as inaugurated by Awolowo) to get or to get over certain things in Abuja and elsewhere.
This class is wont to abandon this Pan Igbo posturing ones that objectives have been attained. There is no doubt at all that all the demeaning sloganeering on Igbo culture and values like the Ahiajoku feast are being hyped by those who will soon reject or even deny their Igboness ones opportunity pushes them out of the eastern enclave to anywhere they believe their paradise can be fabricated.
This is because if one digs deep around the spheres these people operate, he would discover that they themselves are doing little in their homes, places of work or communities in living the Igbo ways they preach. Are institutions in Igbo land truly behaving and doing things in manner that stamps the identity of Igbo norms? Do government's developmental programmes and methods reflect the core values of Igbo ethnic such as prudence, justice, solidarity, and recognition of common aspirations?
Do our politicians in Igbo land recognize the virtues of consensus, sacrifice, service, contentment, brotherhood bond, and our culture of protecting one's brother's interests. What about famous Igbo virtues such as recognizing self-worth, pride in one being himself, justice, fairness, individual's solemn rights, integrity, bravery, and the scripture-recommended good life-since by anthropology, Igbo's have been confirmed to be remnants of the Jewish state of Israel.
Without deceiving ourselves, the present generation of Igbo have been watching their inherited ways of life and values rescinding under the weight of greed, opportunism, betrayal of collective good, and such other granny behaviours now preponderant in our individual, group and institutional intercourse. What we are witnessing today is an Igbo man who has virtually forgotten how to speak his language, talk less write it - at a time when other nations and groups are cueing their languages into international language translation pods like Hollywood, Bollywood, Microsoft, IBM and all such global platforms of mass communication. Yet Igbo has a preponderant of wheeler-dealer tycoons with high net worth investments abroad and elsewhere outside Igbo land.
A certain regional business journal last year disclosed after a year-long survey that Igbo investments outside Igbo land grossed N3 trillion. Unfortunately, even as the drumbeats of discontentment resonate from all groups and nations in the country against perceived threats to their people's interest arising from decades of mismanagement of the commonwealth, Igbo still behave or seem contented in sending signals suggesting she bothers less whither over her destiny lies - even if under the rubble of yet unfounded, but surging smaller power zones.
Come to Abuja and behold how an Igbo man would sit over a bottle of beer and castigate the federal government for ever dreaming of building an East-West rail line because such a project in his crazy illiterate calculations would likely subject his auto-transport business into unprepared competition? Is that a commending Igbo thinking pattern ? When did the Igbo man who until he became a transport magnate was a mere illiterate mechanic and spare parts dealer become a World Bank expert on transport - econometrics?
You hear with annoying dejection how some group of riff-raffs parading themselves as governorship aspirants would want to inaugurate a totally beastly political misbehaviour alien to Igbo norms:- kidnapping of a competitor's old father. From where did we learn all these queer behaviours. Is that truly the issue of political behaviour that can get the Igboman out of his gravely frustrating subsistence in today's congregation of Nnewi one-man factories that used to litter every nook and cranny of Anambra state, even Uba, Abiriba and Orlu. But suddenly, everybody has turned an importer of all sorts of rubbish, jamming Lagos Ports and financing the internally generated revenue box of Lagos, Oyo, Ogun, Kano, Kaduna, Sokoto, Niger, Bauchi, Maiduguri and federal government to mention but these few.
It is not the way of a true Igbo man to withdraw into obscurity when others are standing. Igbo love justice, and by nature are liberals, holding fast to ancestral Jewish norms, yet not encumbered nor trapped by the customs and practices of other non-Igbo. Igbo are easily identifiable in the crowd by their surety and diplomacy but certainly not cowardice. But today, that assertive spirit of the Igboman, especially in Lagos is embarrassingly rescinding.
In the city of Abuja, Kano, Lagos or Ibadan, an Igboman would see his fellow Igbo brother on the street, and instead of greeting him in their mother-tongue-would rather resort to chat in English-even if in mangled phonetics. Rather, it is an Ijaw man or Urhobo man that would see an Igboman and recognizing would first launch into fraternal brotherhood:- "Nna kedu, Ndeewo, while the Igboman would be replying in queer amusing English:- "How is your families," "I loves you," "I greeted you before, but you did not hears me."
This deprecating manifestation from the Igbo folks which is symptomatic of social paranoia, almost bordering on acute inferiority complex had its roots from the long tenured and false orientation of the Igboman that his destiny starts and ends in Nigeria's socio-political architecture-absurdly erected right from the time of Zik's anti-colonial, ultra-nationalist days with the Macaulays, the Ikolis, the Enahoros and co.
Then the botched Ojukwu's secessionist bid of 1967 and its aftermath has only worsened this alarming proclivity of the present day Igboman to want to be eager to please everyone and any element-even when his rights are unduly breached. Why? The Igbo leaders have for a long time cued themselves to the politics of appeasement and settlement - and not politics of principles in recognition of the fact that in as much as Nigeria has not formally balkanized, no one group or nation within Nigeria ought to posture that she is more superior or indispensable than any other.
God is so loving that he did not leave any part of Nigeria barren of anything it can lay hands on to grow into statehood. Yet, even as all these indices glitter in Igbo landscape, contemporary Igbo politicians still tend to cringe and fawn - whereas the natural resources endowments in the Eastern region where Igbo language is major does not beggar attachment or dependence to any quasi-supra authority of any mode. Igbo are proud people, self-willed and confident in their Chineke not to deny his covenant of divine guidance and protection at all time. But nowadays, Igbos patronize Babalawo and all sorts of queer shadowy cults and groupings, especially those living outside Igboland - and this has to a large extent - especially at this end-time eroded her self-confidence because once a people deny that god, their god will deny them. Igbo has adulterated her faith in her "Chi." No "Chi" of any other land can give a people of other lands the kind of complements, comforts and protection the visitor desires.
That is why many Igbo folks at Ojuelegba in Lagos who play Baba Ijebu-lottery bail hardly hit a jackpot. But ones Iya Bisi who sells Amala under the bridge and who can hardly identify an alphabet plays once or twice, she hits jackpot instantly.
It is about time Igbo nation gets its acts together. The 2009 Ahiajoku lecture should provide a last solution ground for Igbo myriad identify problems. The outcome of the lecture, the position papers and all should take irrevocable stands on critical Igbo issues and such positions incorporated into statutes with strong sanctions in the event of breaches. For instance, why should the Igboman be fond of parading Yoruba or Hausa over-starched and bogus shapeless designs as national dress. Is that Igbo dress?
Yet, Igbo elite has cued into this irritating superimposition - and while spotting it, mount rostrums to sermonize on Igbo culture and values. Why is Igbo language getting under at home and outside. When will IBM or Microsoft design Igbo language alphabet keyboards and systems? When will Holywood buffs start sub-lifting their films in Igbo language. How does the Igbo ensure that the greedy Nigerian state, does not bastardize Igbo remaining heritage and resources as she is currently doing to her cousins in the South-South come under a people's culture rights and value in interests without which no other thing can be sustained. Good for Ahiajoku 2009.

Original_Search_Title: Adamawa People Groans As Economic Hardship Bites Harder
Headline: Nigeria: Adamawa People Groans As Economic Hardship Bites Harder
Body_Text:
Yola — People of Adamawa State are celebrating bleak Christmas and New Year festivities as the economic hardship bites harder
Everywhere, it is lack of funds thereby forcing the price of goods and services to a standstill.
The usual bubbling of buying and selling normally associated with Christmas and New Year festivities are completely absent in the state.
The usual rush for buying and selling at this period are virtually non existence at major markets in the state, even as dealers of food stuffs, soup ingredients and wearing apparels are complaining of low patronage.
Mallam Mohammed Saidu, a major foods stuff dealer in Jimeta Modern Market told Vanguard that this is the first time in the history of the state that such dull marketing is being experienced.
Dan Auwalu, a dealer in yams at the same market was also full of lamentation even as the downward trend of the state economy continues unabated.
The poor state of economy of Adamawa State is compounded by the un-ending fuel scarcity.
Endless queues are visible in the few filling stations with fuel, while roadside hawkers are making brisk business, even as a gallon of PMS now goes for between 800 and 900 naira depending on availability.
The economic hardship facing Adamawa State is further compounded by lack of funds to service ministries, parastatals and departments and agencies in the state.
Dependable source disclosed to Vanguard that for the past four months, running costs for such organisations had not been forthcoming from the government.
The resultant effect is that the ministries and departments have been operating on borrowed funds for their services, even as most of them are virtually offering skeletal services due to lack of fund.

Original_Search_Title: Woman in Court Over Yams
Headline: Nigeria: Woman in Court Over Yams
Body_Text:
A middle- aged woman, Uka Amaka, was recently arraigned before an Abuja magistrate court for allegedly selling yams entrusted in her care by one Chinelo Mbaver.
Prosecutor Yulkudi Dugji told the court that the offence which was committed in December 2008 is punishable under Sections 312 and 322 of the Penal Code Law.
He said Chinelo reported to the police that she bought 1000 tubers of yam from the accused at the rate of N75, 000 and stored them in her place with the hope of selling the yams in July 2009.
"The accused criminally sold the yams at the rate of N125, 000 without Chinelo's consent and when the complainant demanded for her money back, the accused only gave her N44, 000," the prosecutor stated.
He revealed that during police investigations, the accused confessed to the crime.
When the charges were read to the accused, she pleaded not guilty and told the court that she gave the complainant N52, 000 and not N42, 000 that she claimed.
The accused, who was not represented by a counsel requested for bail, which was not objected to by the prosecutor.
Magistrate Idayat Akanni granted her bail in the sum of N50, 000 with a surety in like sum and adjourned the case to January 7, 2010 for hearing.

Original_Search_Title: Coke, UAC Food "Delicious Deals" Promo Excites Consumers
Headline: Nigeria: Coke, UAC Food "Delicious Deals" Promo Excites Consumers
Body_Text:
Consumer promotion organized by Coca Cola Nigeria in partnership with UAC Food Court is gathering momentum as anxious consumers who wish to win the exciting prizes troop to the participating restaurants to grab a bite of sumptuous meals and snacks served at the restaurants. UAC Food Court includes Mr. Biggs, Chicken Inn, Village Kitchen and Pizza Inn.
The two super brands recently kicked off the promo tagged "UAC Food Court Delicious Deals" in sync with Coke's commitment to 'Give, Share and Love' this Christmas season. In addition, the two great brands would use the four month promo which ends March, 1010 to moderate the pangs of discomfort that comes with Christmas spending early in the year.
Prizes on offer include a star prize of an all expense paid trip for four lucky winners to South Africa to watch the 2010 World Cup match. Other prizes include first prize of four 32 inch flat screen TV, second prize of double door fridge for four winners, third prize of 20 Nokia E63 model of handsets and the fourth prize of 40 winners of meal vouchers.
The delicious deal also give consumers opportunity to enjoy sumptuous new meals including whole grilled fish, chicken and chips, spaghetti from Mr. Biggs; new great tasting chicken and filleted burger from Chicken Inn; delicious yam from Village Kitchen and veggie and pepperoni plus from Pizza Inn.
To participate in the competition based promo, customers need to buy and consume food, snacks and any Coke drinks worth a minimum of N1000 (one thousand Naira). The customer would receive a raffle ticket which he fills and drops into a box provided in each restaurant. At the end of each month, raffle entry boxes are emptied and sent to reputable auditors for validation of entries. A monthly draw holds at four regions including Lagos, West, East and North.
The grand prize draw will be held in April to determine the four lucky winners that would join the rest of the world to watch the FIFA World Cup live in South Africa next year. Unlike other promotions where winners are required to make arrangement for their visas, UAC and Coke would undertake all travelling arrangement including flight tickets, accommodation in a four star hotel, world cup match tickets, meals, visa, trip allowance, guided tours and gifts.
Speaking at the launch of the promo, the UAC Divisional Marketing Director, Adeola Essien "This Christmas season unlike any other is full of great expectations and hopes for the future, thankfulness for the present and exciting opportunities to give, share and love. 2010 takes us to into a season of great football. We are carrying love of this season into winter period, one that is infamous for constituting the antithesis and obverse side of Christmas. We want the season of give, share and love to prove more enduring and to moderate the pangs of discomfort that come with finding momentum early in the New Year."
In her own remark, National Key Accounts Manager, Nigeria Bottling Company (NBC) Ms. Juliet Ifebue said the partnership which dates back to many years affords the two brands and opportunity not only to give back to society but also to spread love among Nigerians during the yuletide.

Original_Search_Title: Price of Fuel, Food Items Rise in Imo
Headline: Nigeria: Price of Fuel, Food Items Rise in Imo
Body_Text:
Owerri — Cost of food stuffs and petroleum products in Owerri, Imo State capital, has hit the roof tops ahead of the Christmas and New Year.
Vanguard investigations revealed that most of the filling stations operated by independent marketers have adjusted their pump prices to between N80 and N90 per litre.
Some commercial vehicle drivers who spoke to Vanguard on the issue, wondered why petroleum marketers unleashed what they termed "economic terrorism" on innocent citizens every December.
"It is sad that our own people can be this wicked during all festivities, especially Christmas and New Year. I do not know what else to call economic terrorism," Anthony Chigbo lamented.
According to the commercial driver, "we are into a legitimate motor business and it should be expected that we will automatically adjust our prices and transfer the extra petroleum bills to those who require our services."
Meanwhile, rice, yams, chickens and other food items have gone out of the reach of the common man, even as most civil servants who spoke to Vanguard on grounds of strict anonymity, claimed that they were yet to get their November salaries.
"I am not buying for Christmas. You can see that the hike in prices of food stuff has badly hit civil servants in the state," the lady said.

Original_Search_Title: School Donates to Military Pensioners
Headline: Nigeria: School Donates to Military Pensioners
Body_Text:
Abuja — The management and student of Pacesetters Academy, Abuja, have donated food items and clothing to retired military pensioners in the state.
The donation of bags of rice, detergent, and semovita, cartons of noodles, tubers of yam and clothes was made to the Military Pensions Board in Abuja for onward distribution to the pensioners.
Director of the Board, Brigadier General Bitrus Kwaji, who received the donation on behalf of the pensioners, expressed gratitude to the academy, saying that the gesture was in accordance with the public-private partnership initiative.
While disclosing that the government had been gracious in addressing the difficulties of the pensioners with the recent review of their financial packages, he appealed to Nigerians to appreciate the retired soldiers who fought to safe guard the country's unity. "That it is a great thing to do, for them to have thought of honoring the retirees because when you show love to the old, they will have a sense of belonging and I think others should follow in this example."
Chairman of the Pacesetters Academy, Barrister Kenneth Imansuangbon commended the officers of the Nigerian military for their efforts to promote unity in the country and Africa. He said the school deemed it fit to honour and appreciate their efforts and service to the country.
He stressed the need for private organisations to complement the efforts of the government in ensuring an improved living standard for Nigerians and the retired soldiers who made a lot of sacrifice for the future of the country.
"They are about the best in the continent, they kept the country together when we needed them to do so, even though they are retired, we do not want to forget them because they are the true pacesetters of the country, so in the wisdom of the school, we felt we needed to say well done to them and also trigger other private sector players to complement the role of government to these men who served the country well."
Responding on behalf of the pensioners, Ex-master Warrant Officer, Alex Igbazende, said they never expected such gesture from a private organisation, saying that it was the first time a private organisation would donate to the pensioners who he said served in the Nigerian civil war, UN Peacekeeping Force and the ECOMOG Monitoring Group.
"It is indeed a good thing to see a private firm donate to pensioners, rather than expect from the government, they have come to give and help the government. They deemed it fit to say thank you to retired soldiers who have gone through the rigors of war in Africa and have come home to rest, we are very appreciative of their gesture."

Original_Search_Title: Obesity is Gateway to Major Diseases - Dr. Aiyesimoju
Headline: Nigeria: Obesity is Gateway to Major Diseases - Dr. Aiyesimoju
Body_Text:
IN a bid to ensure good health for all, a medical consultant has warned against the dangers of obesity, noting that it is a gateway to other deadly diseases and sudden death.
Dr. Abbey Aiyesimoju of Healthville Medical Consultants, Ikeja, Lagos, who stated this during a recent health lecture series on the theme: Obesity is Gateway to Killer Diseases, affirmed that good health sector should not rest on their oars in the campaign against poor health attitude even as he urged them to include dangers of obesity in their campaign to create awareness to the obese and overweight people.
While lamenting the untold health risks the obese people face, he also blamed them for their poor health habits which, he said, included overfeeding, imbalanced diets as well as lack of exercise.
He cited an instance that over-dependence on carbohydrates or starchy foods and lack of exercise were the major causes of obesity, and overweight, warning that those who indulge in them should desist forthwith if they must enjoy a healthy living.
While giving a detailed analysis of factors that encourage obesity, Dr. Aiyesimoju cited technology as one of them, saying that while people are supposed to use staircase, they use lift to get to short destinations within their offices, while they should trek short distances they take a ride, while they need to use pester and mortar to pound their yams, they use pounding machine, while they need grinding stone to grind their pepper, and tomatoes they use blender etc. He stated that all these exercises, if embarked upon by self, could burn up calories and check against obesity and overweight.
On the advantages of obesity control, the doctor said it enhances immunity to other related diseases such as hypertension, diabetes, flatulence, constipation.
Determined to continue with the weight control and good health programme, the clinic has lined up a series of monthly lectures. Among them is, The Prostate: A time Bomb Ticking in Men's Plumbing holding on Saturday, December 13, 2009 at Forever Living Product Complex, Ikeja, Lagos.
The lectures which hold on Saturdays are attended by both men and women wishing to check their weight and keep healthy as well as those wishing to learn about good health habits.
On whether slimming programes have any side effects, Dr. Aiyesimoju said if done by a medical expert, using appropriate drugs or theraphy, there would be no side effect, but if wrongly done, side effects were certain.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo, South-West Governors Meet
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo, South-West Governors Meet
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Former president, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday met with all the four Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) governors from the South west of the country in Abeokuta, Ogun State capital.
But, the visit turned dramatic, when Osun State governor, Prince Olagunsoye Oyinlola while reacting to a question from newsmen on why the meeting lasted close to two hours, said that the former president was taking his time to prepare pounded yam for them.
His Ogun State counterpart, Otunba Gbenga Daniel was responding to the purpose of the visit, when the smart Oyinlola, who is believed to be having the ears of the former president more than any of the four governors interjected to Daniel's response.
Daniel had told newsmen that the visit was meant to pay yuletide visit to Obasanjo, but when protruded further as to why such seasons' greeting visit would take about two hours, Oyinlola threw the newsmen in uncontrollable laughter, saying " Baba was preparing pounded yam for us."
Though details of the meeting were kept closed to the chest governors and their host, Obasanjo, THISDAY gathered that it might been more than "yuletide" visit, as pressing national issues, including the ailing President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua.
The calculation was that it is necessary to sensitize themselves on where to go in the case of the President having to resign his post. In attendance were the Ogun state governor, Otunba Gbenga Daniel, Otunba Alao Akala (Oyo), Prince Olagunsoye Oyinlola (Osun) and Mr. Segun Oni (Ekiti).

Original_Search_Title: Who is Afraid of Eze Ndigbo?
Headline: Nigeria: Who is Afraid of Eze Ndigbo?
Body_Text:
Over the past decade or so, there has been a lingering controversy over the desirability or otherwise of the emergent institutions of Eze Ndigbo in several locations outside the South East. How did this 'problem' originate?
Various sources point to the June 12 imbroglio in 1993, when Ndigbo fled their various stations en-masse for the South East. The troubles they endured on that unscheduled exodus and the losses they suffered, brought to the fore a glaring dearth of non-partisan leadership to ensure and protect their fundamental rights and interests. Many traced the seeming cohesion of other ethnic groups outside their native homelands to the availability of such traditional offices. Hausa speaking settlers, for example, have a Sarkin Hausawa among them to provide the required leadership.
Neither Ohaneze Ndigbo, the apex Igbo socio-cultural organization, nor Aka Ikenga, a loose association of Igbo professionals could fill the gap. A growing number of young hawks who had made their marks in business and acquired chieftaincy titles from various communities in Igboland waited in the wings to assume the mantle. They reasoned, perhaps correctly, that though Ohaneze Ndigbo signifies the masses and leaders of the Igbos, the organization was flying like a one-winged bird outside the homeland: the masses, (oha), were there, but the leadership, (eze), was unavailable.
They sought the support of respected elders across the Niger. One of such personages was Emeka Odumegwu Ojukwu, the Ikemba Nnewi, who found willing allies in these men and encouraged them to form the Igbo Council of Chiefs (ICC) in Lagos in 1998. Furthermore, the ICC received the blessings of Igwe Emeka Nnaji, then chairman of the Council of Traditional Rulers of the seven Igbo speaking states of the federation. At first, the ICC was led by Chief Ralph Uwazuruike, who would go on in latter years to form the Movement for the Actualization of the Sovereign State of Biafra (MASSOB).
Uwazuruike and the ICC supported the Ikemba in his quest to give de facto stamp to his undisputed place in Igbo history by foisting the title of Eze Igbo Gburugburu (Leader of all Igbos) on himself. Indeed, Ikemba Emeka Ojukwu and ICC were one and the same. Its motto read, OJUKWU BU EZE IGBO DUM (Ojukwu is Leader of all the Igbos). ICC had its secretariat at 29 Queens Drive, the Ikemba's residence in Ikoyi, Lagos. Nonetheless, the creation of the Gburugburu title tallied perfectly with the designs to fashion a new political reality for Ndigbo by creating the required machinery to uphold and protect their group interests outside their home region.
Ikemba Ojukwu gave every moral support to the ICC. An ICC report on "Consolidation Efforts" of the Council referred copiously to his advice for the adoption of a constitution for the Council and the Bill of Unity he had sponsored. The document spoke also of incipient disputes within the ranks of the ICC and several petitions to "Eze Anyi" (Our Leader), as the Ikemba was addressed.
These disputes arose from the ambitions of various Council members to be crowned Eze Ndigbo Lagos (Leader of the Igbos in Lagos). Further leadership tussles down the line to the local government levels heightened tension within the ICC and raised serious questions about the need for such an institution outside the Igbo homeland. The ICC remained in a fractious condition until late I998, when a constitution was adopted and Igwe Ohazulike Omereoha, a businessman residing at Ifako, Ijaiye, was crowned Eze Ndigbo Lagos. The ICC endorsed his selection for the stool, so also did Ohaneze Ndigbo, Lagos.
According to the ICC document earlier referred to, "without the Igbo Council of Chiefs" Igwe Ohazuluike Omeroha could not have been recognized by Ikemba Ojukwu. The document also declared that, "Ezeigbo Lagos filled a form and promised to obey the rules and regulations of the Igbo Council of Chiefs."
These efforts prove very clearly that the Ezeigbo phenomenon enjoyed the support and blessing of prominent Igbos who had long since desired a non-partisan platform for the protection of their group interests. Soon, the institution spread to different parts of the country and various personalities were installed Ezeigbo of various towns and states of the federation. However, acrimonious squabbles among wealthy Igbo traders and businessmen for the position, notably in Taraba and Oyo states, made it extremely hard to defend the institution as a unifying platform. Arguments for the scrapping of the office began to make the rounds in the newspapers. Inevitably, some of these were the results of disenchantment between the Ohaneze leadership and Ikemba Chukwuemeka Ojukwu, who was rightly seen by many as architect of the Ezeigbo institution.
Very many Igbo do not reckon with the highfaluting office of ezeigbo. The educated professional classes find it to be such poor comedy. "Ezecrap!" scoffs a computer engineer who would rather not be named in this write up. Commentators such as Pini Jason, Livy Iwunze and others have inveighed against the phenomenon in their columns. Yet, there is no denying the pull of ezeigbo among segments of the populace, especially the petty traders and merchant groups.
Nonetheless, the office continued to thrive until recently when the Council of South East Traditional Rulers mounted sustained pressure on various states governors and traditional rulers around the country to withdraw their recognition of ezeigbos. These efforts paid off in Lagos and Osun states whose governors made policy statements to the effect that no one could parade themselves any longer as ezeigbo in these states.
Not everyone agrees with the purported reasons for this 'ban' and the role of the Council of South East Traditional Rulers in this development. "The power of any traditional ruler in Igboland should begin and end in his domain," says Chief Jude Offor, a business man at the Computer Village, Ikeja. "The entire framework of ezeigbo is a means of organizing our people for their social and economic progress. We even have ezeigbos among the Igbo student communities in the universities. How then can anyone say they are banning the institution?"
Chief Offor's position is corroborated by Chief Dan Nwankwo, a clearing and forwarding agent with offices in Apapa. "You can not be eze in Igboland and eze in Lagos. They (traditional rulers) get subvention and allowances from government, but ezeigbos in Lagos and other places spend their own money to support the cause of the people. They should be encouraged and not condemned."
Both chiefs are members of the ezeigbo's cabinet in Ikeja. Chief Offor is also peeved that when it pleases the authorities, they use the ezeigbos to rally the people. He avers that the ezeigbo was very instrumental to Chief Olusegun Obasanjo's campaign efforts in 2003. "Through him Igbo people from all walks of life attended Obasanjo's political rally here in Lagos."
This organizational clout may now be working against the interests of the Eze Ndigbo in Lagos. Certain politicians pointedly accuse him of supporting the PDP gubernatorial aspirant in Lagos during the 2007 elections. His various business interests and overt involvement in land matters may also be a cause for concern in some quarters. Says Ifeanyi "Owelle" Ayoka, a general goods merchant in Amuwo-Odofin, "Our people always compare the ezeigbo to the sarki, but the Hausa leader does not involve himself in petty politics and land matters. He uses a lot of fronts to get things done. Our own people are too aggressive, that's why they attract so much envy and hatred to themselves. Ezeigbos should insulate themselves from petty businesses so that they can maintain the dignity of their office."
The generally boisterous attitude of the average ezeigbo may be attributable to their orientation as hard-headed businessmen with very limited formal education and sophistication. Some of them are also accused of carrying the toga of their achievement to their hometowns and villages where they conduct themselves as the equals, even superiors, of the homebred traditional rulers. This, say some observers, is the covert reason behind the campaigns against them by the Council of South East Traditional Rulers.
But the ezeigbos seem determined to keep their hard earned recognition among the people. When Igwe Ohazuluike Omeroha celebrated the 2009 New Yam Festival sometime recently, his fellow ezeigbos turned out en-masse from several locations around the country to give him their rousing support. This should send signal enough that the last has not been heard about this lingering controversy. Perhaps, it is merely a storm in a steaming soup pot, but who really is afraid of ezeigbo?
More importantly, why should anyone consider them a threat to their own political interests? If the Sarkin Hausawa and various other ethnic chieftains are allowed to carry on their assigned functions to the people they are supposed to represent, how can the Ezeigbos be stopped without infringing on the people's rights to freedom of association?

Original_Search_Title: Eid El Kabir - Prices of Food Stuffs Remain Stable in Enugu
Headline: Nigeria: Eid El Kabir - Prices of Food Stuffs Remain Stable in Enugu
Body_Text:
Enugu — Prices of food stuffs have remained stable in Enugu as Muslims celebrate the Eid el Kabir festival this weekend.
A survey conducted by NAN at the Ogbete Main Market during the week, showed that 50kg bag of local rice sells for N6,000 or N7,000 depending on the brand while imported rice goes for between N8,700 and N10,200.
A rice seller, Mr Onyema Eze, told NAN that the prices had been the same in the past three months. He said patronage had been low. While a bag of onions sold for N7, 000 and N17, 000 depending on the size, a medium sized basin of gari goes for between N3, 000 and N3, 700.
A big bag of beans goes for N15, 000 while yam tuber goes for N200 and N250. A 10kg of vegetable oil sells for N1, 700, the same price for palm oil. At the new Artisan Livestock Market, a medium sized ram that was sold for N12, 000 last year now goes for between N23, 000 and N25, 000.

Original_Search_Title: Igboesa People Celebrate New Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Igboesa People Celebrate New Yam
Body_Text:
The Igboesa sons and daughters from Ishielu Local Government Area of Ebonyi State, resident in the Federal Capital Territory recently gathered to relish their local delicacies of ukpo okro with pounded yam, to mark this year's new yam festival.
The ceremony was held at Cachez garden in Abuja, where food and drinks were served to their invited guests.
In a communiqué issued at the end of their monthly meeting which was co- signed by their chairman and the secretary, Mr. Vin Eze Ede and Mr. Offorbuike Aliedema, the association condemned the communal clash between Ezillo and Ezza communities in the local government area, saying that the clash had devastated the economy and general well being of the masses.
The association also called on all stakeholders, the leaders of the communities, the chairman Ishielu LGA, Barrister Onwa Ali Onwa, Senator Julius Ucha and the governor of Ebonyi State, to ensure speedy restoration of peace in the affected area.

Original_Search_Title: Passengers Decry Overloading in 'El-Rufai' Buses
Headline: Nigeria: Passengers Decry Overloading in 'El-Rufai' Buses
Body_Text:
Passengers who board Abuja Urban Mass Transit buses (AUMT), popularly called el-Rufai buses in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) and its suburbs last week complained about overloading in the buses.
The commuters accused the conductors of the buses of being concerned about their profits, rather than the safety of passengers. One of the commuters, Hassan Ibro told LEADERSHIP that he has complained to the management of the companies on several occasions without anything being done.
"Whenever such drivers are corrected by passengers on the capacity load, it results to quarrels," he said. Arubi Agocha, who boards the bus from Masaka to Area 1, describes it as a harrowing experience. "I prefer boarding the 'el-Rufai buses because of it is cheap. But, there is no comfort entering the buses. The congestion could cause danger to one's life.
They load passengers like yam tubers." Another passenger, Lilian Agana, on her own part said, "el-Rufai buses are very good, if only the drivers and their conductors could load according to the requirement.
"Some people climb each other which is not the best way." She further revealed that in such situations, conductors collect money without giving receipts to the passengers." "The management should stop them from loading beyond the capacity." she said. At the Abuja Urban Mass Transit Company, the manager was not available to speak on the matter.

Original_Search_Title: Taking Justice Into the Eastern Heartland
Headline: Nigeria: Taking Justice Into the Eastern Heartland
Body_Text:
Lagos — Strains of highlife music which suddenly segued into martial style music, drum roll and then, the piercing voice of a drill sergeant. Strange goings-on for any location but most of all, for the Court of Appeal.
This was no ordinary court however, and this was no ordinary day in Owerri, capital of Imo State. The Court of Appeal, Owerri Division was inaugurated last Thursday after a very long wait. This eagerly awaited new division of the Court of Appeal joins the 10 others created over the 33 year period of the court's existence.
The establishment of the Owerri Division of the Court of Appeal is a welcome development for lawyers and litigants in Abia and Imo States and the southeast of Nigeria in general. In the past, lawyers in both Abia and Imo have had to make their way to Port-Harcourt to have their appeals heard.
According to the Attorney-General of Imo State, Mr. Ken Njemanze, SAN, Abia and Imo States account for over 60% of the appeals pending at the Court of Appeal in Port-Harcourt.
With that amount of traffic, there is no doubt that the bar in the region must have heaved a collective sigh of relief when the decision to establish a new Division finally came through.
Financing Justice
From all reports, the speed with which the Division has been established is in no small part due to the pressure mounted by the former Governor of Imo State, Chief Achike Udenwa and the immense financial outlay of the present administration of Ikedi Ohakim.
The court complex was donated by the Ohakim administration as a temporary measure whilst the permanent court complex is under construction. The two-storey building provided by the state government is housed within expansive grounds.
The Imo State Government has also provided temporary accommodation for the Justices pending completion of their permanent residence.
In his speech, Governor Ohakim stated that his administration considers the independence of the judiciary a must for advancement of society. In his words, 'Courts must be seen as temples of justice and not theatres of gambling'.
To support the state judiciary itself, the Governor has increased salaries by 100% and all judges in the state have been provided brand new Toyota cars. In addition, the government has provided 30kVA generators for each judge's residence and they all have laptops with wi-fi connection.
In his address, President of the Court of Appeal, Faruk Umar Abdullahi, also referred to the enormous support that the Court of Appeal had received from the Ohakim administration.
'He pursued it from day one as top priority' said Abdullahi.
Apart from the temporary building provided, the Governor has also provided land for the permanent premises and residential buildings.
History of Struggle
'This division has a long history of struggle starting from the inauguration of the Port-Harcourt division'. Those were the opening words of His Lordship, Justice Abdullahi's speech during the inaugural court session.
This thought was echoed by the Attorneys-General of Imo and Abia States but none quite as hilariously as Chief Mike Ahamba, SAN who spoke on behalf of the combined state bar associations.
The learned SAN caused the solemn court session to break out in laughter when he spoke about the long struggle to establish the Owerri division of the Court of Appeal soon after the Port-Harcourt division.
'At the time when his Lordship [referring to Justice Abdullahi] and his entourage were going to Port-Harcourt to establish the Division, they passed through the expressway and not through Owerri, otherwise, we would have deflated their tyres'.
Ahamba merely underscored the desperation of the bar to have a Court of Appeal Division established in the state.
'A sizeable portion of our income was paid to the Port-Harcourt hotels' he said, to more laughter.
For the visitors to Owerri however, his comments about not offering the Justices of the court of Appeal Ojukwu tomatoes caused puzzlement and a lot of head scratching. What he meant? THISDAY lawyer knows but isn't telling.
Dissention in the Ranks ?
Or is it a case of healthy rivalry. We might never be able to tell but the Attorney-General of Abia State, Mr. C.A Imo, caused quite a stir in the court room when he stated that the bulk of the appeals heard at the Court of Appeal in Port-Harcourt emanated from Aba, which is the commercial nerve centre of south-eastern Nigeria and as such, special sessions of the Court should hold in Umuahia.
'What does he mean?', one Owerri lawyer spluttered in obvious indignation.
Another, a member of the House of Representatives in Owerri, turned to his neighbor and said, 'See these people, they didn't even know when we started agitating for this court. See him now Â´
Obviously, the Imo state bar isn't quite as generous with their new court as the Governor of the state is.
Culture and the Law
The general atmosphere in Owerri during the inaugural session of the court and reception afterwards was one of celebration. There were several elements which served to remind all and sundry that we were in the eastern heartland. There was the Ohafia war dance which is quite famous and is performed at all important ceremonies. Also, the traditional giving of Kola, because [according to Chinua Achebe in 'Things fall Apart'] he who brings Kola, brings life.
One could hardly miss the yam roasting in the background or the Abacha and Nkwobi served to the guests at lunch. If one failed to notice all this, then, the red earth sticking to the shoes is sufficient proof that one has been to the heartland.
Eastern Bar Rejoices
It is going to make litigation easier in Owerri. It is a welcome development and will remove the pressure of going to Port-Harcourt for Appeals - Ndidi Anike
This is bringing justice closer to the people. It was difficult going to Port-Harcourt and getting dates for appeals. We experienced severe delays but thank God for this opening. It is a good development and our people will get justice. - Victor Nwaugo
I am glad about the new division. We used to encounter a lot of hardships going to Port-Harcourt. There was one morning that we were travelling for an appeal and were attacked by robbers just after Elele. We had to take shelter in a primary school and I am surprised that Chief Ahamba SAN, did not mention it in his speech as we took shelter together in that school for over one hour -Ama Akalonu

Original_Search_Title: Doctors At War - You're a Disgrace, Says Governor Orji
Headline: Nigeria: Doctors At War - You're a Disgrace, Says Governor Orji
Body_Text:
Umuahia — Governor Theodore Orji of Abia state has expressed sadness at the failure of medical doctors in the state to move its health delivery services forward. The Governor made the remark when the leadership of the Nigeria Medical Association (NMA) paid him a courtesy call at Government House, Umuahia at the weekend.
Led by their chairman, Dr. Bernard Orji the doctors had told the governors that they were in his office to protest the state of things at the Abia State University Teaching Hospital (ABSUTH) Aba.
The doctors had accused one of their colleagues. Dr Ogwo, who is director of administration of various wrong doings including financial mismanagement and bribery.
They also accused the principal of school of Nursing Mrs. N. Nwogu, of financial mismanagement and compromised admission of students.
They urged Gov Orji to sack the officer and to investigation the operations of the two institutions in the interest of the state and good health care delivery in particular.
According to the apex medical body, the Director of Administration approves N1 million monthly as salary for himself whereas a senior consultant at the hospital earns barely N100,000. even as he allegedly collects another N740,000 as rent subsidy besides N1 million he pockets as annual board meeting sitting allowance.
The strong-worded petition to the governor against the officers reads, in part:
"several projects that were either not done or over-inflated were fronted by those officials including fumigating the old site of the hospital for over N4 million, printing of bank withdrawal booklets and cheques for N2 million within a month. As for food supplies, it is on record that a tuber of yam is purchased by Dr. Ogwo at N1500 while a ball of akara bought at N52.00 and a wrap of agidi at N250. As at now, in-patients are no longer fed even after they had paid for food".
The Director of Administration was further accused of changing the existing organogram of the hospital and adopted a new one alien to hospital management which enables him to usurp the powers of the Chief Medical Director (CMD).
He was also accused of employing 250 staff (some of them in their late 50s) after allegedly collecting financial inducement of N350,000 each.
In the same vein, the principal, school of Nursing was accused of admitting candidates without recourse to their qualifications but to the highest bidder.
The association also decried the poor state of health institutions in the state which they said was in near collapse, and called for the immediate redeployment, investigation and prosecution of the two officials.
"We have watched in studied silence and with deep anguish the rapid deterioration of quality and quantum of service in the three tiers of health care system in the state".
Responding, Gov. Orji expressed shock over the level of corruption and decay bedeviling the health sector but however, lambasted the delegation for not bringing the matter to his notice on time, arguing that the accused were all doctors.
"Now am I being called upon to clean up a mess by your colleagues? Since the creation of Abia State , doctors have been commissioners of health. Now show me any new invention by doctors at ABSUTH unlike your counterparts in other teaching hospitals. You don't initiate research projects and all of you have successful private clinics but find it difficult to maintain government hospitals", the governor wondered.
He however, promised to look into their complaints with a view to saving the health sector from further deterioration.
Later in an interview with our correspondent, the Director of Administration Dr. E. Ogwo denied all the allegations and said they were politically-engineered by his detractors to smear his reputation.
"It has a political connotation because I am related to the former governor. They felt that when the man goes away, I am supposed to move. But when they found out that I have not moved it angered them. It is just like calling a dog a bad name if you want to hang him".
He said that his actions so far at the hospital as the DA had the approval of the Chief Medical Director, arguing that any CMD remote-controlled by the DA is not supposed to occupy such a position.
"How can I usurp the powers of the CMD. Any CMD whose position is usurped is supposed to be sacked. Every employment that is done at ABSUTH has the mandate of the CMD. In the letter of appointment, it is written 'I am directed to....' In case of contracts, the accountant will not pay except the CMD puts his signature" he argued.
On N350,000 inducement from job applicants, Dr. Ogwo said, "for God's sake if a clerk that earns N10,000 to N12,000 has N350,000 bribe to give to somebody, he doesn't need that job. I am talking facts now, if you collect N350,000 from somebody and the application letter for employment is directed to the CMD and not the DA, who is the culprit?"
Clearing the air on food supplies, he said that every procurement is handled by the catering committee who goes for market survey before approval is given for order.
Meanwhile, he blamed his woes on his kinsmen from Ohafia league whom he said were not comfortable that he still has many years to serve seeing that the position of DA is zoned to Abia North Senatorial District where they all come from.
"The people in the Medical Association are mainly from Ohafia bloc. Even if the government removes me and brings another person who is not their own, the person will suffer the same problem. The Ohafia people cannot break even. They cannot, because the consultants are diverting patients. Any little provocation they go on strike. Once they go on strike, their hospitals are booming. Before they attend to patients they charge their own fees and put in the pockets that is why in FMC Umuahia, the Internally Generated Revenue is N20 million to N30 million but here in ABSUTH we cannot boast of N10 million" Ogwo alleged.
Similarly, the Principal School of Nursing, Mrs. Nwogu absolved herself from any wrong and said she was being persecuted because her people think she is a relation of the former governor Dr Orji Uzor Kalu.
"All these allegations are lies. Because people think I am Orji Uzor Kalu's sister they want to execute me. I come from Umuahia and am married at Ngwa. Don't mind them. Anybody fighting me is fighting against God for my battle is God's".
she however expressed her willingness to vacate her seat to give peace a chance.

Original_Search_Title: Tafa - A Disaster Waiting to Happen?
Headline: Nigeria: Tafa - A Disaster Waiting to Happen?
Body_Text:
Abuja — Tafa is immensely popular among drivers of large petrol tankers who stop there to rest for a day or two. But is there danger lurking around the corner?
Auwal Usman usually looks forward to meeting Salamat, his sweetheart of over five years. Although, he is already married with five children in Kano, he still holds Salamat very dearly. He says "owing to the nature of my job, I travel a lot. I convey petroleum tanker loaded with petroleum from Lagos to virtually all the parts of the country. So most of the time, I spend the entire day on the road driving. Since this has its own consequences on the human body, I always park here in Tafa to relax and enjoy the company of my wife, who will cook my favorite meal for me and make sure that I am well refreshed before continuing the journey." Usman spends up to two weeks with Salamat during which he relishes the comfort and warmth of other drivers, who have come to enjoy the same treat from their spouses, before proceeding on their arduous journey.
Similarly, Moses Ajayi, Usman's professional colleague equally shares in the warmth and comfort which Tafa brings to the petrol tanker drivers. His long haulage tanker could be seen parked very precariously close to the ever busy Abuja Kaduna expressway, while he idles around smoking profusely. When asked about the significance of the transit camp to him, he quickly explains that "this place is where we tanker drivers usually rest after several hours of a long and painful journey. For instance, I just returned from Lagos two days ago, with my tanker load of petrol for delivery at Kano. So in order to put body and soul together, I decided to take some rest here before proceeding." But unlike Usman who has a regular spouse in the locality, Ajayi prefers to patronize ladies of easy virtue who abound in the area. In his words, "I don't have any wife here, because; I already have three wives in Ibadan. That is where my family is based but you know that since body no be wood, I have some girls which I patronize to refresh my system, before I continue with my journey."
However, findings by this medium also reveal that while most of the tanker drivers park their vehicles along the road to rest, other drivers are forced to seek solace in the shanty community whenever their trucks break down. According to Ibrahim Saleh who drives one of the Sani brothers' trucks, "it is not all the drivers you see here that come here for enjoyment. Some of them like me are forced to stop here so that we can repair our vehicles which might take days or even weeks, because the mechanics will have to go to the nearest town to get the spare parts. So while that is going on, we take time off to relax and rest."
Welcome to Tafa, a sleepy suburb in Kagarko local government of Kaduna State, which has a reputation of being the convergence point of petrol tankers and trucks from various parts of the country. As you approach Tafa from the Abuja end of the road en route Kaduna, you are confronted by scores of trailers and petrol tankers parked haphazardly by the road side, while their various drivers could be seen having the time of their life in the make shift houses that dot the entire settlement. Then there are the activities of the Gbagyi women who proudly display their yams beside the parked trailers very close to the expressway, in defiance of the impending disaster that might arise from their action. The apparent contest between the tanker drivers and the Gbagy yam settlers for space, has been a constant threat to motorists and commuters who ply the busy road. A commercial bus driver who plies the Abuja and Kaduna route told this medium that "Anyone who is not a stranger to Tafa and its environs will know the menace and the nuisance tanker drivers constitute, though they provide a sort of emergency service to the society due to the importance of fuel. Because of the sheer size of the tankers they drive, these vehicles cannot help but be a problem on the roads. They are the ones who park indiscriminately by the side of the road, taking up a good portion of what constitutes the road," he said.
A motorist who gave his name as Fidelis Onuorah lamented to Daily Trust that "Tafa, like many Nigerian communities, has become ravaged by the greed and insensitivity of man. Tafa is a disaster waiting to happen and it seems we are all helpless. Driving through this place requires you to recite Psalm 23, verse 4: "Though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil", because the petrol tankers have taken over the long stretch on both sides of the Express. The stench that oozes out of the place would repel even the dirtiest animal. The highly inflammable products are parked not too far from the many local restaurants, and the street hawkers, who seem to be totally oblivious of the explosive danger around them."
Pius Ogbeche who describes himself as a regular driver along the road, explained "Other road users are expected to manage whatever the tanker drivers have left for them.
