With the volume of traffic on this road every day, the roads become virtually impassable. Of course, tanker drivers are not blind. They see all these and yet will still not desist from parking illegally by the roadside."
While sharing Onuorah's concerns, Issa Mohammed, who resides in Kaduna, narrated to this medium on how hell was recently let loose in the area when a kerosene tanker on its way to Kaduna, lost control while trying to avoid a head on collision with one of the stationary tankers parked by the roadside. It fell and burst into flames. " At least five people were confirmed dead, two of them killed by flames inside their homes, and many others were hospitalized with severe burns in the unfortunate event. Several other vehicles parked by the roadside, were also burnt to ashes. Our people are just not concerned about their safety and to make matters worse, the government seems to be ignoring the place."
For Andrew Elegbeleye, the menace of petrol tankers is not only limited to Tafa, in Kaduna state, 'they are all over Nigeria. The one at Ogere, between Lagos and Ibadan, along the expressway is even worse. Petrol tankers park their vehicles carelessly on the road ,without giving any consideration for other road users. It is so sad especially when one considers the fact that the Ogun State government had actually built an ultra modern park for these people, who obviously refused to use the facilities, but preferred to be a nuisance to the society at large and a grave danger to the environment. I was told these truck drivers usually blackmail the government with the threat of a debilitating strike, if they were ever touched or asked to move. What nonsense is this?" he asked rhetorically.
But while drivers and other road users expressed fear and concern about their safety, for Salamat and other traders who thronged the settlement on a daily basis, the chaos offers them the opportunity to make brisk business. According to Salamat who operates a local restaurant in Tafa, "This place is good and everybody is peace loving. We sell food, cigarettes and drinks to drivers who park here to relax before they continue their journey." Also speaking to Daily Trust, Bala Inuwa, who resides in Tafa describes it as a fun place to be. According to him, "you need to come here during weekends beginning from Friday night, and see how this place comes alive. We have a local club and a DJ where people enjoy themselves and have fun.
The tanker drivers add life to this place and they are always around. As some of them are leaving, others are arriving, so the fun is endless." While stating further that Tafa and the tanker drivers have a history that is as old as twenty years, the settlement serves as a kind of meeting place for the drivers who, apart from relaxing, find time to service their lorries and carry out general repairs before proceeding with their journey.
Investigation by this medium also reveals that apart from local restaurants bursting with activities, Tafa is also a small commercial settlement as residents eke out living by selling commodities such as kola nuts, yams, cigarettes, local drinks and sugar cane.
While suggesting a way out of the problem, Bello Sadiq, a concerned driver explains "The government should begin to think seriously about implementing whatever plans it has on the rail project. Rather than these vehicles taking over the roads due to their size and numerical strength, it makes a lot of sense if their cargo is transported by rail. With that, the volume of these vehicles on the road will definitely decrease. It would also help if refineries in the country are made to work so that dependence on imported petrol will greatly reduce. The companies that own the oil tankers would do well to provide facilities for these tanker drivers to park, and hotels to stay at, while the drivers themselves should be told that holding everyone to ransom, may not be the only way to approach every problem."

Original_Search_Title: MDGs - Abaribe Wants Abia Consultant Replaced
Headline: Nigeria: MDGs - Abaribe Wants Abia Consultant Replaced
Body_Text:
Abuja — Senator Enyinnaya Abaribe (PDP, Abia) has written the Senior Special Assistant to President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua , over what he described as the 'unresponsiveness of the Abia State Consultant of the Millennium Development (MDGs).
He asked the presidency to intervene and cause MDGs to send another consultant to the state to supervise the contracts.
An obviously angry Abaribe also complained about the sub-standard and poorly constructed furniture that Yam Habilmus, the contractor handling the supply of school furniture to primary schools in some communities in his Abia South Senatorial District, particularly the sets, supplied to Umunisii, Umuekechi and Akanu Primary schools.
Based on the poor job which has been rejected by the benefitting schools, he asked the government to prevail on the MDG to stop further payment to the contractor until standard furniture as specified by MDG is supplied.
Senator Abaribe in the letter of October 12, said that his complaint was based on the refusal of the host communities to accept the furniture supplied by Yam Habilmus, and the attendant protest of the benefitting school authorities on the poor quality of the furniture.
"We cannot accept a job so poorly done under the Federal Government programme.
I therefore, appeal that the MDG should stop further payment to Yam Habilmus Enterprises Nigeria Limited, until standard furniture as specified is supplied to the protesting primary schools.
"I will not fold my arms and watch people destroy the confidence of this present administration because of their selfish interests", he said.
However, the senator disclosed that the late development had given a lie to the unfortunate insinuation that senators and other lawmakers do corner the contracts meant for their constituencies.

Original_Search_Title: The NPA Scam - Is Two and Half Years Enough?
Headline: Nigeria: The NPA Scam - Is Two and Half Years Enough?
Body_Text:
THE conviction and sentencing of erstwhile chairman of the Nigerian Ports Authority, Chief Olabode George and five other members of the NPA board under him to two and half years in prison each, seems to have elated many Nigerians.
Chief George and his co-convicts were found guilty of abuse of office, disobedience to lawful authority and financial impropriety to the tune of N84 billion. The news seems to have caught Nigerians unawares. This, I presume, was not because Nigerians were unaware that investigations into the NPA contract scandal were due for judgment.
The case had been on even while Olabode George's supposed godfather and benefactor, former President Olusegun Obasanjo, was still in office. Joseph Oyewole, the trial judge, had deferred judgment till October 26 even when both the prosecution and defendants had concluded their addresses since September 15.
That many were caught unawares was a function, I suspect, more of the belief among Nigerians that no former state official, especially a senior member of the ruling party, could be convicted for corruption offences.
The series of "paddy-paddy government", administrations run on the basis of cronyism and allegiance to cultic codes had ensured that no member of such cultic clubs of politicians suffered as a consequence of offences committed while in office.
For this reason more than anything else, Nigerians have been both sceptical of and learnt to distrust any corruption probe initiated by government as was the one that led to the conviction of the NPA leadership under Bode George.
This was the reason for the apparent shock that greeted the announcement of the conviction. But many were soon to replace disbelief with elation, praising the judgment in a manner that could have led a watcher on the sideline into believing that Nigerians derive sadistic pleasure in the downfall of their leaders.
It's nothing new or strange, though, that so-called masses tend to chafe at the scandalous activities and enjoy the eventual fall of powerful or famous people. Nigerians are by no means unique in this regard and it is not always because people hate the famous or powerful as that they hate the sense of impunity with which such frontliners tend to conduct themselves.
Thus when the news got abroad that a leader of Nigeria's ruling PDP had been sentenced to two and half years in prison many chose to disbelieve the evidence before them, seeking confirmation from others they imagine might tell them something they, the people, didn't know. With confirmation came elation and praise for the 'activist' judge.
To be sure, Justice Oyewole displayed rare courage in the manner he handled the case. Yet, a cursory look at the roll of high profile cases he had handled in the past, including the EFCC/Emmanuel Nwude and Anajemba case, the Reverend Emeka Ezeugo aka, Rev King case and the Major Hamza Al Mustapha case among others demonstrate his no-nonsense pedigree.
So then we must praise Justice Oyewole. But in praising him we must, like Oliver Twist, ask for more- ask if two half years can actually be enough punishment for misappropriating N84 billion. This is the question that continues to worry many Nigerians.
In this era of corruption in high and low places, when many are content to join the bandwagon of Nigeria-wreckers, a man that'd turn his back on such lucre and insist on justice, even if only symbolically, deserves the praise of all.
One, however, cannot help but wonder if the state couldn't have done more to recover the looted money that is but a few million less than a trillion Naira! That can go a long way in bringing needed infrastructure to the life of hundreds of millions of Nigerians.
How much is a two-and-half-year sentence, many ask, to a group with such arsenal of wealth to fight back and ensure they continue to live like monarchs even while behind bars?
There are many such cases of wealthy convicts and/or detainees who have become virtual lords behind bars. And for examples of such group, we need not look beyond the group of persons listed above whose cases have at one time or another been handled by Justice Oyewole himself.
Both Al Mustapha and Rev. King are notorious for their 'big man' lifestyles in jail. What is more, a group of people with close to a trillion Naira in safe custody could afford to consider a two-and-half-year sentence a minor irritant that could and should be taken in their strides.
Before you know it, the sentence is over and they are back on the streets strutting as saints where they are not employing their vast wealth in creating a political dynasty of surrogate children whose goal would be to conclude the unfinished business of their benefactors- loot the treasury.
Today we hear of politicians sponsored for elective offices by persons, the sources of whose wealth would make Anini squirm in shame.
Such ex-convicts and would-be convicts are today's godfathers and godmothers of our leading politicians. It is perhaps in recognition of the potential for such misuse of purloined wealth that Justice Oyewole gave no option of fine in his judgment.
The stigma of conviction for corruption might hopefully do what money might have made impossible: undercut any sense of importance in flaunting stolen wealth.
So far the only people visibly unhappy with the judgment are, not unexpectedly, politicians, many of them members of the ruling PDP who describe the judgment as too harsh.
Considering the many cases of impunity in the past, punishing a crime like the one perpetrated at the NPA, must seem harsh indeed. But while many poor Nigerians spend years behind bars for stealing tubers of yam, to say nothing of China where similar offence as the one committed at the NPA would attract a death sentence, the NPA convicts should thank their stars and rededicate themselves to worthier causes.

Original_Search_Title: NDLEA Recovers 20kg of Weeds At Lagos Airport
Headline: Nigeria: NDLEA Recovers 20kg of Weeds At Lagos Airport
Body_Text:
A large quantity of cannabis, also known as Indian hemp, has been recovered from a dust bin at the Murtala Mohammed International Airport (MMIA), Lagos by operatives of the National Drugs Law Enforcement Agency (NDLEA).
The cannabis weighing 20.8 kilograms was "neatly compressed" in 19 waterproof bags and was hidden in a dustbin at the security area of the airport.
The head of public affairs of the agency, Mr. Ofoyeju Mitchell said in a telephone interview that NDLEA operatives have are investigating its source. He said investigation conducted so far revealed that the cannabis might have been kept by officials of the airport with the intent to smuggle it into a foreign bound flight.
"You are aware that the NDLEA command of the agency has so far arrested a five-man syndicate of airport staff using the conveyor belt of the airport to hide cocaine, heroin and cannabis.
They use dummy bags containing yams or clothes to clear at our security counter and transfer the security clearance tags of those bags to similar bags containing illicit drugs and smuggle them into foreign bound planes.
"When we discovered them, we arrested five members of the syndicate in two separate operations and recovered exhibits from them.
We believe other members of the syndicate that are yet to be arrested may be the ones responsible for stocking the dustbin with the cannabis.
We have beefed up security at the airport and are determined to arrest and prosecute all those involved in the act. Our operatives are on alert to frustrate any plan to smuggle illicit drugs in and out of the country," he said.
Mr. Mitchell said drug barons are now threatening to kill narcotic operatives because "we have succeeded in making life very difficult for them by arresting and prosecuting them.
He said since the adoption of new measures by the agency that led to the arrest and confiscation of exhibits from some barons, top officials of the agency have been receiving text messages on their cell phones threatening their lives.

Original_Search_Title: Anxiety Over Petrol Price Hike...as Full Deregulation Begins Nov 1
Headline: Nigeria: Anxiety Over Petrol Price Hike...as Full Deregulation Begins Nov 1
Body_Text:
Deregulation of the downstream sector of Nigeria's oil and gas industry has been on the front burner of public discourse for quite a while now, and as it fully takes effect, November 1, according to government, it becomes highly imperative for the public to understand what deregulation as well as the furore trailing its introduction is all about.
Deregulation implies that the government either state or federal would not be involved in the business of petroleum products supply, marketing and distribution, as well as refining. It means that the private sector would have the sole responsibility of deciding how much Nigerians would buy a liter of petrol, just as it is with Dual Purpose Kerosene (DPK) and Automotive Gas Oil (AGO), where the price fluctuates based on the decision of the importers of the products. The importers have argued that they can not afford to sell the products at prices lower than their import costs.
It is generally believed that it is sad that Nigeria, OPEC's sixth largest crude oil producer, still imports refined products whereas it has crude in abundance, due to provocative policies formulation and implementation by insensitive leaders. Lately, the reason put forward, among others, is that deregulated economic policies will attract investors into the (oil and gas sector) downstream/upstream sector and engender competition that would in turn bring down the prices of petroleum products.
Secondly, that the cost of subsidizing importation has become unbearable for government to sustain. The Federal Government says it has through NNPC been spending N400million daily to subsidize fuel imports. As part of the deregulation policy, the Federal Government stopped the sale of the crude to NNPC at the preferential cost of $18 per barrel.
NNPC now buys at the prevailing international price since its refineries are down, thus exports and uses the proceeds to import refined fuel for local consumption. Consequently, the people are saddled with continuous increase in the cost of locally consumed fuel. NNPC, major and independent marketers have become importers of petroleum products, a situation that has left the pricing at the mercy of market forces.
Increase in petrol price and government revenue Fuel price increase has negative significance on government revenue. In 1982, fuel price increased to 20kobo, while total federally collected revenue fell to N 11,764million. In 1986, fuel price rose to 39.5kobo, while government revenue fell to N12,382million.
However, the fuel price, in 1993 and 1994, fell to N3.50 and N 11.00 per litre respectively, while government revenue rose to N138,874million and N 201,911million respectively. In 1998, fuel price rose to N 25.00 pre litre, while government revenue fell to N 463,609 million. This inverse relationship can be attributed to labor unrest which resulted in a fall in oil and gas industry activities consequently affecting the revenue that would have accrued to government coffer.
Increase in fuel price and unemployment
Whereas labour demand is influenced by money wage rate, labour supply is influenced by expected real wage rate. Thus, wage legislation has affected labour market negatively.
In 1982, petrol price rose to 20kobo, while unemployment stood at 4.1%. During the period 1986-1990, fuel price rose to 60kobo, while unemployment fell to 3.4%.Between 1991 and 1992, fuel price rose to 70kobo, while unemployment stood at 3.8% and 4.0% respectively. However, the inverse relationship witnessed during the period 1986-1990 can be attributed to the positive impact of SAP on the economy, which eroded in 1991.
Increase in fuel price and inflation
In1978, petrol price increased to 15kobo,while inflation rate rose to 21.7%. However, in 1993, petrol price fell to N 3.25, while inflation rose to 57.20%. In 1998, fuel price rose to N 25 .00,while inflation rate stood at 10.00%. In 1999, the price fell to N20.00, while inflation rate declined to 6.6%. The inverse relationship can be attributed to deficit financing by the Federal Government.
Deregulation: The PEF, PPPRA angle
In view of the controversy which has trailed deregulation, the Federal Government has proposed a bill to the National Assembly, also referred to as the Petroleum Industry Bill (PIB). The bill is expected to look critically into deregulation as well as other issues bedeviling the growth of Nigeria's most important industry.
With National Assembly winding up its public hearing on the matter last July, Nigerians are awaiting what would eventually come out of the two chambers as an Act for the president to endorse or reject.
But of major concern to the people is how the proposed legislation would affect their well-being. And when institutions that the PIB seeks to modify are considered, two stand out as having direct bearing on the day-to-day lives of Nigerians. These are the Petroleum Products Pricing Regulatory Agency (PPPRA) and the Petroleum Equalisation Fund Management Board, PEF (M) Board. Part 2 Section 75 and Part 2 Section 200 of the PIB guarantee the existence of the PPPRA and the PEF (M) Board.
The PIB made a wide ranging recommendations on PPPRA and the "Board", but all with the view of enhancing their efficiency. The PPPRA was set up by the PPPRA Act of 2003 to regulate pricing of petroleum products and to handle the Petroleum Support Fund (PSF).
PEF was created by Decree No.9 of 1975 (which was subsequently amended by Decree No.32 of 1989). Its main aim was to see that prices of petroleum products are uniform throughout the county, as wide differentials in prices from zone to zone at that time had led to chaos in the sector. It has since been playing that role.
The funding of PEF is from the net surplus revenue recovered from oil marketing companies.
This fund is used for the reimbursement of petroleum products marketing companies for any losses sustained by them as a result of sales of petroleum products at uniform prices throughout the country. But pundits of full blast deregulation of the oil and gas sector are said to be lobbying members of the National Assembly to throw away PEF and the PPPRA.
They argue that with the determined pursuit of the present administration for a full blast deregulation of the downstream petroleum sector, there is no wisdom in retaining these agencies.
Indeed, at the public hearing on the Bill, major marketers, under the umbrella Major Oil Markers Association of Nigerian (MOMAN), asked that they be allowed to take over PEF and run it at a profit.
Misconception on equalisation subsidy
There is the misrepresentation that government is spending so much though the PEF Management Board and the PPPRA to subsidise mainly petrol Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) for domestic consumption.
While, it is quite true for PPPRA which has the mandate of running the multi-billion Petroleum Support Fund (PSF), the PEF Management Board does not take a penny from government to reimburse marketers. The "Board" gets its funds from consumers of PMS. At the point of loading imported PMS, oil marketing companies pay bridging fund (plus marine transport average) of N3.95 per litre.
The "Board" collects this money at the ports and subsequently uses it to offset transport and bridging cost to all marketers so that prices of petrol would remain fairly the same all over the county. Consumers of petrol eventually pay marketers the N3.95 per litre when they buy fuel at filling stations, because that amount is part of the N65 per litre they pay.
Therefore, government does not spend a penny on equalisation.
However, the money collected by PEF runs into billions, when you think of the fact that Nigerians consume an average of 32 million litres per day of PMS, 85% of which is imported.
But the need for equalisation regime cannot be overemphasised, given the grim reality of living in Nigeria. Those calling for the killing of the PPPRA and PEF are doing so to pave the way for massive exploitation in the name of full deregulation.
In the first place, the idea of calling for full deregulation of the petroleum industry, with the way things stand today, is a clear call for anarchy; and if government is for the people, elected by the people, then it must distance itself from such clamour.
Deregulation of diesel
Going by what is happening on diesel, there is no more doubt that deregulation, whether we like it or not, simply means "increase in prices". At the time government had control of the sale of diesel, its average price was about N45 per liter.
But with full deregulation today, a combination of largely domestic issues, such as high interest rates by Nigerian banks, bridging and transportation costs, all kinds of taxes has led to diesel selling for between N105 and N120 per litre.
In some remote areas, it goes for as high as N250 per litre. The international price of crude seems to matter less here, because even when the price of crude oil came down to $45 per barrel some months back, the price of diesel kept climbing marginally.
Now, the effect of that is that manufacturers now spend exorbitantly to keep their factories running on generators in the face of poor power supply, according to the director general of Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN), Mr. Bashir Borodo.
If the Nigerian power utility were working, that cost of energy could have been minimal. But the manufacturer must, at the end of production, make profit. The consumer is made to pay for it. That is part of the reason increasing inflation is making rubbish of the pay of the people.
So, the idea in any guise that full deregulation would set in competition and drive the price of PMS down is not true given the diesel scenario. And if prices must remain fairly equal across the country, it would be wrong to eliminate the Equalisation Fund.
Nigeria could do other things with the billions of PSF
Many Nigerians find fault with the argument that the PSF could be put to "better" use than in being used to subsidise fuel. This is because if government were to pay back to Nigerians what most of us, except those in power, subsidise for the failure of government, none of them in power would be able to receive a kobo as remuneration, not to talk of the billions they appropriate for themselves each year.
Nigerians, today, build their homes, produce their electricity, provide water for themselves and arrange their own security. If that cost were to be computed, it would certainly be in trillions of Naira monthly.
While most of the people in power today went to school on government scholarship, they have ruined the educational system so much so that only private schools remain the main places to get children fairly educated, at prohibitive costs to parents. So, if the Federal Government spends N350 billion in a year to stabilise petrol at N65 per litre, people argue that it is not too much.
And they seem to have a case! If you allow the price of petrol to go to say N70 per litre, the price of staple foods like gari and yam will go up because transportation fare will rise. The local barber will increase his price, because he generates his own power with petrol. The spare parts seller will hike his price.
Even GSM operators will start arguing for increased call rate, and it would make sense since they all generate the power to run their base stations with diesel and petrol generators. Clinics will make marginal increases on their services to alleviate the increase in generating energy. The landlord will increase his rent, arguing that what he takes as rent cannot support him again.
History of fuel price increase
October, 1 1978: from 8.45k to 15.3k
April 20 1982: from 15.3k to 20k
March 31 1986: from 20k to 39.5k
April 10 1988: from 39.5k to 42k
January 1, 1989: from 42k to 42k commercial vehicles,60k private.
December 19, 1989: moved to uniform price of 60k
March,6, 1991: from 60k to 70k
November 8, 1993: from 70k to N5.00
November 22,1993: petrol price drops from N5.00 to N3.25k
October,2,1994: from N3.25k to N15.00
October, 4,1994: petrol price drops from N15.00 to N11.00
December, 20, 1998: from N11.00 to N25.00
January,6,1999: petrol price drops from N25.00 to N20.00
June,1,2000: from N20.00 to N30.00
June,8,2000: Petrol price reduced to N25.00
June,13,2000: petrol price further reduces to N22.00
January, 1,2002 from N22.00 to N26.00
June,23,2003: from N26.00 to N40.00
June,2009: Petrol increased to N70.00 a liter.

Original_Search_Title: From Uyo to Madrid
Headline: Nigeria: From Uyo to Madrid
Body_Text:
Lagos — I was unable to write this column in the last two weeks. This is due to my engagements in my second professional constituency, law. First, as our country's 49th Independence anniversary approached, the Nigerian Bar Association (NBA) under the leadership of Mr. Oluwarotimi Akeredolu (SAN) gathered some of its top members at the Le Meridien Ibom Hotel in Uyo, the capital of Akwa Ibom State to deliberate on the review of the nation's constitution and electoral reforms.
The exercise was planned under the auspices of the Legislative Advocacy Group of the NBA as an intervention by the body of lawyers with the aim of providing a useful reference to the National Assembly which is also undertaking a similar exercise. Though still very young at the Bar, I was co-opted into the NBA retreat by the publicity secretary, Mr. Murtala Abdulrasheed, as an observer. I ended up being the secretary of the sub-committee on electoral reforms. It was a great experience for me. Not only was I able to once again get a useful glimpse of some of the problems which spawned the Niger-Delta people's grumble about the injustice in the Nigerian nation, I also saw some commendable efforts being made by the state governor to bring development to his people.
More importantly, I participated in serious arguments on the provisions of our present constitution and the recommendations of the Uwais panel. Many lawyers who have impacted on the present constitution by using legal submissions before the courts to give new meanings to the provisions re-enacted the act during the retreat. At the end of the three days which the retreat lasted, about 24 specific and broad conclusions were reached. These include the recommendations that the constitution should be amended in sectors not in one fell swoop as being planned by the National Assembly; that state police is a desirability in the Nigeria of today; that an Independent Police Complaint Commission should be established as well as a constitutional court with an appellate court; that funding of the police should be a first line charge on the Consolidated Revenue Fund; that there should be established an Electoral Offences Commission and that the chairman and members should also be appointed by the president on the recommendation of the National Judicial Council (NJC).
Other key recommendations from the retreat are the reconstitution of the NJC; removal of INEC from the list of Federal Executive bodies; moderation of the powers of nolle prosequi exercised by the attorney general in such a way that he becomes accountable to the legislature on a yearly basis concerning the use of the power; a comprehensive review of the legislative lists to ensure that there is a balance between the federal and state governments and a review of the revenue sharing formula. At the end, the retreat agreed that the NBA will institute a body to prepare a draft constitution which will be presented to the National Assembly.
The NBA effort will surely help the federal legislature and that is why the body needs to act fast so that its contribution can get to the legislative house now that it is still searching for the right formula for collating the views of Nigerians on how to evolve the durable basic laws of the land.
Early in the morning of October 1, many of the participants in the retreat hurried out of their hotel rooms to catch a flight to their various destinations either from Calabar or Port Harcourt. The fear of the bad portions of the road between Uyo and Calabar and the ongoing repair of the Margaret Ekpo Airport which had reduced the number of incoming and outgoing flights forced many to leave early. The desperation by many of the participants to leave Uyo early was due to the fact that in less than 48 hours many of them would have to catch a flight again to Madrid, Spain, where they were to join their colleagues from across the world for this year's annual conference of the International Bar Association (IBA). For a journalist/lawyer like me, I still had to fulfill my obligation as a newspaper editor by spending the hours between Thursday afternoon and Saturday morning to send the bulk of the THISDAY on Sunday pages to bed before flying out to Madrid.
I had joined the IBA three years ago and never gave any thought to attending its annual conference which I thought was just a mere jamboree. I was contented with getting the various legal publications from the body and the opportunity of accessing various sites for research offered members. However, out of curiosity, I decided to participate in this year's conference.
The Madrid conference was a trial that convinced me. It was one conference in which the organisers packed a whole lot of intellectual engagements alongside social functions and, yet make participants have the feel of holidaying. The conference was designed to ensure that almost all issues under the sun came for discussion by experts and participants, particularly under the legal purview. There was so much to learn and a broad range of experiences from various nations and continents were brought to bear on the discussions.
Right from the opening ceremony at the big Palacio Municipal de Congresos, the mix of the intellectual and social setting that the conference would represent became obvious. Presided by King Juan Carlos, the monarch of Spain, the ceremony witnessed talks on Towards a New International Order: the Geopolitics of the Global Recession by Quetin Peel, the international affairs editor of the Financial Times of London. Peel in his lecture said new rules of the conduct of international trade are emerging from the reactions of the international community to the current global recession.
He said rules administered by the World Trade Organisation (WTO) and G-20 perfectly demonstrate that the new world order can help to stabilise economies worldwide and avoid a repeat of a "classic recession after a classic boom bubble." Peel warned that individual countries could be too concerned with domestic worries to take their place as leaders in a new global frame work of co-operation and consolidation. While canvassing balance of powers towards global leadership, he recommends multilateralism, a system that relies on a unified and functional set of rules.
The opening ceremony was capped by the beautiful performance of delectable Flamenco artiste, Estrella Morente who was accompanied on stage by a guitarist. Morente, according to a report in the Daily News, the conference's official newspaper, is the classically trained artiste whose music is influenced by more modern aesthetics and of a neo-classical generation. While I was not particularly thrilled by her kind of music, her sonorous voice and her beauty are something one could not but cheer.
The opening day events ended with a lavish welcome party in the Palacio del Negralejo, the palace of King Carlos. The party was so impressive that you would hardly imagine about 5000 people feasting in one enclosure alongside many entertainers, yet there was no feeling of space constraints of any kind. The palace was so spacious that one cannot imagine anything close to it in Nigeria. The welcome party was also like a tour of Spanish history. Buildings, war implements and an array of objects which represent the ancient civilisation in Europe were arranged around the palace. I joined Justice Alaba Ajileye of the Kogi State judiciary, Mr. Joe Abrahams, the attorney general of Kogi State, Mutiu Ganiyu and Wale Omoniyi from the chambers of Chief Wole Olanipekun (SAN) on a tour of the palace. At every point, we would guess the age of one royal cart or another ancient item. The discussions in that palace began the series of talks we were to later hold about the situation in our country.
Back to the conference proper. With over 50 sessions taking place in a day and in about five contiguous centres, participants had the opportunity of learning so much. The issues ranged from investments, labour, immigration, taxation, sports, sexuality, construction, project finance, infrastructural development, mergers and acquisition, global recession, terrorism, war and issues arising from it to human rights and core issues of management and other developments in the court rooms and the law firms. Each session took about three hours with coffee break in between. The sessions in the morning were different from the ones in the afternoon. A participant had the option of listening to one or two sessions each in the morning and afternoon.
The titles of the various sessions were captioned in such a captivating manner that one would think they were the handiwork of a copywriter in an advertising agency. The topics further created confusion for a freelance participant who did not make up his mind from his home country on what areas of law he would like to attain fresh or more knowledge. The sessions had experts from all over the world leading the discussions and people freely exchanged ideas and experiences.
Apart from the enlightening sessions, there were the social functions, including tours to interesting places in and around Madrid. The most popular place to visit in the Spanish capital for a football buff like me is the Real Madrid stadium at the Santiago Bernabeu. What a magnificent edifice! It has 80,354 seats. It is in the heart of the business district in Madrid and surrounded by banks and top business headquarters in the Chamartin district. The four training pitches located inside the club's training camp at Valdebebas where IBA participants from Latin America tackled their counterparts from the rest of the world were so beautiful that the best pitch here in Nigeria cannot match any of them. There are many other areas one can visit as part of the IBA conference, provided one has a fat wallet consisting of Euros.
Another opportunity the IBA conference provided is the space to network. The participants were able to meet colleagues from around the world, exchange ideas and initiate professional and business relationships. Indeed, many Nigerian lawyers used the opportunity to network among themselves and get to build profitable relationships. For some of our judges who attended, the conference provided opportunity for them to step down from the isolation on the elevated bench and move around freely, listen to fresh ideas and cautiously relate with people. For example, I took a walk from the hotel with Justices Ajileye and Okuwobi of the Lagos High Court at different times to board the metro and I was impressed with the way their lordships gracefully carried themselves. I also sat with Justice Oyekan Abdullahi at the breakfast table. My lord, Justice Oshodi appeared on the football pitch at Valdebebas looking so smart and sporty like an Under-21 player.
There was also the Nigerian Bar Association party held at the residence of our ambassador in Madrid. It was an opportunity for participants at the conference to eat local foods like amala, pounded yam, moinmoin and efo riro. Many participants also turned out in beautiful local attires while local music were played. It was an avenue to give local Nigerian flavour to the conference.
More importantly, the IBA conference provided a variety of opportunities for lawyers wishing to stock their libraries. Variety of law book publishers used the expansive registration centre on the third floor of the Palacios de Municipal Congressos to display books for sale. Some books can be ordered to be delivered in the participants' home countries. The book sellers included LexisNexis, Kluwer Law International, Bloomsbury Professional, Oxford Publications, i-law.com., among others. There are other exhibitors. And many of these exhibitors give out several souvenirs, free journals and other mementoes. With so many books that can be purchased at reduced prices, so many free journals and souvenirs added to the other personal effects that can be purchased in the city shops, it is no surprise that many Nigerians paid for extra luggage at the airport. In all, Madrid 2009 was a good experience as incident of attack on participants by urchins was not as common as it was in Buenos Aires last year. Well, till we meet again in Vancouver, Canada next year, GRATIAS.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra Political Debacle, a Shame - Anya
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra Political Debacle, a Shame - Anya
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chairman of Nigerian National Merit Award (NNMA) and former Chairman Nigerian Economic Summit Group (NESG), Prof. Anya O. Anya in this interview with Charles Ajunwa, spoke on the political crisis in Anambra State, current war against corruption and Nigeria's 49th independence anniversary
What are your views on the recent political development in Anambra State?
It's a shame, but then we now have a new generation who has no shame. So, what do you do? But that will give way; they are playing themselves out, so sooner or later it will lead to new things entirely. Anambra is at the lowest now and it has no way out. You know when you are down you have no other way to go but up. So, whether Anambra people like it or not sooner or later things will start sorting out by themselves.
As a concerned national leader, what do you think is the way out of the political logjam in Anambra?
Well, the way to sort out problems is not usually in the market place. Some of us will put our heads together and we will certainly put a plan in place. But what is happening makes it easier to start sorting out the problems of Igbo land.
But don't you think that the if Justice Mohammed Uwais-led electoral reform committee's recommendation had been implemented, it would have prevented the political logjam in Anambra debacle?
You see any reform has to start from the hearts of people. If you don't have the hearts and attitudes of people reformed, your reforms will not work. Even, if you say that one man one vote, we have always been saying one man one vote. What has happened? We have not allowed the people to vote, that has been the problem. So, it wasn't because the rules of voting were not there but because people did not have the attitude to know that each man is entitled to a vote. And each man should be allowed to vote. In fact, part of Anambra problem right now is that there are some people who don't want that election, the governorship election to take place because they don't want an open contest. But fortunately democracy says there must always be contest and whatever emerges out of that contest is the leadership.
Are you satisfied with the current fight against corruption in the country?
No. I cannot be satisfied because I have said many things about corruption. Let me put it this way, a country where it's said publicly that three former Heads of State accepted bribe from Halliburton and we are all sleeping over it. Nobody has asked questions about it, something is fundamentally wrong. A country where, whether it's right or wrong, a former governor is being accused of being an ex-convict and we are all treating it as if it's not important then something is wrong. But I also believe that because these things are coming out into the public domain now something new is happening in Nigeria. It means that the time of change, the trade shows for transformation, is now close. Is like when you have a pipe that is dripping water it keeps on going kpom, kpom kpom until slowly that cup is full. But the point it reaches just one drop it will start overflowing. I think Nigeria is close to over flowing. But my hope is that we should start the re-organisation and do the right things before we reach that point of over flowing.
But leadership has been described as the bane of Nigeria's development. Do you agree to this?
No, no. I'm part of the leadership so I cannot generalise. I will say what Emeka Anyaoku said in a certain context. I live a life that may be God has been kind to me, I can eat, I can do a few other things but I can account for every kobo that is under my control. I wish those who call themselves leaders in Nigeria could account for what they have because if they could we may discover that they themselves will come to the conclusion that they don't even need what they have.
But it's also what I alluded in my speech earlier, you must get to a time when you recognise that what God has given you is for you to use it to help other people. What you eat is minimum. In fact, as you get older you start eating less. So, let us learn that what God has given to us is meant to be used, to help other people in this country and improve the situation in the country. A country where 35 per cent of your youths, six years out of the university, they have no job something is wrong. Anybody who says he is a leader in such a society should be ashamed as I am.
Nigeria has just marked its 49th independence anniversary, but some Nigerians are saying that as a nation we really do not have anything to celebrate?
Let me say two or three things. First, Nigerians must know that there is something called self-fulfilling prophesy. There is a way you keep on talking about your self and about your country that the things you pronounce with your mouth start happening. That is probably happening to Nigeria. You see God created the world by word of mouth. So, what we say is very important and I therefore believe that we should start reducing the negative things that we say. Yes, we could have done better than we have done but let it now be an opportunity for us to say we should from today now do better. And purely on the spiritual level you know that people say that seven is a hallowed number; it has special significance in spiritual terms. 49, is seven times seven. So, to that extent there must be something new that will start happening in Nigeria. Because we have spent the year seven times seven, which is 49, which is the year before the year of jubilee, which is 50
years. So, I believe that on the spiritual plain the change in Nigeria has begun. Those who steal money, let them continue because the judgment of God will soon descend on this country.
During the celebration of the Igbo annual new yam festival in Lagos recently, you implored Igbos resident in Lagos State to organise themselves well. Can you elaborate more on this?
Well, there is nothing else I can say. I have given you the relative statistics for the census figures in Lagos State. If the Igbos are more than 40 per cent as in the population of Lagos State, you know the conservatives are struggling to form a new party in Britain. You know how much they have? They have 41 per cent but they will need the majority if you go round the world the parties that are forming governments now are having 40 per cent of the population. What I'm saying is because Igbos are already 40 per cent they can form a government in Lagos State if they organise themselves or you cannot form a government without reference to them. It's as simple as that. That is simple politics.
Some people have argued that the removal of the eight banks chief executives was a wrong decision by the Governor of Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Lamido Sanusi. Do you agree?
Did the bank chiefs misappropriate public funds? If the answer is yes, what do you expect the central bank to do after recapitalization? Did you give large sums of money to you and your friends and companies under your control? If the answer is yes, then that is not what banking is about. So, you have done something wrong. So, you should not be in the banking system. It's as simple as that. All the other things you are talking about are irrelevant.
What is your reaction to the two weeks suspension of the strike embarked upon by the Academic Staff Union of Universities (ASUU)?
I'm happy that the ASUU thing is now coming to a resolution. It's not over yet. The reason I'm happy about it is that first, for the first time a government in Nigeria has recognised that cannot shout people in the universities down. You have to sit down and talk with them and find out what the problems are. Secondly, a government in Nigeria is humble enough to say okay if this is the issue I'm prepared to return to the negotiating table with you. So, because of that I wish them well. But let me say this, I spent 37 years of my life in the university, I'm a National Merit Award winner today because I have foreign money to do research in Nigeria. No Nigerian government has made the provision for proper research to be done and for proper teaching to be done. But let us hope that out of this ASUU strike we will now pay the attention we ought to pay to higher education. Because you are not going to be among the 20 largest economies in 2020 or 2050 unless you pay attention to education, unless you pay attention to science and technology and unless you upgrade your universities. It's as simple as that.
What is your assessment of the Lagos State government under Babatunde Fashola?
Governor Fashola is doing a good job. You see anybody who is doing a good work, I will shake the person's hand. I will praise any person that is doing well, and Fashola is doing well. There is no question about that.
But what is your advice for him?
If I have advice to give him, it will be let him broaden his relationship with those who contributed to his success. Two, and most important it's good to find money as every good work needs money. As they say money makes a good soup. We know Fashola needs money to do the work he is doing but the way his people are looking for the money is too much. So, it is now close to extortion. Tax is important but double payment is not good. The whole thing is changing on a daily basis and before you knew it one has paid five different taxes on the same thing under one year. It's not good. Otherwise, Fashola is doing well.
There was an attempt last week to kidnap the Obi of Onitsha, can you comment on this ugly development in the Southeast?
Well, anybody attempting to kidnap the Obi of Onitsha God Himself will punish the person beecause our people say that things have a limit. What people do not know is that kingship in Igbo land has religious as well as political connotation and if you therefore attempt to abuse the person of a proper ruler in Igbo land the wrath of God will visit you because Igwes and Ezes properly constituted. Those who come through inherited lines and not those who made money and made themselves Ezes. The proper Ezes are anointed by God and anybody who touches them as the Bible says "Touch not my anointed." So, if such a thing was attempted the person who has attempted it is in trouble.

Original_Search_Title: Nobody Can Ban Ezeigbo - Ohazulike
Headline: Nigeria: Nobody Can Ban Ezeigbo - Ohazulike
Body_Text:
Lagos — Embattled Eze-Igbo Lagos Chief Nwabueze Ohazulike, has said that the Igbo institution in the country has come to stay.
He also described the attempt to ban his office by the Lagos state government and the South East Traditional Rulers as mere distraction from the real issues facing the Igbo people.
The South East monarchs had condemned the existence of the office of the Eze Igbo outside Igboland and had written all state Governors to derecognise the offices in their states, forcing the Lagos state government to ban the office in the state.
But at the annual New Yam Festival (Iri Ji) of the Igbo weekend, in Fagba, Lagos, Ohazulike said the move will fail as his group, having fulfilled the Corporate Affairs Commission requirements 10 years ago, is a legal entity with all rights guaranteed by the constitution.
Ohazulike accused the South East Traditional Rulers of anti party activities and reminded the crowd that attended his festival of how the Eze Igbo institution came to be in Lagos, with the presentation of the organisation by the late Oba of Lagos, Adeyinka Oyekan 11, as sole custodian of Igbo culture and tradition in the state in 2000.
"We are a legal person with all rights and benefits as enshrined in the constitution of this country," he argued.

Original_Search_Title: Young Journalists Hold Youth Ambassadors to a Draw
Headline: Gambia: Young Journalists Hold Youth Ambassadors to a Draw
Body_Text:
Serrekunda — The Young Journalist Association of the Gambia yesterday played draw to the Youth Ambassadors in an all away visit at the Serrekunda East.
The youth ambassadors first opened the scores from re-start through Abdou Sanyang who benefited from a goal mouth scramble to puck the ball past the opposing journalist shot stopper to the bottom corner of the net.
The journalist after a lackluster first-half display mounted pressure at the YAMS defense forcing them to frequent clearance in front of goal. The Young writing journalist level the scores following a double substitution and Sports Digest's Alagie Nyockeh was at hand to deliver the needed results with a spectacular thunderous bulleting smack, pass the Youth Ambassadors glove man into the net just minutes from time.

Original_Search_Title: Lagos - Ignore Ndigbo At Your Peril -- Anya
Headline: Nigeria: Lagos - Ignore Ndigbo At Your Peril -- Anya
Body_Text:
EMINENT scientist and chairman of Nigerian National Merit Award (NNMA) Prof. Anya O. Anya has warned that it would be political suicide for Ndigbo to be ignored in politics of Lagos State.
Speaking at New Yam festival hosted by Eze Ndigbo Lagos, Eze Hyacint Ohazulike on Saturday, Prof. Anya said the sheer numerical strength of Ndigbo cannot be toyed with in Lagos politics.
According to him, the last national census in Lagos State showed that Ndigbo constituted over 40 per cent of the Lagos population.
He said this much was also attested by ex-governor Bola Tinubu's state census.
The eminent scholar said if the sheer population of Ndigbo is put to better use, the ethnic group would have greater say in who becomes the Lagos State governor.
He said Ndigbo need better organisation in Lagos so that he should not be ignored in the scheme of things.
Prof. Anya said the investment of Ndigbo in Lagos is monumental, revealing that the huge Gross Domestic Product (GDP) of Lagos could not have been possible without the input of Ndigbo.
He said it is important to encourage the development of Ndigbo, stressing that Nigeria would develop if Ndigbo was developed.
Anya advised Ndigbo to invest at home to create an enabling economic environment that would ensure the growth of the region. He said the contribution of Lagos to Nigerian economy is 60 per cent while that of five South-East States remains 7 per cent.
Prof. Anya who is also former Chairman Nigerian Economic Summit Group (NESG), lamented the marginalisation of Ndigbo.
According to him, Ndigbo did not deserve to be treated as second class citizens in Nigeria where they had made enormous contributions to its development.
He enjoined the people to use the occasion of the new yam festival to reflect on the situation. Ndigbo were facing in Nigeria, stressing that they were their own worst enemies.
He tasked political leaders of the South East to be alive to their responsibilities, citing the exemplary action of Senator Bola Tinubu of Lagos State who as governor simultaneously conducted census in Lagos as the National Population Commission (NPC) was conducting its own.
Arguing that this would not give Ndigbo the required respects in the assessment of groups to the Nigerian economy,Prof. Anya also advised Ndigbo to organise themselves, explaining that it should be duty of wealthy Igbos to ensure that their less fortunate kinsmen were doing well in Lagos. He advised the rich Igbos to help their kins in the economic front, arguing that this would help to bring out the potentials of Ndigbo.
He said the China revolution started when Chinese scattered all over Europe and America returned home and put their potentials to work.
Prof. Anya thanked Eze Ohazulike for providing a forum which was doing much to enhance Igbo unity and promotion of Igbo language and culture in Lagos. He said the contribution of Ohazulike to the advancement of Ndigbo was commendable and urged all to support him so that Ndigbo would forge ahead.
Chairman of Ohanaeze Ndigbo, Lagos State chapter, in message read on his behalf by Chief Chinenye Thompson Ohia, commended Ohazulike for his effort to promote Igbo culture among Ndigbo in Lagos.
He said Ohanaeze has set up a committee to investigate the Eze Ndigbo issue in Lagos and assured that the panel will soon turn in its report. He said Ndigbo should continue to support all efforts to keep Igbo language and culture alive.
The new yam festival is an annual event in Lagos package by Eze Ohazulike to eat the new yam. Many cultural dances, masquerades and some royal fathers from South East zone added colour to the event.
Eze Ohazulike at the celebration gave 10 sewing machines, 15 bags of rice, 35 pieces of cloth and four grinding machines to the Lagos State chapter of Ohanaeze Ndigbo as part of his poverty alleviation scheme to make life better for the less-privileged Ndigbo in Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: 'Here, We Recreate Local Cuisines'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Here, We Recreate Local Cuisines'
Body_Text:
Lagos — SENATOR IROEGBU writes on the innovation and recreation of local Nigerian cuisines through an apprenticeship programme introduced by one of the international hotels in the country to boost tourism. The programme which the initiators said is yielding fruits is set out to present local cuisines in a classic form
'Obodo Amerika' is a popular hit track of evergreen high life musician, Bright Chimezie through which he portrayed how Nigerians can go extra mile to have a feel of their beloved local meals (Fufu, Amala, and Tuwo Shinkafa amongst others), especially when far away from home.
Nigerians who might have been fortunate to travel abroad will testify to the fact that the country is abundantly endowed with sumptuous meals of various types, owing to her diverse cultures. It makes the people one of the few nations that can boast of more than three to four varieties of staple meals, which accounts for nostalgic feeling whenever they happen to travel outside the country.
From the east to the west, north to south Nigerian meals are lavishly savouring enough for first time visitors to the country to enjoy it. It is to the credit of the never say die attitude of Nigerians towards their foods that our local cuisines are thriving today alongside western food despite the heavy encroachment of foreign meals.
Placed side by side with its foreign counterparts, Nigerian made dishes have always come tops in the scale of preference. However, the country's hospitality and tourism industry has done well to accommodate the interest of their diverse customers, which is why most of the hotels and eateries are saturated with various local meals laced with foreign oriented cuisines.
The success story of some Nigerian hotels today have a lot to do with the versatility in their ever dynamic menu list and ability to offer wide ranges of customers with most types of meal on demand.
Taking this to the next level is one of the flagships of the Nigeria's hospitality industry, Transcorp Hilton, Abuja, which has created a niche in the area of cuisine innovation for customer satisfaction. The ever busy five-star hotel boasts of various types of global first class restaurant comprising traditional Nigerian cuisine where one can dine with guest to feast on continental favourites from the buffet in the Bukka Restaurant.
Similarly, because of the cosmopolitan nature of its customers, the hotel boast of Zuma Restaurant where the ambient low lighting forms the backdrop to romantic dining overlooking the water fountain of which one can choose from the à la carte menu of Mediterranean favourites and light tasty dishes. In the same vein, the Oriental Restaurant offers fashionable locals and business guests savour Oriental cuisine and Mongolian fare from the à la carte menu where one can dine amid Oriental chandeliers, mosaics and maroon sofas and enjoy themed buffets on selected evenings.
Meanwhile, the Fulani Pool Bar with its traditional thatched roofing, mats and statues, is a design that reflects the culture of Nigeria's Fulani people where a guest can order barbecue à la carte specialties for lunch or dinner. One can as well unwind with a snack as he takes in views of the hotel's beautifully landscaped gardens. While, the Capital Bar provides the facilities where one can unwind with colleagues or friends. At this contemporary sports bar with low lighting, leisure and business guests and fashionable young locals enjoy live music, snack on a spring roll, chicken wings or burger.
Coming back to the Bukka Restaurant where African-style dark woods blend with light walls as natural light streams in to make meals available for breakfast, lunch and dinner. The management of the hotel has made it a priority to offer some innovative menu to their customers to complement with their ever changing preferences that is either influenced by constant interaction with foreign cultures and menu or the dire need to satisfy their unflinching thirst for the local delicacies.
According to the Public Relations Manager, Transcorp Hilton, Shola Adeyemo, the chefs create and recreate menu everyday at the Bukka to satisfy their customer wishes, 80 percent of whom are Nigerians.
"Yes we give some innovation by moving around to research, and seeking for new ideas. We properly research and plan our menu. We create our menu everyday, and every time. This is the creative side of us, which we do through proper planning.
"It is our duty to anticipate our guest needs because if you are in this kind of business, it is your duty to anticipate in order to plan well. By the time you plan well you will exceed the customers' expectation. So even if they come in millions, we are ever ready at every point in time and we have been doing it well and we would continue to doing it.
"You know that 80 per cent of most guests here are Nigerians, so in Bukka we fully deal with Nigerian meals especially vegetable soups, Edinkaninkong, fresh okra soup amongst others. Others meals include Yam Pando (pounded yam), Semovita, and Amala. Whether Calabar (Efik/Ibibio), Igbo, Yoruba or any part of the country, if you place your order we are there to satisfy your needs at any point in time. Take for instance, in the Igbo Bukka setup we have Isi-ewu, Nkwobi at the cooking side, and we have Ugba salad, but we do not serve Massa, though people keep on asking for it.
"We have defined suppliers that are well registered. In terms of hygiene, our standard is very high and has been certified by the main authority. This is how we source our food. But before we receive the food, we have specialist involved in checking the quality of the products or items," Adeyemo said. He noted that meals are served fresh and can be made according to individual tastes and preferences, especially for those who require a recommended kind of meal for health reasons.
"Yes it is so fresh at the time of delivery or presentation. If you go to the receiving bay you would see for yourself that we don't joke with such areas. Our main target is in the quality checking," he said. Similarly, last June, Transcorp Hilton in line with its predatory instinct for customer satisfaction blazed another trail in the evolution of cuisines in Nigeria as it introduced Nigerian Nouvelle Cuisine to its discerning guests, which is being offered for dinner at the hotel's restaurant, the Zuma Grill.
The Nigerian Nouvelle cuisine, which is a product of the hotels Kitchen Apprenticeship Programme, is a departure from the traditional buffet style of presenting Nigerian dishes with emphasises on freshness, lightness, and clarity of flavour. It came in response to some of the richer and more calorie-laden extravagances of classic cuisine, and seeks to emphasise the natural flavours, textures, and colours of foodstuffs.
Speaking to THISDAY, the hotel's spokesman said the basic characteristics of nouvelle cuisine include the use of sauces thickened not by roux (a cooked mixture of flour and fat) but rather by purees of vegetables or fruits; the serving of novel combinations of foods in very small quantity that are artistically arranged on large plates, observing simplicity in cooking methods and infinite attention to texture and detail. Every dish provides additional sophistication with an elegant sauce you are sure to enjoy.
"Our aim is to showcase that Nigerian traditional menu can prepared and presented in such a sophisticated manner comparable to western cuisines," Adeyemo said. The launch of the Nigerian Nouvelle cuisine was the climax of over two years of culinary research by the executive chef, Frank Noack and his team of Nigerian chefs through the Transcorp Hilton Kitchen Apprenticeship Programme.
The team found out that a fine, exotic cuisine can be presented from traditional Nigerian ingredients, and became an instant hit when it was introduced to a select number of guests. The positive reviews then encouraged the management to offer it to guests earlier than the planned launch date.
The Kitchen Apprenticeship Programme was introduced in 1996 and had since produced three sets of graduates, some of whom have risen to become celebrated chefs and executive managers of various kitchens both within and outside the hotel. The core objective of the two-year intensive programme is to take a novice and train him/her to become a world class chef.
Speaking at the final assessment of the class of 2007, Noack explained that training process starts with the introduction to basic theories and practice of cooking through to the fundamentals of attaining the height of an international chef.
"The apprentices are not only trained to become world class chefs, they are also groomed on how to manage a kitchen successfully by working with diverse teams to achieve set goals and objectives. The fruits of the programme are already being reaped as some products of the kitchen apprentice programme have been employed as chef by Hilton Seychelles.
The class of 2007 started out with 18 apprentices. Only six of the apprentices fulfilled all the requirements to qualify for the final assessment. The six finalists can be said to be fully grounded in the art of cooking and each of them has the marking of excellent chefs," Noack said.
The finalists of the apprenticeship programme whose innovative cuisines were introduced include Miss Lilian Abache Oghere, Messer Emmanuel Igboko, Chinedum Godwin, Peterson Osuagwu, Emeka Ekwuru, and John Wodu Ogar. These final participants of the apprentice practical cooking class of 2007 presented their nouvelle cuisines including Amuse bouche made of Nigerian vegetable salad in Yaji dressing.
Starters comprised of classical snail on traditional Eastern abacha and Ugba salad; Quail breast pepper soup; Main dish of Jumbo prawn's in palm butter with Nchanwu rice; and Desert such as Tiro of Nigerian coconut and coffee, tea and chili pralines.
Commenting on the launch of the Nigerian cuisine, the hotel's General Manager, Andre Herrenchmidt said: "We are constantly seeking innovative ways and means of satisfying the culinary of our guests. Today's launch of the Nigerian Nouvelle cuisine is another in the series of innovations we have in stock for our discerning guests".
Herrenchmidt was of the opinion that customers to the ever busy hotel should be expecting a lot of innovative and exotic meals on the offer, especially as the festive season of Christmas beckons.

Original_Search_Title: 91 Years After, Federal Government Intervenes in Nanka Erosion
Headline: Nigeria: 91 Years After, Federal Government Intervenes in Nanka Erosion
Body_Text:
Awka — ATTENTION of the Federal Government has been attracted at last, to the Nanka erosion site, as the Anambra State Federal Ministry of Environment has promised to accommodate the erosion site in its 2010 budget.
Chief Technical Officer of the Ministry of Environment, Mr. Joseph Ogwuche, an engineer announced this at the 2009 new yam festival celebration of Agbiligba Nanka Development Union, Nanka at community Primary School field.
Daily Champion recalls that Nanka erosion crisis started since 1918 when Europeans settled in Nanka.
Ogwuche urged the community to set-up a three-man committee and pursue the ministerial and presidential approval of the erosion site disaster in the 2010 budget.
He applauded the cultural heritage and hospitality of Nanka people in particular and Igbo land arrived the area.
This promise is coming as the 17 families in Agblilgba Nanka led by the Chairman of the Agbililgba -Dimanumoko Nanka Development Union , Mr. Patrick Emezue has appealed to eschew age-long sectional rivalry , conflicts and land disputes and channel their efforts to sustainable development of human and material resources of which advanced societies are envied.
Addressing his people at the occasion, Mr. Emezue said their village can be a citadel of development capable of standing tall in the comity of developed societies the moment they direct their human and material ingenuity not to destroying themselves by age long rivalry and land disputes but for using them to develop themselves while working together hence the more they work together, the happier they should be.
While announcing that some prominent sons of the community would be honoured , Emezue announced that Agbiligba Nanka remained one out of the seven villages in the community ravaged by erosion.
On the Anambra 2010 elections , Emezue said that democracy meant nothing if the people did not vote since the actual meaning of democracy means people and power through the judicious use of the ballot paper just as he advised his people to shun politics of chop and I chop , politics of violence , pervasive use of money and politics of no retreat , no surrender as well as that of do or die as witnessed in 2007 elections.
The community leader appealed to the electoral umpire INEC to be transparent and unbiased in the conduct of the poll.
Chairman of the occasion , Nze Anselem Ezeoke and chairman, planning committee of the Agbiligba Nanka 2009 New yam festival Mr. Felix Ezemba-Aziza thanked God that the event was hitch-free but and for everlasting peace for the community.
In an interview with the Chairman Old Aguata / Orumba North Vigilante Chief Ikechukwu Ezenwankwo appealed to the Anambra State Government to intervene in the dispute in the area before it becomes bloody as that of Umueri and Aguleri communal war.

Original_Search_Title: Vodafone and MTN Support Asogli Traditional Area
Headline: Ghana: Vodafone and MTN Support Asogli Traditional Area
Body_Text:
The staff and management of Vodafone demonstrated their love for the Asogli Traditional Area during their Yam Festival, as the company spent GH¢36,000 on the festival, to make it more attractive to the people, who have remained committed to the company over the years.
The money, which was spent on a state dance, was also a part of donations of one hundred thousand pieces of Vodafon T-shirts, cups, drinks, Vodafone branded water, phones, and a street dance, which saw live displays by artistes like 2-Face and Slim Buster among others.
The Trade and Marketing Manager of Vodafone, Mr. Ekow Blackson, who presented the items at a business center in Ho popularly called Civic Center, said the gesture forms part of the company's social responsibility to support the traditional areas in their development activities.
Mr. Blackson, who educated the people on Vodafone organised promotional activities, said the draw dubbed, 'Stock and Win,' formed part of its outreach programmes, and urged all to participate in it.
Meanwhile, the Volta Region draw saw six persons wining various prizes in Ho.
In a related development, Mobile Telecommunications Network (MTN) also donated GH¢10,000 to the Asogli Traditional Area, as support to the people during the festive period, including some of the company's materials such as T-shirts and cups, which most people who witnessed the presentation, described MTN as committed and a regular supporter of the activities of the Asogli State.
The Acting Cooperate Services Executive of MTN, Madam Eleanor Adjaho Sarpong, who presented the items on behalf of the company, said the move was part of the company's support to traditional areas to assist in their activities.
This year's institutional assistance from Vodafone and MTN to the Asogli Traditional Area could be described as one of the best, as the major streets of Ho, after the festival was over, were left with the memories of the red and yellow colours, as the footprints of Vodafone and MTN.

Original_Search_Title: Asogli State Celebrates Yam Festival
Headline: Ghana: Asogli State Celebrates Yam Festival
Body_Text:
Ho — President John Evans Atta Mills has observed that indiscipline is a major canker that retards the development of the country, and has stressed on the need to fight against such negative practices.
He observed that indiscipline in the society manifested itself in such negative acts as armed robbery, corruption, drug abuse and road accidents. These, he noted, posed a serious threat to national development.
This was contained in a speech read for the President by the Minister for Chieftaincy and Culture, Mr. Alex Asun Ahensah, at this year's grand durbar of chiefs and people of the Asogli Traditional area to climax the yam festival in Ho last Saturday. It was under the theme: "Uniting for Development."
President Atta Mills said indisciplined acts were mostly seen among the youth, and charged parents to live up to their parental responsibilities by disciplining their children who go wayward, adding that parents have a duty of bringing up their children into responsible adulthood.
He said his government was aware of the high expectations of Ghanaians, and was also mindful of the campaign promises the National Democratic Congress (NDC) made to them.
President Mills noted that it was the wish of his government to satisfy such high expectations, but unfortunately, conditions on the ground did not make things possible.
He however pointed out that his government was making frantic efforts to remove all obstacles militating against the fulfillment of the promises made to the people.
President Mills assured Ghanaians that things would soon get better, and pleaded for time and patience, for the government to be able to meet the demands of the people.
He noted that in spite of the difficulties, the government would tackle two promises made to the people of the Volta Region, which he mentioned as the establishment of a public university, and the rehabilitation of the road networks, adding that a committee had been put in place, and was working towards the attainment of these promises.
President Mills urged traditional authorities to use festivals to identify challenging areas of development, and strategise to address them, since the government alone could not meet all the development needs of the people, and commended Togbe Afede XIV, Agbogbomefia of the Asogli Traditional Area, for his developmental initiatives.
Togbe Afede XIV called on Ghanaians to play responsible roles in the development efforts of the country, noting that it was everybody's duty to aspire to make a difference, by bringing their skills to bear on the development of the nation.
He called on chiefs, politicians, religious and opinion leaders in society, to see themselves as partners in development, in order to provide the kind of leadership the people need for development.
The President of the National House of Chiefs, Naa Prof. Nabila, commended the chiefs and people of the Asogli Traditional Area for strengthening their relations with the people of Notse in Togo, and urged them to use such relations to promote peace and unity between the two countries.
The Paramount Chief of Notse, Togbe Agorkorli III, appealed to the governments of Ghana and Togo to make the border at Aflao, which linked the two countries, more accessible, to ensure free movement of goods and services, which he noted, would ultimately benefit the two countries.
Present at the ceremony were the Deputy Minister of Transport, Mrs. Dzifa Ativor, Volta Regional Minister, Mr. Joseph Amenowode, and his Deputy, Cyril Neku, the Member of Parliament (MP) for Ho Central, Capt. George Nfodjoh (rtd), and the Ho Municipal Chief Executive.

Original_Search_Title: How to Wrap Roasted Yam
Headline: Nigeria: How to Wrap Roasted Yam
Body_Text:
Abuja — Many Nigerian professionals, experts and businesses, both at home and in the Diaspora, send hundreds, even thousands of proposals to government ministries and public sector agencies every year. These proposals cover a wide range of products and services which they might have seen elsewhere and thought of introducing back home. Somehow, most of these usually well intentioned and well packaged proposals simply disappear in the labyrinth of government. What happens to them?
A brief excursion into the mindset of the Nigerian civil servant and the workings of public sector organizations would explain why the proposals are hardly replied to, or even acknowledged. It may also explain why those beautifully packaged, articulate and detailed proposals never left somebody's desk, or how it ended up as wrapping for someone's groundnut or suya.
As head of the Ministry, most correspondences from outside are addressed to the 'Honourable Minister'. (Don't mind the misnomer). The Minister is usually too busy to read proposals, so it gets sent to an assistant. The proposal has thus taken its first step towards the basket of the groundnut seller. If it is lucky, it may get sent to the Permanent Secretary who will in turn, direct it to the relevant department or director for 'further necessary action', whatever that means.
Many files and proposals get passed on to ever more junior staff until it gets to the last person on the line. By this time, several pages have been added to it without any work on its actual content being done. The officer with whom the proposal ends up is angry because the file got to him only because there is no money involved. And he is right. If there were the prospect or slightest scent of gratification or money coming from somewhere, those at the top would have done the job themselves or hijacked the proposal.
So the officer keeps the file aside. By this time, the proposal would have been several weeks, even months old. It has not been acknowledged, and its authors have no idea what is happening to what they assumed any sane person would jump at. Sometimes, it gets 'put away'. Sometimes, it gets 'kept in view'. At other times, it is acted on 'accordingly'. But in reality, nobody has taken time to read what the proposal is about. Should the authors persist, or delegate somebody to 'follow the file', they might get to meet some officer 'to discuss some aspects of the proposal'. Eventually, they may write a watery recommendation which is 'humbly submitted' for yet 'further directives'.
At this point, the file begins its convoluted journey back the way it came! It is now twice the size of the original proposal. By the time action is taken, if at all, the owners of the proposal or the officer delegated to track it would have given up. If your company is lucky, it may get a reply to your wonderful proposal - several months after the project, consultancy or even plain idea would have been of any use to anybody.
In many cases, you never get even an acknowledgement. Not even to tell you that your proposal is not needed. There is no number to call, no website to visit, nobody to report to. 'Smart' people, to ensure that their proposals do not get filed away, get a 'connection' or introduction to the Minister or Permanent Secretary. In this case, the proposal may actually be read by these top functionaries of government. But herein lays the danger of this scenario. If there is money to be made from the proposal, a sheep has just walked straight into the lion's den.
The minister or permanent secretary would congratulate you for developing such a wonderful proposal and how Nigeria needs bright young men and women like you. You leave the office elated, perhaps even thinking of the project you're soon to be engaged in, and the money you'd soon be making. Sorry for you.
As soon as you leave, they call their special assistants (in one case, a female minister's brother) who will transfer your entire proposal - wood, stock and barrel to another company letterhead and submit same to the same minister or permanent secretary. So while you go about planning, and even incurring liabilities on the proposed project, your ideas would have been processed, bastardized, shabbily implemented, and 10 times the amount you asked for paid to these fictitious companies. By this time, the minister or permanent secretary (if he is still in the same ministry), would have become totally inaccessible. So much for connections.
One day you open a newspaper or tune in to news to see or watch your proposal being 'commissioned'. This 'direct stealing' of ideas and proposals is not restricted to only the top level management.
