Virtually every civil servant has a company or has access to one that can be used to 'lift' peoples' professional ideas and other consultancy proposals. Even sealed bids have known to be expertly opened, competitors' ideas stolen, so that they usually come up with the most cost effective and most comprehensive submissions. If you think that is far-fetched, you do not know the system.
So, if you have been wondering why those thoughtful, articulate and well-written proposals from experts and professionals to public (and even private) sector organizations never get anywhere, you need to know how roasted yam is wrapped. Then you will see that the proposal was either hijacked or poorly implemented, or someone simply couldn't be bothered. Or worse still, somebody thought it would make good wrapping for roasted yam.
Suleiman is of Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria

Original_Search_Title: Class of '99 - When Men Were Boys
Headline: Nigeria: Class of '99 - When Men Were Boys
Body_Text:
Lagos — ESOSA OMO-USOH has some fond, and not so fond, memories of his time at the Nigerian Law School when his set pioneered the Abuja campus ten years ago
" the gates to hell stand one on its hinge broken the other inscribed with the artless hieroglyphics of judgment " From Dreaming in metaphors
January 26, 1998 marked the beginning of two epochal events for me and, as I suspect, other members of the pioneer class of 1999. The first event was the beginning of our compulsory one year odyssey in legal training at the newly-relocated law school in Bwari, Abuja. The second event was the beginning of the demystification of the law school.
Back in my undergraduate days at the University of Benin, I recall being regaled with awe-inspiring tales of the law school by a couple of my lecturers. Then, we were made to believe that the law school was a revered institution where you are molded both in character and learning in preparation for your admission into the learned profession. Everyone and everything were supposedly prim and proper in the law school.
That illusion was first shattered on January 26, 1998 when I beheld the gates to the law school. It was about 2pm or thereabout when the Edo Line vehicle I had boarded from Benin City (along with four resit students from the class of 1998 who had come to the law school to check the results of their resit examinations) arrived at the law school.
The law school had just relocated from its traditional base in Lagos. As rumours had it then, the relocation was on the instructions of the Abacha Administration. Apparently, the relocation had been done without first putting in place the requisite structures. The property housing the law school was the defunct Centre for Democratic Studies (CDS) established by the Babangida Administration in Bwari, a satellite town lying on the outskirts of the Federal Capital Territory.
The first thing that struck me as we arrived the law school was the gates at the entrance. The state they were in inspired the poem from which the quotation atop this piece was culled. When I got to the admissions office, I met a huge crowd of law graduates from universities across the country. The admissions list was scheduled to be released that afternoon and everyone was waiting anxiously and praying that their name would be on it. Later in the afternoon, the list was released and pasted on a make-shift notice board. This inspired a gold rush of sorts as anxious law graduates buzzed around the notice board like bees in a hive trying desperately to locate their names on the list.
The journey proper began on April 22, 1998 when the law school formally commenced the Bar Part II course programme for the 1998/1999 session. As with the release of the admissions list, the registration process for successful candidates bore no semblance of the famed prim and proper reputation of the law school. Poor organisation and logistics problems made the registration process as tortuous as the seemingly endless queues of law graduates waiting to be attended to by the irritable law school staff in charge of registration.
Concluding the registration process did not guarantee any respite as you then had to contend with the poor arrangements for accommodation. The four halls of residence (Blocks A, B, C and D) were barely completed before the commencement of the Bar Part II course programme. Given the large number of entrants, we were assigned four to a room. Those who were not comfortable with the idea of being paired with strangers from other universities were able (with the right arrangement) to get their friends assigned to the same room with them. Unfortunately, not all the rooms had beds in place. So, those of us unfortunate to be assigned such rooms had to make do with mattresses on the bare floor before our beds arrived.
Feeding posed another great challenge to us as the authorities, apparently in the hurried relocation from Lagos to Bwari, had not made adequate preparation for feeding logistics. In the early days, we had only two food vendors. The first was Mama Nosa; whose make- shift buka located close to the female hostel blocks, originally catered for the construction workers working on the halls of residence. My friends and I fondly referred to her buka as "Mile 12" given its relative distance from the male hostels.
The second vendor was a strikingly dark-complexioned Yoruba woman whose open air buka was located underneath a dogonyaro tree close to the male hostels. Her specialty was fried yam with hot peppery sauce. We fondly referred to her buka as the "Centre for Democratic Feeding" or CDF for short. The name was an obvious play on the name of the previous occupants of the law school premises and the peculiar style of service at the buka whereby patrons milled around the woman and her make shift stove with their plates in hand waiting to be served fried yam and sauce straight from the frying pan.
By the time lectures commenced on May 12, 1998, the auditorium was still under construction and therefore not ready for use. The authorities came up with a temporary solution that lasted the entire one year we spent in the law school. The assembly hall and the canteen were converted into lecture rooms. There were two streams of lectures and depending on your registration number, you sat for the lectures either in the assembly hall or the canteen. The lecturers usually delivered their lectures in the assembly hall and those of us in the canteen listened in via the several speakers embedded in the ceiling of the canteen.
Lectures were billed to commence at 9am for the morning stream and I believe 2pm for the afternoon stream. However, owing to the fact that the sitting arrangements at the lecture venues were usually inadequate for the student population, there was always a mad rush for sitting positions just before the commencement of lectures. During the first term, I recall some students used to wake up as early as 4am in the morning to secure and reserve sitting positions for lectures that were billed to commence at 9am! And there was also the rather sorry spectacle of lawyers-in-training all dressed up in their formal gears with chairs from their hostel rooms slung over their shoulders as they strolled into the assembly hall or canteen to receive their daily lectures.
Curiously, the law school authorities did not find this spectacle or the spectacle of students eating at make-shift canteens under the shade of dongoyaro trees disturbing or unfit for the noble profession. Rather, one day we woke up to hear of a decree (it being the appropriate word) forbidding the wearing of jeans within the campus. Apparently, as we were later to learn, the then Director-General had, on a Sunday visit to the female hostels, encountered a student (a classmate from the University of Benin) clad in a rather hot pair of jeans bum shorts and that outrage had sparked off the ban on jeans on campus.
Another obnoxious decree was the one on restriction of access of male students into female hostels and vice versa. Understandably, this particular decree was not very popular with the student population. Earlier on our arrival on campus, there was an attempt, during a meeting of the student population, to come up with a common objection to this restriction and present same to the authorities during the Director-General's speech scheduled for April 27, 1998. Surprisingly, there was a minority group comprising male students from Northern Universities who were in support of the restriction. During the meeting, their position was vigorously canvassed by one Mahmoud (if memory serves me right) whose memorable, if bordering on extremist, oratory turned comical when in the course of his submission, he cited a judicial authority and surmised thus; " it is a pundamental (i.e. fundamental) frincifle (i.e. principle) of law as stated in the case of Dfifi (i.e. DPP) v. Shaw "
After the Director-General's speech on April 27, 1998, one Oyosoro, on behalf of the student population, volunteered a response and prayed the authorities to reconsider their position on the issue. In his response, the obviously displeased Director-General noted that never in history of the law school had protocol been so flagrantly breached by having someone responding after the Director-General had delivered his speech. Needles to say, the decree stayed and those restless males who could not do without female consort had to hang out in front of the female hostels and send signals of their presence to the appropriate rooms either through the porters or their assistants or even through other female students.
One aspect of our sojourn at the law school that lived up to its hype was the pre-examination fever. The attendant state of near-insanity that heralds the commencement of the Bar final examinations was (and still is) the stuff of legend. As soon as we resumed for the 3rd term at the law school, you could tell the count down was on. You could feel the tension in the air. Frivolities took the backseat and earnest preparation for the Bar final examinations took control of virtually everyone. As D-day drew closer, the more palpable the tension became. At this point, stories started flying around of people buckling under the pressure and electing to defer their sitting for the examinations. Finally, D-day arrived and the Bar final examinations were upon us like a ton of bricks!
After six frenetic days marked by logistics incompetence on the part of the law school authorities, we rode that storm like a crazy bronco and afterwards, everything was beautiful again. At least until the next three moths when the results would be released.
On July 31, 2009, the Bar final examination results were published and the overall performance was sterling and as I understand it, something of a record at that time. The icing on the cake came in September 1999 when we were called to the Bar. Ten years after as I look back, I cannot help but think, it was one hell of a ride!
To the class of 1999, whether in active legal practice or some other endeavour, dead or alive, allow me to propose a toast; Congratulations! It was not easy, but we made it!
To the occupants of "the wigs" and 'Coral's Slit", my experience with you guys is one I will cherish forever! Finally, to Elijah Ahiaba (RIP), the nicest guy who ever lived, thank you for the privilege and honour of your friendship.
OMO-USOH is with Solola & Akpana, Barristers and Solicitors, Port Harcourt.

Original_Search_Title: Over 100 Trucks Stranded At Nkwanta
Headline: Ghana: Over 100 Trucks Stranded At Nkwanta
Body_Text:
Over 100 trucks carrying foodstuffs are stranded in the northern part of the Volta Region following heavy rainfall that has rendered portions of the Nkwanta/Hohoe/Accra road impassable.
Trucks carrying yams, cereals and passengers are deep in mud since Sunday at Salifu on the Nkwanta/Kadjebi road. Other impassable portions of the road are at Kankyi Kura, Kpeve junction and Otikeb.
Mr Joseph Nikab, Nkwanta District Chief Executive who briefed the Ghana News Agency, described the situation as "very serious" and appealed to the Ministry of Roads and Highways and other related agencies to move swiftly to assist.

Original_Search_Title: EGDC Donates N6.5 Million Relief Materials to 32 Communities
Headline: Nigeria: EGDC Donates N6.5 Million Relief Materials to 32 Communities
Body_Text:
Warri — THE Egbema Gbaramatu Community Development Council (EGCDC) has donated relief materials worth over N6.5million to 32 communities in Gbaramatu kingdom, Warri South local government area of Delta State.
Making the presentation to the various communities, the Chairman of the EGCDC, Mr. Edmund Doyah-Tiemo, said the relief materials were part of the council's contribution toward alleviating the attendants sufferings visited on the people in the aftermath of the May 13 militants/JTF clash.
According to him, the food items will go a long way in augmenting whatever is their means of livelihood in the interim.
He said since their return to the village, life had been very difficult, as most of them had to start all over again.
He charged the communities to distribute the items equitably to avoid misunderstanding and unwarranted fighting that would lead to further suffering.
Tiemo also enjoined them to always maintain the peace and endeavour to leave harmoniously with all their neighbours.
Some of the communities that got a larger portion of the relief materials are Okerenkoko Zion, Okerenkoko, Azama, Oporoza Zion, Oporoza, Kunukunuma, Kurutie, Opuede Bubor, Opuede, Benekrukru, Kokodiagbene, Macaraba, Ubenfa, amongst others.
The relief materials, which included beverages, toiletries, detergents, clothing materials, comprise 89 bags of rice, 293 bags of garri, 61 bags of beans, 99 bags of salt, 49 gallon of palm oil, 99 gallons of groundnut oil, 50 cartons of fish, 835 tubers of yam and 56 rams.
Representative of one of the communities, Mr. Mulade, thanked the EGCDC for the gesture and assured that they would be evenly distributed.
He also charged the council to always remember that it would take quite some time for them to fully recover from the calamity brought on them by the unfortunate violence.

Original_Search_Title: The Akanu Ibiam International Airport
Headline: Nigeria: The Akanu Ibiam International Airport
Body_Text:
LAST week, the Federal Executive Council announced that it had approved N4 billion for the upgrade of the Akanu Ibiam International Airport, Enugu. The news was conveyed to the public by the Information minister, Dr. Dora Akunyili.
It immediately elicited a jig from Mr. Ike Ekweremadu, the ranking senator from Enugu and deputy president of the Senate, who immediately began to wax lyrical about Yar'Adua showing the South-East that he cared. Well, really?
In any case, why shouldn't Yar'Adua care? Approving state funds for the upgrade of federal investments in the South East of Nigeria should no longer be considered an act of charity from some god sitting in some splendid remove at Abuja, and dishing largesse to whomsoever he pleases.
It is an economic decision - and it is a sound economic decision to invest in an international airport in Enugu to cater for that segment of a travelling population who are currently inconvenienced by the absence of such a facility.
And the South-East of Nigeria is either part or not of the federation of Nigeria. Besides, I think that the senator Ike Ekweremadu must know that part of the negotiating process and gains of democratic of representation is the willingness to use legislative authority to push for projects and programs towards one's constituency.
The decision to site and in fact upgrade the current facilities of the Enugu airport ought, therefore, to have nothing to do solely on the whims of President Yar'Adua.
Hopefully, it is a well-thought project emanating from the budget office, and taking life from the approbation committee of the various arms of the National Assembly, and of course requiring the affirmation of the president as fait accompli. He would be doing his duty and it would not be an act of charity to the East.
Besides, the money that goes into the upgrade of facilities at the Akanu Ibiam International Airport in Enugu is not an out-of-pocket grant from the president, nor does it come from some inherited fortune or family trust fund accruing to the president by which he endows charitable work; it is money derived from the general revenue of pool of the federation of Nigeria coming mostly from receipts from exports, chief of which currently is oil, and from taxation - personal income tax, value added tax and corporate tax - some of which, I suppose are collected in Enugu particularly, and the South East of Nigeria generally.
I should, therefore, imagine that citizens of these areas must have some benefit of government, and such benefits must not be attributed to an individual, namely the president of the federation, but to the process of collective decisions that fashion and regulate the good conduct of state.
I realize, of course, that much of this question about the power of the Presidency is moot, but if it must take the president to decide the upgrade of the International Airport at Enugu, or even to raise the funds for such an upgrade, it means that the Federal Aviation Authority and the Federal Airports Development Authority, the institutions charged with airport regulation and development in Nigeria are redundant, and these bodies must then be scrapped, and such matters as ought to come under their purview charged henceforth under the office of the president.
But it also must mean that the president has more power than is permissible in a democracy.
It is the power to play God and the Russian roulette with our affairs. The president of the federation must never be in a position where he has the yam as well as the knife.
Such a power as personalizes the powers of state and continues to create the cult of personality that would make the senator from Enugu dance a public Atilogwu and sing the president's panegyric just because the Federal Executive Council announces a mere N4 billion naira for the upgrade of a national infrastructure in his district is dangerous to democracy. We must review such powers if it is to make sense at all.
I have for long been under the impression that the National Assembly of which Ekweremadu is a principal operator, holds the purse strings.
Whatever it approves, the president spends. But I apparently need to be better informed about this. Besides, talk about the upgrade of the Akanu Ibiam international airport has been long on the marks. Indeed, there have been earmarks to it from the federal accounts, yet it has remained more in rhetoric than in fact.
Eastern and indeed many Middle belt international travelers have frequently demanded this upgrade for a number of reasons, chief of which is the inconvenience of always travelling to Lagos, Abuja or Kano, or even Port-Harcourt before they could board or even disembark from an international flight.
The two international gateways to the East, the Port-Harcourt Airport, and the Enugu Airport are underdeveloped and underinvested. This has been a major grouse.
The Enugu facility particularly needs significant upgrade in order to accommodate a certain class of aircraft, and indeed, a certain welcoming ambience befitting a national gateway.
The economic implications and opportunities that might accrue from the operations of an international airport at Enugu are rather too well known to rehash here.
But we need the facilities, and I'm afraid N4billion is rather too meager to effect the kind of upgrade befitting an international Airport in Enugu. It pales so profoundly when we note that about N124 billion was expended in the expansion and upgrade of just the domestic wing of the Ikeja Airport.
If the Federal authorities wish to invest in an international airport, and
the Akanu Ibiam International Airport is worthy of its own name and pedigree, then it must do the right thing: N4billion is unlikely to effect both the aesthetic and utilitarian character of that airport, not if we are thinking about new hangars, new comfortable terminal buildings, new runways, new landing facilities, and so on and so forth.
The Nigerian government and the Aviation authority must do the right thing. It must show us, citizens and potential users of this international airport, the design concept for this upgrade as a matter of public service.
It ought in fact to open public tenders for this design to talented Nigerian and even international designers, builders, an architects, so that we can choose rationally, raise taxes, get the matching grant from government, and build a befitting international airport in Enugu.
Enough of these arbitrary impositions - we citizens of this country should no longer be taken for children to whom candies are frequently handed. Besides, we must get a timeline for this N4 billion for the airport.

Original_Search_Title: Governor Orji Rewards Enyimba, Ultimate Commander, Aligwoekwe for Excellence
Headline: Nigeria: Governor Orji Rewards Enyimba, Ultimate Commander, Aligwoekwe for Excellence
Body_Text:
Lagos — TWO times Confederation of African Football (CAF) Champions League winners, Enyimba International, World Wrestling Professional (WWP) African Heavy Weight Champion, Osita Offor, aka De Ultimate Commander and World/Paralympic Heavyweight Powerlifter, Obioma Aligwoekwe, were among the beneficiaries of Governor Theodore Orji of Abia State's reward for excellence weekend.
The event which took place at the state house, Umuahia, was a carnival as it coincided with the state's new yam celebration. The Abians rolled out their drums, dancing in the streets of the town throughout the night in celebration of their heros
Governor Orji splashed N25 million to reward the sporting youths for their excellence with Enyimba International getting the lion share.
Each of the 25 players and officials got the sum of N500,000 while the Team Manager, Chief Felix Anyansi, and the Club's supporters got the sum of N800,000 and N2 million respectively for winning the Federation Cup which held recently in Lagos and for the excellent performance over the years. Enyimba International will fly the country's flag at the CAF Confederation Cup next year.
De Ultimate Commander and Aligwoekwe received equally, the sum of N500,000.
The Ochendu of Abia, Governor Orji, in his remarks pledged his continued support and reward for the youths in the state who are portraying state in good light in their chosen fields of endeavours. He said the occasion was meant to showcase his administration's resolve to reward excellence.
"Abia under my administration will continue to honour the state's sportsmen and women who excelled in sports and in other endeavours. I want to use this platform to showcase them for the world to know that great things are happening in Abia and for other youths to emulate them and stay off crime and start something meaningful for themselves", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Price of Food Items Skyrocket in FCT
Headline: Nigeria: Price of Food Items Skyrocket in FCT
Body_Text:
Just few days into the Ramadan fast, prices of food items in the Federal capital city have skyrocketed, prompting complaints from residents and marketers.
A survey by our correspondent indicated that prices of food items such as rice, beans, groundnut oil, palm oil, spaghetti, sugar, milk, onions and other related items have increased astronomically.
A measure of rice which was sold at N300 now goes for N350, sugar of N200, now sells for N350, a measure of beans is now being sold at the rate of N200 against its former price of N160. It was also observed that a bottle of groundnut oil that was sold at N200 now goes at N250 and the price of spaghetti has also moved up from N120 to N150 depending on the type.
A bag each of rice and beans sells for N10,000 and N7,000 against their former prices. However, the price of some items in the market still remains the same, like flour, maize, yam, curry, thyme etc.
Prices of tomatoes and pepper has also increased against their former prices.
A vendor, Mrs. Chioma Ezeshe, attributed the price increase to high demand by the residents with the commencement of fasting.
The price of onions has also increased as a small bag of onions is now sold at N1,200 against N800, while a big bag formerly N1,200 now sells at N2,400.
It was also discovered that millet and guinea corn that were sold at a very low price are now being sold at very high prices, because, according to our survey, it is now discovered that most people prefer them, for the affordability.
However, investigatiosn further revealed that the increase in prices of items in most markets in the territory, such as Kuje, Gosa, Bwari, Gwagwalada and Lugbe did not affect Wuse market, Garki Model Market and the new Garki Ultra Modern market respectively.

Original_Search_Title: Fire Destroys Goods Worth Millions of Naira
Headline: Nigeria: Fire Guts Multi-Million Naira Goods
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — The popular Eke Market in Afikpo, the second largest city in Ebonyi State, was yesterday razed by fire, which destroyed stalls and goods worth millions of naira.The fire was the third in the series of tragic developments that befell the town coming on the heels of the sudden death of former Deputy Governor of the State, Dr. Emmanuel Oko Isu and another renowned broadcaster from the area, Mr. Jonny Okarafor, also known as soul-to-soul, at the weekend.
THISDAY checks revealed that the tragedies have thrown the entire Afikpo into mourning, with some people contemplating on deserting the town.Informed sources told THISDAY that the fire, which started around eight in the morning quickly spread to other parts of the market.
The fire disaster coincided with the celebration of new yam festival in Afikpo, which prompted the shutting down of the market with all the entrances locked up before the fire started, a development that worsened the situation, as it took several efforts to gain entrance.
Men of the state fire service arrived the scene three hours later, but were prevented from entering the market by irate crowd who threatened to attack them if they don't leave the scene, saying that their arrival was meaningless, as the market had already been razed.But the Deputy Chief Fire Officer, Mr. Simon Amadi Mkpuma, told newsmen at their Abakaliki office, that the distance between Abakaliki and Afikpo, coupled with the bad road network delayed them.When contacted on phone, Chairman, Afikpo North Local Government area, Mr. Obinna Agha, said the level of destruction was monumental, and called on the State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA) to come to the aid of the victims.He also appealed to the state government to decentralise fire stations, saying that each of the senatorial zones in the state should have a fire station, adding that a situation where the state fire station at Abakaliki services the entire state will make it look as if government is not serious in helping the people.
He commended the construction giant, Julius Berger, for its quick response, arriving almost immediately with its water truck from a nearby quarry site.

Original_Search_Title: Pounded Yam And Ogbono Soup
Headline: Nigeria: Pounded Yam And Ogbono Soup
Body_Text:
Lagos — Organisers of the Berlin 2009 12th IAAF World Championships meant well when they offered to quarter the entire athletes and officials of the competing countries at the imposing Berlin-Berlin Hotel in the German capital city.
For Most Nigerian athletes and officials, Berlin-Berlin was a dream come true four-star abode. But after two days of eating strange meals, most officials now make a dash to a Nigerian eatery, some ten kilometres away, for go at home-type delicacies. The place, called 50-50 Restaurant, boasts of meals like Pounded Yam and Egusi or Ogbono soups. At just ten Euros (N2, 300) per meal, nobody seems to be missing their kitchen here except, perhaps, officials that are yet to get their allowances.
Lost in the U2-Bahn
Those familiar with the workings of trains in most European cities would readily attest to the fact that the long, movement machineries remain the best means getting commuters to their destinations. Thanks to these means of transportation, commuters are saved the usual traffic bottlenecks in cities like London and Paris.
It is the same thing in Berlin. Since last Saturday when the Championships kicked off here at the Olympic Stadium (yeah, the same ground where Jesse Owen won four Olympic gold medals in 1936), most fans who throng the 74,500 seating capacity main-bowl of the venue have found the train network a most useful means of commuting. Of course, for the four accredited journalists from Nigeria covering the events (Onochie Anibeze, Vanguard, Dare Esan Complete Sports, Ben Efe, Sportsday and yours sincerely), each ride brings a fresh experience.
For Efe and yours sincerely, going from the Olympia Stadion to Landsberger Allee where our Park Plaza Hotel is located takes just 20 minutes after changing train at the Westkruz Station. But on Wednesday (two days ago), we had to endure a different experience, all thanks to one of our traveling mates, the well-traveled Esan.
While going to see Mr Anibeze who was slightly indisposed and could not turn up at the stadium, our 'guide', 'Esan' who is used to taking the U2 Bahn underground train, led us into a near run-round the city. In the beginning, we had no reason to doubt the widely traveled journalist's professed knowledge of the city. He was here during the last FIFA World Cup Finals of Germany in 2006 after all, so we had no reason to believe it was a well-worn route for him.
But something just went wrong and we got lost and it would take a return to the Zoologister Garten Station for us to be able to re-navigate to our destination; the Abba Berlin Hotel residence of Mr. Anibeze. How much did we pay for the journey? Nothing! Just like during the World Cup Finals, the accreditation card allows anyone willing to stay permanent in the trains to do so at no fee at all. By the way, can anyone imagine the chaos that our roads will become if Abuja offered to host any of these global events? Don't ask me about Lagos!
Now I know Why Fans Seek Autographs
I have never fancied the idea of collecting autographs from sports personalities, or understand why people want to collect it. Even when I attended global events and sports fans were felling over themselves for the autographs of famous stars, I usually just laugh and wave off such past times.
But during the week here at the World Championships, a visit to the athletes' camp changed went on to illuminate my mind as to their reasons. While going to the first floor to see Olusoji Fasuba for a chat, behold side-by-side with me on the staircase, was the only man on earth to have run the 100m in under 9.6secs, Jamaican Usain Bolt.
I was struck by the simplicity of the 22-year-old world record holder. Wearing the Jamaican leaf green tee shirt and black shorts, Bolt cuts the image of just any other athlete at the camp, not the image that winning the 100m usually confers on an athlete in a championships of this nature. There was no air being put on by him, and the usual arrogant mien that some past champions exhibited was absent. He returned greetings and politely excused himself from an exclusive chat.
Later same day, Maurice Green, the American whose ten-year old World Championships record of 9.80, set at the Seville edition, was rewritten by Bolt, was guest of Adidas at their stand at the main entrance into the Olympic Stadium.
I could recall vividly the image of Green when he won the 2000 Sydney Olympics 100m event, and was immediately reminded of that famous mannerism of his, twisting his head from side to side like someone high on something I must not mention. When reminded of it, he laughed it off, attributing it to youthful exuberance. Again, like it happened inside the lift with Bolt, I was tempted to ask for an autograph but immediately remembered my earlier distaste for it. It was then the reason fans seek autograph started being clear to me.

Original_Search_Title: Okada Ban - Apugo Calls For Proper Engagement of Youths
Headline: Nigeria: Okada Ban - Apugo Calls For Proper Engagement of Youths
Body_Text:
Umuahia — Worried by the rampant cases of kidnapping and armed robbery in Abia State, a member of the Board of Trustees of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), Prince Benjamin Apugo, has called on the state government to provide alternative means of livelihood for the youths who were banned from commercial motorcycle operations in the state, so as to reduce its menace.
Apugo made the call when women of Ibeku kingdom paid him a homage and presented their case to him at his country home in Umuahia North Local Government Area as part of the traditional rites for this year's new yam festival scheduled for August 29, this year.
The solidarity visit was also in recognition of his philanthropic gestures to Ibeku people and Nigerians in general.
Apugo, observed that the suddenly ban on the operation of commercial motorcycles otherwise known as Okada had compounded the transport situation in the state, as the ban was implemented without an alternative arrangement for those affected who depend on commercial motorcycle operation for their livelihood.
He pointed out that in states that had implemented such policies before Abia, adequate arrangements and alternatives were put in place.
He called on the state government to vigorously pursue the development of the state as it is their statutory responsibility to the people.

Original_Search_Title: Abuja the Unforgettable
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja the Unforgettable
Body_Text:
Lagos — I have been accused of neglecting the nation's capital in my column while promoting other cities across great Nigeria. "Located in the geographic center of Nigeria, Abuja is a modern city by any standard. The roads are well laid out, and the population is not so dense. Beautiful buildings adorn the city center.
Abuja has one airport - the Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport. It's got a domestic and an international wing. The international wing handles a few airlines like BA, Virgin, Lufthansa and a couple of domestic airlines. The domestic wing is the real busy one. A whole bunch of domestic flights operate daily, bringing people in and out of the various states of Nigeria. Most flights shuttle hourly between Abuja and Lagos or Port Harcourt. Domestic Air fares to Abuja are about N14, 000 (approx: $100) although fare-crashing airlines have specials as low as N5,000 for patrons who pay online ahead of flights. Defaulters pay a penalty if there scheduled dates are not kept to .
There are lots of 'luxurious bus' (passenger-liner bus) stations, rides from Abuja to major towns all over the country. These rides are very comfortable, and cost much less than air travel. They however take much longer, with some of the longest trips lasting up to 12 hours.
The luxurious buses usually have air conditioning, movies and meals. The average bus trip costs less than N5,000.
Most people in Abuja have their own cars. The good road network makes it a pleasure to drive around. There are hardly ever traffic jams, unless you leave the city center, where the roads narrow down. Within the city center, there are typically 4 - 6 lane roads. There are street and traffic lights at most junctions, though, due to power failure, the junctions are often manned by 'yellow fever' traffic police.
The cheapest way to get around is by mini bus. These buses shuttle between most of the bus stops in Abuja and its satellite towns. The bigger buses also ply similar routes, and are becoming more popular.
Abuja reportedly has the best collection of taxis in Nigeria. The introduction of air conditioned Peugeot 307 cars, dubbed Abuja Green cabs and lots of privately owned cabs, make getting around Abuja easy and pleasant. Average cab rides range from N200 - N4000, depending on the type of cab, the duration of hire and the route.
Motorbikes no longer ply the city center, but are allowed to carry passengers in the satellite towns around Abuja. The replacement for motorbikes are the three wheelers, popularly known as 'Keke NAPEP'. NAPEP is the Government poverty alleviation agency that funded the purchase of these three wheelers for small scale entrepreneurs. These usually go short distances, usually to and from markets within the city.
There are tree -shaded sidewalks all over Abuja, and walking is a good way of getting around short distances in good weather.
The major religions in Abuja are Christianity and Islam, with Churches and Mosques in every district of Abuja. There are other religions, just as in other major cities and towns. On Fridays most offices close early or go on break to enable Muslims go to the Mosque. Christians usually go to Church on Sundays.
Abuja seeks to be the Leisure and Entertainment capital of the nation too. Take your business associates on an adventure in the nature treats of Abuja! Zuma Rock, Zuma Rock Village, and Aso Rock - the main natural attractions of the city boast a magnificent panorama of the dramatic landscape. Katampe Hill provides a striking view of the surrounding cityscape. Lower Hassan Dam offers lovely, serenee picnic sites.
Cultural treats would include visits to the Ushafa Pottery Centre, Shehu Musa Yar' Adua Centre, National Mosque, National Ecumenical Centre, Millennium Park, Abuja Council for Arts and Culture, and the Three Arms Zone.
There are lots of parks and gardens in the FCT. There's a growing culture of relaxing in parks. Places like the Millennium Park, Children's Park and Zoo, and the Wonderland Amusement Park near the Abuja Stadium, get swarmed with leisure seekers especially on weekends.
Places to hang out and enjoy good entertainment are springing up all over Abuja these days. There are clubs and pubs, suya, chicken, pizza, point-and-kill-pepper soup, ice cream and shawarma spots and joints dotting the town. Some of these are stand-alone, while others are attached to supermarkets. Goat meat, fresh fish pepper soup and 'Point and Kill' joints are popular favourite with Abuja residents and visitors.
There is a wide variety of restaurants and eateries all over Abuja. These range from basic Nigerian bukas and restaurants to European, Asian and Middle Eastern restaurants. Arirang, a Korean Restaurant, opened a few months back.
Local food is generally affordable, with meals going for as low as N100 in the 'mama put' or 'bukka' to meals that cost N2500 or more in the upscale restaurants.
Fast food outlets are also common, offering chicken and chips, rice based meals and local food like yam pottage, also available as 'take away'. Service is relatively good, and in clean surroundings. Bottled drinking water is in abundance alongside various bottled and canned drinks.
Shopping in the FCT is restricted mainly to district shopping centers, markets and supermarkets. Shopping centers, or 'Plazas' as they are usually referred to, consist of neatly arranged lock up stores that sell anything from mobile phones to cooking pots!
Street hawking is not encouraged, although newspaper vendors still take adorn major junctions and traffic lights. There are major markets in every district of the FCT, in line the master plan. Basic home shopping can be done in these markets, as they stock pretty much 'everything'. Home delivery of goods and meals has become increasingly available too.
The supermarkets stock a more select choice of goods, mostly imported and local goods of high quality. Online shopping is largely non-existent, although credit and debit cards are accepted in select outlets. There is, however, a growing presence of shopping directories such as The Abuja Yellow pages, Greensheets and the Street Hawker which feature price comparisons, and contact details for shopping destinations.
Good health care is readily available. There are Government hospitals in every district, and these are pretty well kept and affordable. However, they tend to be crowded. For more specialized attention, there are the options of private hospitals. These hospitals and health centers usually cost a lot more than the Government owned ones, often offer much better facilities, and are less crowded.

Original_Search_Title: Uyo Residents Urge ASUU to Call Off Strike
Headline: Nigeria: Uyo Residents Urge ASUU to Call Off Strike
Body_Text:
Parents and guardians in Uyo have called on the striking members of ASUU to call of their strike and return to the negotiation table over their grievances.
Mr Sammuel Akpan, a Secondary School Teacher in the Akwa Ibom in his reaction, said the Federal Government was "not sincere in asking ASUU to negotiate with individual universities".
In an interview with the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) in Uyo on Thursday, Akpan said the federal universities were owned by the Federal Government
and so the government could negotiate and sign agreement with ASUU.
Akpan, however, appealed to ASUU members to return to their individual universities for negotiation if government insisted on that in the interest of their students.
Sunday Okon, a yam trader at Akpan Andem Market in Uyo, said though he did not understand what both parties were talking about, his ward a university student had stayed at home for about two months now.
He said it was waste of time and money for him having paid school fees for the whole session and there was no teaching for his child.
"I now have to provide additional money for the child's upkeep as he stays at home," he said.
Mrs Mariam Gibbs, a House Wife, said the "fruitless negotiations" between the Federal Government and ASUU had done a lot of harm to the nation's educational system.
She appealed to ASUU to call off the strike, accept the much it achieved during the negotiations and return to the class room, adding "he who fight and run away lives to fight another day".
A 400 level student of the University of Uyo, Miss Helen Bassey, said she was so upset because with only one semester to leave school, she could not graduate.
"My final year project has been abandoned. I can no longer read and 'am tired of staying at home, please return to the classroom," she appealed to the striking lecturers.

Original_Search_Title: Motorists Protest Police Extortion
Headline: Nigeria: Motorists Protest Police Extortion
Body_Text:
Abakaliki — Transport operators numbering over 200, yesterday blocked the less than 60kilometre Abakaliki-Afikpo expressway for over three hours, preventing commuters, including top government functionaries from passing through.
The motorists were protesting the alleged mounting of over 20 road blocks in the expressway by the police, for the sole aim of extorting money.
Dignitaries trapped during the protest were, member representing Ikwo/Ezza South Federal Constituency in the House of Representatives, Honourable Ugo Chima, Chairman, Association of Local Governments of Nigeria (ALGON), Barrister Celestine Nweme Igberi, Chairman, Ezza South Local Government area, Barrister Marcellus Nwokporo and a host of others on the way to participate in the new yam festival of the Ezza people in Onueke area.
It took the intervention of the Commissioner for Boarder Security, Honourable Agu Okpani and the state Chairman of National Union of Road Transport Workers (NURTW), Comrade Douglas Ogbonna, to open up the road after several appeals to the transporters, which comprised mini-bus and taxi drivers plying the route.
The transporters had complained that a road with a distance of less than 60 kilometers had 20 police checkpoints, alleging that police at each of the checkpoints extort at least N40 from every commercial motorist, a situation that made them operate at a loss.
The drivers said even when all their papers are complete, the police would delay them and their passengers for hours, just to compel them to give them money.
They listed the checkpoints to include; Timber shed, Edufu Ibina, First Gate Army Barrack, Ikwo Junction, Akpara, Onuzornwedu and Country man checkpoints.Others are; Amuzu School, Amudo, Oriegbe Junction, Abomege, Akpoha Bridge, St Peter's Church, Ezza East, Imoha, Mbamoko and Amasiri/Afikpo Junction checkpoints.
The drivers, who refused to give their names to newsmen, lamented that the road which originally had only four recognised checkpoints now have 20, all mounted by aggressive-looking policemen who harass them on regular basis, insisting that the extortion has forced them to increase transport fare.Addressing the drivers, Okpani pleaded with them to disband and formerly present their grievances to the appropriate authorities. He said it was wrong to have 20 police checkpoints on one route in this age of modern communication facilities, and promised that the state government would liaise with police authorities to address the matter.Ogbonna advised the drivers not to take laws into their hands but to complain to the appropriate quarters, saying they should conduct themselves as people who are responsible.When contacted, the State Police Public Relations Officer (PPRO) ASP Chris Anyanwu, told our reporter that the protest was stage-managed and wondered whether the motorists don't want police to do their job.Anyanwu dismissed the allegation of extortion, saying the motorists should report any particular officer found extorting money.
He, however, said police checkpoints along the route were not up to 20, but noted that even if there is only one checkpoint, the idea of extorting money was not official and should be reported to police authorities.

Original_Search_Title: Benyiwa Doe is Courageous
Headline: Ghana: Benyiwa Doe is Courageous
Body_Text:
The Association of Gomoa West Queenmothers, on Friday, described the Central Regional Minister, Mrs. Ama Benyiwa Doe, as a courageous woman, who braved all odds in the political circles to rise up to the position of Central Regional Minister.
The President of the Queens Association and Queenmother of Gomoa Ngyiresi, Nana Araba Ahema, who made this known during a courtesy call on the Minister in Cape Coast, said Benyiwa Doe's combative spirit from former President Jerry John Rawlings' regime, as Deputy Minister for Social Welfare and Employment, was something to cheer about, and that she did not lose hope when she lost her parliamentary seat in 2004.
According to her, when things were difficult, Benyiwa Doe did not give up, and continued the fight until the National Democratic Congress (NDC) party regained power in 2008.
She expressed the queens' gratefulness to President John Evans Atta Mills for recognising the hard work of the Minister, and appointing her to oversee the Central Region.
The queenmothers stated that they came to the office of the Minister to congratulate her as a former member of their association, and wished her a successful tenure of office.
The Vice President of the association, Nana Esi Twuwah, Queenmother of Gomoa Kyiren, described the Regional Minister as the "Yaa Asantewa" of the Central Region.
On her part, the Minister expressed gratitude to the traditional leaders for their support when she was a Member of Parliament (MP) for the Gomoa West Constituency.
She said she would visit them during her tour of the districts, to ascertain the problems facing them, and how best they could be solved.
Mr. Kwame Oppong, Acting Chief Director of Central Regional Coordinating Council, told the queens that Benyiwa Doe would do everything possible to maintain the honour done her by President Mills.
In a related development, the chiefs and people of Gomoa Abora, in the Gomoa West District of the Central Region, on Friday expressed gratitude to President John Evans Atta Mills for appointing Mrs. Ama Benyiwa Doe, a former queen of the town, as Central Regional Minister.
A nine-member delegation, consisting of three women and six men, sent by the Odikro of Gomoa Abora, Nana Kwesi Hamma V, led by Kwame Amoo, said the appointment of their native had elevated the image of the town.
"The appointment of Mrs. Benyiwa Doe, as Central Regional Minister, is a glory to the people of Gomoa Abora, and we thank the President for the honour done us," he added.
They presented a sheep, tubers of yam, eggs and cloths as gifts to their daughter for rising to an enviable position in the region.
They used the occasion to invite the Regional Minister to their annual festival, which comes off next month.
In response, Mrs. Benyiwa Doe thanked the people for the love they had shown, and assured them that she would not disappoint them and the President as Regional Minister.
She promised to visit them as part of her tour to the various parts of the region to discuss issues concerning the town.
Mr. Kwame Oppong welcomed the delegation to the minister's office.

Original_Search_Title: ...work And Cook At Ease
Headline: Nigeria: ...work And Cook At Ease
Body_Text:
Lagos — For many career women faced with the difficult challenge of managing office demands and running the home, relief is around the corner. A service outfit called esay shop and easy cook, managed by Saudat Salami, a young mother of two is set to alleviate those challenges but at a cost.
Over the years, managing the home and career has been a problem that most Lagos career women have had to contend with. No thanks to Lagos traffic jam that keeps many on the road for hours. While many have left their homes to domestic staffs to be managed, others have resulted to fast food. But with the new outfit in town, you can work at ease and still manage your home well. Welcome to the world of Saudat Salami, CEO, Easy Shop, Easy Cook, a computer expert turned home manager. Born 35 years ago in Lagos into the family of Mr. and Mrs. Giwa. Young Saudat grew under the tutelage of a lawyer father, who held sway as First Bank legal adviser for 25 years and an entrepreneur mother who deals in lace materials.
After her primary school at Mainland Preparatory School and Federal Government College, Jos in Plateau State, she was left with the dilemma of career prospect. "I left FGC, Jos in Plateau and thought about what I wanted to do. Though I passed my Jamb, the course I was offered didn't make sense to me. Again, I have always had a flair for information Technology. My father supported that idea and I was enrolled at a computer school. I enrolled for a computer programme at the University of Lagos. Then Microsoft Certified Examination was in vogue . I registered for it, did desktop publishing and many other courses. The computer work was so exciting to me that I found myself doing website development. After all the computer certificates, I got a job as a computer analyst in a company. That was how my career in IT began. Along the line, I was developing myself. I started work in 1994 till about 2001.
Having worked as an IT person for seven years, Salami had a passion which guarded her intellectual pursuit. But surprisingly, her passion differs. It was a passion she had developed over the years. "I was tired of what I was doing then. And I remember while I was growing up, my mother was and still a good cook. I used to follow her to the market. I liked shopping, going here and there. I realized that there are things I could do. I am somebody that likes doing things for people".
"After quitting my job as an IT person, I asked myself What kind of business can I do that would fit into my passion". My friends and I were writing all kinds of businesses we could do. She said that since I like helping people to buy stuffs, why can't I run a business like that?" I went on line because that is what I know how to do best. I started researching about shopping, buying things for people, logistics etc. I downloaded a business plan template and studied it. When my sister was about to get married, I helped her to do the runaround. I later started doing it for my friends. It was going on well and I felt I could turn it into a business. To further enhance my skills, I went to Fate Foundation to brush up myself and know what it takes to set up and run a business. I floated Easy Shop, Easy Cook Services in 2004. I realized that if you shop easily you can cook easily. That was how the business grew as 90 per cents of my friends are my customers. I didn't stop there, I went to banks, sent out questionnaire "You don't have time to go to the market, how do you shop presently? Would you mind if someone comes to do your shopping for you without cooking the food? I realised during my research that some husbands would not like it if you are cooking for them. But wouldn't mind if the food is brought to the kitchen and the wife prepares it. The problem with Lagos career women is time. Most women don't have the time to buy food stuff because of their busy schedule.
What we do in Easy Shop Easy Cook Services is going to the market and buys the food for them. We clean your meat; grind your pepper if you want. It is a customised service because we adhere strictly to what our clients want. You know people shop differently and taste differs as well. For instance, we are all eating egusi soup but cook it differently. So, we help you clean your egunsi the way you want it, grind your pepper, help you cut your meat and cut your vegetable. So that when you come back from work, you can quickly cook your egusi in your own special way. We take off the burden of running your office job and thinking about shopping for home. I am a Nigerian woman. If your home is not in order, no matter how successful you are it is zero. We make you comfortable when you are working. We buy everything you want from food stuff, toiletries, house items to the smallest thing you want in your home. If you are not ready to cook we can shop ahead for you against weekend. So you can have time for your husband and children and reduce the time you stay in the kitchen. Also, if you want to organise a small party over the weekend, and you don't have the time to buy birthday presents, just give specific and your budget. It is a five year old girl/boy. We would buy it for you and bring it to your doorsteps. The idea is to make you happy when you are working".
How has it been since it kicked off five years ago. It's been fantastic is her response. "When you get a positive feedback from your clients, it gives you a lot of satisfaction. There are big companies that do similar things and we have customers who had used them before. And when they use us they get excited".
How much does it cost to engage her services? "Of course we get paid for the services rendered". Our customers have a minimum order and we charge a 'service charge'. We also charge you for service delivery depending on your location.
Salami said her services are not restricted to Lagosians alone as her company gets patronage from Nigerians in Diaspora. Hear her. "We also have Nigerian customers who are based abroad with families here. When our customers send us money, we stock their families' house with food stuff or what they want".
Food is a sensitive aspect that requires trust in terms of hygiene. How has she been able to convince her customers to sleep with both eyes closed while handling their food? "Our packaging. Our packaging is fantastic. Though we buy it from the local market, we bring it back to the hygienic environment and wash them. We don't use colored nylon. We pack them in a transparent nylon bag as required by our clients and they are well labeled. So that when you bring it out from your freezer, it makes cooking easy for you. We deliver in basket, help you arrange it in your freezer the way you want it".
What have been the Challenges? "Shopping for women. You know women are very meticulous. The good news is that I try as much as possible to offer money back guarantee. We enjoy a cordial relationship with our suppliers. For instance, if you buy a tuber of yam, from the outside don't know it is bad. If such happens, we will collect it and take it back to the suppliers. And things we can't replace we compensate. Because at end of the day, I am selling a service and I have to make my customers happy".
The last five years is been wonderful; the business she says is challenging everyday. We are learning everyday about what our customers want. We are trying to build our business in such a away to make our customers happy".
Where does she see her company in the next five years ."We are going fully on-line to re-position the company. As a monthly income earner, you have a responsibility to yourself and home. So to remove that stress of working satisfactorily for your employee and still come home to be a dutiful wife and loving mother to your children, Easy Shop and Easy Cook Services is ready to serve you better".

Original_Search_Title: Pru District Appeals for Help
Headline: Ghana: Pru District Appeals for Help
Body_Text:
The chiefs and people of Pru District in the Brong-Ahafo Region have appealed to His Excellency Prof. John Evans Atta-Mills to use his good office, as the President, to provide them with a pontoon for use at Yeji on the Volta Lake.
According to them, the ferry, when provided, would replace the rickety one currently plying the lake, runs on one engine, since the other is damaged beyond repair.
In a statement read on their behalf by the District Chief Executive (DCE), Masawud Mohamed, during a meeting with a section of the media in the region at Yeji, the chiefs and people complained that the pontoon carts people, vehicles, truck loads of yam, cattle and other foodstuff from the northern part of Ghana to the South and vice-versa, since it is the shortest route.
They stated there was a high demand for the services of the ferry, but due to its obsolete condition, moves only once in a day, thereby leaving truck loads of goods and foodstuff at the other end.
"The current ferry takes close to two hours, from Yeji to Makango, instead of the normal 45 minutes," they emphasised.
According to the DCE, the request of the people of the district was in pursuance of the National Democratic Congress (NDC) manifesto page 90, which states that, "an NDC government will establish two more landing sites, on either side of the Volta Lake, to reduce traveling distance between settlements and existing landing sites. The necessary support would be given to companies removing tree stumps in the lake, to complete the exercise quickly to improve navigation and reduce boat accidents on the lake." " .Additional ferry would be installed for more frequent and reliable crossing on the Volta Lake at Yeji, to redistribute road transport services more competitively among the Western, Central and Eastern corridors."
In the statement, it was stated that the Pru District had no Government District Hospital that caters for the health needs of the people, with only the Mathias Hospital of Yeji, which currently serves the people, not only in Pru, but neighboring districts such as East Gonja and Atebubu/Amantin.
"In line with NDC manifesto on page 67 for providing a district hospital in each of the districts, we humbly remind the government to consider the Pru District with a hospital, to make healthcare services more accessible to our people," they reiterated.
Aside this, the chiefs and people also appealed to the government to consider siting a health training institute in the district, which would serve Ejura, Sene, Atebubu and Pru, to help reduce shortage of health personnel, especially nurses, some of whom refused postings to the area.
They added that the Yeji abandoned fish market had been proposed for the said Hhealth training institute.
The Pru District, they stated, has two Senior High Schools, with none of the two better off than the other, as they all lack infrastructure such as, classrooms, libraries, dormitories and teachers accommodation.
The Prang Senior High School was picked as a model school during the New Patriotic Party (NPP) government, but according the them, unfortunately, not even a single block was moulded during the eight year term of the NPP government, therefore the NDC government should come and upgrade at least one of the Senior High Schools to a modern status with modern facilities, to provide quality secondary education to the people.

Original_Search_Title: Imo Governor's Wife Donates to Motherless Babies
Headline: Nigeria: Imo Governor's Wife Donates to Motherless Babies
Body_Text:
Owerri — Items worth thousands of Naira have been donated to children at the Owerri Motherless Babies Home and the Okwelle Motherless Babies Home in Imo State by the wife of the governor, Mrs.Chioma Ohakim as part of the activities marking the 10th anniversary of democratic rule in the country.
Items donated included Rice, disinfectants, towels, tissue, trays, Vaseline jelly, baby food, detergent, yams, soap, and pampers.
Mrs Ohakim said she came to celebrate the day with the children, noting that there was the tendency for them to be ignored in the euphoria of the day. She charged the children to face their studies squarely and commended staff of the home for their dedication and commitment. The first lady spoke on the significance of the day to the children. According to her, it is important that the children are taught about everyday life experience because they will join the society at some point, hence the need to teach them everything, including current affairs and governance. She advocated for a system where the children after sometime can be returned to their families or given to foster parents who will in turn re-integrate them into society. Receiving Mrs.Ohakim, Reverend Sister Benedicta Ezeobi commended her for the gesture and promised to always commit her family to God in prayers.

Original_Search_Title: Community Leaders Write Obi On Plans to Dethrone Monarch
Headline: Nigeria: Community Leaders Write Obi On Plans to Dethrone Monarch
Body_Text:
Awka — PEACE in Ihembosi community in Ekwusigo Local Government Area of Anambra State is being threatened, as some leaders of thought in the area have written Governor Peter Obi, notifying him of their decision to dethrone their traditional ruler, Igwe Jerry Okoli over allegation of desecrating the customs and traditions of the community.
They have also served the traditional ruler a letter of impeachment dated June 29, 2009.
In two separate letters originating from the Ihembosi Progress Assembly (IPA), by Chief Dr. I.G. Nnubia, Nze K.C. Ewuzie Prof A.U.J. Kanem, Ichie F.N.D. Nwosu and Ajie Mathias Uzokwe, President General, Secretary General, Traditional Prime Minister, Secretary of Traditional Council and Deputy Traditional Prime Minster respectively, they accused the traditional ruler of desecrating the customs and traditions of the community.
"It is with hearts wounded and bleeding that Ihembosi Progress Assembly and the entire people of Ihembosi in Ekwusigo Local Government Area of Anambra State notify you of the regrettable but unavoidable dethronement of Jerry Okoli, as the traditional ruler of Ihembosi with immedaite effect," they said.
They alleged that in the past ten years of the traditional ruler's reign, the community has not known peace, adding that what they had experienced discord and lack of agreement, which they claimed was caused by him.
"The climax of his woes is his conspiracy with one Nsioma Kindred and the Ekwusigo Police Command to destroy the ancestral home of Ihembosi called Olulo. The traditional and ancient trees that used to be a tourist attraction in Ihembosi were cut down and carried away, while 14 principal deities were completely destroyed, including Ogunzu Ulasi", their letter stated.
But the embattled traditional ruler dismissed the petitioners, saying that they refused to account for the money given to the community by the state government for developmental purposes.
He said on June 13, 2009 during the IPA general meeting, he asked the President General and his executive, whose tenure had allegedly expired, to give account of N800,000 given to the community in 2007 and N1.5 million in 2009 by Anambra state government and to defend the expenditure of the 2008 New Yam Festival of the community, which they could not, adding that instead, they resorted to threatening him with dethronement and impeachment.

Original_Search_Title: Separating the Siamese Twins
Headline: Nigeria: Separating the Siamese Twins
Body_Text:
Lagos — Depending on where you come from, there are a number of beliefs built around the circumstances of the birth of twins. In my part of the world, the first to arrive is called Odion, which signifies that he is the elder one, while the last is called Ovbokhan, meaning the younger one. There are places, though, where the last to arrive is regarded as Odion while the first is called Ovbokhan.
The belief here is that seniority is ordered from heaven and that the first to arrive, being the younger, is normally dispatched by the elder brother on a sort the of recognizance survey to go and find out how the world is out there. At the highest levels of governance in this land - the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, the Governor of a State and the Chairman of a Local Government, there is a marriage of convenience in which the people are forced by the Constitution into a Siamese twin's birth type of situation. As an instance, section 142(1) of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999 stipulates, ". . . a candidate for an election to the office of President shall not be deemed to be validly nominated unless he nominates another candidate as his associate from the same political party for his running for the office of President, who is to occupy the office of Vice-President and that candidate shall be deemed to have been duly elected to the office of Vice-President if the candidate for an election to the office of President who nominated him as such associate is duly elected as President"
This provision applies mutatis mutandis to States and Local Governments.
Essentially, there is a fantastic relationship between the duo from the very beginning. In the first place, no one would invite an enemy to come and chop with him. At the heat of the campaigns, they are virtually together twenty-four seven. As it were, their relationship is one of swimming or drowning together. As the campaigns progress, they visit churches and mosques together; they visit schools and all campaign arena together; and they fast and eat together. Like the Siamese twins that they really are, their hearts beat together, at least politically. The relationship remains sweet for as long as the campaigns last and they need each other to fight and win the war. But in some cases, as soon as the victory celebrations are over and the job of governance sets in, this sweet wine could gradually begin to turn vinegar. This lends credence to the popular belief that it is not the same eye with which you eat the pounded yam that the meat is shared. Enter Governor Isa Yuguda of Bauchi State and his Deputy, Alhaji Garba Gadi.
During courtship, they were both of the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) but the point of departure soon arrived when Governor Isa Yuguda moved over to the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), soon after the consummation of the wedlock between him and President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua's daughter.
Ordinarily, one would expect the Principal to cross over with his Deputy but Alhaji Gadi would none of it. We hear they have appealed to him; they have cajoled him; and made all entreaties, all to no avail. The man is adamant on remaining in the ANPP. He must now be coerced! We hear that for some time now, the Governor has been subjecting this Deputy to harassment and intimidation of all sorts and the man is not moved. As a follow-up, we hear the Governor has moved to the House of Assembly of the State to request the legislators to set the impeachment procedure in motion to get Gadi out of the way.
Could anyone call Yuguda to order and at least remind him of the inalienable right of Gadi to free association as amply expressed in section 40 of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999?
And so soon, we seem to have lost our sense of history. This is yet another case of a Principal and his Deputy belonging to different political parties. Do we need to remind ourselves of the historical parallel between this case and that of President Obasanjo and Vice-President Atiku Abubakar? At the peak of it all, Vice-President Atiku Abubakar went his separate way when he defected from the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) to the Action Congress (AC) and, indeed, went ahead to pick the presidential ticket of the latter political party. It was at that point that President Obasanjo declared the Vice-President's office vacant. The Vice-President then rushed to court to seek the necessary interpretation and the protection of the law.
On Tuesday, February 20 2007, the Court of Appeal sitting in Abuja and presided over by the President of the Court of Appeal, Hon. Justice Umaru Abdulahi ruled that Atiku Abubakar remains the Vice-President of Nigeria. The Court held that the loyalty of Atiku Abubakar was to the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, not to the President or to the PDP because Atiku Abubakar was not their employee. The court maintained that before election, the presidential candidate who nominated the running mate was perfectly entitled to drop that running mate but "After the election, and they were jointly declared elected upon one becoming President and the other Vice-President, the former loses his discretion to remove the latter at will". That explains why the entire sections 143 and 144 of the 1999 Constitution have been devoted to setting out the conditions for separately removing the President and the Vice-President from office, once elected.
In the words of the respected Appeal Court Justices, "The bond of companionship which compelled them, particularly the vice-presidential candidate, to remain together during the election loosens and would swim to certain extent, separately. On his election, he ceased to be vice-presidential candidate of the sponsoring party and becomes the Vice-President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria by the grace of the electorate and no longer of the President who nominates him as the running mate and the party which sponsored both of them".
The PDP is certainly treading on familiar grounds but we think somebody should point them to order. Back home in Bauchi, they are doing everything to cow Alhaji Gadi into submitting to their scheme. If Gadi does not accept their offer to cross over to the PDP, he must be pushed out of office. Meanwhile, in Abuja , President Musa Yar'Adua is standing on the rooftops, shouting about his electoral reforms, including the fact that cross carpeting by elected political leaders must be totally banned. As a matter of fact, he has forwarded a bill to the National Assembly to this effect. In this double-speak, who really is deceiving whom? Is it that we are not serious about the much touted electoral reform or that the President is working at cross purposes with his party? In either case, PDP is home and dry but unfortunately, their antics would normally rob off on the rest of society.
A Governor might think that in jumping from one political party to another at will, he is not breaking any law. Fine! But must such a Governor drag his deputy along in his flirtation and coquetry? We all have our principles and such principles should be respected. In our selective stance, when it favours the ruling party, they will be quick to point out that a Governor who defects from the party that sponsored his election cannot lose his seat.
Elsewhere, judges and other experts in constitutional engineering will quickly tell you that it is practically impossible to put everything down in a constitution. They are also quick to speak of the minds of the framers of the constitution at the time of its making. If we were to look hard enough, we would find that the much talked about freedom of Governors to jump from one political party to another may have been abhorred, after all. A reading of the minds of the framers of our 1999 Constitution soon drives us to the region of section 192(4), which stipulates, "No person shall be appointed as a Commissioner of a State unless he is qualified for election as a member of the House of Assembly of that State". This also means that if a Commissioner commits an act for which he would have lost his seat in the House were he a legislator, such a Commissioner stands automatically disqualified from the commissionership job. Essentially, the provisions of section 109(1) (g) of our 1999 Constitution, which prohibit a legislator from leaving the party that sponsored his election also apply to a commissioner.
We are still reading the minds of the framers of the constitution. To the extent that the commissioner is appointed by the governor, would the framers of the constitution who felt that the offence of cross carpeting is so serious that it should remove a commissioner from his job be at the same time encouraging the governor who appoints the commissioner to be jumping from one party to another during his tenure? Evidently, the minds of the framers of the constitution at the time of providing the anti-cross carpet clause was to abhor cross carpeting by elected leaders, including the chief executives and their deputies.
It is our submission, therefore, that by moving from the ANPP to the PDP, Governor Isa Yuguda has offended our constitution and he must go! Let him stop pulling his deputy into his iniquities. This is the area that the Bauchi State House of Assembly should be looking at instead of leaving the substance and pursuing the shadow. By implication, too, it is already becoming clear that President Yar'Adua's bill on this subject is superfluous. He must begin to look somewhere else for the real reforms.

Original_Search_Title: KMA Boss Worships With Muslims
Headline: Ghana: KMA Boss Worships With Muslims
Body_Text:
IN A way of showing that he will be a father for all, Mr. Samuel Sarpong, the Metropolitan Chief Executive (MCE) of Kumasi last Friday joined the Muslim brothers to worship at the Kumasi Central mosque.
He was accompanied by some Assembly members and other executives of the Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly (KMA), as well as the Presiding Member (PM) of the Assembly, Nana Kofi Senyah.
Alhaji Amadu Ibrahim, the chief of Wangara, reminded the MCE that the KMA seat is one of the hottest seats in the country, and for that matter he should seek for Allah's guidance whenever he is making any decision.
He also asked the KMA boss to pay critical attention to the youth in the metropolis, especially Muslims, since they are the future leaders.
He pointed out that most Muslim youth were unemployed and for that matter indulged themselves in unscrupulous practices. He then called on the Mayor to help curb that predicament which would prepare a better future for the children.
Mr. Sarpong asked the gathering to channel their problems through their Assembly members and also promised them that he would bring governance "to the door step of the people".
He also said he would make it a priority for children in the metropolis to go to school and also make sure the youth get employment, and further disclosed that the Assembly was preparing a budget which will meet the need of everyone in the metropolis.
Sheikh Abdul Mumuni Alrun, deputy Ashanti Regional Chief Imam thanked the MCE for worshipping with them and prayed for Allah's guidance and wisdom for him.
The KMA boss, after praying with the Muslims, then visited the Kumasi Central Market, where the traders, mostly women, had abandoned their products and could not control their excitement in seeing the KMA boss at the market to interact with them.
Mr. Sarpong visited the local abattoir at the market, also known as "Nankwansere", where the chief butcher, Sani Mohammed extolled him for being the first mayor to visit them in the market.
He also pledged his support to the Assembly and asked the KMA to come to their aid when the need arises. Madam Yaa Fowaa, the queen mother of yam Sellers in the market was also excited of the MCE's move and also promised her support to the mayor to make his reign a success.
Mr. Sarpong unveiled that his plan of visiting the Central market was to introduce himself to the traders and also assure them that he would work hand in hand with them.
He further revealed that a contract has been signed with a company to construct an ultra modern market which will have a car park, a Day-care centre, hospital and access road for fire fighters in case of any outbreak of fire. He said most people do not believe that the market can be rebuilt, since past governments had paid lip service to their promise to construct a new market.
The MCE disclosed that work on the construction of the ultra modern market would begin in August, and reiterated that those who were affected by the May 28 fire outbreak would be given the first priority after its completion.
Mr. Sarpong also asked the affected victims of the fire outbreak to comply with the KMA to relocate them to other temporary markets, until the new market is built.
He again mentioned that KMA will not stop collecting revenue from the traders while the reconstruction of the market goes on.

Original_Search_Title: A Human Side of the Island's Past
Headline: Senegal: A Human Side of the Island's Past
Body_Text:
Nairobi — DESPITE ITS INFAMY courtesy of its slave trading past, Goree Island is also home to churches and buildings that have put it on the world map and has been visited by thousands of tourists and leaders from around the world, from former US president Bill Clinton and former South African president Nelson Mandela.
The most famous of the churches on Goree Island is the St Borromee Catholic Church built in 1829 using slave labour, and named after an Italian archbishop from Barromee Island in Italy.
The church is still used for prayers to date and in 1992, while on a visit to the island, Pope John Paul II visited the church and apologised to the African people for the role the Catholic Church played in the slave trade.
The church was built for rich white slave dealers and not even their rich African collaborators or African chiefs could worship there. They had to follow the mass from the yard.
The second most important building on the island is the first university in West Africa, Ecole Normal, build by William Ponty, a French governor in West Africa from 1913-1937.
It was the training ground for the early elite of West Africa who later became political leaders and even presidents in their respective countries at the time of independence. Many of the school's graduates would one day lead the struggle for independence from France.
THESE WERE FÉLIX HOUphouÃ«t-Boigny and Bernard Binlin Dadié of Côte d'Ivoire, Modibo Keïta of Mali, Hamani Diori and Boubou Hama of Niger, Hubert Maga of Benin (former Dahomey), Mamadou Dia of Senegal and Maurice Yaméogo and Daniel Ouezzin Coulibaly of Burkina Faso (former Upper Volta).
Other students were internationally renown jurists Kéba Mbaye and Ousmane Goundiam and Guinean politician Diallo Telli, who was a founder of the now defunct Organisation of African Unity.
The school had a two-year common core curriculum for students intending to become teachers or administrative clerks.
Those who intended to study medicine, pharmacy or midwifery had one more year of introductory science at William Ponty School before transferring to the National School of Medicine and Pharmacy.
