Goree Island is also home to the first medical school in West Africa, built in 1806 adjacent to the church, and now a hospital run by Catholic nuns. Most of the early doctors in West Africa were trained here.
The church where famous Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama prayed is still standing, currently serving as the island's only police station.
In an attempt to give Goree Island a modern face, the government of Senegal built the Mariama Ba High School, where 25 most intelligent girls in the country receive pre-university training every year.

Original_Search_Title: So That Refugees Can Smile
Headline: Nigeria: So That Refugees Can Smile
Body_Text:
Lagos — The recent attack on the militants by the Joint Task Force (JTF) has thrown many families into refugee camps. But there's a voice in the wilderness calling for caution and to assuage the sufferings of the internally displaced people in that region.
Recently, succour came the way of thousands of persons who were displaced from their places of abode and made refugees in their own community as a result of the three weeks of Joint Task Force (JTF) attack on the militants in the riverine areas of Delta State. The refugees who are mainly women and children and some aged were happy this sunny day in June.
Pastor Ayo Oritsejafor, the General Overseer of the Word of Life Bible Church, Warri, Delta State, and National President of Pentecostal Fellowship of Nigeria (PFN), is a man with an open heart and he showed this when he visited the refugees scattered in different rehabilitation centres. He did not visit them with empty hands, as he went with well packaged food items that is worth over N50 million to alleviate their problems.
Worried by the continued suffering of the people, Ayo went round the three camps within the Warri metropolis, preaching and counseling the disturbed people that were mostly women and children, and whose academic careers had been terminated without any glimmer of hope of when they will be resettled back to their homes.
The pastoral voyage began from his church situated at the Ajamimogha area of the embattled oil city where he told his members that his mind has been troubled concerning the situation of the people, mostly the less privileged. "I get all sorts of reports since the whole thing started about three weeks ago, telling me of the hardship and the difficulties of people running into the marshy bushes for shelter and all that.
"I have been worried over the condition of these people who have been turned refugees in their own land. That is why it took a while for us to put something together, especially for the suffering masses that have no hope," Ayo said. Apart from responding to the needs of the internally displaced, Papa Ayo, who knows how to balance equation also extended high level of sympathy to the soldiers who lost their lives in the on-going battle.
He said, "the soldiers that have lost their lives in this struggle are human beings and above all are Nigerians too, who should have been alive to make their noble contributions to the development of their fatherland." He prayed that God should comfort the families of those that have lost their lives and put an end to the suffering of the people that have been displaced.
While appealing to the Federal Government to find a way to end the conflict, he said "they should find a way to resort to dialogue where we can sit down and work something out. We are prepared to either initiate or participate in any negotiation leading to a peaceful resolution of the crisis because at the end of the day, it is dialogue that will resolve the dispute."
On the sunny day in June, Papa Ayo went to waterside at the creeks, where he outlined reasons why his church had undertaken the task to cushion the pains of the displaced persons. Quoting Jesus statement in the Holy Bible, he said, "I was hungry and you fed me." He continued, "So we are following the injunction of Jesus Christ who preached to the people and when He finished preaching, He made sure the people were fed," he said.
At Ogbe Ijaw, he saw those who were in the bush with children strapped behind their backs and having no place to lay their heads. According to him, the least they could do was to donate the goods worth N5 million to them. In another camp Papa Ayo and his team visited, he encouraged the displaced persons that were mainly of infirm old women and hungry-looking children. He told the refugees that were milled around that what they have received from them was a temporary thing and that God in His infinite mercy will help to bring this whole brouhaha to an end. He told that they would have another opportunity to go back to their homes and reunite with their loved ones.
One of the courageous displaced persons who spoke on behalf of others through an interpreter told Pastor Ayo to mediate on their behalf before President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua. She thanked the PFN president for his kind gesture and urged him to appeal to the Federal Government to see the 'militants' as arrant children fighting for recognition.
"Your kind gesture is a big relief to us here. We appreciate you and hope that more and more people will see the need to identify with our present predicament. Though we appreciate the gifts, there's no place like home and we very much want to go back to our homes," the woman said, while Papa Ayo promised that their message will be conveyed to the appropriate quarters.
Backing words with action, shortly after the courtesy visits to the riverside areas, the PFN helmsman sent a delegation of some pastors and officers of the church to nearly 20 villages in the Gbaramatu Kingdom to deliver about 27 bags of beans, 40 bags of salt, 50 cartons of milk, 1,000 tubers of yam, 100 bags of garri, 40 of 25 litres jerry-can of kerosene, 20 of 25 litres tin of palm oil and 20 of 25-litres tin of groundnut oil.
Other items donated by the cleric include 10 bags of onions, 50 bags of salt, 50 bags of 'Kpokpo' garri, 50 bags of palm nuts, 1 sack of pepper, 50 bags of starch, 50 bags of pure water sachets. The delegation lead by Pastor Francis Okiowei also presented special gift items to a young lady, Mrs. Timighae Dude who was said to have been delivered of a baby boy in the marshy forest while fleeing from the onslaught of the menacing Federal troops.
The young lady, who had her baby cuddled in her own wrapper in the harsh weather, was full of joy and appreciation to the church and its leadership for the recognition accorded her and her first baby. She also appealed to the Federal Government to halt the current hostilities for the benefit of the poor people of the area who had no say in the matter neither do they have a place to run to.
"You know what I have always said that in the event of any military intervention in the Niger Delta crisis, it is only the innocent persons that will suffer the effect. We are therefore appealing to government to keep the door of amnesty open, but above all they should try to stop the military operation because of the innocent persons who are being victims," Papa Ayo pleaded.
Pastor Ayo Oritsejafor has been described as a man with an open heart. This character of his has earned him a lot of esteem both within and outside the Christian fold. In fact, it has also earned him national honour too.
His name is now synonymous to gift, love, kindness and all the qualities of the fruits of the spirit according to Christian faithful. On many occasions, he has proven that the poor that are constantly around the rich ought to be taken care of without them asking for arm.
Apart from preaching the word of God in and out of season, if it were possible for him, he would feed five thousand people with five loaves of bread and two fishes. However, this man of God has been doing the possibilities. At Warri in particular, he has contributed in no small measure to alleviate the suffering of the people by empowering the poor to at least eat two square meals, if not three.
"Some of the people coming to church are not coming to hear the word of God, but are in our midst for their needs to be satisfied. Therefore when we carry the Bible with the right hand, we should also satisfy their needs on the other hand," Ayo ones said.
More so, during calamitous conditions which were sometimes faced by displaced Christians in Maiduguri, Bauchi, Asaba, amongst other states in the country, he has been faithful enough to ensure that they do not lack 'bread'. But most astonishing of one of his visits was at Asaba, Delta State, when he met with both the Christians and Moslems that were internally displaced as a result of religious intolerance. Ayo had to put smile on the faces of those people as they were provided with food stuffs. That was not all for a man that has put his hand on a plow and promised not to look back.

Original_Search_Title: A Market's Duel With the Highway
Headline: Nigeria: A Market's Duel With the Highway
Body_Text:
Abuja — It was a few minutes past noon, but the thickening crowd in the market had already spilled out from its main bowl and taken over a substantial portion of the service lane of the major highway about two kilometers away from the Zuma Rock.
Automobiles - motorcycles, most of them belonging to commercial operators, hooting cars and buses, Abuja's commercial 16-seater and 18-seater green buses, and their 'crazy irritating' drivers' assistants deafening the ears of everyone near enough with the screams of 'Wuse-Berger! Wuse-Berger!' connived with petty traders, most of them of fresh eatables, and other petty consumer items, who would find no open space within the market, as well as petty haulage labourers, to create the usual marketplace confusion.
Officials of the Federal Roads Safety Commission (FRSC) had just retired on recess after a few hours battle with motorists and the perilous commercial motorbike riders, to save the entire road from total conquest, ensuring proper parking, low or controllable speed and smooth and safe flow of vehicular traffic. Another batch of the FRSC officials would assume duty there later in the afternoon.
Any moment from 2 pm on every market day, the thick crowd of traders and buyers often so much threatens to conquer all the two lanes of the road, that a tug of war often ensues between them and officials of the FRSC, Civil Defense Corp and the Police, who would often herd the surging traders, buyers and the auto owners to any safe space down beside the road or, at least, compress them a bit to the edge of the road, even if it means allowing them to occupy a little strip on the service lane of the road.
Road accidents, most of them albeit minor, involving the commercial motorbike operators, are regularly recorded every market day. However, since a fateful market day about nine years ago when a petrol tanker burst into flames by the market, causing an inferno which claimed over 25 human lives on the spot, there hasn't been any accident worthy of remembrance there. "It is only God that saves us!" traders and buyers often exclaim with gratitude.
Madalla Market, situated just outside the door of the Federal Capital Territory, in Suleja Local Government Area of Niger State, is one of the perhaps innumerable markets in human communities 'strategically and advantageously,' but 'extremely perilously,' located beside the highways, and other major intra-city and township roads.
In Northern Nigeria alone, from the Nigeria/Benin/Niger republics border in the west, to the Nigeria/Chad/Cameroon border in the east, the number of these markets, most of them holding on fixed market days, traditionally after seven or five days, seem uncountable.
Although ghastly motor accidents on the roads besides such markets, roasting or crunching many human lives, occur, it is striking to note that such accidents occur after a long time, often seeming like once in ages. Yet, when they occur, several of them, like Madalla Market's petrol-tanker-caused accident of nine years ago, are so grisly indeed, that they render the road-side location of the markets completely unwise on the part of the communities and the government.
Madalla Market, holding its market day every Thursday, is the acclaimed biggest market around the Federal Capital Territory.
"This market, in its present location, is about 28 years old," Mallam Idris Tanko Salisu, the traditional market superintendent (Sarkin Kasuwa), narrated, continuing, "It used to be by the old Lokoja road far inside the town. We relocated it here because we observed that any market located by the highway is more popular and attracts more people. That is why we appealed to the district head who allocated this vast plot of land to which we relocated the market. Initially, it was opened as the yams market only. In due course it grew into a large market."
The market, according to him, has about 98 stalls built by the government, and over 5000 others built by the traders themselves. Right from Wednesday, which is a day before the market, trucks troop from far and near to the market, conveying goods for the trading of Thursday, the market day.
"Initially, we observed with delight, just as I told you earlier, that its location by the highway has so many advantages, but, gradually, we came to grips with the reality of the fact that its proximity to the road is very dangerous because of motor accidents," Mallam Idris Tanko recalled, adding, however, that "we, therefore, had to compel ourselves to remain here because it has here since the time of our fathers."
According to him, because of its proximity to the FCT and its vantage position as the largest market around the territory, it grew rapidly, attracting such a large crowd which has to flood into the road, mainly because residents gradually took over and built their houses on a very large portion of the land allocated to it, which deprives it the sufficient space to expand according to its pace of rapid development.
"This is why the market became too small and the crowd it cannot contain has to flood into the road. We adopted the measure of driving away people from the road or herding them back into the market with the concerted operation of the Road Safety officials, the Police, the Civil Defense Corp and my men, but barely five minutes after every effort, will you see the crowd back on the road as if it has never been herded away," the Sarkin Kasuwa complained.
"I must agree with you that yes! We witness auto accidents on the crowd, most especially commercial motorcycle riders and cars ramming into the crowd of traders and buyers. There were instances of vehicles falling and bursting to blazes on market days, but when the next market day comes round you will see the crowd on the fated spot as if nothing serious took place there the previous week," Mallam Idris lamented.
As a result of the present situation of the market, contract has been awarded for its extension, and most of the residential houses built on the land will be demolished and their owners relocated."
Mallam Muhammadu Bashir Musa is the Secretary of the Madalla branch of the National Union of Road Transport Workers.
"Because of its proximity to Abuja, the market has actually become the silo (food store) of Abuja," he stated, explaining, "so the market grew rapidly with a crowd it cannot contain. In our position as those directly concerned with the vehicles, we have been doing everything we can under the circumstances to avert accidents involving the market crowd, but the flooding crowd and vehicles often render our efforts useless."
He complained: "When a buyer comes to the market, his whole concentration is on buying what he wants, and he doesn't care about where he parks his car. He parks it wherever he feels like. On the market like this, that parking can be on the road, not by the roadside, forgetting that he is in the market, where there is a very large crowd, not at home."
He recalled that his union once joined efforts with the men of the of the Civil Defense Corp to control the market crowd flooding into the road and ensure safe vehicular traffic flow, but the Civil Defense Corp later had to withdraw when they observed that their efforts 'failed.' Presently, only men of the FRSC make some efforts in that regard every market day, he said.
"In my observation, all around the FCT, there is no market as densely populated and as well stocked with most of the essential consumer items, like food stuff, a market on which most Abuja residents rely for such items, as Madalla market. You may wish to go round the entire FCT and assess things yourself. That is why I call it Rumbun Abuja (Abuja's food store) But its location by the Lokoja-Abuja-Kaduna highway is very threatening to the trading crowd," Mallam Muhammadu maintained.
"The Madalla Market happens to be too close to the highway," Hafiz Muhammad Tarauni, the Suleja Unit Commander of the Federal Road Safety Commission (FRSC) observed, emphasizing, "The road is such a busy one that it is the only access from Kano and Kaduna to Abuja and Lokoja. Therefore, the market is just too close to one of the busiest highways in the federation. Vehicles convey goods, traders and buyers and heap all of them by this highway."
He complained: "That is why on the market day, you see the road narrowing. You know, naturally, the narrowing of the road must cause some problems like, first, the difficulty by pedestrians crossing the road. In such a convergence, in such a location, on such a day and for such a purpose as selling and buying, not everyone has the composure to ensure that he or she observes road safety rules or to know when, where and how he or she can cross the road safely. On the part of the motorists, not everyone has the patience to slow down his speed when he approaches the location of such a convergence. Most motorists are always in a hurry."
He observed further: "Imagine the situation where hooting vehicles approaching a frenzying crowd of pedestrians crossing at an uncontrollable speed. It is usual to hit at least a few of the pedestrians. Second, the road is narrowed by the crowd, and many fuel tankers ply it because of the location of fuel depots in Suleja and Minna. Third, the market is located on undulating hills and around road bends. You can see that it is only God that controls the situation, saving many people and a large bulk of goods from being crushed on every market day, especially through our efforts."
The FRSC Unit Commander said the road safety agency sends its men on regular patrol throughout every market day to ensure safety between motorists and the market crowd.
"For the morning shift, we send between fifteen and twenty men there. They will be on patrol there right from 7 am, when the crowd is still light, up to noon or half past noon. Another team will be sent there for the afternoon shift, which lasts up to 6 pm, when the market formally closes," he explained.
"Actually, serious accidents are seldom recorded on the market day there because we are always there on a duty we call 'Road Calming.' After ensuring that vehicles are properly parked, we wave down vehicles advancing at high speed to slow down. This is done by our men right from the U-turn near Zuma Rock. Our vehicle will be shuttling between the U-turn near the Zuba flyover and Zuma Rock throughout the day. This effort helps, but once in a while, minor accidents like a car or motorbike knocking down a pedestrian, occurs. On the market day, we seldom receive cases of hitting down pedestrians to death there. Most of the accidents there are minor," he maintained.
With its continued proximity to one of the busiest highways in the country, it would remain very interesting to observe how Madalla Market's proposed extension inwards could mitigate the threat of road accidents involving the trading crowd there.

Original_Search_Title: Thanks, Governor Lamido Sanusi
Headline: Nigeria: Thanks, Governor Lamido Sanusi
Body_Text:
A foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds, adored by little statesmen. --Ralph Waldo Emerson, 1803-1882.
Hain't we got all the fools in town on our side? And Hain't that a a big enough majority in any town? --Mark Twain, 1835-1910, Vanguard Book of Quotations p.63.
SOMETIMES procrastination might not be the thief of time, as the old adage has it.
It might save one from embarrassments and regrets. The case of the new governor of Central Bank is a case in point.
I had originally planned to carpet the president for what I thought was a lopsided selection of his top staff on the grounds that they violated the Federal Character principle as enshrined in our constitution. But, I was also caught in a dilemma.
For years, I had advocated meritocracy, even if it meant jettisoning the basic principle of equitable representation in government. So when the choice of the new governor was announced, my first instinct was to disagree with it because it violated the principle of Federal Character and because too many of Yar'Adua's appointments have been so parochial they reflect the man's destitution for a national view of selection.
Virtually all the top posts that are worth having in this government have gone to people from three or four states - usually Kano and Katsina.
I was even going to blast all the senators from the three southern zones who would vote for the appointment - irrespective of their political party.
But, then a guiding hand touched me and counseled that I should wait for Sanusi's visit to the Senate; known usually for "take a bow and leave" approach to assessment of candidates for high office.
The Senate president apparently cannot often wait to get to his pounded yam and okoho soup. Thank God this was different and that difference produced a total turnaround in my perception of things.
Strangely enough, the attributes which have now turned me into a praise-singer of the governor, are the very ones that have generated misgivings between him and the Federal Government.
Three statements stand out of the trope of words uttered at the hearings. The first was his description of the seven-point agenda as too vague and without content. The second was his admonition to government to stop visioning and start acting.
The third was his reminder to us that big banks don't necessarily mean healthy banks. For the first he recommended prioritization; for the second he called for real action instead of sloganeering.
And for the third he tried to restrain us from the euphoria associated with banking consolidation by pointing out the N1 trillion exposure being experienced by the banks at the moment.
Let me address the third first, because that had not disturbed the Federal government which might be thinking about reversing the policy underlying consolidation.
If size alone were the sufficient and necessary condition for a sound bank, then none of the leading U.S banks should be bankrupt today.
Size, while it protects against small shocks, is totally worthless when earthquakes arrive. The statement, "the bigger they are, the harder they fall" applies here. One big bank going down can take with it several more enterprises than ten small banks.
And given the great exposure of Nigerian banks, today, and the extent and nature of which are still closely guarded secrets, only a fool will bet that all is well. For lack of a better term let me call what has evolved "consolidation of risk".
Associated with the consolidation of risk are two developments; one of which keeps recurring each time we have a new initiative which proves to be temporarily effective.
That initiative becomes like the only answer to all our problems. But, we need to be reminded about what happened in the early 1990s when banking deregulation was first introduced.
Within months banks were all over the capital market raising funds and declaring unprecedented dividends.
The leading bankers at the time became folk heroes and they dominated the proceedings of the Nigerian Economic Summit Group.
Less than five years after it all started, the bubble collapsed and the "men-about-town" became fugitives from the law.
Then banking regulations had to be strengthened to protect depositors against rapacious bankers or what I called then "robbers in designer suits".
Some "gun-slingers" are still within the system and only time will reveal them.
Furthermore, consolidation had resulted in almost total exclusion of the North from the executive suites of banks.
Of the 24 consolidated banks, only one is headed by a northerner and even the share ownership has shifted strongly against the north.
It is to be expected that the situation, which the former governor regarded as a minor issue will continue to be regarded as some sort of injustice to the north.
Even, the Unity Bank Plc which the north managed to hold on to is a fringe player in the sector.
That is a fact which we ignore at our peril and it might have influenced the decision to appoint a northerner as governor of Central Bank. As for me, I expect a slight change in the consolidation policy any time soon. And, I welcome it.
The comments on seven-point agenda and the Vision 2020 were actually the most surprising.
Hitherto, one had been under the impression that no leading northern personality would openly disagree with a northern president; even if patently wrong. Yar'Adua, like other presidents before him, has quickly acquired an aura of infallibility, judging from the utterances of some of his "Advisers" who two years ago were writing intelligent criticisms of government policies as editors and columnists.
That was expected, it was also par for the course that every minister, contractor, PDP stalwart wanting a slice of the national cake, will openly support the meaningless agenda.
The former governor of CBN went along, in my view, not out of conviction, because he knows enough economics to drill holes in the propaganda, but because he probably thought it would help in his second term ambition.
Had he asked me, I would have told him that his fate was sealed when he announced decimalization.
Two days in Kano and Kaduna talking to "movers and shakers" convinced me of that and I wrote it at the time. Thus, it required an exceptionally courageous man in high government circles to tell the truth which others have been evading.
I have since read the statements of the government's spokesman, who is an amateur in propaganda and probably does not realize that a lot of Nigerians can interpret a statement themselves and don't need any spin doctor to help them out.
The whole attempt at face saving, after that slap in the face, is unconvincing. Prioritisation means you concentrate on one or two and leave the rest; at least for now; nothing more and nothing less.
A government which cannot even write a decent budget has no business talking about seven-point agenda or Vision 2020.
he insistence on consistency by this administration is indeed the hobgoblin of one "little statesman" who can only chair the F-20 Summit because he is not good enough for G-20.
Welcome, Governor Lamido Sanusi; coming from the north will no longer be held against you; at least not in this column.

Original_Search_Title: NDLEA Uncovers Drug Cartel at MMIA
Headline: Nigeria: NDLEA Uncovers Drug Cartel at MMIA
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chief Executive Officer of the National Drug Law Enforcement Agency (NDLEA), Ahmadu Giade, has threatened to deal decisively with every member of a notorious drug cartel operating at the Murtala Mohammed International Airport (MMA), Lagos to put an end to the obnoxious activity of moving illicit drugs through the airport.
Giade made the threat yesterday in Lagos, following the arrest of additional two suspected members of a syndicate that specialises in smuggling unsearched luggage into aircrafts to avoid being detected by security operatives.
Describing members of the cartel as national saboteurs, the NDLEA boss said that the agency would not rest until everyone involved in the criminal act was brought to book and therefore urged Nigerians to remain vigilant and continue to support the agency in its endeavour to stamp out the use and trading of hard drugs in the country.
"Drug barons have no respect for authority and human life. We must therefore be vigilant and resist their antics. When they weirdly evade serving their jail terms after conviction, the agency uncovered it. They resorted to blackmail, they failed. Now, they have devised a new strategy of smuggling unsearched bags into aircrafts. We are poised to stop their next strategy Giade vowed, adding that NDLEA has the responsibility to protect the country from the hands of drug barons.
The agency also explained that "The gang made up of some staff of OTIS, a firm that maintains conveyor belt at the Lagos airport smuggles bags containing drugs at night.
They will then check-in dummy bags during screening and use the tags of the dummy for the ones containing drugs. Two additional suspects were arrested on May 15, 2009."
They are Madu Sunday Emeka, 31, who claimed to be a trader and Okpo Richard, 28, a former staff of Nigerian Aviation Handling Company.
In all, five members of the cartel have been apprehended.
NDLEA detected the horrible activities on May 1, this year, when the anti-narcotic body observed that some conveyor belt technicians hid two bags containing dried weeds suspected to be cannabis (Indian hemp) weighing 48 kilogrammes in the conveyor belt at night.
The agency explained that their plan was to tag the bags with the tags of two dummy luggage containing yams which were checked in by a member of their syndicate.
Three members of the group Hammed Oyesanya Olaniyi, 32, Dosunmu Abiodun Yusuf, 34, and Stephen Adebowale, 33 were nabbed immediately by NDLEA officers at the Lagos airport and the agency said they have made confessional statements on how they hid the bags the previous night and tagged them the following day.
Efforts are however being intensified to arrest other members of the syndicate.

Original_Search_Title: 'Millions Would Have Been Lost From Market Closure'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Millions Would Have Been Lost From Market Closure'
Body_Text:
Traders at Garki II Model Market Wednesday talked of loses they would have incurred if officials of Abuja Environmental Protection Board (AEPB) had gone ahead to seal up their market that day.
Chairman of the market's association, Prince Edozie C. Ugwu said a trader that recently brought fishes worth seven million Naira almost became hypertensive because of the threat.
"Apart from that trader, there are other traders that deal with perishable items and closing down the market would have caused such traders and their families' untold hardship," the market leader stated.
He said the market executive had to go to the police and will embark on an enlightenment mission to intimate the AEPB and other government agencies of the court orders restraining Abuja Market Management Limited from operating in the market.
According to him, AEPB should deal with the market's association on the service charge issue and not AMML.
The market leader accused AMML officials of allocating spaces to hawkers and yam sellers arbitrarily inside the market.
Our reporter observed when the market leader confronted a yam seller and warned her not roast yam close to the market stalls.
"This is wrong as what this woman is doing can cause fire and yet AMML officials will not take note of this before giving out space," he said.
Another trader lamented that when AEPB notice got to the traders on Tuesday evening, panic engulfed the market as traders started selling their wares at give away prices in order to get money.
"We are human beings struggling to make a living through legitimate means and officials of AEPB should note this and treat us with decency," a trader stated.
He advised AEPB management to always think of the cost implications their action would cause before announcing market closures.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Sacks Lafia Residents
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Sacks Lafia Residents
Body_Text:
Lafia — Many residents of Tudun Kauri area of Lafia, Nasarawa State capital ,were Sunday night displaced by rampaging flood. The flood was as a result of a heavy downpour which began some few minutes to 8 p.m lasting for about an hour.
The flood which entered most residential houses especially around Angwan Gwandara along the state Ministry of Education road ,destroyed property in its route, while also displacing several families.
The flood cut off several communities in Lafia, Nasarawa State. Also cut off were federal and state ministries and parastatals like the Federal Road Safety Commission (FRSC), Federal Ministry of Education, Nigerian Metrological Agency, state ministry of education, tourism board, scholarship board, Nasara Water, among others.
Agyaragu Tofa which is also one of the yam havens in the state is among the communities cut off.
Staff of the affected ministries and agencies including commissioners, permanent secretaries and other top personnel had trouble getting to their respective offices.
The area, Daily Trust learnt, is prone to flood every rainy season because water in the tarred part of the town with drainage channels empties onto the road causing severe flooding when it rains.
The water stagnated at a point between the residence of the chairman of the state independent electoral commission, Mallam Musa Agyo and that of staff of Daughters of Mary Schools, preventing motorists, pedestrians and even motor cyclists popular known as Okada getting to their destinations.
Daily Trust discovered on a visit to the area that the water stagnated several days after a heavy down pour because of lack of drainage system which would have allowed it easy passage. As a result of the devastating effect of Sunday's flood, youths in the area blocked access to the state institutions located along the road in protest against what they referred to as government insensitivity to their plights.
The youths in their hundreds bore a gaping hole across the road at a point near the FRSC junction thereby cutting off major access to the area.
Leader of the protesting youth, Mallam Likita Mohammed, said the road would only be made accessible if the state government constructs drainage which would allow water easy access.
Staff of the affected agencies and ministries had to go in circle, through the access link by the Nigeria Custom Service to get to their respective destinations and even that according to a staff of one of the ministries, Joshua Sabo is not easy as the access road is already deplorable due to constant use.
The managing director of the Nasarawa Urban Development Board (NUDB) Mallam Jafaru Ango was said to be away on official assignment when Daily Trust visited the board yesterday. The board secretary, Danjuma Mohammed said only Ango was qualified to speak on the matter.

Original_Search_Title: Democratic Culture Still Lacking -Elechi
Headline: Nigeria: Democratic Culture Still Lacking -Elechi
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ebonyi State Governor, Chief Martin Elechi is of the opinion that most Nigerians are yet to imbibe democratic culture even as he insists that the ruling Peoples Democratic Party(PDP) has performed creditably. In this chat with some newsmen where he also outlined the priorities of his government the governor contends that the nation will record greater strides the moment the masses join forces with government. IHEANACHO NWOSU was there.
How really do you feel about people coming out to call that you remain their governor till 2015?
Once again, I want to sincerely thank you senior journalists for this type of rapport, it is very encouraging and it will bring closer understanding about what we are doing and what the people outside think about us.
Really, as a matter of fact, my focus now is to do the assignments for which I was elected. I try to satisfy my conscience that whatever I'm doing is the right thing. However, the way other people perceive it is a different matter. But when one remains focus on one's activities and people say it is good, then we derive greater encouragement. I said what I said about the need to play down campaign for another election which will be in two years time because quite frankly we have a lot on our hand, those projects are not even accomplished. If I should be thinking of 2011 instead of 2009 and 2010, I may derail myself, so I want to remain focused on them for now. The contractors assured me, a good number of the projects will finish ahead of the time . Most of these projects you saw on the print are programmed to end between August this year and March next year. So, that gives us ample time to look inward, set our tools and see whether it will be worthwhile to continue or whether there is anybody else who will do it better. All our concern is to give the best to the state, whether it is by me or by someone else is immaterial. So, I don't know if that has answered your question but this is the way I look at everything.
What informed your decision to revive the boarding system arrangement in secondary schools in the state?
Well, we belong to the old stock so to speak, I went to Marynor Secondary School in the 50s and that is all the way in Ogoja. There was nobody from my community there except myself, so those of us who gathered there were from different parts of the eastern region and infact some from the then western region and we discovered that throughout our studentship and the years thereafter, we were still relating as brothers. That is what you find not happening these days because there are so many community secondary schools, the students who go to these schools know only their community, they don't know much about the outside world. So there is need for people to know more about their environment and that is what the NYSC programme is all about. Know more about people from outside, that way your interaction, your relationship will then become more smooth and this hostage taking and hostilities can be played down . That is the way I look at it and many people who were of that same old stock believe what I'm saying and agree that returning the schools to the boarding system is very necessary.
We may not succeed in doing that to everybody, but let us make a path. So far, we have twenty-seven (27) of such schools, fourteen (14) are for girls, thirteen (13) are for boys for now and we believe also that if each school expands up to 2,000 people, you will have less need for so many teachers. Many of the schools today have less than 200 students and a school that has 200 student needs almost as many teachers as another school that has 400 or 500 students. So in other to make the work load a distribution of functions, less expenses, it is better to have fewer schools with more students in them, that way the teaching wouldn't suffer. That is the way we are looking at it.
What do you think the state really needs to make it compete with others in the geopolitical zone and do you think your administration has achieved some of them?
Well, it's a mouthful before now. Soon after the state creation, I wrote a book titled '39 year old Ebonyi state',.The agitation lasted for 39 years before the state was created and in the concluding chapters I mentioned certain problems such as those you are now talking about, we are not trying to catch-up with any state, let me be so blunt, we are trying to develop at our own taste and according to our own needs. There is so much effort in neighbouring states , almost in every state to regard what one state is doing as the model, that way we are not being our true self. In other words, we should look at problems, look at our culture, look at our background and decide what is best for us to do.
That is what I keep telling our professors in the universities that when we make our laws they need not be the same as the laws of other universities because we need to complement with different, sometimes conflicting ideas. The essence of a federation is that each of the federating unit should develop according to its own taste, according to its own reporting, according to where the people of that state want to be. We all cant be put in the same mould. So part of the problem is addressing the mindset of those who think we should do things exactly like other state.
You are probably aware of the current agitation of medical doctors to have the same salary as their colleagues in federal government institutions and I said no that is not even the essence of the circular because the circular is targeted to a select institutions belonging to the federal government, it is therefore not binding on states. States are suppose to pay salaries that they can afford and recall that we are not in a unity system of government , we are federating units, each tier of government has its own responsibilities.
Coming to the internally generated revenue, that is a problem, whether we admit it or not, the military did a good job, but they also made some mistakes. The level of workforce in the service is still heavy for our level of revenue but we can't throw people away just because of the problems of keeping them in the service, you met them there, they are there already and you just have to make do with their presence. But for a long time now the culture of taxation seems to have died, it is only those who receive salaries that really pay tax. I don't see in my community what I use to see so many years ago, personal income, tax receipt. So if you start imposing that type of taxation now, it becomes a problem. One, you are alone, no other state around you is doing it. Two, your people are very poor and they need to be helped, that way it really makes our internally generated revenue position very vulnerable.
But through enlightenment we are making progress and if these infrastructure we are putting in place will succeed that will be the basics for economic take-off. We have our cement factory to reactivate and perhaps expand, we have a few industries that we can start but not before we have put the basic infrastructure like roads, water and electricity in place, at that stage the internally generated revenue can improve substantially. So that is the way we are approaching it.
What have been the main challenges of your government?
Well, wherever you have people, the more the challenges. There are people who think I should not be where I am and instigate unfavourable reactions among the populace. For instance, those who are engineering the doctors now to go on strike, not to let go, those who are financing the students to go on strike and things like that. I look down on people with such propensity, but wherever you have human being, there must be problems. Then again, problem of old habit, - I wouldn't say that accountability has reached the level where we should be satisfied with it, we are still trying to let people know that the way they handle their personal business is exactly the way they should handle public business. You don't cheat government because government is not your brother or your father or your village, so the same level of morality is expected from every body. Like in other parts of Nigeria you have a conflict between culture and religion. Part of the PDP manifesto in the 2007 election was that culture should be harmonized in such a way that it will improve governance and so we are right now building cultural centre in different part of the state, it will be an ongoing programme until all parts of the state have cultural center, so that at given time in the year, religious leaders and traditionalists, those in charge of culture can have a meeting ground and maybe have their new yam festival with church service, so as to bring those two cultures in harmony within the state.
So, these are some of the challenges.
You are being asked to continue in Ebonyi but outside the state, many believe the PDP government has not lived to expectation, how do you feel about the campaign against your party?
It is not just making such claims , how do they illustrate it? You must have a basis for saying it. Is not just making a wide allegation that you don't justify with any examples.
People say that the 10 years of the party being in power have led to fixing the energy problem and other infrastructural needs ( Cut in) Is that a function of the political party or a problem of the people?
Again there are claims that corruption war is not being waged the way it should go what is your opinion on that?
What is there that the leadership has not done? You see if you listen to judges, police officers, the leadership of the EFCC, ICPC, they have one thing in common and that is that an accused person is presumed to be innocent until he's proved the guilt that is assumed. So, when people make allegations, those allegations have to be substantiated. The quantum of noise that goes with the arrest of people is not the same when those people are exonerated, nobody says anything. So the general impression is that nothing is good, nothing is going on well. Admittedly the corruption may not have been stamped out completely, but some of us also take time to intellectually investigate the mode, the incidence of corruption, how it came about, how do we interpret it and it makes good sense reaching some of what our philosophers and great scholars say about corruption. There is a great mix-up between our concept of morality in a multi tribal nation state of Nigeria and what the western world preaches. There is a great mix-up between our concept of morality in a multi tribal nation state of Nigeria and what the western world preaches. Sometimes the concept of traditional African hospitality is said to be corruption in the western way of looking at it, and we have no clear direction. These thing comes to the fore front in a seminars, in workshop, and things like that. When the Governors forum had their retreat barely two (2) years ago, the matter came up, what we told the EFCC was that what they were preaching could not be related to the fact on ground. That is not to say there are no corrupt people, but please,it is not the problem of the PDP. Even if you abolish PDP today and have another political party in power, it is only a change of name, the truth is that the people who are now in PDP will be in those other party and will continue to do what they are doing. So it is not the problem of the party, it is the problem our people, of ourselves, as a nation in the process of development and in that process we are trying to imbibe new value system and do away with some of our old value system. And it is not what you achieve in one decade.
Are you comfortable with the pace at which we are going?
Well, the pace at which we are going is inseparable from our make-up as a country. When you become council chairman you will think differently from the way you do think as a journalist. You are dealing with so many people with different ideas, with different expectations, and you have to tolerate them. I believe if I have more resources, I will do better, I would do certain things faster than federal government. That is true of all our governors, because our size and our engagement in that big one nation can be a problem. Like I keep complaining there is no day in a week that we don't have a seminar, either in Abuja or somewhere else in another state capital, asking governors, asking commissioners, asking council chairmen to attend, this is mandatory, this is critical, this is unavoidable. So, even if you spend half your time attending those seminar, you cannot attend one percent of the seminars and so to that extent you are distracted, we should allow the state take greater responsibility and have greater independence, so that they can focus on their own decisions, goals and programmes, that is where I thinks we can do better, we can move faster.
What do you make of the clamour for the reform of the electoral system and how the government has handled the matter so far?
Again, it is the same question, we have asked and tried to answer being put differently. The issue of electoral reform I participated in the last meeting of the National Council of State which deliberated on the report of the panel set-up to examine the electoral issue. You see, the man in the street is so far away in his thought, in his ideas from the man holding the public office. When people criticize INEC, I keep saying that INEC should be understood in different ways and different levels. You have the commission as constituted, the chairman, the commissioners but on the day of election, the INEC is the totality of those who participate in the process, the voter, the poll clerk, those who give out the ballot paper, that is, presiding officers, the collection officers and those who transmit the results. For as long as you have so many people with divergent interests participating, there is no way you can hold Maurice Iwu or any of his commissioners responsible for what goes wrong in the hands of other people whom he cannot control, that is one point. The other point is that in this country we are very bad losers, lets admit that fact. Somebody who doesn't make it in an election fails to admit that he is not wanted by the people, he looks for scapegoat . And to be successful is to incur the envy of unsuccessful candidate, if the PDP is the success that it is today which is generating a lot of jealousy, bad feelings among those who loose to the PDP. Look at all the remark made in the election tribunal.
Each time the PDP wins, people say Judiciary is compromised, whenever PDP looses we have a courageous and Independent Judiciary. That is the price the PDP is paying for being a success story, that is common to human nature. So the election cannot be different from the people who participate in those election, if we are corrupt, if we are violent, that will be transmitted into the election process. If you watched what happened in Ekiti state in the re-run governorship election, the adamant posture of members of AC, the way they refused to be orderly, the way the police were held hostage as well as the INEC commissioner, that is not what we expect in a democracy. But we also know that if you should evoke the full wrath of the law because of the flexibility of the country at the moment, you may bite more than you can chew, that is my personal way of looking at it. Until we know that the election cannot be different from the participant, unless we know that we'll continue blaming the wrong person, so we should blame ourselves it will continue to affect our aspiration to have our electoral system reformed.

Original_Search_Title: Igbo Community Protests Ban On Eze-Ndigbo Title
Headline: Nigeria: Igbo Community Protests Ban On Eze-Ndigbo Title
Body_Text:
Lagos — Some members of Igbo community resident in Lagos last week held a peaceful demonstration against the ban of the use of Eze Igbo title in Lagos by the Lagos State Government.
The demonstrators numbering over one thousand, which included Igbo students of tertiary institutions in Lagos, market women/ men and community leaders led by some members of Ohanaeze in Lagos State, carried placards condemning the ban of the use of Eze Igbo title by leaders of Ndi Igbo in Lagos and called on Lagos State Government to lift the ban.
The demonstrators later converge at the palace of the Eze Igbo of Lagos, Eze Hyacinth Ohazulike (MON) in a show of solidarity.
Some of the people who spoke to Vanguard said that Eze Igbo has been helpful to Igbo in Lagos by awarding scholarship to students, empowering the unemployed, settling disputes and making the people to remember Igbo tradition and festival like new yam festival and others.
They called on Igbo traditional rulers to stop interfering in their affairs in Lagos as they can not rule both in Igbo land and in Lagos at the same time.

Original_Search_Title: Ringo is Star Attraction At Harare Jazz Festival
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ringo is Star Attraction At Harare Jazz Festival
Body_Text:
SOUTH African music maestro Ringo Madlingozi is expected to thrill his fans in Zimbabwe when he makes a guest appearance at the inaugural Harare Jazz Festival to be held at the Celebration Centre on Sunday.
Sam Mataure, the brains behind the festival, said Madlingozi who is popularly known for his vocal prowess had confirmed his attendance.
The irrepressible South African crooner would be at the festival headlined by jazz greats from his country -- Judith Sephuma, Jimmy Dludlu and Ernie Smith as well as Nigerian Kunle Ayo and Malawian Jazz icon Eric Pilianere.
Four Jazz songbirds, Prudence Katomeni Mbofana, Dudu Manhenga, Rute Mbangwa and Kudzai Sevenzo, will be representing Zimbabwe.
"It's definite that Ringo is coming," Mataure said. "He has agreed and I am going to Johannesburg tomorrow to finalise everything with him.
"He is not part of the bill but he will just make a special guest performance.
"It will be just a way of spicing up the festival and we hope people will enjoy it as it promises to be an exciting event."
This would not be the first time for Madlingozi to come to Zimbabwe as he has staged a number of shows in Harare and has even done a duet with superstar Oliver Mtukudzi titled Into Yam'.
Mataure said they had cleared everything with the National Arts Council of Zimbabwe (NACZ) and all the artists had confirmed their attendance.
"We have sorted everything with NCAZ and right now we are doing immigration processes," he said.
NACZ acting assistant arts promotions director, Nicholas Moyo confirmed the developments.
Mataure said they planned to make the festival an annual event to promote jazz music in Zimbabwe.
"We would like to continue promoting jazz in the country so we want to make the festival an annual event," he said.
"Our plans are to hold it on Independence Day but this year out of respect for our Prime Minister Morgan Tsvangirai who lost his wife in March we decided to postpone the festival from April to June."

Original_Search_Title: Country Attends Yokohama African Festa 2009
Headline: Botswana: Country Attends Yokohama African Festa 2009
Body_Text:
Yokohama — Botswana was amongst many African countries that participated in this year's 2009 African Festa in Japan's second largest city,Yokohama, on Saturday.
African Festa is an annual African cultural event that has been taking place in Japan since 1999.
The event is a major showcase of African music, dance, clothes, food, cultures and it presents an opportunity for the Japanese public to learn about Africa through its cultural heritage.
Speaking at this well-attended event the Dean of African Ambassadors in Japan, Gabon's ambassador Jean Christian Obamu said the African Festa is a major conduit through which interest into Africa is generated. He said through the African Festa the Japanese people learn and appreciate that there is more to Africa than the dominant stereotypical images of war, disease, poverty and other ills, which he said are perpetrated by the Western media.
"This is a give-and-take opportunity for Japan to explore ways of cooperation," he said adding that African Festa is about hope.
The highlight of the event was fashion parade of diverse dresses from all over the African continent. From the German print that has now been adopted by most Southern African states such as Lesotho, Swaziland and Botswana as their women national dress to the "Chitenge" in Zambia and Malawi to the flowing robes of North Africa. The West and North African men were elegant in their traditional dresses.
The event was not all about the dresses but about the diversity of Africa in both skin colour and different body structures. The dark and tall men and women around Central Africa sauntered around the stage. The lighter and Arab looking Africans of North Africa were here to show another dimension of the continent.
The lean and dark Africans from the East moved to the crescendo of the African drum.
The more full-bodied Africans of Southern Africa completed the diverse picture of Africa.
In fact if there were to be any prizes to be won for the longest applause on the day it would without doubt go to our Southern African representative from Lesotho. Elegantly dressed in what the Basotho call "Seshoeshoe" she drew whistles and ululations. It was perhaps for the elegant dress and of course for the 'Serowe body figure' mostly common but not limited to Southern Africa. Japanese like most Asians are small in body structure but they seem to appreciate other people of a different mould from them.
In the midst of Japanese shushi and other Western dishes available in most Japanese cities the African Festa is one rare occasion where African gourmet is served galore. There is Foo-foo, Nshima, Yam.
In the midst of all these dishes Botswana's own seswaa and dikgobe hobnobbed with the others with discernible aplomb. The Botswana team of Poppy Majingo, MmaMotsamai, Taboka Matlhabaphiri, Oscar Motswagae and his wife, Tirahalo Sekao had prepared seswaa and dikgobe, which was going for 500 yen (about P40) and a refreshing ginger drink at 100 yen (about P8). Japanese public thronged the Botswana food stall and could not help but lick their fingers as they gobbled all.
The Administrative attaché at the Embassy,MmaMotsama had every reason to feel proud because people just liked her seswaa. At home she does not normally cook and pound seswaa but she has done it here.
"O se gatile seswaa se," we tell her.
The Botswana stall stocked with traditional handicrafts such as baskets , beads and an ostritch eggshell, is another attraction. Scores of them stop at the booth and they admire the baskets and the eggshell. There is a Botswana Tourism Board brochure, which is in Japanese.
Ambassador Motswagae says the proceeds of all the sales made at the Festa shall all go to charity in Botswana.
"The staff at the embassy contributed the food items such as beans, samp and contributed to buy other items. Whatever we raise here shall all be sent to assist an area of need back home," said Ambassador Motswagae.
The Yokohama 2009 African Festa was graced by actors, the Deputy Mayor of Yokohama and senior officials from the Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

Original_Search_Title: Fallout of Ekiti Re-Run - Divided They Stand
Headline: Nigeria: Fallout of Ekiti Re-Run - Divided They Stand
Body_Text:
How did the pounded-yam loving people of Ekiti State decide to pound one another with a huge mortar of violence over elections in just 63 wards?
How did a woman who had been maligned and praised and again maligned in equal measure by two political parties become the object of national treasure?
How did votes in one local government area bridge such a very huge gap in electoral deficit?
All these could only have happened in Ekiti State, with its teeming highly educated populace.
But the lessons from Ekiti State, especially how a once united people became divided and are standing on that division is a metaphor for the larger Nigerian nation which has refused to move forward but is almost always eager to straddle backwards from the modest points of development of the 1960s.
What the election in Ekiti State has shown, glaringly, is that the environment of election in Nigeria is not yet conducive for smooth, free and non violent electoral contest.
Laws were being interpreted from both sides of the mouth on how and when a re-run within a re-run election should be conducted. It is becoming more glaring that there is a limit to what INEC can do in the face of dangerously obvious determination by politicians to thwart and subvert the system through various means.
On this score, the leadership of the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP, and its main opposition Action Congress, AC, are guilty. The level of guilt, however, could be disputed as there can never be a consensus as to who was solely responsible for the level of violence and angst which pervaded that polity.
RECs wield enormous powers when it comes to the issue of gubernatorial election. INEC headquarters, Abuja, does not have powers over any gubernatorial elections - just as was the case in the 2007 elections, for instance. It is becoming clearer that it is the RECs who are responsible for elections at the state level and, therefore, should carry the can for whatever successes or failures arising there from.
The plethora of gubernatorial petitions leading to elections that were upturned were solely the responsibility of RECs. Sections 27, 28, (29) and 69 of the Electoral Act, 2006, vests the powers in RECs.
The entire Electoral Act was silent on politicians' behaviour and that's why Nigerians' attention is being diverted from the real issues of the electoral process. The actions of politicians remain the source of the friction in the system.
The Electoral process, which is based on three pillars - the system, the management body and the participants (the politicians and the voters) has the conduct of the politicians as the most crucial. But what are the penalties for the fraud committed by politicians? Nothing as of now! This explains why Ekiti State was turned into a theater of violence.
So far, the system appears to be okay'
The management body, which is the electoral commission, needs some working on. It is not perfect yet but Nigeria and Nigerians can run and have been running with it. In fact, the National Electoral Commission, NEC, of 1993 produced the June 12, 1993 presidential elections, the freest yet in Nigeria's history.
But when it comes to voters and politicians, they are the problems.
Through a clever web of conspiracy, attention is being shifted almost always to the issues of who appoints the chairman of INEC, while the politicians have never really attempted to discuss the type of punishment or sanctions to be meted on culprits.
True, the issue of appointment of the umpire is a Germaine matter as far as the entire processes are concerned, but if the National Judicial Council, NJC, appoints an INEC chairman, and the politicians continue to issue inducements to electoral officers, what guarantees are there that the umpire would not misbehave.
In an environment with a mindset that thrives on domination at all cost at the very low level of party primaries, how would the appointment by the presidency of an INEC chairman stem that tide, for instance?
Thirdly, the debacle in Ekiti was the creation of both the PDP and the AC.
Again, the fact that RECs are appointed by the Federal Government and that they owe their allegiance to the government is another angle to explore in the divisive game going on in Ekiti.
The investments made by politicians to undermine the electoral umpire through painting it in bad light does not help the process as was evident with the 'hail her', 'crucify her' chants heaped on the head of Mrs. Adebayo. In fact, placing Mrs. Adebayo in a position where she found herself was unfair to her and the process, because whatever she called at the election one side would contest her sincerity.
There have also been serious allegations of attempt by the contending political parties to bribe INEC staff before and during the election in Ekiti.
This reflects the level of insincerity of the politicians and shows that there is really no difference between the politicians and the parties and that they are the source of the lapses they accuse INEC of.
For the hapless people of Ekiti, hope is not lost. The process is still on as the AC candidate, Kayode Fayemi has promised to fight on - legally, though.

Original_Search_Title: Food Stuff Prices Increase in Abuja Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Food Stuff Prices Increase in Abuja Markets
Body_Text:
Prices of food stuff have increased slightly in major markets in the Federal Capital Territory. Our reporter who visited Wuse, Karu and Nyanya markets during the week observed an increase in the prices of garri, rice, bean and yam.
A mudu (small bowl) of rice which sold at N280 now sell for N300, while a mudu of garri has also increased from N80 to N120 or N150, depending on the type.
Five small tubers of yam sells for N1, 200 as against N850 as was previously sold. Other foodstuffs like potatoes, which sold at N600 per basket now goes for N700.
The prices of red beans also increased from N200 to N250, while white beans sold at N200 from N 150. Food ingredients like tomatoes, onions, bell shaped pepper, and small pepper also increased astronomically.
For instance, a basket of tomatoes that was sold for N500 is now N800, while onions which cost N450 per basket now goes for N600 and pepper sells for N500 as against N400.
Our reporter also found customers complaining about the prices of cow and goat meat that have increased sharply.
A shopper, Mrs. Abiodun lamented that the money she brought to the market can not meet up with her budget.
"The amount of money with me cannot buy all I need to buy," she said.
Another shopper, Mrs. Gladys explained that the sudden increase in the prices of foodstuff might not be unconnected with the scarcity of fuel being experience in the country.
She appealed to the federal government to do something about the situation in order to alleviate the suffering of the masses.

Original_Search_Title: AFC's $60 Million Remittance - Self Against National Interest?
Headline: Nigeria: AFC's $60 Million Remittance - Self Against National Interest?
Body_Text:
Our sensibilities are assaulted everyday with media reports of corruption in high places, such that Nigerians now see public service as synonymous with the individual pursuit of narrow self-interest.
In an article titled "AFC, Round tripping and EFCC" published in this column on August 11, 2008, we discussed one such blatant misapplication of public funds on account of self.
The indictment of United Bank for Africa, UBA, a party to the scam, for round tripping in a criminal case in the United States led to the payment of fine of about $15m by the Nigerian bank and subsequently led to a follow-up investigation by a special committee set up by President Yar'Adua. In spite of the observation of collusion between UBA and our CBN Governor in the scam, nothing more has been heard about this case. However, the publications of beneficiaries of CBN's forex sales in March 2009 have once again reminded us of the incestuous relationship between our erudite Governor as an investor and our public treasury over which he presides.
The African Finance Corporation (AFC), which the Presidential Committee recognized as the brainchild of Professor Soludo bought and remitted $60m in two installments of $30m in March from the Central Bank. The advertised purpose of the remittances was the repatriation of investment in money market instruments. Although the specific category of instrument was not mentioned, but we recall the testimony of Austin Omeruwa, the erstwhile CEO of AFC, who claimed that AFC found the market for federal government bills and bonds with its high interest rates as the most profitable investment around! Meanwhile, we note that the CBN Governor who also doubled as AFC Chairman at the time was also responsible for determining the higher rate of return on government instruments, in spite of the greater debt burden on Nigerians! I think the Igbo's have a saying about the freedom of the man who has both the yam and the knife in his hands to slice the yam in his own favour!
We also recall the observation of the Presidential Committee that the source of funds for AFC's investments was the so-called 'idle' funds from the receipts from the sale of government treasury bills in the money market! By a wide stretch of the imagination, I suppose the CBN Governor may claim that Nigerians stood to benefit from the profits AFC made for lending to government money that were originally taken from government coffers! We are told that the AFC has since returned the $460m which it 'quietly' took from the public treasury, but the authorities continue to remain silent on the $7000m that our generous Head Banker also gave to 14 Nigerian banks about three years ago with terms which continue to be shrouded in mystery!
We do not know how much of that fund subsequently came back to Nigeria as foreign direct investment, but it is most likely that these funds have followed the path of 'wisdom' of the AFC investment in treasury bills and bonds! In other words, these banks would also be purchasing and remitting their capital and interest from the Nigerian money market sooner or later and consequently contribute to what is now generally described as capital flight of foreign direct investment! In view of these developments, permit me to take the liberty of refreshing our memories of these weird investments with the content of our article titled "AFC, Round Tripping and EFCC" first published in August last year. Please read on:
"Media reports at the end of July 2007 have lately confirmed that the Presidential Committee which probed the $480 million which the CBN invested in the African Finance Corporation (AFC) has completed its investigations. The Presidency initiated the Committee to address the unease in government with regards to the role played by the CBN Governor in the formation and funding of the AFC. After what appeared to be a painstaking audit exercise over three months, and spanning three continents, the Committee Chairman, Tunde Ogunshakin, disclosed that CBN's equity contribution was actually $462.923 million. However, the Committee noted that a decision for such investment should have required approval of the Federal Executive Council and consequently "frowned at the enormous powers of the CBN Board and recommended that the CBN Act be amended to reduce them" Daily Independent 23/7/08.
"In other words, the CBN Governor acted within the law by creating and funding the AFC. The full text of the Committees report is yet to be formally made public; so, it is not yet clear what the Committee felt about Soludo's headship of the AFC in his personal capacity; thus, even when a new CBN Governor succeeds the incumbent, Soludo would to continue to head the AFC in his private capacity.
"Concerned Nigerians are rightly of the view that if the intentions of Soludo were truly altruistic, Headship of the AFC should have been reserved for whoever wears the cap of CBN Governor at anytime. One wonders if the Committee also fathomed the order of succession as enshrined in the AFC memorandum as it currently stands; that is, would Soludo choose his own successor or enthrone an offspring or sibling as in a family heritage?
"The above notwithstanding, the Committee condemned the apparent sourcing and use of funds by the AFC; it confirmed that the CBN funded the investment from the sale of government treasury bills! The Committee further noted that all AFC funds were invested in money market instruments in Nigeria and described this manner of operation as round-tripping at best and money laundering if viewed from a criminal perspective!
"Now, let us take a closer look at what is actually happening here from a simple commonsense perspective of a rational Nigerian. First, let us look at the source of funding of the AFC equity. The CBN's favourite tools for controlling the omnipresent scourge of excess liquidity every month when naira allocations are substituted for distributable dollar revenue are the ubiquitous instruments of treasury bills and federal government bonds. Now, it is no more a secret that banks' profits have become bloated by the enormous profits they continue to make from the shenanigans of lending to government money that the CBN earlier deposited with them. Mr. President confirmed that this practice cost our country close to N400bn in 2007; indications are that servicing borrowings from treasury bills and bonds may approach N800bn in 2008. This type of government borrowing which is not tied to infrastructural or any form of human capacity development has been subsisting for years now with banks as the prime beneficiaries of the juicy rewards from such risk-free investments; no wonder they pay little attention to credit support of the real sector, with its attendant deprivations!
"Until the bubble of AFC scam burst, the CBN had always maintained that the funds accumulated from such reckless government borrowings were simply sterilized or in layman's language stored inertly in CBN vaults to keep the public and the real sector from accessing these funds if left in the hands of the banks!
"Now, we know better, as the Presidential Committee confirmed that part of these 'idle' funds was actually diverted into funding the AFC. However, since the AFC equity was in dollars, how did the CBN obtain exchange equivalent? The CBN obviously went into the forex market with hordes of naira of over N50bn (enough capital base for two banks) and sourced for the dollar equivalent from Bureau De Change (BDC)! It is inexplicable that the CBN which currently sits on an idle pile of foreign reserves of almost $60bn resorts to the lowly bureau de change to seek its AFC dollars, at higher cost, even when BDC's dollar source is also the same CBN! "What is going on here?" You might ask! Any wonder also that the CBN has, against national interest, sold over $5bn to BDCs from January to June this year? Has anyone ever heard of the Bank of England or the US Federal Reserve allocation forex directly to BDCs on their high streets?
"We now have an idea of how the AFC funds were consolidated, but how were these funds applied and what benefits did Nigerians derive? Well, Austin Ometoruwa, the recently suspended Chief Operating Officer of AFC, in his open letter recently published in the media confirms that the "AFC ... sought to optimize earnings ... by investing part of its short term naira instruments, thereby taking advantage of higher naira interest rates versus those for US dollar deposits". Vanguard pg. A3 4/8/2008! In simple English, this implies that the AFC dollars found its way back to Nigeria, where once again, it was converted to naira, presumably through banks and BDCs and the resultant naira sums were used to purchase treasury bills which had yields of about 10% compared with less than 1% for such risk-free instruments abroad!
"In this event, the AFC with Soludo, the CBN Governor, as life Chairman would be major beneficiary of a high interest rate regime in Nigeria! Meanwhile, the real sector and patriotic Nigerian manufacturers continue to lament excruciating burden of high interest rates and adverse effects on production and employment. Thus, any attempt to remove the costly burden of excess liquidity would be against the interest of the AFC of which our own CBN Governor is the Life President!
I have maintained that the payment of dollar allocations to the three-tiers of government will tame the rampaging ghost of excess liquidity and strengthen the naira, but again, these salutary results would not favour parochial interests of the AFC!
"It would be pertinent for the Presidential Committee to be given a fresh brief to also investigate CBN's largesse of $7bn to 14 Nigerian banks as compensation for beefing up their capital bases, notwithstanding that it has become apparent lately that these acclaimed feats were made possible by the opportunity provided by the CBN for banks to lend their deposits to customers who invest the same funds in the same lending banks!
Some analysts have suggested that prompt refund of the $460m by the AFC to the Nigerian government is an indication that banks and other financial institutions which had participated in the scam were forced to urgently raise over N50bn which was then converted to dollars through BDCs and remitted to AFC account abroad; in "other words, more funds were further round-tripped to save the CBN Governor's bacon with adverse consequences on current stock market capitalisation!
"If the EFCC finds nothing wrong in all this, it would have done disserve to its image as a financial crimes fighter. Reports already confirm that one of the Nigerian banks involved in this scam had been indicted in the US for money laundering, and made to pay a fine of about US$15m."
SAVE THE NAIRA, SAVE NIGERIANS!

Original_Search_Title: Limits of Lies And Oni's Defining Moment
Headline: Nigeria: Limits of Lies And Oni's Defining Moment
Body_Text:
Lagos — Falsehood lives with man. Dines and wines with him. From his Genesis. Till now. Falsehood stands between man and truth, nay dignity. Man succumbs to falsehood whenever he has a need to hide. From truth. From dignity. Man embraces falsehood, most times to cover an evil deed. And for that exercise to last, he needs more dosage of falsehood to cover up the original falsehood. Between facts and fictions stands falsehood. But experience and history have shown that time is a natural, latent denudation of falsehood. Time will always degrade falsehood until it is finally crushed. It was only in times past that falsehood was allowed to live long, buried in the rubble of falsehood. But in contemporary days, the potency of falsehood has suddenly become diluted - perhaps with technology, which comes with civilisation and other factors.
Whether then or now, falsehood as we all know has a limited shelf life and whether or not you renovate falsehood with more falsehood, one thing is sure: falsehood will always wear out. Fictions will fade for facts to surface. In Ekiti State, South-west Nigeria, fictions are fading, giving way to facts. This was demonstrated Saturday April 4, 2009 when President Umaru Musa Yar' Adua, an unpretentious stickler for honesty and integrity described Mr. Segun Oni, the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) candidate in the governorship rerun, as a man who has a sense of accountability and responsibility, pointing out that Oni's achievements within the limited resources at his disposal in 20 months are more than the state had witnessed in 20 years.
That presidential declaration was Oni's defining moment. For the 21 months or so that Oni presided over the affairs of the state, Action Congress (AC), the main opposition party in the state vigorously drove its propaganda vehicle round the state and beyond in an attempt to rubbish the good works of the former governor. That the AC thrives on falsehood is no longer news even to the media which unsuspectingly had sympathy for the party believing many of its cooked-up stories until truth began to manifest. For instance, not a few journalists believed when the party addressed a world press conference in Lagos that it had video and satellite evidence of how the PDP rigged the April 14, 2007 election that brought in Oni as governor of the state. Throughout the protracted legal battle, AC did not present a single video evidence of the much-touted evidence.
That legal battle itself witnessed the most ridiculous display of falsehood when the AC gave its forensic expert, late Adrian Forty, a fake affidavit to present in court and urged the poor Briton to lie on oath that he was the author of the document. But to its chagrin, Forty not only denied authorship of the affidavit, he told a bewildered court that he was actually in London the day the said document was deposed to. Not satisfied, AC stealthily went and stuffed ballot boxes to be used as evidence in court with freshly thumb-printed ballot papers to deceive the court in what is now referred to in Ekiti as "ibo leyin ibo" or "voting after voting".
When that failed, the AC lawyers were later caught tampering with already tendered exhibits, including ballot papers while the court was on recess and none of the other parties in the case was present. Both still and motion pictures of the lawyers shown to the public are still in the archives. All these are on record. It is also on record that against all electoral rules; an AC bigwig went round various polling units in a convoy of not less than 12 loaded cars disrupting the voting exercise.
