That effort made an impact especially among those outside the state who had no opportunity to see what Oni had done to transform the state. Even some inhabitants of the state almost got carried along; more so as Oni failed to invest in propaganda like his immediate predecessor. Mr. Ayodele Fayose is on record as one man who covered his barbaric reign of terror with media razzmatazz. He would tar one kilometre of road and dance for 10 kilometres. His well funded in-house TV crew did a marvellous job of this make-believe that made his "Ekiti Transformation" programme on TV stations look so real that many thought he was actually doing a good job. Those who dared him can no longer tell the story.
Fayose's predecessor, Otunba Niyi Adebayo, was a man of peace. A jolly good fellow and scion of a former governor of the old Western region who left an indelible mark as the "owambe governor" of his time, in recognition of his proclivity for partying. Perhaps imbued by his father's feat, the younger Adebayo got so subsumed in merry-making that he forgot almost completely the actual business of governance. The memory of him today, going by public perception shaped by media reports of his government, is more eloquent in his "jaramba nights" held at the tousled Ikogosi warm spring where disco parties were thrown with religious commitment every fortnight. The good-looking Otunba Adebayo is a chieftain of the opposition AC.
As falsehood fades, Ekiti people now begin to spot the difference. Between light and darkness. Between a violent leader and a peaceful but lacklustre, discotizer vis-à-vis a performing governor, though a little reclusive and averse to personal aggrandizement.
Performance, as I have always posited, is like pregnancy. No matter how much you attempt to cover it, it will come out. And there's no half pregnancy anyway. You either have it or you don't. The achievements of Oni and appreciation of his stewardship reverberate in every community and ward where the rerun has been ordered in 10 local government areas in the state. The huge clan of real, genuine supporters as opposed to sponsored (expectedly fewer) crowd from Lagos and other places give one a sense of joy that Oni has not laboured in vain after all.
Everywhere he goes, Oni's campaign is at ease to point at his various achievements in that particular community. It is to Oni's eternal credit that he spent the entire period he held sway as governor to positively touch the lives of his people. A couple of hours after the President hopped into his car with an agility that made critics of his health condition gape in awe, I went to Okemesi-Ekiti, my birth place in Ekiti West Local Government, to share pounded yam with my party folks as usual and jaw-jaw with them. One of my compatriots, popularly known as "Eleso", meaning the "bearer of fruits", ran up to me on hearing I was in town. He said he was aware I was "struggling" with building a bungalow somewhere in the town and had suspended the project for pecuniary reasons. His position was that whatever the stage of the building, the town would be celebrating the victory party there. That was his message to me. Such is the level of expectation in all the 63 wards slated for rerun and indeed the rest of the state, including those areas AC believes it is popular.
When you look round the state, it is as if the veil has suddenly been lifted off the eyes of our people. That in itself speaks loads about the ephemeral nature of falsehood. Before and after he won the April 14, 2007 election, AC made futile attempts to discredit Oni. It first sent stories round town that Oni was not a registered member of the Council for Registered Engineers of Nigeria (COREN). That failed to gel. Next, they accused Oni's government barely two or three weeks after his inauguration of having borrowed N1 billion from an old generation bank and planning to borrow another N10 billion from a new generation bank. Facts on ground however revealed that from May 29, 2007 to February 17, 2009, no single kobo was borrowed. No bank was even approached for any form of facility on behalf of the state. From the day I reported for work in Ekiti till now, hardly has a single day passed without reacting to one false allegation or the other. The AC's reservoir of lies never runs dry, it seems.
Even at the Tribunal, AC displayed its penchant for dishonesty. The affidavit brought to the Tribunal in the name of Adrian Forty, its forensic expert, had a forged signature which Mr. Forty denied flatly before the Tribunal, insisting that he was actually in London on May 11, 2008 when the affidavit in question was said to have been signed by him, thereby exposing a fundamental irresponsibility in the AC's petition. This was in addition to the discovery in court that its first prosecution witness who claimed that he was disenfranchised was not a registered voter. It is difficult to list out the catalogue of AC's lies in one piece. But I'm glad that Ekiti people are now seeing through veil of falsehood. For nearly two years AC deployed its strength and wealth into discrediting Oni, giving the picture of an incompetent governor who has done nothing to impact on his people so as to pave the way for its candidate. Give it to them the AC made very good use of the media power at its disposal having bought hugely into the industry through the stupendous wealth of its leader, Senator Bola Ahmed Tinubu.
It is however sad that the print and electronic media established, acquired or funded by Tinubu seem to have been given the mandate to propagate falsehood which may put an irremovable stain on the career of those doing the hatchet job. Because of its posturing as progressives or democrats, some independent media houses also inadvertently got into that web. One incident confirmed this to me. A correspondent of Newswatch magazine who came to Ekiti to report on the outcome of the election petitions tribunal which we won escaped death by an air's breadth when he went to the AC Secretariat at Ajilosun in Ado-Ekiti. As soon as he was identified as a journalist, the poor man was beaten to a pulp by AC members who were furious over the judgment. He told me he was rescued from the jaws of death by another party man. While narrating his ordeal to me, the man said: "We (journalists) never knew these people were like this. I actually thought I was going to die when blows came landing on my head from all directions." He said he never knew that AC could encourage violence in anyway, let alone engineering one. But Ekiti people know better.
That the AC is founded on falsehood is no longer news in any part of Ekiti. Recently, the National Chairman of the party, Chief Bisi Akande, called a press conference and announced without recourse to any concrete evidence that the leadership of the PDP was planning to assassinate Tinubu, the financier of the party. When the Inspector-General of Police (IGP), Mr. Mike Okiro, invited Akande and other leaders of the AC to substantiate their claim and help the police in their investigation, none of them showed up. Such is the level of irresponsibility of the leadership of the AC. The new campaign manager of the AC, former governor Fayose, who took TheNews magazine to court for destroying his reputation, was described by the court recently as a pathological liar who has no reputation to defend in the first place. The court struck out the case. Fayose took the magazine to court before the present "contract" between him and Tinubu to campaign for Fayemi.
Tinubu, the managing director of the AC is a microcosm of the entire composition of the AC. Now that it is clear to all that Oni has in truth opened up the entire landscape of the state for development and almost every department of human life in the state has been positively affected what will be the next story line of the AC and its horde of dishonest leaders? Now that the veil appears removed from the eyes of Ekiti people about Tinubu's plan to make Ekiti a branch of his economic empire to complement the Lagos headquarters, what happens? Now that Ekiti people have come to grips with the fact that most Nigerian politicians don't invest their money where they are not sure of returns, how will they open their eyes and surrender their state and sentence themselves into perpetual slavery?
These are some of the facts that have come out bare to all discerning Ekiti people after a long period of carefully guarded falsehood giving weight to the age-long belief of the Yoruba that if falsehood travels for 20 years, it takes just one day for truth to catch up with it. Now that the proverbial "ogbigbo" bird has been encircled on its tree of falsehood, let's see how it will fly. By the time, Oni lifts his voice to God in victory song after the rerun election, it would be clear to the opposition that falsehood, though sometimes sells, has a very short expiry date.
And, who knows, a universal review of the unprecedented appeal court ruling of February 17 which ordered a governor out of office on account of the colour of pen used in the accreditation of voters rather than complicity in the allegation of violence and rigging for which the suit itself was instituted, will then come under scrutiny.
-Jolayemi is spokesman to Oni.

Original_Search_Title: A Journey Through Ikare-Ekiti With Niyi Osundare
Headline: Nigeria: A Journey Through Ikare-Ekiti With Niyi Osundare
Body_Text:
Continued from last week
I was eager to go to Ikare-Ekiti, to dialogue with the rocks. Already I had begun to see the rocks in their rolling essence as they stood protecting Akure from the overarching bravado of Ekitiland. We took off at about 10 am the next day.
Having driven for about twenty minutes, we arrived at Amoye Grammar School, Ikare-Ekiti. As I stepped into the school compound, the rocks rose and welcomed me. It was the brotherhood of the pyramid-shaped Oroole, its imposing stare on the school (See Osundare's poem, "The Rocks Rose to Meet Me"). This was where our dialogue began. A lively, bouncy Osundare took me round the school, showing his former classrooms, his dormitory and bed space, the fields, the house master's apartment, and the office of the very revered principal, the late Chief Sammie Fal Adeniran.
"Chief Adeniran was a great and dedicated principal, a rare disciplinarian. He moulded us into what we are today. He never spared any act of indiscipline. He taught me Latin and I performed very well." Osundare remembered that when it was time for light out in those days, he had a contraption in which he hid his lantern and read in order to satisfy his insatiable quest. It was a terrible offence to be seen with light during the light out. But he thought he outsmarted his tough principal because he was never caught and punished. However, many years later, Chief Adeniran told him: "I used to see you disobeying the school rules by reading when you should be sleeping." Astonished, Osundare replied, "But how come you never punished me?" Chief Adeniran, now old, chuckled and said, "Though you were disobeying the rules, I was proud of you." Given his love for books, Osundare ended up as a library prefect, carting away most of the prizes in his class, earning distinctions in all but two of his eight subjects. His scores in three of his subjects were reported to be among the best in the whole of West Africa.
We drove out of Amoye Grammar School, and went to St. Luke's Primary School, where a tender Osundare started school in 1953. A huge, ancient block stood, fenceless, no less a rugged metaphor of the annoying decay in Nigeria's educational sector. Beside it to the right, a block in which Osundare had his primary five, had collapsed, the fallen blocks in utter submission to abandonment. What Osundare remembered about St. Luke's Primary School was his primary five teacher, Mr Bezi, who was a marvellous embodiment of knowledge, love and compassion; and his participation in the school band as the leading drummer, proving himself the son of his father. He also sang and acted in plays, drawing the admiration of parents who usually attended the speech and prize giving days. How did he feel seeing his primary school in this bad state? He became sober, saying: "Our government has killed education. I wish I had money to renovate the school."
We moved to Osundare's family house, a storey building that had seen better days. "When my father built this house, storey buildings were rare and fashionable here," Osundare explained. Osundare's room and his father's were upstairs, and his mother's was downstairs. Now none of the Osundares lives in the house; it is rented out to tenants. In front of the house was a waterway, a stream flowing into river Osun after which the poet Osundare was named.
I was interested in seeing River Osun, the giver of "life" to the poet Osundare. (Really, the meaning of Osundare is "Osun has vindicated me"). Sadly, it was dry season and I could only behold the lean flesh of the great river which enjoyed worship from the community. "When I was growing up, my parents did not allow me go near the river; they feared that Osun would take me away from them. That's why I can't swim, and would have lost my life to Katrina. But my mother tells me that if not for Osun I wouldn't have escaped Katrina."
We detoured to the palace of the Ogoga, the Oba of Ikare. I noticed some vandalised houses by the streets. We had been told in Akure the day before that in Ikare-Ekiti the youths had gone on rampage, demanding the head of the traditional ruler and other prominent people who acceded to what the youths thought was a clever way of denying Ikare-Ekiti its robust College of Education. Governor Segun Oni had established a state university of education to replace the college of education and some sons of Ikare-Ekiti did not foresee the ruse therein. The college of education, seething with students in thousands and offering Bachelor degrees in some courses, had far better economic, social and political benefits than any new specialised university that might not have more than one hundred students.
That was why we met a deserted Oba's palace, with gun-totting policemen scowling at us at the gate. They had order not to let anybody in. But from where we stood, Osundare pointed at the seat of the Oba, the statutes around him, and a room upstairs where Ulli Beier, the German scholar and catalyst of modern written Nigerian literature, used to stay whenever he visited Oba Adegoriola, who was one of his important friends. Behind the palace was Okeruku, the place of the red earth (read Niyi Osundare's "Meet Me at Okeruku"). The earth and the houses here were really red, distinguishing the neighbourhood from any other in Ikare-Ekiti.
Thereafter, we drove to Ado-Ekiti, just twenty minutes from Ikare-Ekiti. It is the capital city of Ekiti State, housing Christ's School, in whose abode Osundare wrote his first award-wining poem. Although Amoye Grammar School remains Osundare's favourite, it was Christ's School that gave him the opportunity to discover himself as a poet, a dramatist, and a teacher. He came to the school from a "bush school" (in spite of the intellectual pundits of Amoye Grammar School), but had a result better than anybody else's. He would revive the school magazine to the admiration of his teachers and fellow students. He would act lead roles in the open air theatre modelled on the traditional Greek theatre. He would take part in the writing of the history of the school, a job given to him because of the tremendous writing skills he had displayed. He would be given his first job, which was to teach in the school. And, expectedly, the college would give him a scholarship to study English at the University of Ibadan. Were there other scholarships apart from the one offered by Christ's School? "Oh yes. Amoye Grammar School insisted they would sponsor me, but Christ's School's offer came first. I wrote a carefully worded letter to Amoye. Although the late Chief Adeniran was disappointed, he understood. Also, I got a federal government scholarship which I turned down, even though my friends urged me to take it. I thought it was unfair to take two scholarships at the same time."
After taking a lunch of pounded yam in Ado-Ekiti, we drove back to Ikare-Ekiti. We went to St Joseph's primary school where a burial ceremony was taking place. We were to meet his childhood friend, Femi Ogunmola, and many other friends who had not seen Osundare for years. I watched the poet prancing around with friends and well-wishers who liked him because of his intelligence and fame, but were not happy that he did not have a house in Ikare-Ekiti. Why didn't the poet have a house in his homeland? "I don't have the money. And even if I do, I cannot manage two houses at the moment. There are other things to do with money." But it is a cultural demand. I provoked the Marxist in him. "Some of our cultural demands turn people into criminals. We have to do away with them while we stick to the ones that point the way forward."
Although he does not have a house in Ikare-Ekiti, I observed the overwhelming respect he commanded among his people. He has instituted a prize at Amoye Grammar School (Osundare Prize for the Best Student in Yoruba and Osundare Prize for the Best Student in Science), and has continued to render assistance to the winners of the prize when they need it. His kinsmen have known him to be consistent in his crusade for service to humanity and social justice, even if they do not like its complexion.
As we drove out of Ikare-Ekiti, the sprawling town crossed by River Osun and bounded by incredible rocks, I suddenly desired to read all of Osundare's volumes again, for I could hear the rhythm of his poetry in every corner of the town. I asked myself if there was any poet in Nigeria who had poetised and popularised his homeland like Osundare. Were his volumes of poetry not bigger than any mansion he had failed to build in Ikare-Ekiti? No mansions could have made Ikare-Ekiti as famous as Osundare's poetry.
Concluded
E. E. Sule, PhD, is a lecturer in Nasarawa State University, Keffi, Nasarawa State.

Original_Search_Title: Oh Eritrea Referee. Oh!
Headline: Nigeria/Mozambique: Oh Eritrea Referee. Oh!
Body_Text:
I am sure you remember Sidat Fezal.
Yes you do.
He is the boyish looking President of the Mozambican Football Federation and member of the Youth Development Committee of CAF.
In Algeria for the 8th CAF U-17 Championship, Fezal told me (and I told you) that a draw against the Super Eagles would be fantastic.
He said how the nation was agog with expectations, looking forward to "one of the best football teams in the world"
Fezal excused himself from the U-17 Championship to go and "prepare" for the visit of the Super Eagles.
When Fezal did not come back to Algeria, I thought I should put a call through to him to "congratulate" him for what has been hailed as a
"fantastic result".
"Fezal, its Paul"
"Hi Paul ... oh, that Eritrean referee oh. That referee killed us"
I said to him that I was calling to hail him for a result he never even expected.
"Oh that referee. Paul did you watch the match?" I said I did not.
He said I should. That I should take a copy of the DVD from officials of the NFF and watch.
"Ask for it, watch it and call me back for your honest opinion. That referee killed us. I do not know what we did to him. I do not know whether we had offended him in the past. He was out for us.
"That old man from Eritrea, that former referee is he still around?"
He was referring to Tesfaye Gebreyesus, the legendary African referee who was in Algeria as a referee's inspector and member of the CAF's referee committee.
"Paul, I need to speak to him to find out whether his countryman had anything against us"
I told him that a lot of Nigerians especially the media also believe that but for the referee, you would have scored a goal.
"A goal? Paul, we scored two goals and they were denied. What about the penalty?"
Which penalty? I asked
You need to watch the match. My player was one on one ready to score when he was brought down. Instead of a red card and the subsequent penalty, only a yellow card and a free kick was awarded.
Is that the rule? Tell me, you are versed in football matters, why did he issue a yellow card if no infringement was committed?"
I confessed that since I have not watched the match, I could not sincerely appreciate his argument.
"We will have to discuss again, after you had watched the match. I do not know why the referee decided to overprotect Nigeria"
From his own assessment, did Nigeria play well? How come it was easy to get a draw and here he was, talking of a probable victory?
"It was a big surprise. We expected more from Nigeria that is why the boys prepared very well.
"They trained hard and were ready to offer their best.
"Do not forget that this is Nigeria that qualified with maximum points from six matches played, one of the best teams not only in Africa, but in the world.
"We knew it was not going to be easy , but we were determined to go down fighting if need be and that is exactly what the boys did.
"Did you read Mustapha's (General Secretary of CAF) reactions in the internet? He said Mozambique surprised him, that we played very well and were unlucky not to win"
Did Nigeria play well?
"Did I see Nigeria? We were interested in ourselves and how we were playing. The boys did us proud. You talked about your media. Also here, we are still angry about the way the referee treated us. People are still talking about it and praising the boys for a job well done"
Did the artificial grass contribute to Nigeria's poor showing. I asked further.
"Maybe? Maybe? I do not know. Is that what they are saying? In Africa artificial turfs are the best you can get and very soon, thanks to FIFA, nearly all African countries will benefit the all purpose surface that is a million times better than the bare surfaces that dot the continent."
Now that the dreaded Nigerian hurdle has been surmounted does Mozambique see herself as a potential world cup qualifier?
"it is still a long way to go. But we believe. In fact the game against Nigeria made us realize that nothing is impossible in football.
"The decider will be the match against Tunisia. That match will throw everything open. With a victory over Kenya at away Tunisia fancy their chances and will want to murder us to remain on top.
"The result of that match will determine a lot.
"We will be at away, the crowd, the weather, everything. After that match, we will know where we stand. But don't forget that against Nigeria the boys got a good lesson in confidence and this, should propel them on"
I have added a voice from abroad to the incessant cries back home of Nigerians who are still bemoaning the loss of two points in Maputo, two points that may just be the difference between Angola and South Africa, next year.
I have said elsewhere that the best of teams do lose. That an opposition could just step up its game plan and drown you, no matter how hard you try.
What Nigerians are saying is that we did not just try at all, that the boys did not do enough to merit victory in Maputo.
Since that match, the Nigeria Football Federation and Amodu have not known peace.
The media is awash with calls for the sack of the technical crew, the re_appraisal of tactics and re_orientation of players.
It has gotten to a point where nearly all the stakeholders are panicking and when you panic, you make mistakes.
Late last week, I read that Amodu and the NFF leadership were bundled before President Yar Adua where among others it was decided to up incentives and allowances. Here we go again.
"That we must up allowances for professional players? That players like Mikel Obi, Joseph Yobo and Martins Obafemi must be offered more money for them to qualify themselves and their country for the World Cup?.
I use to joke about the fact that even if I am offered $200,000 (two hundred thousand dollars) I cannot make the 100 metres in 15 seconds! No, I cannot.
The players we have presently have their limitations and to ask them to do more is like trying to put spike shoes on tortoise legs.
A lot of us believe that what we saw of our players are the best they have to offer and unfortunately their best is just not enough.
During the European Championship League last week a documentary on African stars in the UEFA competition was highlighted and I was thrilled seeing J.J Okocha, Finidi George, Emmanuel Amuneke, Nwankwo Kanu..... our glorious past.
Add to that the current issue of France Football where the salaries of world stars are highlighted. Unlike in the past, no Nigerian football star is worth contending with. Can you say the same of Didier Drogba? Eto 'O Fils.
Cote D'Ivoire are to be feared. Apart from Drogba and the Toure brothers in Arsenal and Barcelona, you have Kone in Marseille, Kaita in Lyon, all earning millions of Euros and this translates positively on the field of play.
I do not know whether, we have lost the chance to effectively rebuild out team after the Ghana Nations Cup disaster. All I know is that Amodu and is crew did a Yeoman's job to get us to this level.
Fortunately, Maputo has proven that our best is not just enough, not yet, so we must dig dip, swallow pride, re_evaluate and re_orientate our players, inject fresh blood and stop panicking.
Rather than contend with qualification we were to plot a better outing at the Mundial
One way of doing that is to keep doctors, carpenters and lawmakers out of it. It is high time football people are allowed to provide the direction that this country desperately needs, on and off the field.
It was at the Beirut Airport, Lebanon that I ran into Ben Murray Bruce and one of the things he said sticks "I do not do the things I do not know. I believe a lot of people who are running football in our country do not know much about the game."
He can say that again.
POUNDED YAM FOR BREAKFAST
Happy to be back.
No place like home, etc etc
One of those who welcomed me back was Onochie Anibeze. He ended by asking whether I had taken my meal of pounded Yam and egusi soup as promised.
I answered "He no gree me swallow"
He laughed back saying "Ogbono should have been better. I wish it was Aso Rock Ogbono, the type common in OBJ era"
See you next week.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Soar By 20 Percent - Survey
Headline: Nigeria: Food Prices Soar By 20 Percent - Survey
Body_Text:
Prices of food staples have soared amidst Easter celebration by the Christians, Daily Trust survey has shown.
The market survey conducted in Abuja, the Federal Capital Territory, neighboring Nasarawa and Niger States revealed that prices of food staples and other condiments have increased by an almost 20% since the last quarter of 2008.
Economists have painted a gloomy picture for the country's economy in 2009.
At the popular Wuse market in Abuja, a bag of caprice rice which used to sell at N8,200 few months before the Easter has risen to an all time high price of N9,300, while a bag of Stallion rice now goes for N8,600 as against the initial price of N8,000.
A bag of local rice which was N7,300 has now risen to N7,700, while a cartoon of Spaghetti now maintained a price of N1,900 from the previous price of N1,800.
Another family delight during periods of festivities, "beans" has equally skyrocketed to N18,000 from its previous price of N15,000, an almost 10% rise which buyers have to contend with.
A bag of semovita has also increased to N1,400 from the initial price of N1,300; and a bag of red Garri which was N7,500 initially has risen to N7,900.
Speaking on the reason for the increase in the prices of food stuff, Malam Sani Husseini who is a grain dealer said that the climb could be attributed to the short supply of dollar which has consequently caused an increase in cost of importation.
"The increase is not sudden. It has been like this for the last 6 months since the country started experiencing the negative effect of the meltdown. Importers have increased their prices in order to break even. We have to increase our own price too", he added.
Another trader, Malam Aliyu Mohammed attributed the increase to the Easter celebration which often comes with increase in virtually all spheres of life.
"The Easter is coinciding with the recent rise in cost of transportation due to the artificial fuel scarcity experienced. We have no choice other than to increase our prices too", said Mr. Emeka, another trader in Wuse market.
However, some food items have dropped in price despite the mad rush for profit maximization by sellers.
A medium sized basket of tomatoes which was sold at N900 during the last two festivities of Sallah and Christmas has now dropped to N600, while a basket of onion now goes for N500 as against N700.
A kilo of beef which was N700 before has reduced to N600, while a small sized stocked fish now goes for N800 from the previous price of N1, 000.
Malam Nura Aliyu, a tomato seller said that the reduction is as a result of the raining season, adding, "cost of perishable goods tend to be low during the raining season".
Investigations by Daily Trust in the popular Maraba food market in Nasarawa state revealed a rise in prices of food items too.
A small bowl of beans which was N150 before the Easter celebration has increased to N250, while a "mudu" of sugar now sells for N200 as against N220.
The story is the same in Niger State as well with prices of staples and other condiments.
Investigation carried out by Daily Trust across major markets in Lagos weekend revealed that prices of food stuffs have been jerked up slightly compared to prices before the Easter festival.
Though some of the food stuffs like semovita, pepper and yam maintained their pre-festive prices, others had their prices increased for about five per cent increment.
Our correspondent gathered from Ikotun, Egbeda and Iyana-Ipaja markets that price of bag of rice which before this period used to go for between N8000 and N8500 is now sold for between N8500 and N9000 depends on the type.

Original_Search_Title: We Have Helped Over 1000 Militants- Rev. Humphrey Nsirim
Headline: Nigeria: We Have Helped Over 1000 Militants- Rev. Humphrey Nsirim
Body_Text:
Lagos — NO fewer than 30,000 youths have died because of hostilities in the Niger Delta since 2003.This is a statistic that came from a religious and non_governmental organisation. The end to these deaths is not near because gangsterism of all sorts is still alive in the land.
When the Inspector General of Police visited Rivers State last week to commission some projects and receive some vehicles for the police, he said 128 people have so far been kidnapped in the state since the vice commenced. But four days after his departure, two kidnappings took place, including a Briton whose police escort was shot dead in Port Harcourt that brought the figure to 130 kidnappings in less than three years. Many efforts have been injected into the system to ease the vice.
These include the setting up of rehabilitation centres by the government and individuals. Achievements have been made here and there but reforming these militants is like demobilising soldiers who have just emerged from the theatre of war. It takes a lot of tactics, strategy, pet talks and promises. Sometimes these promises to the disarmed must be total forgiveness for sins or offence committed while armed.
This brings to the fore the amnesty promise recently muttered by President Umaru YarÁdua. Pundits are of the view that only an amnesty for the misled youths would end the restiveness and a faith_based group which has talking the militias out of the trenches and giving their weapons to the police and security agencies has its main objective has lend its voice to the call for amnesty.
The leader of the group Rev. Humphrey Nsirim, the CEO of Hope for the Hopeless Global Initiative, an NGO that is basically concerned with affecting the lives of the hopeless persons, communities and bringing hope to them spoke to Vanguard Bureau Chief of Port Harcourt George Onah.
Who are these hopeless people, are they those who do not have parents, financial means, the homeless or who specifically.
We are concerned with those who are hopeless as it relates to their present situation. Specifically, those who are hopeless because of the environment they came from, some through poor upbringing and have gone into militancy and do not have options towards bettering their lives. These are people who have embraced restiveness, gangersterism, kidnappings and all manner of vices.
There are communities that are also hopeless because of the situation they find themselves, they cannot help themselves. So, as an NGO we embark on hope talks and in the end try to bring them to realise where they are and where they should be for the betterment of themselves and society.
We try to mediate where communities are at war or persons engaged in prolonged fighting and not hope of ending such fights, we intervene in such hopeless situations. Again, for those who are in prisons and do not have anyone to talk for them, we advocate for them and pray that we would be able to give them hope. Even ex_prisoners who do not have place to stay, we have always trust in God to provide where they can stay and get rehabilitated and return to the society again.
How long have you been in this
We started in 2003, without really understanding the situation in Rivers State and the Niger Delta generally. We felt like going to evangelise to them, because we are faith based and in the course of preaching to them we ran into people who were carrying arms. Initially, they objected to our preaching to them but we insisted and they strongly rejected our insistence only for them to agree later on for us to preach to them.
That was where the passion to reach out to those kinds of persons started. Actually, it started in skeletal form, where we would go from place to place searching and reaching out to them. And so, up until 2006, some of them began to come out in trickles.
At that point, we noticed that we had problems on our hands because they were now coming out in their number and we did not have the means to sustain them. So, in 2007, we saw the need to partner with people and we began to reach out to people and requesting for assistance to cope. We were not even registered but in 2008 we got registered with the Federal Government Cooperate Affairs.
We were then privileged to run into the Rivers State Sustainable Development Agency, RSSDA. We later discovered militants can be rehabilitated
that it was a government agency and when we got talking, they got interested and they now provide finance and started sponsoring what we are doing in a micro way, because what we are doing is quite voluminous.
In fact the bulk of the support we have got bothers on the support of the RSSDA. But there again, there were individual donations from people who were convinced about what we were doing and they were giving us one or two or ten naira here and there. Sometimes they bring bag of rice, some beans, yams and so on but from time to time it is not always there.
When you started you said the militants or gunmen, as it were, would not listen to you preach to them. Were you going to the creeks, forests, hideouts or swamps to preach to them.
At first we were going to the waterfronts here, within the town.
Which waterfront
Elechi waterfront. Normally they would come out from the creeks. Actually, there is no difference between the creek and street boys. You see, this gun_carrying thing dates back to 2003 elections, when guns were freely used in the state.
However, the passion just kept rising as we kept meeting these people and so the creek thing started along the line. So as we got talking, they started seeing that we meant well for them and they went back and told their colleagues there that they needed to meet with us and as God would have it, a whole lot of them started coming out. That was how we began to get into the bush and that was how we began to get into the area where they were.
What about their arms
Of course the arm where being turned_in in trickles, but where the challenge was, was having returned the arms, what happens to us.
What about accommodation, feeding and clothing after getting them to drop their weapons.
I did mention RSSDA, the support they gave helped us in renting a place which could accommodate about 50 youths. But where the challenge actually is, is in the feeding of the youths. I did not understand what it was going to be until I started feeding them. Sometimes, you plan that you are going to use two million naira for one month but you see that you are expending four million naira for one month. You must feed them, if you don't give them food that is another problem.
The next aspect is sustainable projects, which is what we are getting into now and we do not want to depend on grants and donations anymore. We wish to make them acquire skills and move them out so that others can come into the camp and we keep doing that, which is our plan.
Where do you train them to acquire this skill
We have a place at Igwruta, which is our centre. We also send to a private enterprise, which offered to train the boys free of charge in pipeline work. We also hired and paid a man who trains them in aluminium and glass work. We also want to open a leather workshop where they can do shoes bags and a few other things.
How many of these "hopeless" have you actually rehabilitated since you got into contact with them.
This rehabilitation is categorised. If it is to make them withdraw from restiveness, drugs and the trenches then we have helped over 1000. However, if it is to help build their capacity to make them regain themselves and probably, stand on their own, we could say between 20 and 25. This cuts across communities.
Right now we are in Rumuolumeni Community, Gbor, Gbor Danya, to stem the spate of kidnap, fighting and so on, and the youths have accepted us. Our programme goes from rescue, redirect, rehabilitate and released as ambassadors of transformation of peace.
It is a five_fold project. We rescue them first that is to bring them out, talk to them and then redirect, which is our reorientation programme, that is making them feel where they are going to go and how they want to live, make them acquire skill which is part of rehabilitation, before we now get into the release.
I also want to emphasise that even after some of them have left they still come back for money and other things because having told them to drop the guns, which was their source of livelihood, they come to ask for assistance here and there.
What about clothing, bedding, shoes and others, apart from feeding.
What we do is, if I know the ones that can wear what I wear, of course I make such clothes available to them, I have talked to some people and they are beginning to respond in that area by providing clothing because it is a little difficult to clothe a thousand people on your own without assistance. We encourage them to do menial jobs such as going to building sites to earn some money to enable them change their clothes so that they don't appear too rugged for the society to behold.
In this respect, RSSDA gave us eight million naira but we have spent close to thirty million naira. Rumuekpe matter alone took about five million naira plus. At Rumuekpe, it was not just clothing them, we had to provide relief materials such as food, bedding and so on. These people have been devastated for over five years or thereabout. There are oil companies in that community but they have refused to respond.
Is this your organisation, is it limited to the youths or it extends to elders as well
No, it is not limited to the youthsWe accommodate people just released from prison and these are not youths, these are people in their 40s and 50s. For this sort of people we take them for a while, just a redirection programme. When we redirect them, we set them free so that they do not stay for too long to attract being traumatised. We also talk to commercial sex workers but we don't bring them to our place because we cannot put such class of people with male youths together. It would be disastrous. We also have a team who specialise in children.
When you talk to these youths in the creeks, do you try to get to inside of their minds to find out what made them get to where you found them
There are two classes of them, one, has to do with the struggle for development, not just developing the land but human development.
This whole problem about what we are talking about borders on ignorance and illiteracy. The literate ones know what they want and what they are looking for.
The picture I paint here is this, the privilege ones who discover a problem of lack of development and they begin to fight to gain dominion in an enclave. And in fighting that, they came to a point where this privileged ones begin to fight among themselves in the bid to see who would rule, leaving the less privileged and the poor. But when the fight gets to the point between the privileged and literate gets tough and because they don't want to die, they now initiate the poor who have been living nomadic life.
When you introduce nomads into a struggle for affluence it becomes disastrous because they approach it with nomadic thinking. Again when you introduce arms into it and these nomads are armed, it is what them gives rise to militancy.
Looking at it holistically, you see that one group is fighting for their stomach, so they fight to die while others are fighting for affluence so they fight to live, which is what has given birth go kidnappings and so on. These people who fought for the privileged are later abandoned but the fighters on their own now say, since we have been abandoned, we have to fight for our survival.
Do you actually have people fighting for the land, just as Isaac Adaka Boro did, as things have turned out.
There are still some genuine fighters for the resource control of the land but they have been dominated by the selfish ones. There are quite some who are not fighting for money but fighting for development. This is just like what we are doing, if we were looking or fighting for money, we would have gotten the money.
We have results in what we are doing and the boys have given us their words that if only amnesty is granted, we would drop our guns, give us freedom (amnesty) and we will stop this thing (militancy). I must inform you that some people have come to meet with us asking us to go for money from the government but I tell them I can't because I am a pastor, how do I account for it. Integrity is involved in this thing and that is why many miss it.
How many arms would you say you have helped the government or society to recover from these boys.
Over twenty arms and several ammunition and many are still there but why we don't want to push too much because we are already having problem taking care of the ones that have come out. They tell us we are ready to drop the gun but how do we cope with them when they come out. But if there was a platform where those who have dropped their arms , something good has happened to them, we are sure that they would turn_in both local and foreign made guns.
There is this organisation backed by government, which is led by Chief A.K. Horsefall, why have you not approached that outfit for assistance
Well, we have talked and they have perspective and have a limit which they are working and this limit, I respect it. They are working based on instructions and they have goals which are different from our goals. We believe in what they are doing and I give assistance where I can and I want to continue with what I am doing.
Look at the mind of these boys in the bush, what do you perceive of their minds, are they tired of fighting, are they willing to continue, or are saying, if I drop my gun now what will the government offer me, how do you rate the thinking of these boys right now
They are 80 percent tired but devastated and thinking power is broken by reason of disposition, the faculty has been overtaken by life's travails and events. So, 80 percent ready to give up and 20 percent ready to die just in case it happens, this is the gap where you can now make then realise what has happened.
The statistics we have is that over 30,000 of our youths have died since this whole thing started in 2003 and most of those who died are from the riverine. This creates the impression that the Niger Delta does not have a future.

Original_Search_Title: 19 Perish in Road Accident
Headline: Nigeria: 19 Perish in Road Accident
Body_Text:
Lagos — No fewer than 19 people were feared dead yesterday after a ghastly motor accident along Makurdi - Aliade road. Eighteen of the victims were said to be members of a catholic organization, SS Peter and Paul, Makurdi.
The members of the catholic organisation, according to THISDAY Checks, were going to Otukpo in Benue South Senatorial District to attend a thanks-giving service.
A reliable source who pleaded anonymity, said "I saw the bus in which the victims were travelling, coming out of a filling station when suddenly a trailer carrying petroleum product, just rammed into them very close to the office of the Federal Road Safety Commission, Makurdi.
"The sight was so bad to look at as mangled bodies of the victims could be seen scattered all over even as officials of the FRSC could be seen attending to them, infact, none of the occupants of the bus survived because they all died including the driver', he said.
The occupants of the vehicle who are all members of the said catholic organisation were of Idoma extraction.
The husband of one of the victims simply called Mr. Abah, whose wife was a caterer and yam merchant with tears rolling down his cheeks said, "Oh! God, I'm now totally in ruin with my family, my wife is dead and what am I going to do now particularly with the children without their mother by my side to assist?"
Abah's compound was filled with sympathisers because his late wife, Rose, was a strong member of the SS Peter and Paul.
As at the time of filing this report, various districts of the Idoma community residing in Makurdi, could be seen making arrangements to convey their late members' remains home.
The remains of the victims were deposited at the Federal Medical Centre, Makurdi even as efforts to get the comment of the police spokesman in the state to comment on the accident proved abortive as his telephone lines and that of the Commissioner of Police, Alhaji Ibrahim Mohammed could not be reached.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Meltdown - Oyo Moves to Boost Tourism
Headline: Nigeria: Economic Meltdown - Oyo Moves to Boost Tourism
Body_Text:
Ibadan — Oyo State government has unveiled a blueprint to boost its Internally Generated Revenue (IGR) through tourism development to mitigate impact of global crisis and dwindling resources.
The Board has concluded arrangements to stage its first-ever Oyo Festival aimed at celebrating the rich culture of the state.
Chairman of the agency, Otunba Adebayo Raji told journalists in Ibadan the state capital over the weekend that the planned Oyo Festival, which is expected to hold in May this year, will attract tourists from within and outside the country adding that a lot of foreign exchange is would be generated from the project.
"Oyo Festival is a collection of fun-packed events determined to entertain, educate, inform and create a lasting positive image of a state filled with opportunities, tourist attractions and investment opportunities. The festival is a one-week non-stop event laced with competitions, youth initiatives and trade opportunities. It is an annual event."
Otunba Raji maintained that Governor Adebayo Alao-Akala is very serious about tourism development "which the present administration sees as the alternative source of revenue generation presently."
He reeled out the various tourist sites in Oyo State as including the Bower Tower, Agodi Gardens, Ado-Awaye Suspended Lake, Osoogun town which is Bishop Ajayi Crowther's place of birth, the Royal Forest "Igbo Oba" in Igboho; Old Oyo National Park; the mythical Agbele Rock which depicts the picture of a woman carrying a load of dried sliced yams and a baby strapped at her back as well as the over 350 years old wonderful Tortoise "Alagba" domiciled within the Palace of Soun of Ogbomoso.
Most of these tourist sites, Otunba Raji explained, have been given facelift by the present government in the State.
While maintaining that the present administration is very serious over the development of tourism, the Board chairman posited that the on-going massive investment in road network rehabilitation and reconstruction attest to this. saying it was aimed at cushioning the hardship of tourists.
His words: "Akala administration is challenged and working tirelessly to rehabilitate all major roads in all the zones of the State including Oke Ogun area to cushion the hardship of tourists all over the state."
Otunba Raji further disclosed that the State Tourism Board is exploring the possibilities of partnering with some banks and entrepreneurs in the development of the various tourist sites in the state to facilitate efforts at showcasing the rich culture of the state and tap the hidden arts and treasures.
Meanwhile, the tourism board has already produced a comprehensive tourist guide with information about the state tourism potentialities, the chairman stated while calling on all hotels, travelling agencies, tour operators and event centres in the state to register with the National Tourism Development Council (NTDC) and take advantage of the facilities provided by the board.

Original_Search_Title: Sapele PDP Donates to Charity
Headline: Nigeria: Sapele PDP Donates to Charity
Body_Text:
SAPELE-PEOPLE's Democratic Party (PDP) in Sapele Local Government Area of Delta State has donated food items worth over N100, 000 to an orphanage home, Pro-Labore-dei (PLD), a Non Governmental Organization (NGO) and Obrigbo Trado-medical Hospital, all in Sapele, for the upkeep of the less privileged ones in the centres.
Speaking at the orphanage home, leader of the party in the area, Oghenekevwe, said it was the custom of the party to visit orphanages in Sapele.
He said: "It is the culture of the party in Sapele to visit homes or centres like this to present gift items to our loved ones who are living here, because this period is a period of lent, which is being observed by all Christians. This is the right time to show love to these loved ones here."
Comrade Oghenekevwe assured the manager of the home, Madam Buwa, of the party's support at all times and asked her to feel free to call on the party wherever the home needed support in any way.
Responding, Madam Buwa thanked the PDP faithful for their charity work and promised that the bags of rice, tubers of yam and other items would be used carefully.
At the PLD centre, Comrade Oghenekevwe noted that "it is always good to show love to these loved ones who don't have source of their own to feed. God loves a cheerful giver because these loved ones here are also created by the same God who created us."
He also assured the PLD members of the party's constant visit and encouragement to continue to do God's work properly.
Responding, Brother Blessing thanked the PDP chairman and his members for their unexpected visit and appealed for more support from Sapele PDP.
Also at the Obrigbo Tradomedical Hospital, Comrade Oghenekevwe assured them of the party's total support at all times by bringing more gifts to alleviate their sufferings.
Dr. Joseph Obrigbo, in his comment, thanked the Sapele PDP members for their kind gesture and also appealed for more assistance from them.

Original_Search_Title: Rivers State - Where are the Men of Honour?
Headline: Nigeria: Rivers State - Where are the Men of Honour?
Body_Text:
Dr Peter Odili may be one person who is not surprised to see politics of antagonism and hatred elevated to the status of statecraft. He knew this from the pressure that always mounted on him to allow vengeance to reign.
This was always so each time he won back a lost ship, an opponent who came back to the mainstream. Even some of his closest followers usually murmured at his back, accusing him of being too easy to forgive. They would wring their fingers and say all manner of things they would have done to extract a pound of flesh to political recants.
Though these followers disliked the magnanimity of their leader, the world did not fail to salute Odili's large hearts and the spirit to forgive. This could be why most of those who eventually played prominent roles in the later days of his administration were kingpins in the opposition. Odili knew how to win an opponent into his fold. Even in the heat of fierce political combat, most of the opposition generals were on direct lines with the then governor.
What was significant then was the use of royal fathers and other prominent leaders to reach out and build consensus. Often, it was either that Odili was resorting to the natural rulers to create bridges of peace and consensus with his opponents or the opponents were stretching to reach him through these monarchs.
This helped very much in building Rivers Consensus that took the nation by storm, such that the late elder and south-south leader, A.K Dikibo, confessed that the Rivers caucus had become so powerful that any proceeding taken behind them at the national ruling caucus would be repeated. "If a major decision is taken without a hero, it would be revisited".
It was also this attribute that made it easy to resolve most matters and conflicts. The Monarchs and spiritual leaders in the state were so accorded a place of reverence that they often prevailed on him to revisit issues or reconcile with some notable personalities from time to time.
Now, it appears that the consensus platform has disintegrated. Else, how could any outsider stand in the centre of the special banquet hall built by Odili to rubbish and insult him and the collective intelligence of the Rivers people and the people clapped? How would the people sit and applaud when the revered stool of the ancient Bonny Kingdom was brought to public ridicule and the people sat and clapped? How would a man who served successfully for eight years as deputy governor clap hands for children who took pleasure in attempting to humiliate an administration they all laboured to serve?
What did Odili do to Governor Chibuike Amaechi that is so grievous and beyond the elders of the state to resolve? What is on record is that Amaechi came into Odili's life a little fellow. He came with no political or social clout, and so, everything he is worth today came from being associated with Dr Odili. Sure, this is a line of argument Amaechi's new friends hate to hear, but is true.
By the time Odili first entered into office as deputy governor, he brought along his boy and made him available to serve Rufus Ada-George as a personal assistant. When Odili now became governor in 1999, Amaechi was nothing, having lost election to be a law maker. Amaechi went to the tribunal and lost. Was it not the same Odili that wheeled the post of Speaker to Ikwerre and to his constituency, an action that made the elders to think of supporting Amaechi to eventual victory at the Appeal Court?
Did Odili not give him a conducive atmosphere to reign in the House for eight years when other speakers were falling by the wayside every day? Did Odili not work assiduously with him to prop the Ikwerre interest up to the end? Has Amaechi himself not confessed that all he went into government to achieve, with Odili, he achieved all, both for himself and his people?
Whether his followers accept it or not, it is on record that Odili accepted Amaechi and brought him up in life; Odili made him speaker and gave him the platform to climb; Odili worked him into the state and national political mainstream structure; Odili gave him the PDP ticket and took the flaks from those Amaechi now calls true friends; Odili gave him money to challenge the substitution in court; etc. In return, it is on record that he has been abusing Odili and his wife; has been aiding his aides to abuse his own master, has been running anti-Odili administration, has been denying what his master did in the state, always starting every programme by saying the sector was a disaster, declaring states of emergency in sectors where Odili sank huge sums so as to rubbish him.
To crown it all, he set up a commission to probe his master's administration, or how else can anyone describe the Eso panel or Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) that actually had no reconciliation agenda, and no truth to find. If it can find the truth, how come the truth of the Okrika violence began and ended with the Ateke Tom and Dr Abiye Sekibo era, when indeed Ada George and the Bush Boys is of greater significance? How come the numerous inter and intra-community clashes in the state, even those that took place before the Odili era have been subsumed into the place which was said to be within earshot of Odili's residence. Even when the militants boasted of striking on august 15, 2005, Odili's birthday, just to humiliate them. Perhaps, it was also Odili that sponsored those ones.
The point is that Amaechi is today governor, just as Odili was. He has yam and knife, just as Odili had. We know how much Odili helped him with the knife and yam, as he admitted on March 10, 2009. We also know what he is doing today with his own yam and knife to those who built the party.
But while this hate enterprise is going on, where are those elders and royal fathers who used to throng the seat of power intervening on behalf of one erring politician or the other? Where are Rivers men of honour, that a war will continue to rage between Odili and Amaechi, which can destroy the unity of the state and the PDP, and no one wants to say anything? They pretend not to see, hear of smell anything.
History is there to record what Odili did to Amaechi and what Amaechi is doing to him. History will also ask the elders and royal fathers one or two questions on why they have chosen to watch when in the past, they had always intervened.
Madu contributed his piece from University of Port Harcourt, Rivers State

Original_Search_Title: Between Excess Crude and the Termites
Headline: Nigeria: Between Excess Crude and the Termites
Body_Text:
BEFORE now not being an economist would have posed some hindrance in penning this essay-not anymore.
Any shyness on my part has since melted away; actually I can even boast that in a way I am probably more capable of talking about the current global financial meltdown and pointing fingers at those who caused it and where they failed.
Meaning that if the best and brightest can fail so catastrophically, what makes a none- expert like me not competent in taking them on and dismissing any pretences of superior knowledge?
As everyone knows late last year, the 'vast ignorant public' in panic over the depth and extent of global financial meltdown, which was threatening to sweep the world like a tsunami, were searching for some answers, our CBN governor, Prof Charles Soludo, an expert and a respected economist assured everyone on his honour that Nigeria was insulated and would not suffer one way or the other.
He appeared before the Senate to defend his position, taking pride in his big consolidated banks as the buffer that would ensure Nigeria's economy is protected from this tidal wave. He was taking pride in his vast foreign reserves and in his expertise; a smooth talker, Prof Soludo worked his charm and could have gotten away with his fancy footwork, except that the relentless denuding of the value of the Naira, the tottering Stock Exchange and the crumbling oil prices showed clearly that somebody is not doing his sums right.
The Naira, Soludo once boasted, would be the currency of choice over and above the dollar is at this stage losing its luster. His mega banks are looking increasingly vulnerable and desperate to survive. Meanwhile, the word on the street is that before the middle of the year, many of the banks, which are increasingly refusing to lend to each other may well seek a bailout, notwithstanding all the vehemence from the CBN and the banks that no such thing will happen. Worryingly for them, the colony of optimists is thinning out by the second.
So if Soludo prepared his back for the whip non-economists like me are using, I cannot blame him for global financial meltdown, but his posture may have caused a number of persons their fortune if he had taken steps to advise government and prepare every other ignorant person about what to expect. After all why is he the expert? And why is he in government?
Another person who should be getting the whip for a different reason is Dr Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, for not minding her business and offering unsolicited advice, which reminded everyone that she may well have been part of the problem. What do I mean? On Monday March 17 this Ivy League intellectual at a public lecture in Abuja declared that the time has come for Nigeria to spend from the excess crude savings to cushion the effect of global financial meltdown. What? Is somebody getting carried away by the sound of their voice? My other instinct was-have we not been in a meltdown all these years? To quote Dr Okonjo-Iweala: "This is the right time to use the money that we have saved. This is the time to use some of these monies, but we need to be careful on what we use the excess crude proceeds for. An example is the $ 5.3 billion investment envisaged in the NIPP, which will make a difference provided it goes into the designated activity".
Now with an economy that has long been in the woods, with very weak or none existing infrastructure, you have to scratch your head in wonderment. Our sea ports have long been in need of expansion and modernisation; our economy is one without a national railway system. Power is barely functioning; the road network has collapsed and other social structures are either poorly performing or not functioning at all. And Dr Okonjo-Iweala believes her prescriptions make sense at a time when the stock market since mid-last year lost N8 trillion and over 70 per cent in value. At a time when the Naira, the national currency, crashed by 25 per cent from N117 to the present N200 and still counting.
Ignorance can sometimes be a blessing and not being an economist makes me ask the following questions. Does it make sense that this country under President Obasanjo paid $12 billion and had $18 billion written off, when she had not been able to resolve to some considerable degree the infrastructure difficulty it is facing? (When that decision was made in 2005, I recall writing a piece in which I denounced the then President for committing economic sabotage against the country. I still stand by that declaration). Supposing Nigeria had invested the $12 billion in some of the infrastructure it needed and rescheduled some of the debts it had, would it not have been beneficial to the country in the long run?
But what did Dr Okonjo-Iweala and Obasanjo decide to do? They paid off the bulk of foreign and some local debts and warehoused the balance, called excess crude, in some foreign and local banks. To ask-what exactly has been the benefit to the people of this country the high oil revenue to the country. It is hard to point at anything. Is it that the roads are better, or that there is light, or that the refineries are working, or that there is more food. What? What?
Now the aforesaid reminds one of the lamentations by Delta State governor, Dr Emmanuel Uduaghan (a none-economist by the way) who in a rather earthy remark pointed out what is obvious when he said: "I raised the alarm last year and that is what has happened now.
"The money we say we are saving, what is the value today? I said it last year that why are you saving money when there are problems on ground: you don't have food to eat and you say let me keep my yam and you are hungry. Then the day you want to eat the yam 'kokoro' (weevil) has finished the yam.
That is a situation we have found ourselves(in) today; if we had used some of those money to fix Shagamu-Benin highway, it would have improved on our economy. So many people spend hours and hours on that road and nobody has gone to quantify how many man hours we lose."
With the global financial meltdown, the savings made have done little to protect the country from the pangs of the crisis. Oil prices have virtually crashed; credits have dried up; broken infrastructures have not been fixed; poverty is on the increase; insecurity is pervasive.
There must be something Okonjo-Iweala and Obasanjo saw in adopting this policy, but whatever it is there are not many people I know who think it is such a brilliant idea. However, if you find them brilliant, forgive me, I am not an economist, and I know am better off not being one.
Mr. Odili, a journalist, writes from Asaba, Delta State.

Original_Search_Title: Adamawa CJ Tasks Muslims, Christians on Peace
Headline: Nigeria: Adamawa CJ Tasks Muslims, Christians on Peace
Body_Text:
The Chief Judge of Adamawa State, Justice Bamare S. Bansi, has taken a look at the incessant religious disturbances in some parts of the country, particularly Northern states and attributed them to the fact that some Muslims and Christians only paid lip service to their religions and never adhered strictly to their teachings.
Bansi told Daily Trust that the problem was that Christianity and Islam have always been put aside not really assimilated by the followers, pointing out that the relationship between these two religions and the followers has always been that of "Yam and Palm oil".
"As the palm oil have never penetrated to yam, the Islam and Christianity have never penetrated to the souls of some Christians and Muslims, stating that they only professed the religions in their mouths but not really in their minds", he said.
He said some Muslims and Christians who go to Mosques and Churches to worship are just "infantile prayers" and are never serious in their worship and therefore they have no fear of God in their minds. "Otherwise why should they take arms ad start killing each other on a slightest provocation". He wandered.
Justice Bansi said Nigerians just take interest in killing one another on an inexcusable reasons, stating that the religious disturbances in Kano, Jos, Bauchi, Gombe, Kaduna and in any other part of the country emanated from just on a simple miss-understanding which could have been avoided if the adherents of the two religious have really embraced the principles of Islam and Christianity.
"Nigerians have no cause to fighting each other, because neither Christianity nor Islam accommodate killing of human beings", he said.
Justice Bansi said ignorance, hunger, disease lack of shelter and poverty are other serious factors that contributed to this phenomenon, but which people always hinged them on religion to fight each other.
He therefore called on Nigerians to eschew religious intolerance and to live in peace with one another rather than confrontation.
He also advised Muslims and Christians to follow the teachings of their religions which always preach peace and tolerance among them.
The chief judge admonished the adherents of the two religions to stop using derogatory words against each other, which normally don't go down well with any body.
According to Justice Bansi, Nigeria has come of age for the country to be seen always in the middle of religious conflicts which have "made us divided through the usage of these offensive wards on religious lines.
The chief judge said Christians and Muslims should particularly cling and hold fast on to Psalms Chapter 23 in the Holy Bible and Surahtu Kafirun in he Holy Qur'an respectively for peaceful coexistence in this world, and "we should be our brother's keeper as was enacted in our 1999 constitutions of the Federal Republic of Nigeria".

Original_Search_Title: Lagos Bomb Blast Victim Died of Ignorance - Army
Headline: Nigeria: Lagos Bomb Blast Victim Died of Ignorance - Army
Body_Text:
The Nigerian Army has absolved itself of blame over Tuesday's bomb blast that killed a school boy at the Ikeja Military Cantonment in Lagos.The military authorities said the victim, Master Joseph Tim, a secondary school student in Nasarawa State, died as a result of ignorance and total disregard to order.
Speaking with journalists yesterday at the cantonment while reacting to the incident, the Public Relations Officer of 9th Brigade, Major Emmanuel Adeniyi, said the area where the explosion took place has been condoned off for long and that an embargo has been placed against the making of fire in the area and its environ by the authorities.
Adeniyi also said it was one of the ordinances that were buried under the spot where the deceased was roasting the yam that exploded, resulting in the death and a severe injury on another who was with the deceased as at the time of the incident.
He said combing of the area was still ongoing to detect more ordinances, saying many foreign and local bomb detonating experts were invited to clear the relics after the bloody bomb explosion at the cantonment seven years ago.
"After the last explosion in 2002, the authorities invited foreign experts who really did their best in clearing the relics. Even home base experts were invited and they are still working in the area", he said.
On insinuations that by members of the public that the cantonment's vicinity was not yet safe for people to live, the army officer said even after the Second World War, relics of bomb were still found here and there.
The spokesman said the authorities had since Tuesday sent a delegation from Abuja to the parents of the deceased on a condolence visit as a sign of responsiveness on the side of the Nigerian Army.
He also said the General Officer Commanding (GOC) 81 division of the Nigerian Army had paid a inspection visit to the area.
He did not however say whether compensation to the family of the deceased would be paid, saying the matter was still under investigation and too premature to be determined.
The deceased, identified as Joseph Tim, a student of a Government Secondary School in Nasarawa State, who was on a visit to his uncle at block A3 of the cantonment, was killed in the process of roasting three tubers of yam opposite the area where a similar incident occurred about seven years ago.

Original_Search_Title: Bomb Blast - Army Blames Victim
Headline: Nigeria: Bomb Blast - Army Blames Victim
Body_Text:
Lagos — NIGERIA Army (NA) yesterday claimed that Master Joseph Tim, the victim of Tuesday's bomb blast at Ikeja military cantonment committed "suicide" for flouting safety restrictions there.
It also ruled out possible compensations for the family of the bomb blast victim, saying 'we are still investigating the matter and the issue of compensation was still premature.
This is as the Army allayed fear of possible explosions of undetonated bombs, saying a proper surface sweeping of the environment has been conducted by experts.
Consequently, the Public Relations Officer of the 9th Brigade of the Nigerian Army has expressed skepticism over the likelihood of compensation to the family of the deceased.
Daily Champion recalled that Master Tim, a student of Government Secondary School Nasarawa State, who was on a visit to his uncle at the cantonment was killed by bomb explosion on Tuesday at the Ikeja Military Cantonment while roasting three tubers of yam on the same spot that explosion occurred seven years ago.
The Public Relations Officer (PRO) of the 9th Brigade of the Nigerian Army, Major Emmanuel Adeniyi said these at a press conference yesterday in Ikeja. "The area has been condoned off," adding that embargo had been placed against making fire in the area and its environ by the Army authorities.
"It is one of the ordinances buried under the spot where the deceased was roasting the yam that exploded, resulting in Tim's death and severe injury on another who was with him at the time of the incident," he said.
He however said that the combing of the area is on going to detect more ordinances, explaining that several foreign and local bomb experts have been invited to clear the relics in 2002.
His words, "After last explosion in 2002, the Authority invited foreign experts who really did their best in clearing the relics. Even home based experts were invited and they are still working in the area."
Reacting to claims that the place is not safe despite assurances by the Army Authority, Adeniyi said even after the second World War, there are still some relics found on one spot or the other, adding that clearing exercise is continuous process.
With Tuesday's incident, he said, the Army has condoned off the area and also improved its warning against making of fire in the area, warning that any discovery of ordinances of similar objects should be reported to the authority immediately.
According to him, the Army Authorities had since Tuesday sent a powerful delegation from Abuja to the parents of the deceased on a condolence visit as a sign of responsiveness on the side of the authority while General Officer Commanding (GOC) the 81 Division has paid a similar visit and inspection to the area.
When our correspondent visited the residence of the uncle of the deceased, it was deserted.
Adeniyi said that the delegation was led by General Officer Commanding (GOC), 81 divisions, in charge of Policy and Planning, Eugene Nwaguma and Director of Army Public Relations, Chris Olukolade when they visited the scene of the incident.
Similar incident had occurred at the cantonment in 2002 which led to the death of many people.
Several people who were fleeing from the explosions were drowned at Oke-Afa canal in Isolo, Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Day Trigger-Happy Soldier Shot Civilian Dead
Headline: Nigeria: Day Trigger-Happy Soldier Shot Civilian Dead
Body_Text:
Sixty-eight-year-old Hassana Haliru was oblivious of the crowd of sympathisers who trooped into her house for prayers.
"She has been like that since the news of her son, Mika'ilu's death reached her," one of her nephews told Weekly Trust in a worrisome tone.
Thirty-year-old Mika'ilu was shot in the back by a soldier simply identified as Solomon while brokering peace between another soldier and a youth in the area.
According to an eyewitness, the soldier who was among the anti-robbery squad known as "Operation Flush," stationed in Murya village, was disciplining another youth from the area for wearing military camouflage trousers near the late Mika'ilu's shop. The deceased was said to have decided to intervene by pleading with the soldiers to pardon the "erring" young man.
One of the soldiers, according to accounts, on arriving the scene, had ordered all the peacemakers to leave the area or he will shoot, priming his rifle in the process. Haliru was said to have obeyed the order and was turning to leave when he was shot in the back. The bullet was said to have pierced through him and wounded one of the soldiers and a woman in a nearby shop.
The wounded soldier, one Bukar, was said to have been in critical condition in the Dalhat Araf Specialist Hospital, Lafia, where he was rushed for medical attention.
The incident, Weekly Trust learnt, triggered spontaneous reactions from youths in the area.
Solomon was said to have escaped by whiskers but his motorcycle was burnt by the rampaging mob. One of the soldiers was however not lucky as he was said to have been beaten to coma and would have been killed but for the timely intervention of some elders.
The rampaging youths then barricaded the ever-busy Lafia/Makurdi highway, leaving hundreds of motorists and commuters stranded for hours.
Bonfires were mounted in four designated locations on the highway. The youths stood their ground despite the presence of the combined team of police and military personnel deployed to restore peace in the town. Community leaders and elders had to halt what observers believed would have been a drift into anarchy. Part of the peace process was to ensure that the remains of the deceased which was taken by the security personnel to an unknown destinations be returned to the elders and family for burial the following morning. The Commissioner of Police, Mr. Shehu Babalola, was reluctant at first but when he was informed that the only panacea to peace in the area is the immediate release of the corpse for burial, he gave up.
Mika'ilu's immediate elder brother, Malam Abubakar said the family has left everything in the hands of Allah and will not seek justice anywhere, noting that "justice belongs to God." He said it was destined by Allah that the deceased will meet his death that fateful day through that means and that there was nothing anybody could have done to change the situation.
"He was our bread-winner," Alhaji Danladi Haliru, another elder brother of the deceased explained, describing the decreased as industrious and hard working. He said his late brother is a self made young man who was seeing to his education and that of his brothers as well as sisters before his demise penultimate week. Mika'ilu was said to have learnt the art of buying and selling from childhood.
"He started petty trading in grains and yams from the age of 15 and by the time he was 20 and above, his business had grown so well. He had taken delivery of some goods on the day he was killed," Abubakar explained.
He graduated from the state owned College of Education, Akwanga, through self sponsorship. He was also said to be sponsoring three of his brothers and two of his sisters in various institutions in and outside the state in addition to providing for the daily needs of other family members.
How will the family cope in the absence of the bread winner? Danlandi said it was going to be difficult. He said although no amount of compensation can bring back the deceased back, they expected some kind of assistance to cushion the effects his death will have on the family.
"At least some kind of Diyyah (compensation as prescribed in Islam) ought to be paid to the family of the deceased. However the military authority have visited the family about four times since the demise of Mika'ilu but they have offered nothing other than condoling the family," Danlandi explained.
Apart from Hassana who is mourning, Ralia, Mikai'lu's fiancée is also in pain.
As the two most important women in late Mukai'lu's life bear their irreparable and painful loss, the question on everybody's lips is the whereabouts of the trigger-happy Solomon.
Danladi said the military officers who paid condolence visits to the family house had informed them that the embattled soldier would not be spared by the authorities and that he is facing punishment in the barracks in Keffi.
The Nasarawa state police public relations officer, Richard Akoji (ASP), told journalists Wednesday that he just received the signal about the incident without even the name of the culprit. No arrest, he said, has been made.
However, the chairman of the Nigeria Bar Association (NBA), Nasarawa State, Barrister Afis Adigun, said the military has no right to keep a suspected murderer in their custody.

Original_Search_Title: Cobwebs in My Head - Letter to Koku Anyidoho
Headline: Ghana: Cobwebs in My Head - Letter to Koku Anyidoho
Body_Text:
Efo Koku, You are a very perceptive man. I am very impressed with your diagnosis of the "cobwebs" in my head. I appreciate your attempt to have them removed. Sadly, you failed. Your prescription for removing the "cobwebs" wasn't effective because you overlooked the basic reasons why they got into my 'coconut' in the first place.
