The discussion focused on the Ivorian peace process and the next report to be published by the Carter Center on the political situation in Cote d'Ivoire.
5. On bilateral issues, a prominent story in Fraternite Matin said a meeting Tuesday between French Secretary of State in charge of Cooperation and Francophonie Alain Joyandet and President Gbagbo paved the way for a new chapter in France-Cote d'Ivoire relations. "I'm here to reaffirm France's commitment to see the achievement of the electoral process. It's also a strong signal to President Gbagbo that France is ready to resume cooperation with Cote d'Ivoire once the elections are conducted," the paper quoted the French Minister as saying. The report further said that the case of Jean Paul Ney [a French journalist jailed in Cote d'Ivoire in 2008 for "plotting against the authority of the state"] was high on the agenda during the meeting between Gbagbo and Joyandet. On this issue, the French minister reportedly said: "I'm in the position to tell you that, in principle, he should be released on bail tomorrow [today]."
6. The case of another French-Canadian journalist who disappeared in Cote d'Ivoire in April 2004 was also the subject of a front-page story in Notre Voie (a daily close to the ruling FPI party). Referring to what it called secret documents on the plot, the paper speculated on how France was involved in the disappearance of Guy-Andre Kieffer.
7. On economic issues, Fraternite Matin reported that the World Bank has provided 71.8 billion F CFA (about $147 million) to Cote d'Ivoire to support this country as part of efforts to pay its domestic debt. This was announced Tuesday by an official from the Ministry of Economy and Finance. On other economic issues, a report in Notre Heure (a daily close to the opposition) said that cocoa production in Cote d'Ivoire has dropped by about 15 per cent this year as a result of lack of investment. The report further said that in a bid to boost production, stakeholders have set up a center on cocoa research and development whose role is to improve the quality of cocoa and other local products including coffee and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Book Seller Wins Cellcom U.S.$10,000 - Draw Reaches U.S.$80,000
Headline: Liberia: Book Seller Wins Cellcom U.S.$10,000 - Draw Reaches U.S.$80,000
Body_Text:
Monrovia — As Cellcom’s raffle draw continues, 55 years old book seller, Robert Baryogar has emerged as the 8th winner of Cellcom ‘Be a Millionaire’ raffle draw in Monrovia.Announcing the result Saturday at the headquarters of the GSM Company, Cellcom’s Public Relations Officer T-Max Jlateh said after dozens of judges made a search of the winning number, they declared Mr. Baryogar a winner of the raffle draw.
Mr. Bareyogar, became a winner by subscribing with Cellcom GSM Company and making a call above US$ 2.00.Mr. Baryogar was so excited on the phone that he was told by T-Max that he didn’t need a Cellcom car to pick him up to receive his US$10,000 check. Mr. Baryogar ran from his house in slippers to Cellcom’s headquarters to collect his prize.
Baryogar, 55, works as a stationery and Book seller under the Ministry of Education building on Broad Street, Monrovia.As in past draws, the crowd of hundreds was entertained by a number of Liberia entertainers, including the fantastic HipCo performance of the Dream Team and their popular song, “One Man, One Cup,” as well as amazing dance performances by a cultural troupes.
Mr. Baryogar has joined seven others Cellcom raffle draw winners in receiving US$10,000 The Saturday draw brings to US$ 80,000.00.Next Saturday the Cellcom raffle draw will be held in the Port City of Buchanan, Grand Bassa County.
The decision to host the $10,000 raffle draw in locations other than Monrovia was an easy one to make, according to Avishai Marziano Cellcom’s CEO. “The ‘Be A Millionaire’ campaign is for all of Liberia, not just Monrovia. and although the $10,000 give-away is the highlight of the drawing-the celebration, the music.The Chief Executive Officer of Cellcom, Mr. Mariana said being with Cellcom and winning US$10,000 is a joyous experience for anyone.The Raffle draw is an important part of Cellcom’s giving back to the community.” Cellcom’s “Be A Millionaire” campaign is a tangible way of expressing the company’s gratitude to customers for their loyalty.
The winners of Cellcom raffle draws include, A 55 years old book seller, Robert Baryogar, A cassava Gravy seller, Matenken Boyo, A tailor shop owner, Bill Sheriff, and a twenty years old Tupee Tequah of LAC, also a 10th grade student and a tailor, Samuel Bowin, a house wife, Fatumata Konneh of St. Francis community, William Blamo, and Mrs. Cordelia Gibson.

Original_Search_Title: No Writing Lagos to Death -Toni Kani
Headline: Nigeria: No Writing Lagos to Death -Toni Kani
Body_Text:
The landscape of literature in Nigeria is not the same anymore, in comparison with what it was a few years ago. This is an unequivocal testimony to the high number of literary converts being won on daily basis by the active performers in the literary world.
The recent presentation of the new works of Toni Kan Onwordi, Nights of the Creaking Bed, a compilation of short stories and Songs of absence and despair, an anthology of poems, an enterprising writer and appealing poet, can be described as one of the engaging scenes of a silent but consistent literary revolution.
Terra Kulture on Tiamiyu Savage Street, Victoria Island, venue of the presentation was filled to the brim and the audience looked very expectant and really, at the end, they were not only soaked with the showers of literature caused by the likes of Toni Kan, Toyin Akinosho, Bibi Bakare-Yusuf, the compere, Seyi Law among others, they ended up as involved, happy participants.
The event was strictly informal and literarily inclined. Mr. and Mrs. Onwordi, Toni's parents were also in attendance to celebrate one of the best days of their son.
Kan did not disappoint the audience in any way. From the manner of the organisation to the actual book presentation, logistics to seating arrangements, the author's style stood out like in a uniquely conspicuous system. It was all done using the Kan approach: orderly disorganised.
Suffice to say therefore that scores of Nigerian writers based in the different corners of the globe are returning home in their droves, so are critics, journalists, publishing houses and lovers of literature increasing their stake in the field. This has led to the staging of more literary fora, organisation of more readings and sessions and improved level of writers' outputs.
The event was like a reading session to many but the author achieved his objective, albeit, a wonderful method. Poetry renditions were done by a host of poets and performing wordsmiths before Toyin
Akinosho of The Guardian also read a witty and amusing portion of one out of the collection of short stories.
When it was time for Kan to embark on introducing the audience to his newly delivered works, much more than a joyous mother, the creative restlessness that has come to be identified with him once again came to the fore.
Kan said he came to Lagos when he was still a young boy, precisely at the age of 11 and since then, Lagos has become an inalienable part of him.
Commenting on the setting of the stories in the collections of short stories and poetry at the part of the event where introduction had become mixed with interrogation, he said: "the book is about urban Lagos. Lagos will always get at you one way or the other either you are in your car, in your cozy world, held in traffic or just trekking on the road."
While attempting to trace the beginning of his creative voyage, Toni said he remembered his dad sending him books that made him cry while reading and he therefore told himself that he will, one day, write a book that will make somebody cry. In the same vein, he announced that if there is anybody in the audience that has cried while reading any of his works, such a person should say it so that he can stop writing. Specifically, Toni declared that Night of the Creaking Bed starts with happiness and ends with sadness.
In his collection of poems, he reflects on the experiences of some folks who travel abroad and keeps their wives behind at home.
He immediately delved into reading one of the short stories in the collection, The Devil's Overtime and afterwards stated that the stories are reflections of real lives, in which he gave the example of a lady that abandoned her newborn baby in an aircraft recently as one of the kind of significant situations he regularly tries to highlight. But before anybody could categorise him along with the so-called socio-politically focused writers, he says he does not write about the state of affairs of things in the country. He continued that he feels the country has had enough of politics and social issues and so should be ready for fresh scenarios in the thematic preoccupation of works.
"Who among the fellow young writers do you like and why?" Toni was asked. First he went on to say if he begins to mention names, he might leave some names out, which was the reason he did not like such questions but in one breath, he said, "I like Sefi Atta most because she has a love affair with Lagos."
Thus, a highway of discourse was laid in the reaction of Toyin Akinosho who wonders if Lagos would not be "done to death" the way all the writers around, with Chimamanda Adichie being the only exception, saying their works are focused on Lagos one way or the other.
Dele Olojede, publisher of Next, in his response said he did not think Lagos could be done to death.
"I don't think we should prescribe to writers what they should write. If there are 17 million people in Lagos, there should be 17 million distinct stories as a result. So, they cannot write Lagos to death. A writer sometimes doesn't know what his subject matter is until he writes. Whatever moves your spirit is what you should follow," he said.
Toni Kan's romantic/erotic tendency was the next controversial topic, placed side by side with the author's Hints background. To him, it was an opportunity to put the record straight. He listed Helon Habila, Jude Dibia, Reuben Abati and many others among those produced by Hints and did not shy away from the fact that it was a good breeding ground for him and a major influence of his worldview. He totally rejected the erotic tag and said many more writers are more inclined to drag the reader into the bedroom, a thing he has only attempted to do for the first time in Nights of the Creaking Bed, a short story in the collection, which happens to be the title of the book. According to Akinosho, the book has been so planned and portably packaged that a reader can flip through and digest at a trip to the beach. He said the book is one of the best books published in the last five years. He however noted that editing infrastructure is weak in Nigeria and authors need to strive to settle for neat publications. On the same note, Bibi Bakare-Yusuf, publisher, Cassava Republic Press, who was the publisher of Toni's books, said the five book reviewers who read through the books recommended its publication within a little spate of time.
The audience was all on Kan as if he had killed an orphan. One of the attendees, a lady, tasked him to criticise his works as he usually does as a critic. Kan riotously chorused: I love my book. All I believe is that if you want to be a good writer, don't write, to start with. Read what you love reading. This of course is my strength."
When asked to comment on the uniqueness of his style as a writer especially on the issue of moral, sex and relationship, Toni Kan said, "My style is my style. The only message in most of my books is nothing for you to enjoy yourself. I neither teach moral nor corrupt my reader. Make your choice."
The curtain was drawn with the book signing after a brief vote of thanks from the publisher.

Original_Search_Title: A Treasure Found Among Blood Diamonds
Headline: Sierra Leone: A Treasure Found Among Blood Diamonds
Body_Text:
Deborah George says Baindu stole her heart when she met the young girl at a center for children who'd been kidnapped during Sierra Leone's war.
"Today, the kid who ran through the bush to escape the rebels is the fastest girl on her soccer team."
Every war brings new words and phrases into the lexicon. During the civil war in Sierra Leone, Americans learned about "blood diamonds." For more than a decade, rebels in that country kidnapped thousands of children to fight with them and to help them extract the diamonds that lay under the earth. Those diamonds were then traded for guns and ammunition. In 2001, I was in Sierra Leone reporting on how the worldwide trade in illicit diamonds was fueling the war.
It was the rainy season, and I was on my way east to the diamond fields on a road lined with burnt-out cars and buses. In some places, there were pits filled with muddy water. They were formed when the rebels had forced people to dig for the diamonds that lay just under the ground. Near one village that had been raided by the rebels, I came across a center for children who'd been kidnapped during the war.
These children had either escaped or been released during negotiations that were then going on between the government and the rebels. Most of them had missed years of school and even some of the older teenagers were just learning the alphabet. The boys had fought as child soldiers. There were girls there, too, and I asked if I might speak to some of them, one at a time.
Baindu's Story
The girls described how they'd been kidnapped and forced to work in the diamond mines. They told me about seeing airplanes landing in the bush. The planes carried white men who brought drugs and weapons to the rebels and left with the diamonds. One of the littlest girls I interviewed looked like an old woman in an oversized shirt. While she talked, her tiny hands twisted the cloth of her faded lappa.
She told me her name was Baindu. I asked her if she'd ever spoken into a microphone before. "No," she said, and I rewound the tape a little and held my headphones to her ear so she could hear her own voice. Then I pushed the record button. "We are starting?" she said. I laughed and thought, "She's a smart one."
Baindu told me that one day when she was about 8 years old, she and some other children had gone into the bush near her village to pick cassava leaves. They'd bring them home and pound them with wooden mortars. And their mothers would make a thick stew to eat with rice.
All of a sudden a group of rebels attacked and carried the children off. They were brought to a rebel camp and forced to work in the diamond pits. While men with AK-47s stood watch, the littlest children dug and sifted the mud with wooden shakers looking for diamonds.
One day, Baindu persuaded the rebels to let her walk a little into the bush to relieve herself. When she was just out of sight, she took off. She ran barefoot for miles until she found her way to a road and then found someone to help her get back to her village. While she'd been gone, her parents had been killed in a raid.
The little girl was angry about what had happened to her. Now, she said, she had one wish: to go to school and become a teacher.
A Lightning Bolt To The Heart
It was time to go, so I took her picture and left. I was standing by the gate to the compound waiting for a ride when it started raining. All of a sudden the little girl was at my side with a big black umbrella, trying to hoist it over my head. I took it so we were both covered. Then she put her hand in mine and we waited together.
The French use a warlike phrase to describe love at first sight -- they call it a "coup de foudre" -- meaning a kind of lightning bolt to the heart. That's what happened to me. In those few minutes there in the rain. The little girl's fierce longing seized my heart. She was way too young to have her dreams be over. And out of all the children I'd met, I thought, this one could make up for the lost time. She could have her childhood back. Together, we could defeat war itself.
I got back to the States and sent her picture to a friend, a Sierra Leonean journalist who went and found her. I sent money for her school fees and eventually placed her in a boarding school that reopened when the war ended. There were no books, running water or electricity, but it was safe. After several years of red tape, adoption regulations and a child-trafficking investigation, Baindu finally came to the U.S. on a plane, by herself.
Today, the kid who ran through the bush to escape the rebels is the fastest girl on her soccer team, covering the ground with long easy lopes. She's in 11th grade and loves hip hop and hamburgers. She doesn't like to talk about the past. She doesn't forget, she says. When she's old, she'll still remember her stories. But, it's better, she says, to look to the future.
Deborah George is a writer and radio producer who lives in Takoma Park, Md.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Gravy Seller Wins Cellcom's US$10,000 Raffle
Headline: Liberia: Cassava Gravy Seller Wins Cellcom's US$10,000 Raffle
Body_Text:
Monrovia — A 24-year-old cassava gravy sellern a resident of Matadi Estate in the Sinkor suburb of Monrovia has become the 7th lucky winner of Cellcom’s weekly “Be A Millionaire Campaign”, walking away with US$10,000 last Saturday.
Miss Matenken Boyo walked into the hall of fortune when her cell number corresponded with the lucky raffle number, after the company failed to find a winner during the first round of the raffle.
Cellcom PRO T-Max Jlateh made the pronouncement when Miss Boyo’s number was selected as a result of a five-digit series number picked by volunteers including innocent children in the Matadi Estate.
Last Saturday’s “Be a Millionaire Campaign” took place at the SOS Children Village with several Liberian musicians taking the stage to entertain hundreds of participants that graced that raffle draw.
The draw was held in collaboration with YMCA homecoming program.The crowd was stunned when the first person called never answered his/her phone.
Cellcom Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Avishai Marziano said cellcom hope that the US$ 10,000 winners of the “Be a Millionaire Campaign” will make face brighter future through transformed lives.
The occasion was graced by several personalities including the judges and officials from the International Bank.
Madam Boyo, is the 7th winner and 4th female that have been risen from the cell of poverty since the campaign begun nearly two months ago.
All Cellcom subscribers automatically become participants of the draw by talking for at least US$2. Every Saturday a draw attended by hundreds of subscribers and the public is held to announce the lucky winner of the week.

Original_Search_Title: Lagos in Weekend of Literature, Paintings, Entertainment
Headline: Nigeria: Lagos in Weekend of Literature, Paintings, Entertainment
Body_Text:
Lagos — In what is expected to be a congregation of Nigeria's literati, the upscale Terra Kulture on Tiamiyu Savage Street, Victoria Island, Lagos, will come alive later tomorrow, Saturday, April 18, 2009, when one of the nation's finest in poetry, prose and corporate communications, Toni Kan presents two of his recently published works to the public. Although, the two books, respectively titled Nights Of The Creaking Bed and Songs Of Absence And Despair, have earlier appeared on bookshelves across the nation, they would be officially unveiled to the reading public and the media community by their publishers, Cassava Republic Press. The lunch programme is scheduled to commence at exactly 2p.m. at the Island based culture centre.
Nights Of The Creaking Bed which is a collection of some selected 14 different short stories about life and death, which the author said is set mostly in Lagos even when the setting is not clearly alluded to such, has been described as not only one of the best recent literary works in recent times on lyricism but a fine anthology, well written and well presented. While the award-winning novelist and arguably one with one of the fastest rising profiles on the Nigerian literary landscape, Seffi Atta is convinced that the book is essentially a rare kind that begins with "deliberate brevity and ends on a note of lush lyricism," foremost publisher and columnist, Toyin Akinoso said Nights Of The Creaking Bed is indeed a pleasant anthology of interesting short stories that "are so well written in clean, elegant prose." Toyin believes "If there is anything the book can teach, it is English grammar."
Toyin's judgement of Kan's work should not in any way astonish genuine followers of the Nigerian literary realm, especially those who are well acquainted with Kan's pedigree and antecedents as an author, particularly during his days as a columnist in the old romance and true-life publication, Hints magazine. To describe Toni Kan as a thoroughbred literary mind is to state the obvious, as he cuts across as an essayist, prose stylist, book reviewer and poet extraordinaire. Presently, he serves as the Head of Corporate Communications to Visafone, a fixed wireless telecoms provider in Lagos.
Kan holds both a Bachelor of Arts (B.A.) and a Masters of Arts (M.A) degrees in English (Literature) from the Universities of Jos and Lagos respectively. His works have been published widely. He has also had short stories published in anthologies like We-Men, Little Drops (1) and Diamond and Ashes. An award winning poet, essayist and short story writer, his awards have taken him to Scotland and Switzerland.
The short stories featured in Nights Of The Creaking Bed revolve around a girl who is rejected in love because she has three brothers to look after; a middle aged housewife who finds love again but has an impossible decision to make; a young man who cannot get the image of his naked, beautiful mother out of his mind; a child so poor he has to hawk onions on Christmas day, and many others. Some, initially full of hope, find their lives blighted by the cruelty of others, or by being in the wrong place at the wrong time, or by just not knowing the 'right' people.
Kan's other book to be launched tomorrow is Songs Of Absence And Despair like Nights Of The Creaking Bed is also an anthology but unlike the first book, it is exclusively poetry. It contains some 33 poems, which the author collected over a specific period of time, specifically from 2004. The poetry book is essentially a lyrically evocative and rhythmic collection on, as its title suggests, the twin theme of absence and despair, two issues that have come to define the present generation of Nigerians who have been forced by the imperatives of survival, especially the nation's unfriendly economic situations, to dwell abroad or in separate cities, men and women forced to survive on a heady cocktail of regrets, nostalgia and memories.
Parts of activities lined up to add further entertainments to the event are live music performances, poetry recitations and vivacious comedy by a topflight comedian, Seyi Law.
Also tomorrow, Saturday, April 18, 2009, Kingsley Okonkwo, a.k.a KC of the popular Hip-hop group, KC-Presh would pump champagne, blow out some candle lights and give thanks to the almighty God for making him complete another calendar year in his life, as he adds a year.
The Uli, Ihiala LGA Anambra State-born artiste who came to limelight in 2002 as part-winner (with his partner, Presh) in the maiden edition of the popular Nigerian Breweries-sponsored Star Quest would throw a one-in-town birthday bash at Da-Grotto situated on Etim Inyang Crescent, Victoria Island, Lagos.
Alongside his partner, Presh, KC would equally seize the opportunity of the occasion to perform the group's new single Ginger Your Swagger which features rave-of-the-moment Niger Delta act, Timaya, to give attendees a treat to remember.
"We are celebrating big time with a big party and a lot of performances. You can't afford to miss this one," KC said.

Original_Search_Title: Skills Building, Key to SSE Development - SSEDAN Boss
Headline: Nigeria: Skills Building, Key to SSE Development - SSEDAN Boss
Body_Text:
Lagos — Attempts to develop the micro, small and medium enterprise sub-sector in Nigeria should be focused first on building the capacity of micro and small business entrepreneurs.
The Director-General of Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency (SMEDAN), Muhammad Nadada Umar, made the assertion on Wednesday in his opening address at a two-day seminar jointly organised by SMEDAN and Federal Institute for Industrial Research, Oshodi (FIIRO), Lagos.
Umar informed the audience that finance should be the last challenge to be considered in enterprise development.
"Access to fund should be the last issue to be trashed in addressing the needs of micro and small entrepreneurs. Emphasis should be placed on the content, the idea, the entrepreneur's capacity to manage the business and access to market for the product or service.
"Here lies the reason while SMEDAN emphasize training and capacity building for micro, small and medium enterprise promoters. If you go on to source for fund without addressing the issue of capacity, the business will sooner or later crash and the money you put into it will go down the drain," he said.
The SMEDAN chief executive charged further that all Nigerians owe it a duty to develop the MSME sub-sector.
"Nigeria will not develop without the independent of this all-important sub-sector because substantial portion of the nation's population falls within the micro, small and medium sub-sector.
"The food sub-sector can develop partly by subsidising tariffs and rates. We should endeavour to push players in the industry towards development through participation in seminars like this one and creating awareness for them to embrace capacity building.
"A trained and empowered MSMEs will address some of the issues in the President's 7-point Agenda and Vision 20:20-20. A vibrant micro and small enterprise sub-sector will create wealth, jobs and guarantee security of life and property. If we fail to develop the sub-sector, it will be difficult to achieve the ideals of Vision 20:20-20," he added.
Director-General of FIIRO, Dr. Oluwole Olatunji, who represented the Minister for Science and Technology, Dr. Alhassan Bako Zaku, said that about 500 Nigerians had benefited from the research breakthroughs of FIIRO in the last 10 years.
Olatunji said that FIIRO has done successive work on cassava research for bread baking and industrial raw materials.
He said the institute has also improved on the "poundo yam" it was known for before now.
"We now have instant pounded yam and we have recorded some strides in weaning formulas and concentrates," he stressed.

Original_Search_Title: The Background Music to My Writing Life -Toni Kan
Headline: Nigeria: The Background Music to My Writing Life -Toni Kan
Body_Text:
Essayist, prose stylist, book reviewer and poet, Toni Kan works as Head Corporate Communications of Visafone. He will be presenting two of his recent books to the public at Terra Kulture, Tiamiyu Savage, Victoria Island, by Saturday April 18, 2009. He has been 'accused' severally of being too 'feminine friendly' in his writing style though he tried to defend it. Most writers know him as a romance writer. Author of the critically acclaimed and some say, erotically charged collection of short stories, 'Nights of The Creaking Bed' and the new poetry collection 'Songs of Absence and Despair,' all published by Cassava Republic Press tells Yemi Adebisi in this interview, about the sounds and songs that have become the background to his life as a writer and lover of music. Excerpts:
What was the first song you remember liking?
My parents had lots of records back in the day. I remember my mother on Saturday mornings, cleaning the house and singing along to Bob Marley or Andy Kim or Skeeter Davis or Boney M or ABBA. My dad, even though he bought all the records, was partial to the blues and R&B and Soul. I remember Commodores, Isaac Hayes, Hot Chocolate and many others. There were also Nigerian albums from groups like Blo, Ofege, and others. Hot Chocolate, I remember because of the album cover even though I am not sure I ever listened to any of their tracks. I remember an album by this blind guy, Clarence Carter or something, singing: "I was caught, making love, to another man's wife." It was illicit and forbidden and sweet and I think I found it more forbidding than Sexual Healing, which caused a lot of hoopla when I was about 10. What I couldn't get past was a blind man getting it on with another man's wife. I remember sitting and wondering what would happen if the woman's husband caught them. How would he escape? I think that was how I began to exercise my imagination. I remember my father's colleague, a principal throwing his daughter's stuff out of the house after he came back home and caught her listening to Sexual Healing. Times have changed you know. Now, kids listen to songs like I wanna sex you up and no one cares.
What song made the most impact on you as a young boy, developing a literary consciousness?
The song that hit me the most and thinking back now, I suppose I was already forging a literary consciousness even back then, was Skeeter Davis' Dear John and End of the World. I liked the melancholy and heartfelt sadness the songs evoked. Dear John was particularly so because of the sadness in the singers voice as she wrote to her lover telling him of the end of their relationship. I was seven or eight, but I knew that there was heartbreak waiting. Tell me why you won't get caught by a song that says "Don't they know it's the end of the world 'cos you don't love me any more."
I guess I have always liked songs/books/movies that touch the heart. Then there were also cuts like Let it be me a duet between Skeeter Davis and somebody I can't recall. Then there was also another track I liked I'm a lover, not a fighter.
It's funny how I recall country music songs because if you had asked me like an hour ago whether I liked country music, I would have said no.
I think I also liked Dolly Parton and Kenny Rogers especially Kenny's song about a gambler. But the one that killed me dead each time I heard it (and still does today) was Islands in the stream a duet between Dolly and Kenny.
There was this album by a Nigerian musician. I can't recall his name now but he had a song that went something like this: "yesterday, kisses and today quarrels, mixed feelings, ye ye killing suspense."
What was the first cassette or album or CD you ever bought and what did it teach you as a writer?
The first cassette I remember buying was a Yellowman cassette. Back in secondary school in the late 80s, Yellowman was a big deal and I think that was the first cassette I ever bought. I remember songs like Jamaica Nice, London Cold and stuff.
What did it teach me as a writer? I remember Yellowman liked to make lots of rhymes, you know, end rhymes. I don't remember anything apart from that. I don't think reggae acts speak good English.
You write a lot about love, in fact many people still see you as a romance writer. What song was playing when you first fell in love, something like a soundtrack to your first love?
I don't remember this because I am a hopeless romantic. I fall in love at the drop of a hat and I generally do not have a logical reason. It is for me like Eliot wrote: "Is it perfume from a dress/that makes me so digress." I could fall in love with a girl for wearing a white top over black slacks or for licking ice cream with her head bent. But I remember the song that was playing the day I had my first kiss. We had gone to visit a lady who just had a child. When we got there, the new mother was eating and said we should go see the baby in her room. The girl and I was, who technically wasn't my girlfriend, bent and kissed the girl on her forehead then we looked at each other, smiled and kissed. She was older and she taught me. Bobby Brown's Ronnie was playing.
What song do you listen to when you are writing?
I was writing my book Nights of the Creaking Bed. It was a long process but because a couple of them were written while I was on fellowship in Germany in 2003, I remember listening to Seal over and over as I wrote my novella Ballad of Rage and then the short stories. I recall songs like Heavenly from the Seal IV album. I think that's his best work yet.
On a good day, when I'm writing, I'd be listening to jazz or classical music. My favourite writing sound is Vivaldi's Four Seasons. Maik Nwosu gave me the CD in 2003 or 2004; I am not so sure. Then I could also be listening to Enya or Arturo Sandoval or some jazz standards. I've got CDs with different artistes on them.
What are your plans for your twin launch?
I'll be presenting the books: Nights of the Creaking Bed, a collection of fourteen short stories and Songs of Absence and Despair, a collection of poems published by the Cassava Republic Press.
The short story collection has been described by Molara Wood, Assistant Managing Editor, Arts & Culture at Next as "a cohesive and stylish collection, with atmospheric scenes and noir elements" while novelist Sefi Atta and author of Everything Good Will Come has described the work as one that begins with "deliberate brevity and ends on a note of lush lyricism."
Publisher and columnist, Toyin Akinosho says the short stories "are so well written, in clean, and elegant prose. If there's anything the book can teach it is English Grammar."
The book of 33 poems which were collected over a period of time beginning in 2004 is a lyrically evocative and almost funereal collection which focuses on the twin themes of absence and despair, two issues that have come to define the present generation of Nigerians who have been forced by the imperatives of survival to dwell abroad or in separate cities; men and women forced to survive on a heady cocktail of regret, nostalgia and memories. The event will feature poetry, comedy and live music.
Finally, you write a lot about Lagos. If you were to suggest a soundtrack for Lagos what would it be?
That's a simple one. Fela already gave us the soundtrack to Lagos: Confusion breaki bone o, ye pa!

Original_Search_Title: A Treasure Found Among Blood Diamonds
Headline: Sierra Leone: A Treasure Found Among Blood Diamonds
Body_Text:
Deborah George says Baindu stole her heart when she met the young girl at a center for children who'd been kidnapped during Sierra Leone's war.
"Today, the kid who ran through the bush to escape the rebels is the fastest girl on her soccer team."
Every war brings new words and phrases into the lexicon. During the civil war in Sierra Leone, Americans learned about "blood diamonds." For more than a decade, rebels in that country kidnapped thousands of children to fight with them and to help them extract the diamonds that lay under the earth. Those diamonds were then traded for guns and ammunition. In 2001, I was in Sierra Leone reporting on how the worldwide trade in illicit diamonds was fueling the war.
It was the rainy season, and I was on my way east to the diamond fields on a road lined with burnt-out cars and buses. In some places, there were pits filled with muddy water. They were formed when the rebels had forced people to dig for the diamonds that lay just under the ground. Near one village that had been raided by the rebels, I came across a center for children who'd been kidnapped during the war.
These children had either escaped or been released during negotiations that were then going on between the government and the rebels. Most of them had missed years of school and even some of the older teenagers were just learning the alphabet. The boys had fought as child soldiers. There were girls there, too, and I asked if I might speak to some of them, one at a time.
Baindu's Story
The girls described how they'd been kidnapped and forced to work in the diamond mines. They told me about seeing airplanes landing in the bush. The planes carried white men who brought drugs and weapons to the rebels and left with the diamonds. One of the littlest girls I interviewed looked like an old woman in an oversized shirt. While she talked, her tiny hands twisted the cloth of her faded lappa.
She told me her name was Baindu. I asked her if she'd ever spoken into a microphone before. "No," she said, and I rewound the tape a little and held my headphones to her ear so she could hear her own voice. Then I pushed the record button. "We are starting?" she said. I laughed and thought, "She's a smart one."
Baindu told me that one day when she was about 8 years old, she and some other children had gone into the bush near her village to pick cassava leaves. They'd bring them home and pound them with wooden mortars. And their mothers would make a thick stew to eat with rice.
All of a sudden a group of rebels attacked and carried the children off. They were brought to a rebel camp and forced to work in the diamond pits. While men with AK-47s stood watch, the littlest children dug and sifted the mud with wooden shakers looking for diamonds.
One day, Baindu persuaded the rebels to let her walk a little into the bush to relieve herself. When she was just out of sight, she took off. She ran barefoot for miles until she found her way to a road and then found someone to help her get back to her village. While she'd been gone, her parents had been killed in a raid.
The little girl was angry about what had happened to her. Now, she said, she had one wish: to go to school and become a teacher.
A Lightning Bolt To The Heart
It was time to go, so I took her picture and left. I was standing by the gate to the compound waiting for a ride when it started raining. All of a sudden the little girl was at my side with a big black umbrella, trying to hoist it over my head. I took it so we were both covered. Then she put her hand in mine and we waited together.
The French use a warlike phrase to describe love at first sight -- they call it a "coup de foudre" -- meaning a kind of lightning bolt to the heart. That's what happened to me. In those few minutes there in the rain. The little girl's fierce longing seized my heart. She was way too young to have her dreams be over. And out of all the children I'd met, I thought, this one could make up for the lost time. She could have her childhood back. Together, we could defeat war itself.
I got back to the States and sent her picture to a friend, a Sierra Leonean journalist who went and found her. I sent money for her school fees and eventually placed her in a boarding school that reopened when the war ended. There were no books, running water or electricity, but it was safe. After several years of red tape, adoption regulations and a child-trafficking investigation, Baindu finally came to the U.S. on a plane, by herself.
Today, the kid who ran through the bush to escape the rebels is the fastest girl on her soccer team, covering the ground with long easy lopes. She's in 11th grade and loves hip hop and hamburgers. She doesn't like to talk about the past. She doesn't forget, she says. When she's old, she'll still remember her stories. But, it's better, she says, to look to the future.
Deborah George is a writer and radio producer who lives in Takoma Park, Md.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Gravy Seller Wins Cellcom's US$10,000 Raffle
Headline: Liberia: Cassava Gravy Seller Wins Cellcom's US$10,000 Raffle
Body_Text:
Monrovia — A 24-year-old cassava gravy sellern a resident of Matadi Estate in the Sinkor suburb of Monrovia has become the 7th lucky winner of Cellcom’s weekly “Be A Millionaire Campaign”, walking away with US$10,000 last Saturday.
Miss Matenken Boyo walked into the hall of fortune when her cell number corresponded with the lucky raffle number, after the company failed to find a winner during the first round of the raffle.
Cellcom PRO T-Max Jlateh made the pronouncement when Miss Boyo’s number was selected as a result of a five-digit series number picked by volunteers including innocent children in the Matadi Estate.
Last Saturday’s “Be a Millionaire Campaign” took place at the SOS Children Village with several Liberian musicians taking the stage to entertain hundreds of participants that graced that raffle draw.
The draw was held in collaboration with YMCA homecoming program.The crowd was stunned when the first person called never answered his/her phone.
Cellcom Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Avishai Marziano said cellcom hope that the US$ 10,000 winners of the “Be a Millionaire Campaign” will make face brighter future through transformed lives.
The occasion was graced by several personalities including the judges and officials from the International Bank.
Madam Boyo, is the 7th winner and 4th female that have been risen from the cell of poverty since the campaign begun nearly two months ago.
All Cellcom subscribers automatically become participants of the draw by talking for at least US$2. Every Saturday a draw attended by hundreds of subscribers and the public is held to announce the lucky winner of the week.

Although, the two books, respectively titled Nights Of The Creaking Bed and Songs Of Absence And Despair, have earlier appeared on bookshelves across the nation, they would be officially unveiled to the reading public and the media community by their publishers, Cassava Republic Press. The lunch programme is scheduled to commence at exactly 2p.m. at the Island based culture centre.
Nights Of The Creaking Bed which is a collection of some selected 14 different short stories about life and death, which the author said is set mostly in Lagos even when the setting is not clearly alluded to such, has been described as not only one of the best recent literary works in recent times on lyricism but a fine anthology, well written and well presented. While the award-winning novelist and arguably one with one of the fastest rising profiles on the Nigerian literary landscape, Seffi Atta is convinced that the book is essentially a rare kind that begins with "deliberate brevity and ends on a note of lush lyricism," foremost publisher and columnist, Toyin Akinoso said Nights Of The Creaking Bed is indeed a pleasant anthology of interesting short stories that "are so well written in clean, elegant prose." Toyin believes "If there is anything the book can teach, it is English grammar."
Toyin's judgement of Kan's work should not in any way astonish genuine followers of the Nigerian literary realm, especially those who are well acquainted with Kan's pedigree and antecedents as an author, particularly during his days as a columnist in the old romance and true-life publication, Hints magazine. To describe Toni Kan as a thoroughbred literary mind is to state the obvious, as he cuts across as an essayist, prose stylist, book reviewer and poet extraordinaire. Presently, he serves as the Head of Corporate Communications to Visafone, a fixed wireless telecoms provider in Lagos.
Kan holds both a Bachelor of Arts (B.A.) and a Masters of Arts (M.A) degrees in English (Literature) from the Universities of Jos and Lagos respectively. His works have been published widely. He has also had short stories published in anthologies like We-Men, Little Drops (1) and Diamond and Ashes. An award winning poet, essayist and short story writer, his awards have taken him to Scotland and Switzerland.
The short stories featured in Nights Of The Creaking Bed revolve around a girl who is rejected in love because she has three brothers to look after; a middle aged housewife who finds love again but has an impossible decision to make; a young man who cannot get the image of his naked, beautiful mother out of his mind; a child so poor he has to hawk onions on Christmas day, and many others. Some, initially full of hope, find their lives blighted by the cruelty of others, or by being in the wrong place at the wrong time, or by just not knowing the 'right' people.
Kan's other book to be launched tomorrow is Songs Of Absence And Despair like Nights Of The Creaking Bed is also an anthology but unlike the first book, it is exclusively poetry. It contains some 33 poems, which the author collected over a specific period of time, specifically from 2004. If you go on to source for fund without addressing the issue of capacity, the business will sooner or later crash and the money you put into it will go down the drain," he said.
The SMEDAN chief executive charged further that all Nigerians owe it a duty to develop the MSME sub-sector.
"Nigeria will not develop without the independent of this all-important sub-sector because substantial portion of the nation's population falls within the micro, small and medium sub-sector.
"The food sub-sector can develop partly by subsidising tariffs and rates. We should endeavour to push players in the industry towards development through participation in seminars like this one and creating awareness for them to embrace capacity building.
"A trained and empowered MSMEs will address some of the issues in the President's 7-point Agenda and Vision 20:20-20. A vibrant micro and small enterprise sub-sector will create wealth, jobs and guarantee security of life and property. If we fail to develop the sub-sector, it will be difficult to achieve the ideals of Vision 20:20-20," he added.
Director-General of FIIRO, Dr. Oluwole Olatunji, who represented the Minister for Science and Technology, Dr. Alhassan Bako Zaku, said that about 500 Nigerians had benefited from the research breakthroughs of FIIRO in the last 10 years.
Olatunji said that FIIRO has done successive work on cassava research for bread baking and industrial raw materials.
He said the institute has also improved on the "poundo yam" it was known for before now.
"We now have instant pounded yam and we have recorded some strides in weaning formulas and concentrates," he stressed.

Original_Search_Title: The Background Music to My Writing Life -Toni Kan
Headline: Nigeria: The Background Music to My Writing Life -Toni Kan
Body_Text:
Essayist, prose stylist, book reviewer and poet, Toni Kan works as Head Corporate Communications of Visafone. He will be presenting two of his recent books to the public at Terra Kulture, Tiamiyu Savage, Victoria Island, by Saturday April 18, 2009. He has been 'accused' severally of being too 'feminine friendly' in his writing style though he tried to defend it. Most writers know him as a romance writer. Author of the critically acclaimed and some say, erotically charged collection of short stories, 'Nights of The Creaking Bed' and the new poetry collection 'Songs of Absence and Despair,' all published by Cassava Republic Press tells Yemi Adebisi in this interview, about the sounds and songs that have become the background to his life as a writer and lover of music. Excerpts:
What was the first song you remember liking?
My parents had lots of records back in the day. I remember my mother on Saturday mornings, cleaning the house and singing along to Bob Marley or Andy Kim or Skeeter Davis or Boney M or ABBA. My dad, even though he bought all the records, was partial to the blues and R&B and Soul. I remember Commodores, Isaac Hayes, Hot Chocolate and many others. There were also Nigerian albums from groups like Blo, Ofege, and others. Hot Chocolate, I remember because of the album cover even though I am not sure I ever listened to any of their tracks. I remember an album by this blind guy, Clarence Carter or something, singing: "I was caught, making love, to another man's wife." It was illicit and forbidden and sweet and I think I found it more forbidding than Sexual Healing, which caused a lot of hoopla when I was about 10. What I couldn't get past was a blind man getting it on with another man's wife. I remember sitting and wondering what would happen if the woman's husband caught them. How would he escape? I think that was how I began to exercise my imagination. I remember my father's colleague, a principal throwing his daughter's stuff out of the house after he came back home and caught her listening to Sexual Healing. Times have changed you know. Now, kids listen to songs like I wanna sex you up and no one cares.
What song made the most impact on you as a young boy, developing a literary consciousness?
The song that hit me the most and thinking back now, I suppose I was already forging a literary consciousness even back then, was Skeeter Davis' Dear John and End of the World. I liked the melancholy and heartfelt sadness the songs evoked. Dear John was particularly so because of the sadness in the singers voice as she wrote to her lover telling him of the end of their relationship. I was seven or eight, but I knew that there was heartbreak waiting. Tell me why you won't get caught by a song that says "Don't they know it's the end of the world 'cos you don't love me any more."
I guess I have always liked songs/books/movies that touch the heart. Then there were also cuts like Let it be me a duet between Skeeter Davis and somebody I can't recall. Then there was also another track I liked I'm a lover, not a fighter.
It's funny how I recall country music songs because if you had asked me like an hour ago whether I liked country music, I would have said no.
I think I also liked Dolly Parton and Kenny Rogers especially Kenny's song about a gambler. But the one that killed me dead each time I heard it (and still does today) was Islands in the stream a duet between Dolly and Kenny.
There was this album by a Nigerian musician. I can't recall his name now but he had a song that went something like this: "yesterday, kisses and today quarrels, mixed feelings, ye ye killing suspense."
What was the first cassette or album or CD you ever bought and what did it teach you as a writer?
The first cassette I remember buying was a Yellowman cassette. Back in secondary school in the late 80s, Yellowman was a big deal and I think that was the first cassette I ever bought. I remember songs like Jamaica Nice, London Cold and stuff.
What did it teach me as a writer? I remember Yellowman liked to make lots of rhymes, you know, end rhymes. I don't remember anything apart from that. I don't think reggae acts speak good English.
You write a lot about love, in fact many people still see you as a romance writer. What song was playing when you first fell in love, something like a soundtrack to your first love?
I don't remember this because I am a hopeless romantic. I fall in love at the drop of a hat and I generally do not have a logical reason. It is for me like Eliot wrote: "Is it perfume from a dress/that makes me so digress." I could fall in love with a girl for wearing a white top over black slacks or for licking ice cream with her head bent. But I remember the song that was playing the day I had my first kiss. We had gone to visit a lady who just had a child. When we got there, the new mother was eating and said we should go see the baby in her room. The girl and I was, who technically wasn't my girlfriend, bent and kissed the girl on her forehead then we looked at each other, smiled and kissed. She was older and she taught me. Bobby Brown's Ronnie was playing.
What song do you listen to when you are writing?
I was writing my book Nights of the Creaking Bed. It was a long process but because a couple of them were written while I was on fellowship in Germany in 2003, I remember listening to Seal over and over as I wrote my novella Ballad of Rage and then the short stories. I recall songs like Heavenly from the Seal IV album. I think that's his best work yet.
On a good day, when I'm writing, I'd be listening to jazz or classical music. My favourite writing sound is Vivaldi's Four Seasons. Maik Nwosu gave me the CD in 2003 or 2004; I am not so sure. Then I could also be listening to Enya or Arturo Sandoval or some jazz standards. I've got CDs with different artistes on them.
What are your plans for your twin launch?
I'll be presenting the books: Nights of the Creaking Bed, a collection of fourteen short stories and Songs of Absence and Despair, a collection of poems published by the Cassava Republic Press.
The short story collection has been described by Molara Wood, Assistant Managing Editor, Arts & Culture at Next as "a cohesive and stylish collection, with atmospheric scenes and noir elements" while novelist Sefi Atta and author of Everything Good Will Come has described the work as one that begins with "deliberate brevity and ends on a note of lush lyricism."
Publisher and columnist, Toyin Akinosho says the short stories "are so well written, in clean, and elegant prose. If there's anything the book can teach it is English Grammar."
The book of 33 poems which were collected over a period of time beginning in 2004 is a lyrically evocative and almost funereal collection which focuses on the twin themes of absence and despair, two issues that have come to define the present generation of Nigerians who have been forced by the imperatives of survival to dwell abroad or in separate cities; men and women forced to survive on a heady cocktail of regret, nostalgia and memories. The event will feature poetry, comedy and live music.
Finally, you write a lot about Lagos. If you were to suggest a soundtrack for Lagos what would it be?
That's a simple one. Fela already gave us the soundtrack to Lagos: Confusion breaki bone o, ye pa!

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Rate Slows
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Rate Slows
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA'S annual inflation rate slowed to 14.0% in March from a revised 14.8% in February, according to the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS).
Also, core inflation, which includes items like fuel, electricity and metered water, slowed to 12.1% from 13.1% in February. The figure was revised up from 12.6%.
However, the highlights released last Tuesday by UBOS show that food prices in March increased by 0.7% due to high demand for foodstuffs locally and in the neighbouring Southern Sudan and the DR. Congo.
"The main inflation driver remains food inflation, which was at 28.32% in March from 30.39% in February," UBOS said.
The bureau said the prices for food went up in most centres, with significant rises in the prices of matooke, sweet potatoes, cassava, and Irish potatoes. Areas which registered increases in food prices in March include Gulu, Mbarara and Mbale districts.

Original_Search_Title: Judge Irked By Petition
Headline: Uganda: Judge Irked By Petition
Body_Text:
JINJA High Court judge Justice Vincent Zehurikize is concerned over the continued delay of court proceedings in an election petition filed by Salaamu Musumba.
Musumba's is challenging the Attorney General and others in the 2007 Kamuli district LC5 elections.
"It is with regret that I am adjourning the hearing of an election petition filed two years ago. All election petitions are supposed to be cleared within six months from the date of election," Zehurikize said.
He said on Wednesday that he had fixed the first three days of April for hearing of the petition but he had to adjourn the hearing following a request by Musumba's lawyer, Geoffrey Komakech.
Komakech had served the Attorney General with 86 affidavits instead of 149.
Zehurikize gave Komakech one month in which to serve the remaining 53 affidavits to the Attorney General.
Musumba (FDC) contested the 2007 LC5 election which was won by Stephen Mubiru (NRM) amidst alleged massive vote rigging and election malpractice.
Mubiru garnered 46,322 votes, Musumba 44,424 and the third candidate, Paul Lubowa, got 7,171 votes.
Musumba said she had proved to the world, particularly to the people of Kamuli, that she could not be bought like 'cassava or potatoes'.

Original_Search_Title: Tears On a Tea Estate II
Headline: Uganda: Tears On a Tea Estate II
Body_Text:
Kampala — In a continuation from last week, a Sunday Vision reporter, who spent a week working undercover as a tea picker on a tea estate, narrates her painful experience
- Saturday, February 21, 2009
By 7:00am, I am already in a taxi heading to Nakigalala. Today we are working in a place called Kimese. The veterans say it's easier to pick tea leaves from this side. All signs, though, point to yet another hot day.
I look for Asaba to give me my spreadsheet, but he is nowhere to be seen. His friend tells me he is sick and that he wants me to visit him at home after work. This is a prelude to a bad day. No teacher or free tea leaves at the end of the day. I choose a row and start picking tea leaves, though I feel bored and the work is more hectic today.
I look around for someone to talk to and, at the end of the day give me some tea leaves, but none seems bait-able. The field officer asks me why Asaba has not come and I just stare at him.
He begins making advances at me. He says we would make a good couple. I keep on picking the leaves, but this time taking my time because I fear they might order me to do it again. In addition to the sweltering heat, a pungent smell hangs over the place today.
During mealtimes, the labourers load their plates as though they were eating for the last time in preparation for the return of the Messiah. No wonder, the estate is littered with human waste. One would think the tea pickers live by the 'commandment': "Man eateth and defecateth where he worketh." Whoever feels like taking a long call does it on the estate. Is it for manure? Well, I guess that's the secret behind the great taste of tea.
Being a Saturday, the women are happy that they will find time to attend to their gardens after work.
What I have been dreading all these days finally shows up - a snake. It is spotted in time though, and chased away. A sudden chill runs through my body and a thought creeps into my mind: I must stop here and compile my experience only up to this point.
Before making up my mind, I ask a fellow worker what I should do when I encounter a snake. As if she had been expecting the question, she readily answers: "That would be an accident because these days snakes are rare on the estate. They use chemicals to scare them away."
A lady called Nalongo comes to work with cooked sweet potatoes and cassava packed in a Kaveera (polythene bag). She offers a piece to everyone around her, including me. Much as I appreciate her generosity, it's disgusting to see her dip her dirty hand into the black kaveera and fish out a piece for me. I want to refuse the offer, but the people around me are happy because they have got something to eat. With my eyes closed, I painfully munch on the potato. Surprisingly, it's tasty.
I pick tea quietly till 1:30pm when we break off for lunch. Work ends at 2:00pm today, which means we are free to go home early. After lunch, the leaves are weighed and I have 5kgs worth sh50, but I'm not bothered because I worked half-day and Asaba did not come today.
- Monday, February 22, 2009
I arrive at 7:30am and get a stern warning from the field officer that I should never arrive after 7:00am. This is my fourth day on the estate, but I'm not yet used to getting up early. I lay out my spreadsheet and head to the field, where most people are already busy at work.
As the officer registers the labourers, my colleagues start to acknowledge that I'm a "serious girl" because they thought I would not last two days on the job.
Most workers at the estate speak Ateso and Acholi and they live a wretched life. There is a woman who has only two teeth in her mouth, but she makes a lot of noise.
Asaba has not yet turned up and I didn't check on him on Saturday. The labourers are becoming friendlier to me, despite their tribalistic tendencies. The few Baganda around are an arrogant lot, treating the other tribes with disdain. "Mwe Abalugwala gino gye mirimu gyammwe (you Lugbara, this is the kind of work that suits you)."
The offended party shoots back: "Then what are you doing here?"
"I'm only looking for capital. When I get it, I will quit," the Muganda defends himself. One of the field officers is an Etesot, so he joins his group to take on the rest.
There is a very old hardworking lady. Despite her advanced age, she doesn't survive the banter. The men ask her what she was doing in her youthful days. They taunt and mock her, saying she will not get rich, however hard she tries. The lady shoots back, saying whereas she spent her youthful days having fun, they are spending theirs picking tea leaves.
There is a lot of noise in the field today. The smokers are outdoing themselves. I want to rest, but I fear lying down because of the faeces scattered all over the place.
The field officer is working with his wife. While he is supervising, the wife is picking tea leaves and the labourers are gossiping about the couple.
"I cannot risk having an affair with a woman who works on a tea estate," a labourer says.
I soon realise that although they gossip a lot, these labourers are very hardworking. Most of them are looking for school fees for their children who go to the nearby Nakigalala Estate Primary School.
At 1:30pm we queue for lunch: the usual posho and beans. Apparently, lunch also serves as their supper, because the company does not cater for that.
After lunch, everybody works with lots of vigour for it is the last hour and it determines how much money one will take home. It is so hot that one would faint. I keep checking on my watch as I count down to 3:30pm. People have several sacks of tea leaves, but they are still struggling to pick much more.
As soon as they see the truck, they hurry to take the leaves for weighing. Today I have 8kgs, so I will earn sh300, which cannot even cover half of my transport fare. It's 4:15pm and with an aching back, I leave the estate.
- Tuesday, February 23, 2009
It's raining cats and dogs in Kampala, so the taxi takes long to fill up. It's 7:30am and I have not yet arrived at the tea estate. I finally arrive at 8:20am, change my clothes and head to the field. The field officer is seething.
Before I pick a single leaf, he orders me to go back home. I try to explain that there was a very ugly traffic jam in town. I ask him to give me a second chance since I'm new on the job, but this gets him even angrier,
He starts telling me stories: "A person like Charles (Emmanuel) Mutebile is already in his office, yet he works in a bank and for you, you are just reporting; go back."
Initially I think he is just playing hard, but he insists. I even offer to pick the leaves and give them out to someone else, but he still says no.
"You have to respect your job. That is why we put "terms and conditions apply". The names of those present have already been taken down so that we know how much food to prepare for them," he adds.
"If the Muyindi (Asian) comes here and discovers that the number of people in the field is more than those registered, do you think I will have a job?"
I appreciate the officer's concern, but I still ask for another chance. I ask him what I should do about my transport and he angrily walks away. I talk to his colleague, hoping to get sympathy, but he tells me: "Johnson is my boss so his word is final. You will come back tomorrow."
I pick up my bag and leave the estate. As I walk away, one of the labourers called Lillian says: "Kale bambi turansipoti wo. Saba wano enyumba (Sorry about the money you spent on transport. You better find accommodation nearby)."
At the gate, the askari asks me whether I am sick. I tell him the officer has sent me away because I came late.
"Where do you stay? Come and we stay together," he offers.
I tell him that he should give me some time to think about his proposal. He enters his house and returns with some jackfruit, which we eat as I get more information from him.
"These Asians are mistreating us. I am just working here for a few months before I leave," he says.
I ask him how Asians mistreat them and he sadly says: "If you are sick and don't work for three days, you could lose your job. I hate Asians."
I finish my share of the jackfruit and walk out of the gate.
- Wednesday, February 24
I arrive at the estate by 7:00am. Everyone is happy to see me again. "Diana, I'm glad you managed to come early today," the field officer who sent me away yesterday tells me.
Most workers are already picking leaves. I am looking for Asaba to give me my spreadsheet. I see him at a distance looking at me, but today he doesn't seem happy to see me. I ask for my spreadsheet and he throws it at my feet.
I choose a row and start picking my own tea.
- February 24
It is 2:00pm and the officers are ordering us to go back to work. In the field, I work next to a Rwandan man who is smoking like a chimney. Charles soon joins him in the smoking.
As I struggle with the leaves, a woman called Mercy comes to me with "good" news: "I have got for you a job as a housegirl. Leave this job. It will make you age quickly."
I thank her and promise to get back to her on Friday. By this time, I cannot bend because my back is hurting. I am also scared of the red ants. I am counting down to 3:30pm.
The truck finally arrives to take our leaves. A certain man who has been making advances at me offers to give me some of his leaves so that I get more kilos. This is the only way men express their feelings. I accept his 6kgs of leaves, but not his advances.
I have 11kgs, from which I earn sh600. The most hardworking labourers pick 40kgs worth sh2,000. After weighing my leaves, I head back to Kajjansi. My back and legs are hurting.
- February 25
This is my last day here and I am curious to visit the workers' quarters. Considering their meagre earnings, I imagine they live in squalid conditions. Since I've arrived early, I head straight to the quarters before signing in.
No surprises for me - the place is filthy, just like I thought. Faeces dropped indiscriminately by the many children in the compound welcome me to the quarters. I have to watch my step to avoid stepping on the faeces which are scattered all over the place like landmines. The faeces look dry and scaly, like they have been there for some time. Firewood rests on the verandah.
Two or three workers share a room, depending on what their Asian bosses decide. The two-cubicle toilets are grimy, you have to step over urine and faeces to get to the hole. Since most of the men have not yet gone to work, I ask Kasajja to let me see how large the rooms are. They are more like pig sties than human habitats. The walls are stained with soot from cooking indoors on firewood.
Kasajja's roommate has already left for work, leaving his bed unmade. There isn't much of a bed to make anyway, since the blanket is in tatters and the mattress is old and thin.
It is not even possible to tell the colour of the bedsheets as layers of dirt conceal the original colour. It is easy to tell that the owner only bathes when rain traps him out in the field picking tea.
The room itself has such an acrid smell emanating from within. I want to hold my nose, but fear that I might offend my host. I finally take leave, hoping to find a tidier home next door.
The occupants are a couple, so they are a bit more organised, but still the smoke stains on the walls make the house dirty. It is still morning, but the workers' babies are out playing, half-naked. They look malnourished. Most of the kids have running noses and when they cough, it is like they have amplifiers in their throats.
Some workers have school-going children. At 8:00am they are hurrying off to school; bare-footed, wearing dirty torn uniforms and carrying their books in black buveera (polythene bags). A lucky few first take breakfast at home -sweet potatoes and black tea (no doubt without sugar).
A wave of pity engulfs me as I see a girl seemingly happy with her breakfast. "Muninde. Kamalewo lumonde wange (Wait for me to finish my sweet potato)," she calls after her friends who are hurrying off to school with unkempt hair.
As I move around the workers' quarters, I notice one of my colleagues did not work. She is not feeling well. Her back is hurting. So she is going to get herself medicine at the clinic. The estate provides free medicine to sick workers. She heads to the clinic to get some tablets.
In a place where boiled water is a luxury, the sick woman uses water from a dirty jerrycan to swallow her medicine. Her fingers are black from picking tea leaves over a long period of time.
"I joined the estate in 2007 when I lost my parents. One of my relatives advised me to pick tea leaves for a living. It was not easy, but I am used and I work with my husband," she says.
She has scars on her legs, which she says resulted from being scratched by tea branches.
I keep around the quarters with the children, but I cannot understand what they are saying as most of them speak Acholi.
At 1:30pm, I return to the field. It is lunch time and the labourers are queuing for food. Some of them are joined by their children, who share their lunch. Some parents escape and take food for their children at home. Some of these children are still babies, but they eat posho and beans.
Since I have not worked today, I leave the estate at 3:00pm.
For the one week I have worked here, I earned a mere sh3,000, which cannot even cover my transport fare for one day.
Labourers answer nature's call in the tea bushes
- Wednesday, February 24
It's only 10:00am and it's already very hot. As I pick the tea leaves, the field officer (Charles) joins me. We start a conversation and he eventually opens up. He says when he was new at the estate, working here was hard, but he persevered, until he became a senior tea picker. His boss then promoted him to field officer.
As he talks, the speed at which he picks the leaves increases and soon, his sack is full.
"Where is this expired man?" he asks about a certain old man who has been working in the field.
I ask him why he is so disrespectful and he arrogantly says: "He is old and useless to the Government."
Most of the pickers today are women. When Charles moves ahead, Akwi squats and a few seconds later, huge, green flies converge onto the scene. It is a long call. Some people have seen her, but no one seems to be bothered.
The tea leaves on this side of the estate are soon finished, so we head to the side, where some men are spraying the tea bushes. Apart from boots, they are not wearing any other protective gear. This field is fresh. The bushes are short, so we have to bend low to pick the leaves, which makes work harder. No wonder the workers are reluctant to work here.
As lunch time draws nearer, the workers wash yesterday's remains off their plates. As we eat, a woman called Ayo turns up. She is late, but the lady serving food thinks she has already eaten.
Ayo loses her cool and throws her plateful of food into the saucepan containing other labourers' soup. The battle line is drawn.
"Some of us don't eat pork, but she is pouring back her soup in our soup. Let her apologise," Grace calls out.
"Try to behave. This is not your house. Stop your arrogance and bush manners," says Johnson, the field officer.
Ayo misses lunch, but returns to the field to toil under the hot sun. It is very hot. Ayo's behaviour is the topic of discussion this afternoon. "That lady eats python meat and now she is using her plate to pour soup into our soup," a labourer shouts out.
