Original_Search_Title: Storm Blows Away School Roofs
Headline: Uganda: Storm Blows Away School Roofs
Body_Text:
Kampala — A storm on Saturday destroyed school buildings and a hospital in Ngora county, Kumi district.
The storm that left people homeless, also killed birds and felled trees and electricity poles.
Several banana and cassava gardens were also destroyed.
An eldery woman, Gladys Oluka, was injured after a tree fell on Silas Oluka's house in Bishop Kitching Christian Training Complex.
It also blew away the roofs in Ngora High School, Ngora Girls' Secondary School, Ngora Girls' Boarding Primary School and Ngora School for the Deaf and the Blind.
Others affected were Ngora Comprehensive Nursing and Midwifery School, Ngora Freda Carr Hospital and Ngora Boys' Primary School.
Ngora High School director of studies John Michael Odeke said the storm started at about 6:00pm.

Original_Search_Title: Former Rebel Re-Settles Child Soldiers
Headline: Uganda: Former Rebel Re-Settles Child Soldiers
Body_Text:
Kampala — GRACE Arach has seen tough times - abducted by rebels, raped, coerced into marriage, forced to kill, until she said: "Enough is enough." The 23-year-old escaped from the captivity of the Lord's Resistance Army, a rebel group that has abducted, massacred and maimed people in northern Uganda for 20 years.
Sitting pensively with her palm on her cheek, Arach speaks as if she was still in throes of abduction. But as she recovers from the horror of war, Arach has one thing on her mind: to help former abducted children settle in the communities.
Through Children / Youth Peace Builders (CAP), a local advocacy community organisation, Arach is teaching her community how to treat the former child abductees, help the reintergrate into society and learn their responsibility towards the community.
CAP-Gulu was formed in 2000 and is affiliated to CAP International which deals with horrors arising from the effects of war in conflict regions like Colombia, Angola and Thailand.
In only nine years, Arach and her team have undertaken counselling, social interaction and peace building programmes in Gulu.
With a team of 90 youth, she has trained former child soldiers in vocational skills to generate income and also learn how to live in society positively.
"We encourage them to go back to school. Some went but later dropped out due to lack of school fees," Arach said. They also teach former child-mothers who intend to get married on how to pick the right partner. They have also helped counsel and rehabilitate children who have suffered trauma.
According to Human Rights Watch, more than 20,000 children have been abducted by the LRA since 1986. However, some who manage to escape from captivity have no home to return to while others are neglected.
"The community rejects them saying they killed people, stole and looted their property," Arach explains.
Arach, a native of Pawel village, Amuru district was only 12 and in P7, when she was abducted by the LRA.
On March 23, 1996 when she was coming from Pabbo to visit her grandmother in Pawel, Fr Santo, a priest in Pabbo, was going in the same direction and her mother requested him for a lift."
"We were approaching Pawel when people dressed in army uniform stopped us. "We were six people, five men and I. The rebels pulled me out of the car and removed my shoes. They put a land mine in the vehicle and burnt it. We moved through the bush the whole day and reached a trading centre called Ajulu where they looted shops and released several prisoners in Lugore prison in Patiko sub-county," she says.
Arach says after moving several kilometres, her feet were swollen. The rebels released the priest and the rest of the men but took her with them: "I was given 50kg of sugar to carry. We moved from Ajulu, via Pabbo and finally reached Atiak.
"The rebels said they wanted me registered. I thought they were going to write my name, but instead gave me 30 strokes of the cane and told me to do the same to other new recruits."
"I was tired and could not move any farther. I fell down and it is at this point that the UPDF soldiers attacked us and all the rebels ran in different directions. I hid under a cassava tree. After a while, the UPDF went away.
"Otti Lagony, the second in command of LRA came back, tied me with a rope so that I could not escape. I was made to carry beans, posho saucepans and sugar.
"We went to Otti's place in Pabbo village in Amuru district. I wanted to run away but he put me at gun point and raped me."
She adds that she stayed in Otti's place for a month and was returned to Lagony as his seventh wife.
She was trained as a soldier and sent for duty. Under Lagony, their areas of operation were Apach, Goma and Pabbo.
"Shortly after that, the UPDF attacked our camp. They sprayed the camp with bullets and as I ran, they shot me through the right breast and I fell down but I quickly got up and ran. We won the battle, but the UPDF helicopter gunships surrounded us.
"As I was healing, the rebels went and abducted the Aboke girls. The Sister followed them and the rebels released about 105 and kept 30 beautiful ones.
"In February 1997, we heard news over BBC that Lagony and a group of 3,000 rebels had surrendered to the government, yet it was not true. Kony and the Sudanese government were upset. They gave Lagony only five days to leave Uganda. We battled for two days with UPDF until we crossed into Sudan."
"We helped the government of Sudan to fight the Sudan People's Liberation Army (SPLA). They used to sprinkle us with oil and water to cleanse us of 'demons'. Kony used to believe in the Bible and spirits. There was a spirit for operations and discipline. But these spirits used to come in form of Kony himself. He would become possessed and speak in different tongues.
"It prepared us for operations. It made us courageous and disciplined.
"In 1999, cholera attacked our camp and I got infected. They threw us out of the camp sayingh we shall spread to others. Many people died because there was no medication. I stayed out of the camp for three days without food," Arach says.
Meanwhile, Kony and Lagony had developed a poor relationship. Kony suspected Lagony wanted to kill him and take over.
"Lagony was arrested and killed. A certain man called John Tata took over as the overall commander, but had little military experience. Many of the fighters became demoralised and escaped. That is the time I also planned to escape.
"I was given to a man called Odongo as the 10th wife. I spent two days with him and was sent to Uganda for operations. The Sudan government had grown impatient with Kony and stopped giving us food and ammunition. This is because Kony had killed Lagony, whom they trusted.
A week after Odongo had left, Arach talked to two child mothers about the plan to escape but they were scared. Luckily, Kony had left the camp for a meeting in Sudan and there was little military presence.
"I pulled the two mothers and we escaped. We moved for two days and ended up in the hands of Arab soldiers who then handed us to UNICEF. We were flown to Khartoum and later to Uganda in 2001. I was received by World Vision.
"After a week I was admitted to a school in Gulu. I sat for mid-term exams and scored Aggregate 30. "I realised my potential and saw the importance of rejoining school. I had not registered for exams, so I never did PLE. In 2002, I was taken to Aboke Girls by a lady who paid my fees. I was admitted to S.1, but cautioned that I would not sit for S.4 if I did not have a PLE certificate. I registered for PLE in a nearby school where I could study Form One and also do PLE," says Arach.
She scored Aggregate 21 in PLE. In 2004, she sat O' Level and passed in Division 2. "I was given History, Economics, Divinity and Geography at A' Level." Arach later scored 8 points and was admitted to Gulu University, where she is doing a diploma in development studies.
At the same time, she volunteers in CAP-Gulu. The team has undertaken peace building efforts to show to the community the effects of war. "We use drama and radio talkshows to show the people that war is bad. We stage scenes like how abductions go on in the bush. It helps them to know that the children were forced into war and need community acceptance.
Through the sexual violence study, CAP found out how the children in the community are abused. "We aimed at finding out the real cause of the rampant sexual violence on children and how to find a solution," she says.
Arach has also attended international conferences on former child abductees. "In 2003, in the US, I met former child soldiers from Colombia, Sierra Leone and many other countries. We discussed how to prevent children from being involved in war and good enough, the UN resolution 1612 was passed to protect children."
Last year, Arach went to Sierra Leone to learn how they have managed to restore peace after decades of war. Despite this success, Arach says she is facing some challenges. "I want to go for further studies, but I do not have school fees. I got admission to study a degree at Mukono University, but because I had no fees, I opted for a diploma in Gulu University."
For the moment, she is happy to be helping her community. "My people are voiceless and I am working hard to improve their lives," she says.

Original_Search_Title: Tears On a Tea Estate II
Headline: Uganda: Tears On a Tea Estate II
Body_Text:
Kampala — In a continuation from last week, a Sunday Vision reporter, who spent a week working undercover as a tea picker on a tea estate, narrates her painful experience
- Saturday, February 21, 2009
By 7:00am, I am already in a taxi heading to Nakigalala. Today we are working in a place called Kimese. The veterans say it's easier to pick tea leaves from this side. All signs, though, point to yet another hot day.
I look for Asaba to give me my spreadsheet, but he is nowhere to be seen. His friend tells me he is sick and that he wants me to visit him at home after work. This is a prelude to a bad day. No teacher or free tea leaves at the end of the day. I choose a row and start picking tea leaves, though I feel bored and the work is more hectic today.
I look around for someone to talk to and, at the end of the day give me some tea leaves, but none seems bait-able. The field officer asks me why Asaba has not come and I just stare at him.
He begins making advances at me. He says we would make a good couple. I keep on picking the leaves, but this time taking my time because I fear they might order me to do it again. In addition to the sweltering heat, a pungent smell hangs over the place today.
During mealtimes, the labourers load their plates as though they were eating for the last time in preparation for the return of the Messiah. No wonder, the estate is littered with human waste. One would think the tea pickers live by the 'commandment': "Man eateth and defecateth where he worketh." Whoever feels like taking a long call does it on the estate. Is it for manure? Well, I guess that's the secret behind the great taste of tea.
Being a Saturday, the women are happy that they will find time to attend to their gardens after work.
What I have been dreading all these days finally shows up - a snake. It is spotted in time though, and chased away. A sudden chill runs through my body and a thought creeps into my mind: I must stop here and compile my experience only up to this point.
Before making up my mind, I ask a fellow worker what I should do when I encounter a snake. As if she had been expecting the question, she readily answers: "That would be an accident because these days snakes are rare on the estate. They use chemicals to scare them away."
A lady called Nalongo comes to work with cooked sweet potatoes and cassava packed in a Kaveera (polythene bag). She offers a piece to everyone around her, including me. Much as I appreciate her generosity, it's disgusting to see her dip her dirty hand into the black kaveera and fish out a piece for me. I want to refuse the offer, but the people around me are happy because they have got something to eat. With my eyes closed, I painfully munch on the potato. Surprisingly, it's tasty.
I pick tea quietly till 1:30pm when we break off for lunch. Work ends at 2:00pm today, which means we are free to go home early. After lunch, the leaves are weighed and I have 5kgs worth sh50, but I'm not bothered because I worked half-day and Asaba did not come today.
- Monday, February 22, 2009
I arrive at 7:30am and get a stern warning from the field officer that I should never arrive after 7:00am. This is my fourth day on the estate, but I'm not yet used to getting up early. I lay out my spreadsheet and head to the field, where most people are already busy at work.
As the officer registers the labourers, my colleagues start to acknowledge that I'm a "serious girl" because they thought I would not last two days on the job.
Most workers at the estate speak Ateso and Acholi and they live a wretched life. There is a woman who has only two teeth in her mouth, but she makes a lot of noise.
Asaba has not yet turned up and I didn't check on him on Saturday. The labourers are becoming friendlier to me, despite their tribalistic tendencies. The few Baganda around are an arrogant lot, treating the other tribes with disdain. "Mwe Abalugwala gino gye mirimu gyammwe (you Lugbara, this is the kind of work that suits you)."
The offended party shoots back: "Then what are you doing here?"
"I'm only looking for capital. When I get it, I will quit," the Muganda defends himself. One of the field officers is an Etesot, so he joins his group to take on the rest.
There is a very old hardworking lady. Despite her advanced age, she doesn't survive the banter. The men ask her what she was doing in her youthful days. They taunt and mock her, saying she will not get rich, however hard she tries. The lady shoots back, saying whereas she spent her youthful days having fun, they are spending theirs picking tea leaves.
There is a lot of noise in the field today. The smokers are outdoing themselves. I want to rest, but I fear lying down because of the faeces scattered all over the place.
The field officer is working with his wife. While he is supervising, the wife is picking tea leaves and the labourers are gossiping about the couple.
"I cannot risk having an affair with a woman who works on a tea estate," a labourer says.
I soon realise that although they gossip a lot, these labourers are very hardworking. Most of them are looking for school fees for their children who go to the nearby Nakigalala Estate Primary School.
At 1:30pm we queue for lunch: the usual posho and beans. Apparently, lunch also serves as their supper, because the company does not cater for that.
After lunch, everybody works with lots of vigour for it is the last hour and it determines how much money one will take home. It is so hot that one would faint. I keep checking on my watch as I count down to 3:30pm. People have several sacks of tea leaves, but they are still struggling to pick much more.
As soon as they see the truck, they hurry to take the leaves for weighing. Today I have 8kgs, so I will earn sh300, which cannot even cover half of my transport fare. It's 4:15pm and with an aching back, I leave the estate.
- Tuesday, February 23, 2009
It's raining cats and dogs in Kampala, so the taxi takes long to fill up. It's 7:30am and I have not yet arrived at the tea estate. I finally arrive at 8:20am, change my clothes and head to the field. The field officer is seething.
Before I pick a single leaf, he orders me to go back home. I try to explain that there was a very ugly traffic jam in town. I ask him to give me a second chance since I'm new on the job, but this gets him even angrier,
He starts telling me stories: "A person like Charles (Emmanuel) Mutebile is already in his office, yet he works in a bank and for you, you are just reporting; go back."
Initially I think he is just playing hard, but he insists. I even offer to pick the leaves and give them out to someone else, but he still says no.
"You have to respect your job. That is why we put "terms and conditions apply". The names of those present have already been taken down so that we know how much food to prepare for them," he adds.
"If the Muyindi (Asian) comes here and discovers that the number of people in the field is more than those registered, do you think I will have a job?"
I appreciate the officer's concern, but I still ask for another chance. I ask him what I should do about my transport and he angrily walks away. I talk to his colleague, hoping to get sympathy, but he tells me: "Johnson is my boss so his word is final. You will come back tomorrow."
I pick up my bag and leave the estate. As I walk away, one of the labourers called Lillian says: "Kale bambi turansipoti wo. Saba wano enyumba (Sorry about the money you spent on transport. You better find accommodation nearby)."
At the gate, the askari asks me whether I am sick. I tell him the officer has sent me away because I came late.
"Where do you stay? Come and we stay together," he offers.
I tell him that he should give me some time to think about his proposal. He enters his house and returns with some jackfruit, which we eat as I get more information from him.
"These Asians are mistreating us. I am just working here for a few months before I leave," he says.
I ask him how Asians mistreat them and he sadly says: "If you are sick and don't work for three days, you could lose your job. I hate Asians."
I finish my share of the jackfruit and walk out of the gate.
- Wednesday, February 24
I arrive at the estate by 7:00am. Everyone is happy to see me again. "Diana, I'm glad you managed to come early today," the field officer who sent me away yesterday tells me.
Most workers are already picking leaves. I am looking for Asaba to give me my spreadsheet. I see him at a distance looking at me, but today he doesn't seem happy to see me. I ask for my spreadsheet and he throws it at my feet.
I choose a row and start picking my own tea.
- February 24
It is 2:00pm and the officers are ordering us to go back to work. In the field, I work next to a Rwandan man who is smoking like a chimney. Charles soon joins him in the smoking.
As I struggle with the leaves, a woman called Mercy comes to me with "good" news: "I have got for you a job as a housegirl. Leave this job. It will make you age quickly."
I thank her and promise to get back to her on Friday. By this time, I cannot bend because my back is hurting. I am also scared of the red ants. I am counting down to 3:30pm.
The truck finally arrives to take our leaves. A certain man who has been making advances at me offers to give me some of his leaves so that I get more kilos. This is the only way men express their feelings. I accept his 6kgs of leaves, but not his advances.
I have 11kgs, from which I earn sh600. The most hardworking labourers pick 40kgs worth sh2,000. After weighing my leaves, I head back to Kajjansi. My back and legs are hurting.
- February 25
This is my last day here and I am curious to visit the workers' quarters. Considering their meagre earnings, I imagine they live in squalid conditions. Since I've arrived early, I head straight to the quarters before signing in.
No surprises for me - the place is filthy, just like I thought. Faeces dropped indiscriminately by the many children in the compound welcome me to the quarters. I have to watch my step to avoid stepping on the faeces which are scattered all over the place like landmines. The faeces look dry and scaly, like they have been there for some time. Firewood rests on the verandah.
Two or three workers share a room, depending on what their Asian bosses decide. The two-cubicle toilets are grimy, you have to step over urine and faeces to get to the hole. Since most of the men have not yet gone to work, I ask Kasajja to let me see how large the rooms are. They are more like pig sties than human habitats. The walls are stained with soot from cooking indoors on firewood.
Kasajja's roommate has already left for work, leaving his bed unmade. There isn't much of a bed to make anyway, since the blanket is in tatters and the mattress is old and thin.
It is not even possible to tell the colour of the bedsheets as layers of dirt conceal the original colour. It is easy to tell that the owner only bathes when rain traps him out in the field picking tea.
The room itself has such an acrid smell emanating from within. I want to hold my nose, but fear that I might offend my host. I finally take leave, hoping to find a tidier home next door.
The occupants are a couple, so they are a bit more organised, but still the smoke stains on the walls make the house dirty. It is still morning, but the workers' babies are out playing, half-naked. They look malnourished. Most of the kids have running noses and when they cough, it is like they have amplifiers in their throats.
Some workers have school-going children. At 8:00am they are hurrying off to school; bare-footed, wearing dirty torn uniforms and carrying their books in black buveera (polythene bags). A lucky few first take breakfast at home -sweet potatoes and black tea (no doubt without sugar).
A wave of pity engulfs me as I see a girl seemingly happy with her breakfast. "Muninde. Kamalewo lumonde wange (Wait for me to finish my sweet potato)," she calls after her friends who are hurrying off to school with unkempt hair.
As I move around the workers' quarters, I notice one of my colleagues did not work. She is not feeling well. Her back is hurting. So she is going to get herself medicine at the clinic. The estate provides free medicine to sick workers. She heads to the clinic to get some tablets.
In a place where boiled water is a luxury, the sick woman uses water from a dirty jerrycan to swallow her medicine. Her fingers are black from picking tea leaves over a long period of time.
"I joined the estate in 2007 when I lost my parents. One of my relatives advised me to pick tea leaves for a living. It was not easy, but I am used and I work with my husband," she says.
She has scars on her legs, which she says resulted from being scratched by tea branches.
I keep around the quarters with the children, but I cannot understand what they are saying as most of them speak Acholi.
At 1:30pm, I return to the field. It is lunch time and the labourers are queuing for food. Some of them are joined by their children, who share their lunch. Some parents escape and take food for their children at home. Some of these children are still babies, but they eat posho and beans.
Since I have not worked today, I leave the estate at 3:00pm.
For the one week I have worked here, I earned a mere sh3,000, which cannot even cover my transport fare for one day.
Labourers answer nature's call in the tea bushes
- Wednesday, February 24
It's only 10:00am and it's already very hot. As I pick the tea leaves, the field officer (Charles) joins me. We start a conversation and he eventually opens up. He says when he was new at the estate, working here was hard, but he persevered, until he became a senior tea picker. His boss then promoted him to field officer.
As he talks, the speed at which he picks the leaves increases and soon, his sack is full.
"Where is this expired man?" he asks about a certain old man who has been working in the field.
I ask him why he is so disrespectful and he arrogantly says: "He is old and useless to the Government."
Most of the pickers today are women. When Charles moves ahead, Akwi squats and a few seconds later, huge, green flies converge onto the scene. It is a long call. Some people have seen her, but no one seems to be bothered.
The tea leaves on this side of the estate are soon finished, so we head to the side, where some men are spraying the tea bushes. Apart from boots, they are not wearing any other protective gear. This field is fresh. The bushes are short, so we have to bend low to pick the leaves, which makes work harder. No wonder the workers are reluctant to work here.
As lunch time draws nearer, the workers wash yesterday's remains off their plates. As we eat, a woman called Ayo turns up. She is late, but the lady serving food thinks she has already eaten.
Ayo loses her cool and throws her plateful of food into the saucepan containing other labourers' soup. The battle line is drawn.
"Some of us don't eat pork, but she is pouring back her soup in our soup. Let her apologise," Grace calls out.
"Try to behave. This is not your house. Stop your arrogance and bush manners," says Johnson, the field officer.
Ayo misses lunch, but returns to the field to toil under the hot sun. It is very hot. Ayo's behaviour is the topic of discussion this afternoon. "That lady eats python meat and now she is using her plate to pour soup into our soup," a labourer shouts out.

Original_Search_Title: Storm Blows Away School Roofs
Headline: Uganda: Storm Blows Away School Roofs
Body_Text:
Kampala — A storm on Saturday destroyed school buildings and a hospital in Ngora county, Kumi district.
The storm that left people homeless, also killed birds and felled trees and electricity poles.
Several banana and cassava gardens were also destroyed.
An eldery woman, Gladys Oluka, was injured after a tree fell on Silas Oluka's house in Bishop Kitching Christian Training Complex.
It also blew away the roofs in Ngora High School, Ngora Girls' Secondary School, Ngora Girls' Boarding Primary School and Ngora School for the Deaf and the Blind.
Others affected were Ngora Comprehensive Nursing and Midwifery School, Ngora Freda Carr Hospital and Ngora Boys' Primary School.
Ngora High School director of studies John Michael Odeke said the storm started at about 6:00pm.

Through Children / Youth Peace Builders (CAP), a local advocacy community organisation, Arach is teaching her community how to treat the former child abductees, help the reintergrate into society and learn their responsibility towards the community.
CAP-Gulu was formed in 2000 and is affiliated to CAP International which deals with horrors arising from the effects of war in conflict regions like Colombia, Angola and Thailand.

"We encourage them to go back to school. Some went but later dropped out due to lack of school fees," Arach said. They also teach former child-mothers who intend to get married on how to pick the right partner. They have also helped counsel and rehabilitate children who have suffered trauma.
According to Human Rights Watch, more than 20,000 children have been abducted by the LRA since 1986. However, some who manage to escape from captivity have no home to return to while others are neglected.

Original_Search_Title: Smedan to Market Naseni Technology to Msmes
Headline: Nigeria: Smedan to Market Naseni Technology to Msmes
Body_Text:
The Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria (SMEDAN) and the National Agency for Science and Engineering Infrastructure [NASENI] will soon sign a memorandum of understanding for a major collaboration that will enable SMEDAN have access to NASENI's facilities nationwide and market its technology to micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs) in Nigeria.
This was recently disclosed by the Director-General of NASENI, Professor Olusegun Adewoye while receiving his SMEDAN counterpart, Alhaji Muhammad Nadada Umar, in his Idu Office in the Federal Capital Territory.
Pofessor Adewoye described the visit of the Director-General of SMEDAN as a good omen, lamenting that previous efforts by both federal government agencies to collaborate had failed.
Professor Adewoye also disclosed that NASENI has a virtual laboratory in its Abuja Office linked to eight NASENI research centres and nine associate institutes across the country. He stated further that these facilities as well as the 74 mini-foundries located in some tertiary and technical institutions across the country are available for SMEDAN's use.
NASENI, he said, is currently working with some public and private sector organisations in the development of science and engineering infrastructure and that the Agency has a databank of cassava processing machines with advanced technology ready to be commissioned.
Earlier, the Director-General of SMEDAN, Alhaji Muhammad Nadada Umar, noted that Nigeria would not be able to develop without the active collaboration of the various government agencies involved in enterprise development. He added that investment in science and engineering infrastructure was imperative for economic development and growth.

Original_Search_Title: PMDC Office in Kenema Shut Down
Headline: Sierra Leone: PMDC Office in Kenema Shut Down
Body_Text:
Freetown — Activities of the Peoples Movement for Democratic Change (PMDC) in Kenema were on Sunday abruptly halted after the Dama Road building that houses the party's office was unexpected bolted by the PMDC Trustee eastern region, preventing members from accessing the building.
Sources close to the party in Kenema claimed that Lucinda Dasama decided to seal off the building on the grounds that the Muslim wing of the PMDC was on a clandestine move to unseat the leadership of the party, which according to her, was an attempt to undermine her good relationship with current leader, Charles Francis Margai.
When contacted on her cell phone number 076-659-768, Dasama could not respond to the issue and instead switched off her phone after realizing that the call was being made by this reporter.
Meanwhile, PMDC stalwarts were said to have collected items, including flowers, cola nuts, cassava and other materials on Sunday to offer sacrifices for good leadership and progress in the party. The ceremony was said to have been held in favor of one of the prospective contenders for the party's leadership, Dauda Tombo Bangura.
"We are totally against the current leadership of Margai; that is why we are offering this sacrifice for spiritual intervention to ensure a change within the party's ranks," Amadu Komeh, a stout PMDC support in Kenema told Concord Times.
He alleged that Dasama has been in the usual habit of dragging party members to the police whenever there was problem within their ranks, despite a common understanding among members that all squabbles should be settled at the party's secretariat.
He said the prayers and sacrifice were for peace and progress in the party.
"This is not the first time she has taken us to the police. It is true that she is the owner of the building but the party has rented it for a certain period of time. We cannot continue to allow her to be abusing our rights, so we've now decided to write a formal letter to the party headquarters in Freetown about her unpleasant behavior," Komeh said.
However, national publicity secretary of the PMDC, Mohamed Bangura said it was against their constitution for any individual to close the party's office without any reason.
He observed that the tension in Kenema might not be unconnected with the party's delegates' conference scheduled to be held shortly in the area.
"I don't know much about the incident in Kenema but I am sure it is because of the upcoming convention as all party members have their own camps," Bangura said.
Head of police media unit in Kenema, PC 10322 Kamara said the brief commotion that ensued as a result of the closure of the party's office was only quelled down with the swift intervention of the police. He said a team of police personnel were immediately dispatched to the scene to keep the peace.

Original_Search_Title: The Macabre Dance in Delta Council
Headline: Nigeria: The Macabre Dance in Delta Council
Body_Text:
Asaba — Drums of war beats echoing from the Isoko South Local Government Area of Delta State which started to blare since December 2008 appears to reverberate across the gamut of actors in the in the state chapter of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). The crisis between a section of the legislative arm of the council led by the Leader, Goodnews Obiodeh and the council chairman, Chief Askia Ogieh close observers believe, has got to its crescendo and possesses the power to undermine the peace and security agenda of the Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan administration.
The situation in the council at the moment could best be described and likened to the Biblical Tower of Babel, characterised by confusion, claims and counter claims. While a faction of the legislature claims to have suspended their colleagues, the latter on their part also claimed they have suspended the former. The Odiodeh-led faction has also passed a resolution removing the council chairman from office. But the state PDP on its part has gone ahead to suspend the 10 councillors, calling for the head of the council chairman.
Trouble actually started when Obiodeh leading nine other councillors - Majority Leader, Alex Izogo, Chief Whip, Omoehefe Ohoriemu, Deputy Majority Leader, Jephthar Odharo, Bono Obowomamo, Felix Okpoudhu, Kingsley Obeidhoko, Kenneth Owolo and Ajiri Atonuje, out of the 20 in the state resolved and wrote to the Speaker of the State House of Assembly in a letter dated, December 9, 2008 accusing Ogieh, of financial impropriety. He alleged that the chairman had misappropriated the council's fund to the tune of N1.2 billion. Other allegations included absence of visible projects on ground throughout the local government area to justify the arbitrary spending of the allocation; arbitrary awards of contracts to fictitious unincorporated companies and payments of over 70 per cent mobilisation to some of the companies in total disregard of the Delta State government directive not to pay mobilisation fees to any contractor. Other allegations included: "Failure to comply with the fiscal responsibility bill by running the local government budget into deficit in the sum of N39.6 million, which was not appropriated for; Inability to furnish the legislative House with income and expenditure analysis from May 15 till date; extraordinary inflation of environmental sanitation bill to the tune of N22 million in a day." They also accused the council chairman of "misappropriation of funds through false declaration of secondary school enrolment figures" and "diversion of N16 million meant for the payment of teachers salaries, extravagant spending on entertainment, excessive spending on security vote."
This they followed up with a letter on December 10 to relevant financial institutions and bodies, informing that in view of the alleged inability of the council chairman to furnish the House with the income and expenditure, the council's accounts have been frozen.
But Blessing Ese, deputy leader of the House and nine others in a letter the following day, December 11, also responded. Their words: "It is necessary to state that the executive chairman of Isoko South LG Council had always presented the income and expenditure to the legislative arm till date, in accordance with the Delta State Local Government Law...We hereby resolved that the purported letter dated 10th December 2008 freezing the council account is hereby, dismissed and should be confined to the dustbin, as it is a product of mischief and perverse minds," and charge "the banks concerned and the general public to continue their normal financial transaction with Isoko South Local Government Council.
The 10 councillors alleging threat to their lives, had to leave the state and relocate to Lagos where they also briefed the press on these allegations. But Ese, and her group including Beatrice Igogo, Meeting Edobebe, Obaro Godspower, Victor Eterigho, Henry Iviero, Peter Sylvester, Thompson Edygbo, Reuben Idama and Glory Ukpeowe also took the matter to the public.
Ese's group in their briefing observed thus: "We have taken pains as co-councillors to study and investigate the various allegations against the Executive Chairman, Chief Askia Ogieh by our colleagues and we have not been able to ascertain any fiscal misdeed, misappropriation, profligacy or diversion of council funds as alleged. The records are verifiable.
"As councillors, we are empowered by Section 23 of the Delta State Local Government Law to approve the budgetary proposals of the council and ensure strict compliance with the budgetary bye-law. On our part, collectively we have not found the chairman wanting. While we condemn in strongest terms, suitable, that strokes of macabre dance exhibited by our colleagues in the media house in Lagos, with all the trappings of falsehood and misinformation, we must point out, clearly that the legislative mandate vested on us as councillors of Isoko South Local Government Council is exercisable, only at the hallowed chambers of the House at Oleh, the headquarters of the council."
They accused the leader of the House "and his co-travellers" of having "abdicated their duty and legislative mandate to forge a synergy with the executive arm of the council to move Isoko South Local Government Council to the next level of infrastructural and human resources development. At present the leader of the legislative arm does not enjoy the confidence and loyalty of the majority of councillors in Isoko South Local Government Area. His actions are antithetical to the noble ideals of democracy."
Observers have wondered on what the possible factors that could have bred this state of affairs in the council. Each party, as expected, has come up with reasons that justify their positions. Ogieh, and the deputy leader, all blamed the action of the opponents on the chairman's refusal to succumb to their demand to have 90 per cent of the council's allocations for Christmas gifts. Ese, deputy leader of the House during his briefing had observed, "the prognosis of the crisis in the Isoko South Legislative Arm which has dislocated and hamstrung our legislative duties...arose from the unbridled avarice of our co-councillors that wanted 90 per cent of the entire budgetary appropriation for Christmas given to them at the expense of other stakeholders in the LGA. When it dawned on them that they could not have their way, they embarked on this criminal misadventure of unsubstantiated allegations against the office and person of the executive chairman, Chief Askia Ogieh."
A group that styles itself as the Concerned Citizens of Isoko South also echoed this position. The group in a statement endorsed by one Akpofure Joe alleged, "the origin of their campaign of calumny against our amiable chairman is traceable to the refusal of the chairman to give them 90 per cent of the budgetary appropriation for Christmas. Our investigation revealed that on December 8, 2008, the chairman invited all the 20 councillors of Isoko South to a meeting to discuss modalities for sharing the Christmas gifts which were appropriated for in the 2008 supplementary budget for the council. Nineteen of the councillors were present at the meeting where the chairman said that N8 million was appropriated for Christmas gifts. This was to be shared among six interest groups including members of the National Union of Local Government Employees (NULGE). The chairman suggested that N2 million be given to NULGE while the balance N6 million should be shared among the other groups including the councillors. Surprisingly, this did not go down well with the Majority Leader, Alex Izogo, who said that the N6 million be given to the councillors and that the chairman should source other extra budgetary money from the council treasury for the other groups. He was supported by some councillors including Goodnews Obiode (leader), Ohoriemu Omohefe (chief whip), Solomon Odumoson.
"In the heat of the argument over how to share the Christmas gift, Goodnews Obiode and eight other councillors stormed out of the meeting; their action is propelled, no doubt, by greed and avarice in total disregard for the principles of transparency, accountability and probity which the Ogieh administration strives to uphold strictly in the conduct of council affairs."
Interests, loyal to the council chairman have also pointed accusing fingers at an unnamed Lagos-based politician, said to be bankrolling their hotel accommodation. But Obiode and his group have described the two explanations as cheap blackmails orchestrated by the council chairman to cover up his financial impropriety.
In the midst of the battle, the Delta South chapter of the PDP came up with a resolution suspending Obiode and his followers from the party. The decision was on Monday, February 2, ratified by the State Executive of the party. The party rising from an emergency meeting announced that it had "approved the suspension of" the affected councillors "in Isoko South Local Government Area by the Delta South Senatorial District of PDP." State Publicity Secretary of the party, Newworld Safugha, who conveyed the party's decision to reporters, added that "the 10 councillors, were to face the state disciplinary committee of the party in line with Article 21.4 of the party constitution. The party also warned its members, particularly councillors, to explore every available means within the party to settle their differences.
In what appeared like a crafted script, while the PDP was suspending the 'anti-chairman" group, their colleague also held another session on the same February 3 and resolved that the 10 members had been suspended from the House indefinitely, ordering them to "submit all official properties in their possession within 48 hours forfeit all benefits and privileges of the office; that a vote of no confidence was passed on Obiodeh and subsequently he was impeached by a unanimous vote from being the leader of the House; that following an election on the floor of the House, Blessing Ese was now the leader of the Isoko South Legislative House while Chief Mrs. Beatrice Igogo was elected as the deputy leade.
Odiodeh's group the following day also came out with a resolution dated January 30 where they claimed to have voted to remove the chairman from office and urged the state assembly vested with the power to remove the chairman to act accordingly and remove him from office on allegations bordering on financial impropriety. "unlawful, illegal, unconstitutional."
According to them, the action was based on the chairman's misappropriation of council's fund to the tune of N1.2 billion, adding that there was "no visible project on ground throughout the local government area to justify the arbitrary spending of the allocation.
The councillors also alleged, "threat to life by the use of thugs on legislative members of the House."
In another resolution, the House claimed to have suspended five members for three months for holding illegal meetings.
Close watchers of the unfolding drama have come to a conclusion that there was more to the crisis than meet the eyes. According to them, there were both internal and external evidence that suggest that certain parochial and vested interest were at play in the area, which is using either of the factions in the legislature to push for the realisation of their ends. Camp loyal to the chairman is pointing accusing fingers at certain top government officials of being behind moves to remove the chairman at all cost. Daily Independent investigation revealed that the forces against Ogieh believed he has not lived up to his billing in delivering democracy dividends to the people. But he and his loyalties viewed such as warped reasoning and cheap blackmail coated and calculated at giving a dog a bad name to hang it. The Concerned Citizens of Isoko for instance noted that since the chairman assumed office, he had "pursued with vigour the execution of his electoral promises within the context of the three-point agenda of the executive governor of Delta State, Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan.
"Some of these projects are the construction of a new legislative building, construction of water board road in Emede with interlock tiles, construction of drainage system in Oleh motor park, establishment of a cassava processing industry, which is at an advanced stage, rehabilitation of Oleh abattoir; and construction of toilets and water boreholes for Ozaha Primary School, Oleh and staff of the Local Education Authority."
But pundits believe that the leadership of the PDP in the state has not done enough to arrest the situation from degenerating to its present level. To them, since all the actors in the crisis were from the same party, the leadership should have nipped the crisis in the bud than allowing it to take the present dimension. Suspending the anti-chairman group, itself, according to observers were not the best approach to resolving whatever issues may have propped up the situation.
It would be interesting to see how this cup boils over.

Original_Search_Title: Smedan to Market Naseni Technology to Msmes
Headline: Nigeria: Smedan to Market Naseni Technology to Msmes
Body_Text:
The Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria (SMEDAN) and the National Agency for Science and Engineering Infrastructure [NASENI] will soon sign a memorandum of understanding for a major collaboration that will enable SMEDAN have access to NASENI's facilities nationwide and market its technology to micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs) in Nigeria.
This was recently disclosed by the Director-General of NASENI, Professor Olusegun Adewoye while receiving his SMEDAN counterpart, Alhaji Muhammad Nadada Umar, in his Idu Office in the Federal Capital Territory.
Pofessor Adewoye described the visit of the Director-General of SMEDAN as a good omen, lamenting that previous efforts by both federal government agencies to collaborate had failed.
Professor Adewoye also disclosed that NASENI has a virtual laboratory in its Abuja Office linked to eight NASENI research centres and nine associate institutes across the country. He stated further that these facilities as well as the 74 mini-foundries located in some tertiary and technical institutions across the country are available for SMEDAN's use.
NASENI, he said, is currently working with some public and private sector organisations in the development of science and engineering infrastructure and that the Agency has a databank of cassava processing machines with advanced technology ready to be commissioned.
Earlier, the Director-General of SMEDAN, Alhaji Muhammad Nadada Umar, noted that Nigeria would not be able to develop without the active collaboration of the various government agencies involved in enterprise development. He added that investment in science and engineering infrastructure was imperative for economic development and growth.
"Local processes should be made to be more efficient with science and engineering infrastructure and independent technological backing is a crucial assignment for us," he said.
He expressed confidence in NASENI's ability to contribute meaningfully to the current battle against poverty and unemployment in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Have Zero Tolerance for Child Sacrifice
Headline: Uganda: Have Zero Tolerance for Child Sacrifice
Body_Text:
Kampala — I must thank the President and some Church leaders who have come out and demanded capital punishment for those involved in the human sacrifice of our children. I thank the press which has exposed these heinous crimes, telling us that many of the huge buildings we see are built on the bones of the sacrificed children and sprinkled with the blood of the innocent.
I also thank the Inspector General of Police, General Kale Kaihura who as the chief law enforcer, has responded to the scourge decisively by arresting the business tycoon Kato Kajubi. However, inspite of this arrest, Ugandans are skeptical because Kato Kajubi is not the first. Very many others are as free as a bird and Kajubi's case is reportedly being heard in camera.
I must be one of many Ugandans who have waited for the recent huge expression of a collective moral outrage against the barbaric and primitive acts by the rich and the politically powerful who in a callous pursuance of their diabolic ambitions shamelessly patronise the witchdoctors, publicly parade themselves in the shrines to the stunned silence and sometimes admiration of Ugandans.
Uganda is a country of contradictions because one notices a massive display of religiosity whereby churches and mosques overflow with worshipers. Take the mushrooming of the new churches and religions. In the old days religious revivals signaled a return to the morals and values which had been eroded. Unfortunately, this is the opposite in Uganda because the level of immorality in some of these organisations is almost diabolic.
These very same people who as in the case of Christians go to Holy Communion every Sunday are the biggest patrons of witchcraft and the shrine bosses. For many Ugandans including some religious leaders there is no moral contradiction in all this as long generous donations keep flowing. Sadly we have for several years seen this evil cancer spreading and actively eating away the moral fabric of the nation. Because of the extensive erosion of the moral fibre and basic religious values, Uganda is witnessing a massive resurgency of and the popularity of witchcraft, witchdoctors and the shrine bosses as well as human sacrifice. All this is in the name of African culture.
Such an extraordinary reversal of values can be measured by the patronage enjoyed by the witchdoctors and their shrines.
There is enough evidence to prove that it is in such shrines that the majority of sacrifices of innocent children is planned and executed.
It is equally important to recognise that there is a very thin line between those who ruthlessly pursue political power and those who ruthlessly search for riches. They are both the patrons of the shrine bosses and it is the patronage of the rich which sustains the supply side which is provided by the shrine bosses. Evidence of the moral decadence is how those who arrive with the help of human sacrifice are cheered and congratulated for their smartness and success. Unfortunately, in today's Uganda these are the role models for the young generation who also aspire to acquire the same political power and wealth.
Demand and supply
The sustainability of these heinous crimes has a demand and supply side because the scarifying of children cannot thrive without the shrine bosses. They too cannot thrive without the patronage of those who believe that power and money need human sacrifices to be got and retained. The Government cannot root out this cancer without recognising that the increase in child sacrifices is the result of a sustained demand.
The audacious patronage enjoyed by the witchdoctors has emboldened these shrine bosses to fear nothing not even the law because none of them has been hanged for their crimes. What we mean by the supply side is the infrastructure of witchcraft as a profession or business plus the witchdoctors and their shrines.
The demand side has no obvious infrastructure because the majority of them, seldom parade themselves and for that reason the Government may find it difficult to launch a frontal attack on them.
However even if the supply side infrastructure is destroyed or it is made difficult to operate, even if expressions of moral outrage is sustained, these by themselves will not be enough to deter the demand side because the hankering for power and wealth are overwhelming. Let us face it. There is a very thin line between the business tycoon who buy children's tongues and gorged out eyes and the politicians. Both seek the same power and wealth and the means to acquire these is by the witchdoctors.
For too long these rich and politically powerful have as the saying goes, got away with murder. Not too long ago, in Natete, the press reported about those who were involved in the sacrifice of a small boy. But there has never been a single court conviction. And yet petty thieves are killed by the mob for stealing a few tubers of cassava or a chicken. Because the courts will not convict those with money and political power, I can foresee a problem unless as the President suggested, these murderers are dealt with by firing squad.
We have watched the continuing erosion of Uganda's moral fibre as exemplified by the absence of court conviction of the numerous barbaric acts which include pouring acid on people. I equate such acts with murder or attempted murder. We see men and women who have been permanently maimed and disfigured for life by acid action if they are lucky to survive. In the old days there used to be not only laws governing the possession, sale and use of dangerous chemicals but such laws were enforced. Not today.
It is not necessarily the absence of laws, but their enforcement and the will of the courts to convict the criminals. The so called police investigations can go on endlessly until they are forgotten and the Police as we are sometimes told loses interest in the case. All this is a manifestation of moral decay.
Evidence of Uganda's moral decadence surrounds us. Take the schools and institutions of higher learning. We see graduates with degrees who can hardly write a grammatically correct paragraph of English. There are numerous law makers who have degrees by purchase.
These days in Uganda you no longer ask how such young men and women got into the universities? To make matters worse, they can even graduate with first class degrees, because grades are awarded for reasons other than academic performance.
The beneficiaries of these immoral practices are tomorrow's leaders. Since they will have arrived through moral decay, they cannot be expected to be the defenders or guardians of the nation's moral fibre. This is the tragedy of our time. I am forced to repeat that we have seen this coming. It has not happened overnight. Countries seldom decay abruptly unless they have undergone a violent revolution.
The recent ritual murder and sacrifices in Masaka which have finally provoked the outrage are not the first. There have been very many. There are other equally worrying developments of domestic violence.
The vernacular press in particular publishes almost daily, pictures of horrific murders on the front pages. Husbands or lovers killing their wives and vice versa. Children killing their aged parents for a piece of land. Last year the press reported that 78 wives were killed by their husbands and 40 husbands were murdered by their wives.
Historians will tell us that these happenings are not unique to Uganda nor to the contemporary world because throughout history societies periodically suffer from outrageous decline of morals. But that is not a consolation for Uganda and it should not be an attitude of surrender. My cause for despondence is not the absence of laws. What is missing is the will to enforce them.
What about the inevitable plea of the so called African culture?
We must ask where is the demarcation between African culture and the resurgency of witchcraft? My view is that while it is important for us as a nation to preserve African culture, protection should not extend to those who invoke it in order to commit crimes.
The challenge
As a country we have many challenges. But here I address the challenge of restoring the moral values which have been so massively eroded. Normally, religion can be counted on as a bastion of morality. But that can only be the case when religion is not driven by money. Because we cannot count on religion, we must revert to the law.
As I conclude, I am forced to say that although moral outrage has been expressed and it made me feel good; I must emphasise that lamentation is not enough because it will not stop the politically rapacious and those in search of wealth because they are determined to get their goals at any cost, from using the witch doctors and shrine bosses as partners in crime.
The country needs deterrent sentences. Here I will give an example of New York City where I lived for 8 years. The New York of the 1980s had deteriorated into the crime capital of the West. Businesses and investments deserted the city and relocated in other States. That was when Guillema was elected as mayor in early 1990s.
He set a policy of zero tolerance for crimes with the result that by the mid 1990s, criminals had to relocate elsewhere. I am giving this example merely to show that crime can be fought. It is time for Uganda to deal with this scourge ruthlessly provided we can avoid the convenient mix-up of African culture to justify moral decadence.
The writer is the Minister of State for Finance, Planning and Economic Development

Ese, deputy leader of the House during his briefing had observed, "the prognosis of the crisis in the Isoko South Legislative Arm which has dislocated and hamstrung our legislative duties...arose from the unbridled avarice of our co-councillors that wanted 90 per cent of the entire budgetary appropriation for Christmas given to them at the expense of other stakeholders in the LGA. When it dawned on them that they could not have their way, they embarked on this criminal misadventure of unsubstantiated allegations against the office and person of the executive chairman, Chief Askia Ogieh."
A group that styles itself as the Concerned Citizens of Isoko South also echoed this position. The group in a statement endorsed by one Akpofure Joe alleged, "the origin of their campaign of calumny against our amiable chairman is traceable to the refusal of the chairman to give them 90 per cent of the budgetary appropriation for Christmas. Our investigation revealed that on December 8, 2008, the chairman invited all the 20 councillors of Isoko South to a meeting to discuss modalities for sharing the Christmas gifts which were appropriated for in the 2008 supplementary budget for the council. Nineteen of the councillors were present at the meeting where the chairman said that N8 million was appropriated for Christmas gifts. This was to be shared among six interest groups including members of the National Union of Local Government Employees (NULGE). The chairman suggested that N2 million be given to NULGE while the balance N6 million should be shared among the other groups including the councillors. Surprisingly, this did not go down well with the Majority Leader, Alex Izogo, who said that the N6 million be given to the councillors and that the chairman should source other extra budgetary money from the council treasury for the other groups. He was supported by some councillors including Goodnews Obiode (leader), Ohoriemu Omohefe (chief whip), Solomon Odumoson.
"In the heat of the argument over how to share the Christmas gift, Goodnews Obiode and eight other councillors stormed out of the meeting; their action is propelled, no doubt, by greed and avarice in total disregard for the principles of transparency, accountability and probity which the Ogieh administration strives to uphold strictly in the conduct of council affairs."
Interests, loyal to the council chairman have also pointed accusing fingers at an unnamed Lagos-based politician, said to be bankrolling their hotel accommodation. But Obiode and his group have described the two explanations as cheap blackmails orchestrated by the council chairman to cover up his financial impropriety.
In the midst of the battle, the Delta South chapter of the PDP came up with a resolution suspending Obiode and his followers from the party. The decision was on Monday, February 2, ratified by the State Executive of the party. The party rising from an emergency meeting announced that it had "approved the suspension of" the affected councillors "in Isoko South Local Government Area by the Delta South Senatorial District of PDP." State Publicity Secretary of the party, Newworld Safugha, who conveyed the party's decision to reporters, added that "the 10 councillors, were to face the state disciplinary committee of the party in line with Article 21.4 of the party constitution. The party also warned its members, particularly councillors, to explore every available means within the party to settle their differences.
In what appeared like a crafted script, while the PDP was suspending the 'anti-chairman" group, their colleague also held another session on the same February 3 and resolved that the 10 members had been suspended from the House indefinitely, ordering them to "submit all official properties in their possession within 48 hours forfeit all benefits and privileges of the office; that a vote of no confidence was passed on Obiodeh and subsequently he was impeached by a unanimous vote from being the leader of the House; that following an election on the floor of the House, Blessing Ese was now the leader of the Isoko South Legislative House while Chief Mrs. Beatrice Igogo was elected as the deputy leade.
Odiodeh's group the following day also came out with a resolution dated January 30 where they claimed to have voted to remove the chairman from office and urged the state assembly vested with the power to remove the chairman to act accordingly and remove him from office on allegations bordering on financial impropriety. "unlawful, illegal, unconstitutional."
According to them, the action was based on the chairman's misappropriation of council's fund to the tune of N1.2 billion, adding that there was "no visible project on ground throughout the local government area to justify the arbitrary spending of the allocation.
The councillors also alleged, "threat to life by the use of thugs on legislative members of the House."
In another resolution, the House claimed to have suspended five members for three months for holding illegal meetings.
Close watchers of the unfolding drama have come to a conclusion that there was more to the crisis than meet the eyes. According to them, there were both internal and external evidence that suggest that certain parochial and vested interest were at play in the area, which is using either of the factions in the legislature to push for the realisation of their ends. Camp loyal to the chairman is pointing accusing fingers at certain top government officials of being behind moves to remove the chairman at all cost. Daily Independent investigation revealed that the forces against Ogieh believed he has not lived up to his billing in delivering democracy dividends to the people. But he and his loyalties viewed such as warped reasoning and cheap blackmail coated and calculated at giving a dog a bad name to hang it. The Concerned Citizens of Isoko for instance noted that since the chairman assumed office, he had "pursued with vigour the execution of his electoral promises within the context of the three-point agenda of the executive governor of Delta State, Dr. Emmanuel Uduaghan.
"Some of these projects are the construction of a new legislative building, construction of water board road in Emede with interlock tiles, construction of drainage system in Oleh motor park, establishment of a cassava processing industry, which is at an advanced stage, rehabilitation of Oleh abattoir; and construction of toilets and water boreholes for Ozaha Primary School, Oleh and staff of the Local Education Authority."
But pundits believe that the leadership of the PDP in the state has not done enough to arrest the situation from degenerating to its present level. To them, since all the actors in the crisis were from the same party, the leadership should have nipped the crisis in the bud than allowing it to take the present dimension. Suspending the anti-chairman group, itself, according to observers were not the best approach to resolving whatever issues may have propped up the situation.
It would be interesting to see how this cup boils over.

Original_Search_Title: Have Zero Tolerance for Child Sacrifice
Headline: Uganda: Have Zero Tolerance for Child Sacrifice
Body_Text:
Kampala — I must thank the President and some Church leaders who have come out and demanded capital punishment for those involved in the human sacrifice of our children. I thank the press which has exposed these heinous crimes, telling us that many of the huge buildings we see are built on the bones of the sacrificed children and sprinkled with the blood of the innocent.
I also thank the Inspector General of Police, General Kale Kaihura who as the chief law enforcer, has responded to the scourge decisively by arresting the business tycoon Kato Kajubi. However, inspite of this arrest, Ugandans are skeptical because Kato Kajubi is not the first. Very many others are as free as a bird and Kajubi's case is reportedly being heard in camera.
I must be one of many Ugandans who have waited for the recent huge expression of a collective moral outrage against the barbaric and primitive acts by the rich and the politically powerful who in a callous pursuance of their diabolic ambitions shamelessly patronise the witchdoctors, publicly parade themselves in the shrines to the stunned silence and sometimes admiration of Ugandans.
Uganda is a country of contradictions because one notices a massive display of religiosity whereby churches and mosques overflow with worshipers. Take the mushrooming of the new churches and religions. In the old days religious revivals signaled a return to the morals and values which had been eroded. Unfortunately, this is the opposite in Uganda because the level of immorality in some of these organisations is almost diabolic.
These very same people who as in the case of Christians go to Holy Communion every Sunday are the biggest patrons of witchcraft and the shrine bosses. For many Ugandans including some religious leaders there is no moral contradiction in all this as long generous donations keep flowing. Sadly we have for several years seen this evil cancer spreading and actively eating away the moral fabric of the nation. Because of the extensive erosion of the moral fibre and basic religious values, Uganda is witnessing a massive resurgency of and the popularity of witchcraft, witchdoctors and the shrine bosses as well as human sacrifice. All this is in the name of African culture.
Such an extraordinary reversal of values can be measured by the patronage enjoyed by the witchdoctors and their shrines.
There is enough evidence to prove that it is in such shrines that the majority of sacrifices of innocent children is planned and executed.
It is equally important to recognise that there is a very thin line between those who ruthlessly pursue political power and those who ruthlessly search for riches. They are both the patrons of the shrine bosses and it is the patronage of the rich which sustains the supply side which is provided by the shrine bosses. Evidence of the moral decadence is how those who arrive with the help of human sacrifice are cheered and congratulated for their smartness and success. Unfortunately, in today's Uganda these are the role models for the young generation who also aspire to acquire the same political power and wealth.
Demand and supply
The sustainability of these heinous crimes has a demand and supply side because the scarifying of children cannot thrive without the shrine bosses. They too cannot thrive without the patronage of those who believe that power and money need human sacrifices to be got and retained. The Government cannot root out this cancer without recognising that the increase in child sacrifices is the result of a sustained demand.
The audacious patronage enjoyed by the witchdoctors has emboldened these shrine bosses to fear nothing not even the law because none of them has been hanged for their crimes. What we mean by the supply side is the infrastructure of witchcraft as a profession or business plus the witchdoctors and their shrines.
The demand side has no obvious infrastructure because the majority of them, seldom parade themselves and for that reason the Government may find it difficult to launch a frontal attack on them.
However even if the supply side infrastructure is destroyed or it is made difficult to operate, even if expressions of moral outrage is sustained, these by themselves will not be enough to deter the demand side because the hankering for power and wealth are overwhelming. Let us face it. There is a very thin line between the business tycoon who buy children's tongues and gorged out eyes and the politicians. Both seek the same power and wealth and the means to acquire these is by the witchdoctors.
For too long these rich and politically powerful have as the saying goes, got away with murder. Not too long ago, in Natete, the press reported about those who were involved in the sacrifice of a small boy. But there has never been a single court conviction. And yet petty thieves are killed by the mob for stealing a few tubers of cassava or a chicken. Because the courts will not convict those with money and political power, I can foresee a problem unless as the President suggested, these murderers are dealt with by firing squad.
We have watched the continuing erosion of Uganda's moral fibre as exemplified by the absence of court conviction of the numerous barbaric acts which include pouring acid on people. I equate such acts with murder or attempted murder. We see men and women who have been permanently maimed and disfigured for life by acid action if they are lucky to survive. In the old days there used to be not only laws governing the possession, sale and use of dangerous chemicals but such laws were enforced. Not today.
It is not necessarily the absence of laws, but their enforcement and the will of the courts to convict the criminals. The so called police investigations can go on endlessly until they are forgotten and the Police as we are sometimes told loses interest in the case. All this is a manifestation of moral decay.
Evidence of Uganda's moral decadence surrounds us. Take the schools and institutions of higher learning. We see graduates with degrees who can hardly write a grammatically correct paragraph of English. There are numerous law makers who have degrees by purchase.
These days in Uganda you no longer ask how such young men and women got into the universities? To make matters worse, they can even graduate with first class degrees, because grades are awarded for reasons other than academic performance.
The beneficiaries of these immoral practices are tomorrow's leaders. Since they will have arrived through moral decay, they cannot be expected to be the defenders or guardians of the nation's moral fibre. This is the tragedy of our time. I am forced to repeat that we have seen this coming. It has not happened overnight. Countries seldom decay abruptly unless they have undergone a violent revolution.
The recent ritual murder and sacrifices in Masaka which have finally provoked the outrage are not the first. There have been very many. There are other equally worrying developments of domestic violence.
The vernacular press in particular publishes almost daily, pictures of horrific murders on the front pages. Husbands or lovers killing their wives and vice versa. Children killing their aged parents for a piece of land. Last year the press reported that 78 wives were killed by their husbands and 40 husbands were murdered by their wives.
Historians will tell us that these happenings are not unique to Uganda nor to the contemporary world because throughout history societies periodically suffer from outrageous decline of morals. But that is not a consolation for Uganda and it should not be an attitude of surrender. My cause for despondence is not the absence of laws. What is missing is the will to enforce them.
What about the inevitable plea of the so called African culture?
We must ask where is the demarcation between African culture and the resurgency of witchcraft? My view is that while it is important for us as a nation to preserve African culture, protection should not extend to those who invoke it in order to commit crimes.
The challenge
As a country we have many challenges. But here I address the challenge of restoring the moral values which have been so massively eroded. Normally, religion can be counted on as a bastion of morality. But that can only be the case when religion is not driven by money. Because we cannot count on religion, we must revert to the law.
As I conclude, I am forced to say that although moral outrage has been expressed and it made me feel good; I must emphasise that lamentation is not enough because it will not stop the politically rapacious and those in search of wealth because they are determined to get their goals at any cost, from using the witch doctors and shrine bosses as partners in crime.
The country needs deterrent sentences. Here I will give an example of New York City where I lived for 8 years. The New York of the 1980s had deteriorated into the crime capital of the West. Businesses and investments deserted the city and relocated in other States. That was when Guillema was elected as mayor in early 1990s.
He set a policy of zero tolerance for crimes with the result that by the mid 1990s, criminals had to relocate elsewhere. I am giving this example merely to show that crime can be fought. It is time for Uganda to deal with this scourge ruthlessly provided we can avoid the convenient mix-up of African culture to justify moral decadence.
The writer is the Minister of State for Finance, Planning and Economic Development

Original_Search_Title: N100 is the Lowest I've Been Paid as Comedian - I Go Dye
Headline: Nigeria: N100 is the Lowest I've Been Paid as Comedian - I Go Dye
Body_Text:
Francis Agoda, better known as I Go Dye has been a comedian for 16 years. He may be having good success in entertainment but he lives in Benin, Edo State far from the Lagos madness.
Currently involved in an international tour to showcase his comedy, he told Deputy Editor Charles Okogene, in this interview, the story of his life in comedy.
What is your tour all about?
The concept is to preach peace, African unity and to celebrate love. It is not just a tour for Africans, it is for everybody to come and enjoy themselves. It is also aimed at discouraging the young ones, who believe that the only way to success is through drug addiction, prostitution, violence and all that. We want to use the tour to educate them. People who are victims of these unpleasant acts will come on stage during the shows to share their experience. Then, my documentary, which preaches against violence will be on show; it details my experience in the orgy of violence in Niger Delta, how I survived, how I succeeded and today, I'm being celebrated.
What is your take on the bombardment of the Niger Delta by the Joint Military Taskforce (JTF)?
What I have to say to the militants is that they should drop their arms, which I believe they are willing to do and pursue other means of making the area more peaceful. The Federal Government needs to see how it can engender and promote peace in the region, which lays the golden egg for the nation. The truth is that when there is chaos like we have now, only the innocent suffer. If the place is not peaceful, development cannot take place. That is why the two sides need to dialogue over the problem not fight over it. The government needs to listen to their demands and in turn, the militants need to ceasefire to enable government to develop the area. Violence and bloodshed scare people who are interested in developing and investing in the area. I believe the government is working because when I was in Akwa Ibom recently, I saw the new express road from Akwa Ibom to Delta State. It is a development and it is also creating job opportunities for the youths. When there is job opportunity for youths, I don't believe that anybody would want to engage in violent activity. So, there is need for government to create jobs for youths.
Your dress sense of recent has been tilting towards those in the hip-hop genre of music especially with the heavy presence of bling and all that.
First of all, the name in question is I Go Dye and I Go Dye is someone that is unpredictable, someone constantly evolving. I dress like an entertainer; the bling that I put on is something that signifies what I do, entertainment. I just want to create an image and style for myself, something different in the comedy world. I don't just want people to see me as I Go Dye or because I wear suite they will be tempted to say that I dress like Ali Baba, Julius Agwu or Basket Mouth. I've asked myself what will stand me out even before people hear my joke. Fine, they used to know I Go Dye with funny suites and clothes but that was then. I told myself I need to add some beauty to it so I can look more outstanding. That is how the bling came about.
So it is now bye bye to those funny clothes and suits?
In a way yes, but I still I have the clothes I started with 16 years ago. They are the things that make I Go Dye and It will be stupid of me if I throw them away. I have a special wardrobe for them and they are the clothes I cherish most because they remind me of the beginning of my success.
Do you intend to auction them one day?
I don't know but let's see what the future holds.
What is your relationship with other comedians?
I have a good relationship with other comedians. I don't envy or harbour ill feeling against anybody because if I do, success will be far from me. One thing that sets people back is envy; I don't envy anybody. I learn from other comedians just as some of them learn from me. I'm one of the comedians who have performed with all other comedians. That is to show you that I have a good relationship with them, if I do not have, they won't invite me and if they do, I won't honour it.
You perform at every other comedian's show, when are we seeing your own show?
My comedy show is coming after the world tour; we have Lagos, Benin and Abuja. I was supposed to have hosted the show last December but I opted to promote peace in the region before my show. This is because if there is no peace, people will not come out to have fun. That is why I did my documentary, which I titled Peace In Sight so that they can know that there is more to life than violence. After the tour we are coming to Nigeria to do a show called 'I Go Dye Standing, End Of Discussion.'
The belief among entertainers is that except they are in Lagos, they cannot make it, that Lagos is where it is happening. But you seem to have punctured that belief with your success despite the fact that you stay in the ancient Benin Kingdom. What is the magic?
You can only succeed in whatever you do if you are good at it. What stands you out is your effort, competence, good work, creativity, the blessing of God and not where you live or stay. There are some people who live in Lagos but hardly get shows; so it is not about Lagos. Remember that we all must not live in Lagos. The only problem is that sometimes when I tell them (promoters) that I live in Benin or Warri, they think of the transportation and all that, that is just the draw back.
Are you then more expensive than your colleagues who stay in places like Lagos and Abuja, which are the usual venues of some of the shows you feature in?
It is not about being expensive, it is about where you find peace and are more comfortable. As for shows, I do shows the way I want to do it. I can decide to rest this week or month. I have been touring Europe and America since and I came back on May 26 to honour Benue State Governor's 10 Years of Democracy in Nigeria celebration. I left there on May 30 to Akwa Ibom State to honour the Governor Godswill Akpabio's celebration, on June 1 I was in Bayelsa State for the New Yam Festival, so within that week, I did three big events and here I am in Lagos for the Glo Laffta Fest. With these alone, I am okay.
How do you source for your blings?
I source for them when I travel abroad.
Do you put on the blings off stage?
What I wear on stage I don't wear off stage because it is my costume.
