For me, the clothes I wear on stage, I don't wear off stage because I consider the stage as my workshop, off the stage I'm a different person. No blings at all. When I tell people that I'm a very shy person they say, 'how can you who stands in front of thousands of people to crack jokes say you are a shy person?' Some people believe that as a comedian, I talk a lot but when most of them meet me off stage and see that I am very calm, very relaxed, they say, 'you are different from the person we see on stage.' And I tell them, 'if you like I Go Dye, when you meet Francis, you will love him.'
How did you conquer stage fright?
Anybody who lives in Warri does not have stage fright; we can face anybody and any crowd. It is just God's gift, it is inborn in me. I never had stage fright so I cannot tell you that this was how I overcame it. God did it for me in his own way.
Your career has spanned over 15 years; which can you single out as your most memorable show?
All the shows that I have done are memorable in their own way; they are good in one way or the other in the sense that I always give thanks to God no matter how it turns out. I can't say that so and so show is bad or good the only thing I consider is my performance. My desire is to constantly put up good performance. All the shows I have done are good but what I must tell you is that my best is yet to come.
Which show would you say brought you into the spotlight?
That was REEL Awards organised by Encomium magazine in 1999; Azuh Arinze was the editor of the magazine. He saw my performance at (L'agbaja) Motherlan' and he said, 'boy! You are a bundle of talent. You need to come and perform at REEL Awards.'
I told him that I don't even have something to wear for such high-class show and he promised to give me something to wear. He called me days later and gave me a brand new suit and said 'I want you to put on this for the show.' I then told him 'but this is too big' and he said 'that makes you a comedian.' When I put it on, it was my size. Each time I reflect on that incident, I give thanks to God for Azuh Arinze who gave me my first breakthrough at REEL Awards.
Before I met him, I came to Lagos to see if I could perform at Nite of a Thousand Laughs but could not on the grounds that I was not considered good enough. I was about to return to Warri when a friend of mine said to me 'instead of going to Warri today, why not accompany me to L'agbaja's show?' At the show I saw Ali Baba, who was surprised I was at the show. Ali Baba, who is such a free spirited fellow, who is ever willing to help his fellow comedian, said 'oh! Come and perform.' He introduced me on stage; he relaxed the mind of the crowd with the hype he gave to me and I did not disappoint him when I took the microphone. It was there Azuh decided that I must perform at his show. Azuh called me and asked me if I lived in Lagos and I said no. He said that with the kind of show I put up I should be in Lagos. Julius Agwu even offered to rent a house for me so I can move to Lagos. But I told them that I was okay in Warri. Today, I say I am truly grateful to Azuh, Ali Baba and Julius Agwu for their support and words of encouragement. I appreciate them from the bottom of my heart; it is good to be good. You can imagine all these happened 10 to 11 years ago but it seems like it was only yesterday. It means that whatever you do to affect people positively, they will always live to remember you no matter what. It is good to be good and it is bad to be bad.
The name I Go Dye, how did it come about?
I tell people that their belief is what stands them out and it is the beginning of their success. I'm one man who does not believe in name. The name, I Go Dye, is from my surname, which is Agoda. So, it is a direct coinage from my surname. But some people misinterpret the I Go Dye, they believe that it is I Go Dye. It is Dye which means to transform ones state, to change. So what I'm doing is transforming. That is changing people's mood from state of sadness to one of happiness. Then, like I told people who keep asking me why I call myself I Go Dye, who keep telling me to change my name to 'I Go Live, I have seen people who succeed but are not successful. I have seen people who are called Lucky and they have been playing the lottery without winning. So, if names were what make one successful, my name would have been 800 million pounds. So, it is not about the effort you put in whatever you do that makes you succeed. Some people will tell me 'look at Vice President Goodluck Jonathan, luck has been following him.' And I tell them that he has worked hard, that is why luck is following him. Those who believe entirely in luck, chance and opportunity are the people who fail in life. They are the ones that believe that one day they will just see $100 million on the way. Who will lose it? You have to work hard so that when there is an opening people will go looking for you.
What was the lowest amount I Go Dye ever received as fee?
The lowest amount I was paid was also the biggest amount I got. I was paid N100 and that was in 1997 in Warri for a birthday party. I consider it as the biggest amount I ever received so far because when I started out in comedy, though I knew I'd make it big, my family never believed this. So, when they paid me that money to transport myself to the venue of the party, I then told myself that if I can be paid N100 when I have not made an impact in this profession, it means I will get more if I work hard. That N100 was the catalyst I needed to forge ahead. If I were not paid that N100, I would have probably been in the Creeks of the Niger Delta.

Original_Search_Title: Delta - Displaced Communities Get Relief Materials
Headline: Nigeria: Delta - Displaced Communities Get Relief Materials
Body_Text:
The rehabilitation committee set up by Governor Emmanuel Uduaghan in the wake of the crisis between the Joint Task Force (JTF) and militants in Gbaramatu kingdom has distributed relief materials worth several millions of Naira to various Ijaw and Itsekiri communities affected by the clash.
A member of the committee, Couple Oromoni, led the team comprising the Red Cross Society, National Emergency Management Agency and other members of the committee to the communities.
The committee which got the blessing of JTF authorities to distribute the relief to the communities in the area, used a wooden boat known as "Kpekpekpe" for the journey.
Contrary to report in some quarters that the communities were completely deserted, the committee found people in the communities visited and distributed the relief materials at Opuede-bubor, Okpele-Ama, Ugborodo, Azama, Enekorogha, Igoba, Kunukunuma, and Oporoza communities.
Commodities distributed to the communities included bags of rice, bags of beans, tubers of yam, cans of palm oil, bags of salt, bags of garri, eight baskets of starch, cartons of frozen fish, bags of water and sacks of tapioca.
Making the donation at Opuede-bubor, Oromoni said "we are presenting these few items on behalf of the State Government; more items will come. We actually wanted to know whether people have returned back to the communities."
He added: "The State Government wants you to come back and live your normal life. We are all praying for peace to return fully; we are aware of the challenges facing you but the government is doing everything to ensure that you are safe and sound; have no fears; tell your husbands to return home."
However, the JTF is yet to apprehend firebrand militant leader, Government Ekpemupolo (alias Tom Polo), who was declared wanted over alleged criminality on the waterways.
The Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta (MEND) said the task force is chasing a shadow as Tom Polo would be hard to nail by the authorities even as they carpeted the task force for making him a villain when he had been a reliable informant for it over the years
Meanwhile, school children displaced from their communities would now commence their schooling at the General Hospital at Ogbe-Ijoh, headquarters of Warri South West Council.

Original_Search_Title: Blame Our Failure On Obasanjo, PDP - Masari
Headline: Nigeria: Blame Our Failure On Obasanjo, PDP - Masari
Body_Text:
Former Speaker, Bello Masari, may not be as vibrant as his predecessor, Umar Ghali N'Abba. At least there was no real battle between his House and executive. But those who know, insist that he was no less resolute on some critical matters during the period, one of them the infamous third term agenda, allegedly contrived by former President, Olusegun Obasanjo. In this interview with Deputy Editor, SAM AKPE and Senior Assistant Editor AUSTIN IYASHERE, he gives his own verdict on Nigerian democracy at 10 and the role he played.
Excerpts:
Two years out of office as Speaker of the House of Representatives and in the open as the next Nigerian. How have you coped?
Really there has not been much difficulty for me in terms of being what I used to be. Yes it was very eventful then but life has been very normal for me. We are still surviving and contributing the best we can to the polity.
When you say life is okay, how normal is life when you come from Number Four in Nigeria to maybe just the next person?
For me, the first day I entered office was the same day I started preparing to go out. I did not change my friends. But I added new ones. Those are friends some of who I attended primary School with. We are still together with regards to the family, I don't have new addition that came in when I was in that office. For example I warned my wives before knowing that I was ever going to become speaker. My children are grown up and I made sure that I did not drastically change their lives. That also made it easy for them to continue with their life after office. Not that they didn't record an improvement in terms of so many things in their lives, particularly the public attention but by and large, the way and manner one conducts himself while in office determines to a great extent how he is going to manage life after leaving office. As a family, our eating habit did not change, so our menu did not change. Actually it was easy for me and my family to adjust. The difficult thing, which one experiences, is that there are certain things which in my mind I think are not wrong to do but the society will ask why after this high profile you do that.
Can you give us an example of your experience in that regard?
An example was last week in Kaduna. I wanted to visit a popular pounded yam restaurant where we used to eat with my friends but they refused to let me go and insisted that they would rather buy the food and bring to where we were staying. By that, of course, you miss the environment which makes the food what it is. They said the place was too crowded for me to go to particularly in the day time. Personally, I have no difficulty doing those things that I am used to doing but the society will give if different interpretation.
What about other pressures?
Yes, because of the position you held, people believe that you can influence things. But there are other people in various offices now who are charged with these responsibilities. I cannot do what they want because I do not have the authority to do them. Secondly I do not like to beg. Even before becoming speaker, I found it very difficult to go about begging for favours. So these are some of the pressures after office.
How did you survive as Speaker for four stable and turbulent free years?
First, I thank God for giving us the wisdom that helped us survive the four years without major crisis. Essentially, even when I was discussing with my colleagues for support to be Speaker, I told them that I will provide leadership not ruler-ship. There is a great difference in those two. As you know Number Four is only a figure. Particularly in parliament where you are only among equals. As Speaker or Senate President you have power but no authority. When we came into office I ensured that as members, we took all decisions collectively. Again within the leadership, we had a group that we started when we were looking for the office of Speaker and within that group we remained united. Again the body of principal officers worked together I took them into absolute confidence. There was nothing we did that was not discussed and agreed. In dealing with finances and things that would affect the house, at the risk of being immodest, we were very fair and open. We were not greedy at all we were not greedy.
Shortly after you left office, your successor Patricia Etteh ran into trouble. How did you feel about that?
You see, Eteh was part of my inner caucus. I can say safely that she was involved in about 70 percent of my trips and engagements particularly outside Nigeria. I had confidence that she would not face the kind of problems that she faced. But what happened was that she relied on forces outside the House. The support of the party and the executive arm is important for you to get to the office of the Speaker. If you get their support, it makes the race easier and simpler but immediately you get to that office you must start to build your own structures because you simply cannot prepare for a fight on the day of the battle. In peacetime, you must prepare for war, when you are young you prepare for old age. You worship God and do the things you believe are right because you want peace at your old age or when you die. These are practical things anybody should do. So when Etteh came in, she had the full support of the party. We had that too when we went into the job. But as a legislator, if you want to do your job in the best interest of your country, you are bound to have conflicts with the executive. There is no way you won't have but you work to determine the kind of fight and the armour you are required to defend yourself with. So I think Etteh believed that the party could solve that problem. She believed that the executive would come to her aid. Unfortunately, none of the two worked for obvious reasons.
Why?
For example members knew that from the experience in 1999 to 2003 and 2007, when they faced elections, how did the party help? Take the 2007 election for instance. Those who worked for the party, particularly those who laboured for it in the days of the constitutional amendment campaign hardly returned to the legislature. In fact many did not even get the party's ticket. Members do not depend on the leadership of the party. They rather depend on their own structures back home, not here in Abuja because when the chips are down, it is that structure that will make you or mar you, not the people living in Abuja. If I have a problem and you say you are helping me and I know you can help me. But my problem continues to grow by the day, I should know one or two things. It is either you are incapable or you simply are not helping. I must look for other alternatives.
What did the Executive have to do?
When her problem started, the party and the former president gave her all the assistance but her number of supporters kept depleting. From what has happened and what is happening in Nigeria, Etteh's matter was chicken feed. Her problem was that she was not able to carry her colleagues along. That was the simple problem. This ranking business in the National Assembly is very important. Her first line of defence could have been the old members who were in the House from 1999 to 2003 and 2007. Those would been her first line of defence because by that time, the new members had not understood the politics of the House. If she had done those kinds of things maybe her situation would have been different.
Every four years, so many new faces come into the National Assembly. What impacts has this had on the development of the legislature and democracy?
Serious, very serious negative impact. To understand these things, we have to read the history of other countries. Maybe at the initial stage they might have had periods like ours. In our own case, there are so many factors. First, the parliament is new. Secondly, we do not have political parties that engender real democracy within them as institutions that provide the vehicle for contesting election. All the parties, without exception, have failed to provide that window. So there is no democracy, no internal democracy in the parties. So at the state level no matter how popular you were, if your governor does not want you, you are going nowhere. At the council level, the chairman wants to decide who goes to the State Assembly. So everybody was trying to bring in his own boy, not the party and not the people. So as a result, we see high turnover as new members are being brought in by governors and godfathers. In Nigeria what we actually have is institutional godfathers because when government becomes a godfather, that is institutional godfatherism.
What happened to your ambition to become governor of Katsina State?
I thought we were working as a team for the betterment of the people and democracy. Otherwise, there was no point in being Number Four and then coming down to be among the 36. As I have already said, four is a figure but there are political positions that give you the opportunity to affect the lives of people directly. If we are working to develop our democracy, no position is too small or too high. That was the thinking. But when it came to almost the level of decision, I didn't know that those who had authority over the party were not happy with the way we conducted our affairs in the House. So suddenly, all kinds of things began to happen. Delegates were being changed at primaries grounds. Some people felt that they were humbled and wanted revenge while others were simply looking for who to humiliate.
And you did not see this coming?
Actually I was warned much earlier to stay away from that election because it was not going to be possible. Once the election took that dirty shape I decided to withdraw. Anybody who was in Nigeria in 2006 and 2007 knows that without exception, all the parties did not hold anything you can call congresses to choose candidates for elections. Of course PDP was guiltier than the other parties because it was supposed to provide leadership but it provided a very bad leadership. The parties were just parties in name but they were parties without democratic leadership who thought that they hold the power of life and death that is why today in all states except Lagos, incumbents are fighting with the predecessors in office. If they allowed the system to work, they will not have the problems they have now.
With the recent reforms and constitutional amendments in the PDP, is the party better now?
No, restoring the constitution to what it was is only the first step. The major thing is to have leaders who are elected by party. So the state congresses and National Congress should be done in such a way that people feel free to elect the people they want. If they do not do that, then it is all a waste of time and we are not going anywhere. We are now going from bad to worse. We had Solomon Lar who was better than Gamade, we had Gamade was better than Audu Ogbe, we had Audu Ogbe who was 100 percent better than Ahmadu Ali and we had Ahmadu Ali who was better than what is happening today. Ali had courage, but today what do we have?
What was you relationship with Obasanjo?
You must give to Obasanjo some credit that people always want to deny him. Obasanjo had courage and confidence to look you in the face and fight you. If he is fighting you, you can agree to disagree. We had our rough times with him. He didn't get what he wanted and we didn't get what we wanted; so it's a balance. For me, I don't hold much personal grudge against him but I have a grudge against him as a Nigerian. He had a golden opportunity of turning Nigeria around and he wasted it. Look, people are not so concerned about how many bridges and roads government builds or the number of hospitals he build. If he had established a system, the system will produce all that. If Obasanjo had left Nigeria a legacy of a credible system which puts credible people in government at all levels, that system would make it possible for all things to happen because then elected and appointed people would be accountable to the electorate. But in the manner he left, there is no accountability to the electorate. If I know you did not elect me and I am sure that I can manipulate myself back to office without you, why should I feel accountable to you? For that, I hold a strong grudge against Obasanjo and every reasonable Nigerian should hold that grudge. So he had a golden opportunity and wasted it.
But sir, there were also forces that made him do things the way he did them?
Let me tell you; by the time he became president, on the Nigerian soil, only one person, Yakubu Gowon was his senior in public life. All others, whether is business, politics or otherwise were below him. He had tremendous goodwill locally and internationally. He also had on his side, vast years of experience. So if there were forces who forced him to do what he did, the forces he had were much more. So what are we talking about? Nobody had the advantage and opportunities like Obasanjo. The entire country was behind him. He was deliberately brought in to sanitise and unify the polity.
Many people are saying that Nigeria would have been better by now if Obasanjo were still in power. Did you see anything positive in him?
You cannot fault Obasanjo's credentials for his love for the unity of Nigeria . His fault is that he wanted it to be run the way he wanted. That is where he was wrong. Nigeria should not be run the way he thinks. Nigeria should be run the way Nigerians want it. His was to provide leadership and at the critical moment, when it was most desired, he did not do it.
What was that critical moment?
That was the time when he conducted the election; the primaries and elections. You can't fault his love and commitment to this country but the way he went about it didn't produce the ideal result.
Now this administration is two years and half way down its term and democracy is 10 years. What have you seen and what does the future all look like?
I think people are missing the point. If he had done the right thing, If Obasanjo had ensured that the parties did their primaries openly and fairly, if they got it even only up to 70 percent because you may not get it up to 100 percent, if Obasanjo had made sure that INEC conducted an election that was acceptable internationally and nationally, whatever kind of result it produced, nobody would care. They will be the voters' choice. But now the people he brought to power came with a lot of baggages, very heavy weights on them and questions about their legitimacy. And he created it. It was unnecessary. At that time, no matter what happened, PDP would have won the election in Nigeria. No party would have defeated PDP in 2007. PDP did not need to engage in the kind of things people accused it of and now ascribe to its character unless it was done deliberately to achieve what I don't know. There was no basis for PDP to do what it did. It could have won this election confidently, maybe not with the kind of majority we have today but it could have won the election confidently to the admiration of every body.
Even with the burden of legitimacy, can you tell us, two years after how this government has faired?
What we are saying is that you cannot say that the eight years of Obasanjo were all wasted, that he didn't do anything. I don't share that view, I don't believe that. You cannot fault his economic reform with all the defects in it. There was need to reform. So when a PDP government inherited a PDP government what we would have expected to see is continuation and the corrections along the line. I don't want a situation in which PDP inherits PDP as if PDP is inheriting an opposition government or an opposition inheriting the PDP. So really the PDP as a political party is supposed to have a programme, a manifesto, which all its elected people should be implementing. So the issue of major differences between Obasanjo's government and the present one shouldn't have been there. I hope we don't cultivate the culture of government by probes.
Where do you think President Yar'Adua is leading Nigeria? Where are we leading?
Well, actually it is very difficult for me to say that I have a precise understanding of where we are going. What I do know is that President Yar'Adua is a deliberate and calculating manager, different from the approach of Obasanjo. They are two totally different people in their approach to issues. I think really this government, one can say; well you can sympathise with them. They came in with this burden of legitimacy. Again, immediately the government came in, the mainstay of the Nigerian economy, oil, fell to about a third of its price. For all the period Obasanjo was there, there was not a single day the price of oil fell. So in terms of revenue, he was luckier than this government. Again, the structures; you know, certain people in Obasanjo's administration, who were key actors in some areas should have remained in this one, because as I said, it is PDP government. They should have remained so that the little progress that was made during Obasanjo's government would have been built upon with corrections made on the way. There were areas, even with the economic reform programme; there were areas that needed corrections. All the projections of the millions of jobs that the recovery programme was supposed to produce did not come. We needed to ask ourselves why we did not get the jobs, which the programme promised, and make required corrections. So there are those kind of problems. And for me, the way we are going, this vision 2020 programme based on the Seven Point Agenda, we are yet to see the structure that will actualise them.
During your tenure as Speaker, can you tell us one or two occasions in which you found yourself presiding over a matter that you would rather not.
You have to be specific because to ask this question you must have something in mind; a situation in which I found it difficult to take a decision because of the nature of the subject? I can't recall one.
Okey, let us be more specific let us take one that we think was very open. How did you feel about the tenure elongation?
You see; for me, I made a statement, which at the time I gave no negative meaning. When the President came up with the issue of political conference, he invited us and he was thinking whether there was a way we could make it legal maybe by making a law of it. We told him it was unnecessary because it was just an executive body to advise him. So when the budget came, we rejected the provision made for it, that we could not provide for an executive committee to advise the President. You know that before the conference ended there was the issue of adding two years. So when I was invited at a joint sitting to give the vote of thanks, I told Obasanjo that our mandate from Nigerians was for four years, during which he had our guaranteed support. On the same day editors came and when they asked me about additional two years for Obasanjo, I told them it was immoral. Our contract with Nigerians was for four years. There was no way we could change that if midway without going back to them. From there I had taken a decision. But as Speaker, I moderate debates; so whoever brings anything, whether I like if or not, it is my duty, based on the oath of office I took, to ensure that it is presented for members to decide. So really I had no problem because whoever wanted me to do anything other than moderating and going by the majority, was wasting his time because members are independent.
So you were never under any pressure over the third term?
You do not say pressure. You do not pressurise somebody who is not answerable to you. You lobby him and try to bring him to queue into your line. It has happened severally on so many occasions.
Where you under any pressure at all?
Well, I think Nigeria was under pressure. Everybody in Nigeria was under pressure at that time.
Not every Nigerian was under pressure compared to your situation?
No. Well; yet the pressure was relative to the position you occupied so when those things come up, we knew everybody would answers his fathers name.
Were you offered any money? Maybe to bend the rules?
I don't know of any money that was offered to bend the rules. Everything was done in the open. All this money you people are talking about, I don't know about it.
So when the third term agenda collapsed, how did you feel? Justified?
Relieved. Relieved because the entire country was under tension.
As a loyal party person, you remember that the PDP was supporting that third term attempt?
You see, the leadership of the party in Abuja propagated and supported it. But I tell you, majority of party members and leaders at the local level were not supporting. If you say the people in Wadata House were supporting it, yes, but majority of party leaders outside Abuja were not in support of amending the constitution to provide for tenure extension.
What was wrong with the idea?
You see, the problem many people failed to see is the matter precedence. Obasanjo may be the best person in Nigeria. He has done his term. If you say because he has done so well, that he is doing very well, that he needs more time to keep doing well and that you will amend the rule specially for him, what do you do when you have a bad person tomorrow? Some people who are enjoying that bad person would want an extension of tenure for him. So the issue is not about whether anybody is doing well or not. The issue is about creating a dangerous precedence. If you amend the Constitution to allow one person to do a third term, tomorrow another person will say he wants a fourth term because you have created a dangerous precedence.
Now we are talking about another constitutional amendment. If you were given the chance to speak on behalf of all Nigerians, what will be the first and most important amendment you will suggest?
Look when Senate invited me to chair one of their sessions in 2007 in Port Harcourt, the issue of Constitutional amendment came up; I told them that Constitutional amendment from our experience as a nation, must be taken carefully. They should look for those areas that are not contentious and attempt to amend them and see how it goes. But once they bring in contentious issues, amendment will never happen. This is the situation in which we are now. You know the procedure for amending the Constitution. We are already two years into the life of this parliament. It has two years left. Within the next one year they will start to look for re-election, the attendance and attentions to the working of the parliament will be halved. By the time you go into election mood, there won't be attendance or quorum. So we advised them to be cautious. State and local government creation for instance are contentious issue. They affect or are directly related to revenue allocation.
Even in the heat of the amendment debate in 2006 and 2007 when the state creation carrot was dangled to the South East in exchange for support, we all knew that it was not possible. The process is difficult. You had to get the support of 24 states out of 36. Everybody wants local governments created where they would use to get a share of the revenue. We the elite feel that if we get a state created for us, we become governors or godfathers in that area. If you create a local government for me, I become chairman or at least I will be eating from there. It is not about service. If it were, we ought to look at state creation so far and ask ourselves how it has impacted on the lives of the people in the state. Has life become better for them as a result of creation of states? The lives of the ordinary Nigerians remain as if states were not created.
The power play between the House and Senate seems to have stalled the amendment of the constitution?
For me, that crisis was all unnecessary. In fact this kind of situation never came up in our time. In any joint sitting then, the Senate President took precedent. In his absence I presided. I was a member of the Constitution review committee between 1999 and 2003. The deputy Senate President presided and in his absence, the deputy Speaker. So this controversy of position never came up. During the amendment debate however, when the committee was to hold public hearings, we became a bit apprehensive because of the role of some senators. You know they went to various zones and had two days public hearing. We agreed then that the Senate chaired the first day and the House the second because majority of the House members were not disposed to the posture of many Senators. It really wasn't an issue during our time.

Original_Search_Title: Education for All Initiative On Track
Headline: Ghana: Education for All Initiative On Track
Body_Text:
Accra — Almost all countries endorsed by the Education for All Fast Track Initiative (EFA FTI) are on track to achieve a primary school completion rate of at least 80 percent by 2015 as stated in the latest EFA FTI publication entitled "Sounds from the classroom".
"Sounds from the classroom" features five EFA FTI countries (Ghana, Guyana, Madagascar, Mongolia and Yemen) focusing on how basic education programs supported by the EFA FTI partnership have impacted the lives of children, parents and communities positively.
It tells the story of a nine-year old girl from Madagascar who will be the first of her family's eight children going beyond fifth grade and hopes to continue to high school. It is about a grade two student of an elementary school in Accra, Ghana who just loves to go to school everyday to learn and eat her bowl of boiled 'yam and 'kontomire' which keeps her going for the whole day. It is also about a herder in Mongolia's remote steppe who takes his young son to a mobile pre-school by horseback - something that was unimaginable when he was a child.
The past decade has seen unprecedented gains in access to education in developing countries. In sub-Saharan Africa alone, the countries supported by the EFA FTI, an international partnership established in 2002 to help achieve universal primary education, saw a net gain of 15 million primary school students over a six-year period. Despite these high enrollment rates, much more remains to be done, but this is real progress, catalyzed in part by the EFA FTI which has seen the allocation of over US$ 1.4 billion through its main Catalytic Trust Fund.
The Education for All Fast Track Initiative is an international partnership that connects donors with low-income countries on the basis of countries' national education plans. These plans are ambitious and will send many more children around the world to school and give them a chance for a proper education. The EFA FTI manages, among others, a USD 1.5 billion trust fund to support countries in achieving their education goals. It currently endorses the education programs of 37 developing countries, and hopes to help around 18 million more children who are still out of school by 2010.

Original_Search_Title: Identify Your Culture
Headline: Rwanda: Identify Your Culture
Body_Text:
Kigali — Defining culture is no easy task because it means many different things to many people who come from many different places. There are easily as many definitions of culture in the whole world!
But as an individual it's important to identify one's culture more especially in Africa which is the second largest continent in the world and has the most of the world culture heritage.
Some anthrolopologists [people who study human development and culture] consider culture to be social behaviour. Some consider things such as artefacts, dance music, theatre and fashion constitute to cultural heritage.
Others believe that no object can be culture which is philosophical depending upon ones thinking. Culture as defined by the United Nations Education, Scientific, and Cultural Organization [UNESCO] is 'the set of distinctive spiritual, material, intellectual and emotional features of society or a social group and that it encompasses in addiction to art and literature, lifestyle, ways of living together, value systems, traditions and beliefs.
Cultural identity is the feeling of belonging to a certain group or society. Although for cultural groups to form its unity it has to pass though certain norms for members to share the same norms and values.
Although in some cases after some unpleasant events that the society has had to go thoughts difficult to continue implementing unity so that's when culture comes in to unite the society.
Taking our beautiful motherland that experienced a crisis that no other nation should go though, as an example, in her healing from the past the culture of Gacaca was the healing pill that she had to use since it has always been part of the culture of peace and reconciliation that was used in the traditional courts.
So while other groups or society only know what unites them after contact with other cultures and the differences in occurrence is what shapes the cultural identity. So simply define what you are by what you are not.
Cultural diversity must be preserved and promoted, as the lack of knowledge, appreciation and understanding of other cultures often causes conflict and hate between people especially differences between people within any given nation or culture are much greater than differences between groups.
Education, social standing, religion, personality, belief structure, past experience, affection shown in the home and a myriad of other factors will affect human behaviour and culture.
One threat to cultural diversity is cultural imperialism which is a very common aspect in our world today, where one finds our various cultures are being diluted, in an era of globalization; where one dominant culture overpowers others and is seen as more advanced than others which could be positive and superior or negative and most times out weighs the positivity.
Many people believe that Western culture dominates all other cultures in the world today which is quite true. A perfect example of culture imperialism is how the United States is leading the way with McDonaldization and Disneyization of which personally I am a fun of.
But these terms not only describe the spread of American fast food restaurants and tourist attractions to the rest of the World, but also the spread of the principles of running McDonald's and Disneyland to other industries and in different nations which is a first way of making money especially in developing countries. But this shouldn't make us forget our cultural values.
Countries such as France and England are also spreading their culture to former colonies making them lose their natural identify which is very essential for the existence of any society. Another concern is regional cultural imperialism.
This is happening mostly in Asia, where the Koreans are concerned about the spread of Japanese culture, and people in Hong Kong are being influenced by both Korean and Japanese culture.
Many people are afraid that these trends will eventually lead to cultural homogenization and at this point there will be no more need for our different beautiful cultures which make us unique and influence in many different ways.
Sometimes when cultural groups feel threatened by cultural imperialism, they may use the defence of culture and tradition to validate practices that negatively privilege one group over another within a group.
For example, women who object to the unequal treatment that they are subjected to in their religious or ethnic groups are punished symbolically or with force for importing Western ideas. Which isn't entirely their fault?
Paying attention to customs and cultural differences can give some one outside that culture a better chance of assimilation or acceptance well as ignoring these can get an unsuspecting person into trouble.
Much of the differences in culture have to do with food preparation, music and what each culture considers politeness.
West African countries really value their culture of food values I recently was chatting with a friend of mine from Nigeria and we were talking about what we had for lunch so I was busy saying I had chips, rice, sausage and he simply said that he had had yam porridge.
I was deeply impressed to the cultural identity. So as we go around interacting with others of different cultures, there is no specific good substitute for receptiveness to interpersonal feedback, good observation skills, effective questions and some horse sense that you do need.
There is always much to gain by observing how people of the same culture interact with each other because however much our cultures are different they always have one aspect that brings a unique and strong way.
So don't be afraid to ask questions as most people respond very positively and with pride to inquiries about their culture. So go ahead ask a variety of people so that you can balance views.

Original_Search_Title: Lagos Blast Victim Died of Ignorance - Army
Headline: Nigeria: Lagos Blast Victim Died of Ignorance - Army
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Army has absolved itself of blame in the bomb explosion on Tuesday which killed a secondary school student at the Ikeja military cantonment in Lagos.
It said the boy died as a result of ignorance and total disregard of order.
Joseph Tim was until his death a student of Government Secondary School, Nasarawa, from where he visited his uncle in Block A3 at the cantonment.
He was killed in the process of roasting three tubers of yam opposite the area where bomb blasts had occurred seven years ago.
Public Relations Officer of the 9th Brigade, Major Emmanuel Adeniyi, said the area has been condoned off and embargo placed against the making of fire there.
He disclosed that it was one of the ordinances buried under the spot where Tim was roasting yam that exploded, resulting in his death and a severe injury in another person who was with him.
Adeniyi confirmed that "after the last explosion in 2002, the Army authorities invited foreign experts who really did their best in clearing the relics. Even home-based experts were invited and they are still working in the area."
Responding to claims that the area is not safe despite assurances, he said: "Even after the Second World War there are still some relics found on one spot or another" as clearing exercise is a continuos process.
"The Army has fenced off the area and renewed its warning against the making of fire in the vicinity.
"Discovery of ordinances or similar objects should be reported to the authorities immediately,"Adeniyi implored.
It was learnt that experts doing the cleaning job have visited the scene of the latest incident, a stone throw from Ammunition Transit Depot (ATD) where the first explosion occurred on January 27, 2002.
The team includes the General Officer Commanding (GOC) 81 Division, Major General Eugene Nwanguma; Chief of Policy and Plans at Army Headquarters, Major General Abubakar Atofarati; Corps Commander, Army Engineers, Major General Essien; Corps Commander, Army Ordnance, Major General Abioye; and Director of Military Intelligence (DMI), Brigadier General Umoru.
The experts were deployed on the orders of the Chief of Army Staff Lt General, Abdulrahman Dambazau.
Atofarati said the fuse of a bomb, not a bomb itself, may have been what killed Tim.

Original_Search_Title: Bomb Blast - Experts Alert On Strange Objects
Headline: Nigeria: Bomb Blast - Experts Alert On Strange Objects
Body_Text:
Lagos — A team of bomb experts from the Nigerian Army Engineering and Ordinance Corps yesterday visited the Ammunition Transit Depot (ATD) at the Ikeja Cantonment, site of Tuesday's bomb blast which killed a secondary school student.
The delegation was led by Major-General Abubakar Adefarati.
After over two hours of investigation, the team declared that the bombs at the ATD were not at the surface level but were buried underground and as such posed no immediate threat to lives and property in the barracks.
The cantonment houses the 9 Brigade of the Nigerian Army.
Acting at the instance of the Chief of Army Staff, Lt.-General Abdulraman Danbaba, the team, however, advised officers, soldiers and their respective families to report to the army authority when they notice any strange object around their vicinity,
Spokesman of the 9 Brigade, Captain Emmanuel Adeniyi, told THISDAY that the entire area had been condoned off to avoid any re-occurrence.
"A team of engineers from the Nigerian Army Engineering Corps has visited the place. After a thorough investigation, the engineers said that the stored bombs at the ATD were not at the ground surface level and as such pose no immediate treat to lives and property. They advised inhabitants of the barracks to report presence of any strange object in the environment," the command spokesman said.
In a related development, the Nigerian Army has confirmed the death of a school boy in the bomb blast.
Director of Army Information, Brig-Gen. Christopher Olukolade, who confirmed the incident to the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN), also added that a team of bomb experts was sent to Lagos to ascertain the degree of damage in the barracks.
"We are in Lagos on a high-powered delegation sent by the Chief of Army Staff, Lt. Gen. Abdulraman Danbaba, to investigate the incident and to ensure that it did not pose any danger to the area," he said.
He said the delegation had confirmed that the vicinity of the Ikeja cantonment was safe.
"A lot of efforts have been made to make the barracks and its locality safe since the 2002 bomb blast; UK experts teamed up with our own experts to declare the cantonment safe," he said.
Olukolade described the incident as isolated, saying the effect of the blast was not as much as the 2002 experience.
In 2002, explosions rocked Ikeja Cantonment, leaving over 1000 Nigerians dead including those who ran away from the scene and got drowned in the Oke-Afa Canal at Isolo, Lagos.
Meanwhile, security personnel, comprising soldiers and military police, have beefed up security around the barracks to check the influx of people into the barracks.
Visitors were questioned at both entrances to the barracks by stern-looking soldiers to ascertain their destination before they are allowed in.
Many visitors, majority of whom had nothing to do in the area but only came to see the place in the light of the news of the blast, were asked to go back.
Ikeja Cantonment was on Tuesday filled with fear and sad memories as a bomb went off, killing a secondary school student.
The explosion was said to have occurred at noon when the victim, whose name was given as Joseph Tiem, and who was working as a labourer at a building site within the cantonment, went to roast a piece of yam.
Unknown to him, he was roasting the yam directly on top of some bombs buried at the ammunition transit depot where high calibre bombs are stored.
Tiem, said to be a student of Government College, Gudi, Nasarawa State, was killed instantly.

Original_Search_Title: Blast Kills Lagos Kills Student Roasting Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Another Bomb Goes Off At Ikeja Cantonment
Body_Text:
Lagos — Sad memories and fear gripped the Army Cantonment, Ikeja, Lagos, yesterday as a bomb went off and killed a secondary school student.
Coming seven years after the massive explosions at the cantonment which led to the death of over 1000 Nigerians, the latest incident sent residents of the cantonment, which houses the 9 Brigade of the Nigerian Army, into pandemonium.
The explosion was said to have occurred at noon when the victim, whose name was given as Joseph Tiem, and who was working as a labourer at a building site within the cantonment, went to roast a piece of yam.
Unknown to him, he was roasting the yam directly on top of some bombs buried at the ammunition transit depot where high calibre bombs are stored.
Tiem, said to be a student of Government College, Gudi, Nasarawa State, was killed instantly as a bomb went off.
He was said to be on holiday and was staying with his uncle, identified as Staff Sergeant Dominic Tiem. His body has been deposited at the mortuary of the Military Hospital.
Immediately after the blast, soldiers cordoned off the entire area. Only soldiers were allowed to enter the barracks.
Yesterday's explosion occurred despite assurances from some experts from the US who certified the place as 95 per cent "okay". They were in Nigeria to help detonate the buried bombs after the 2002 explosion.
The Army Chief of Policy and Plans NA, Maj. Gen. Abubakar Atofarati, along with some Principal Staff Officers at Nigeria Army headquarters, flew into Lagos at the instance of the Army Chief, Lt Gen. Abdulrahman Dambazou, and headed straight to the accident spot.
Addressing journalists, he said the incident was not a bomb blast but a fuse that went off as a result of the heat from the fire.
He said the army chief had directed that the place be cordoned off and that army experts led by the Director, Army Engineering Corps would visit the place to ensure that it is safe.
He explained that bombs do not like excessive heat, differentiating yesterday's incident from the 2002 tragedy. He assured all soldiers and officers that there was no cause for alarm, explaining that the incident does not mean that bombs are still scattered all over the place.
Recently, the General Officer Commanding (GOC) 81 Division of the Nigerian Army, Maj. Gen. Eugene Waguma, expressed worries over the condition under which arms and ammunition were kept.
He told the Minister of State for Defence, Chief Ademola Seriki, during the latter's visit to Lagos that arms and ammunition received from Ikorodu were being kept in the open.

Original_Search_Title: N11.4 Billion Non-Oil Exports to U.S. Recorded in 2008
Headline: Nigeria: N11.4 Billion Non-Oil Exports to U.S. Recorded in 2008
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC) yesterday said Nigeria recorded N11.4 billion (76 million dollars) as non-oil exports to the United States in 2008.
Acting Director-General of NEPC, Mr Aliyu Lawal, told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) in Abuja that the exports were made up of cocoa, spices, sheer butter, packaged gari and yam flour.
He also said that non-oil exports raked in N270 billion (1.8 billion dollars) for the country in 2008 against N210 billion (1.4 billion dollars) recorded in 2007, attributing the increase to incentives given to exporters to encourage non-oil exports.
"The incentives had great impact in motivating exporters because it reduced the cost of doing business for them," he said.
Lawal noted that in spite of stringent conditions in accessing the US market under the African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA), Nigerian exporters, nevertheless, exported goods to the US
"We in NEPC have been trying to expand the base by promoting made-in-Nigeria commodities to the US
"We had two seminars in Abuja and Lagos to train the entrepreneurs and encourage new ones to export to the U.S. market," he said.
Lawal said in 2007, Nigeria got a re-certification from the US to export sea foods to its country after meeting all the requirements.

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan Loses Three Aides in Katsina Auto Crash
Headline: Nigeria: Jonathan Loses Three Aides in Katsina Auto Crash
Body_Text:
Abuja — Two journalists and a security detail attached to Vice President Goodluck Jonathan died in a car crash on Thursday night on their way to Katsina for the wedding ceremony of Nafisat, daughter of President Umaru Yar'Adua to Governor Isa Yuguda of Bauchi State.
That occurred on the day another fatal auto accident claimed four lives and injured eight others down East in Enugu.
The deceased in the Katsina auto crash are Assistant Chief Information officer, Brian Ata; a cameraman, Emmanuel Babarinde; and Security Officer, Thomas Adeyemi.
Six other officials in the bus in which they were travelling sustained various degrees of injury, and are being treatment at the Katsina General Hospital in Katsina where the bodies of the deceased have also been deposited.
The accident occurred about 8 p.m. in Charanchi town, near Katsina, when the bus veered off its lane and somersaulted.
Jonathan is expected to arrive for the wedding which takes place at Yar'Adua's Quarter, Saulawa, Katsina.
A statement issued by Jonathan's spokesman, Ima Niboro, confirmed the deaths, and quoted the Vice President as expressing "shock and sadness over the incident. He has also condoled with the families of the deceased and prayed God to comfort them, and grant repose to the souls of the departed."
Katsina State Police Commissioner, Dan, Azumi Doma, confirmed the deaths and said three of the wounded "are in critical condition".
Doma also said police from Kankia Local Government division took victims to the hospital.
He assured people coming for the wedding that the Police are on top of security situation in the state.
The Enugu accident involved five vehicles, including two trucks and mini bus, and occurred along Onyema Mine axis of Enugu-Onitsha expressway, known as a hot spot for road accidents.
Witnesses narrated that the accident took place when an Enugu bound trailer loaded with yam lost its brake and rammed into a speeding J5 commercial bus filled with passengers.
Three other oncoming vehicles, including a truck and a bus belonging to Enugu Transport Company (ENTRACO), were consequently affected by the accident.
Three passengers from the J5 vehicle, two men and a woman, lost their life on the spot while nine were fatally wounded.
Officer in Charge of Public Enlightenment, Federal Road Safety Commission (FRSC), Humphrey Emeanuru, confirmed the incident to Saturday Independent.
Emeanuru said one of the injured persons later died, bringing the death toll to four. He said the corpses have been deposited in a morgue while the injured were rushed to the hospital.
He blamed the accident on excessive speed.

Original_Search_Title: Between the Unknown Soldier And the Unknown Civilian
Headline: Nigeria: Between the Unknown Soldier And the Unknown Civilian
Body_Text:
Last week, with great pomp and pageantry, my dear country honoured the unknown soldier. The "unknown soldier" is defined by the Encarta Encyclopaedia as "the unidentified remains of a soldier killed in action." The tradition started in the United States of America when the remains of an unidentified soldier killed in the First World War in France was buried at Arlington National Cemetery, Arlington, Virginia, in 1921 with the inscription on the tomb, "Here rests in honored glory an American soldier known but to God."
The tradition has spread to several countries of the world which have picked days to honour soldiers who die in battle. In Nigeria it is January 15 every year. This ritual was observed last week, but just like the new yam festival, I wonder if it is necessary.
Americans have every reason to celebrate their soldiers. I doubt whether my country has any reason yet (please note the "yet") to celebrate our soldiers. The American Army fought and secured American Independence from Britain in the famous American War of Independence. Nigerian soldiers were instruments used by the British to haunt, harass and suppress Nigerian nationalists who fought for independence for Nigeria. American nationalists who sought and fought for independence were soldiers. Nigerian nationalists who waged an irrepressible campaign for Nigeria's independence were civilians.
The American Civil War was started by civilians who disputed over the slave trade and slavery. But the Army came in to clear the mess. The American soldiers fought in the war to free slaves and enforce the fundamental human rights of all Americans and at the same time keep the United States of America one. The Nigerian Civil War was started by soldiers who had covetous designs on the country and led to the deaths of countless civilians. Soldiers started the war, messed up the country, tore it apart, killed civilians and impoverished the country.
American soldiers have always fought to protect American democracy. Nigerian soldiers have always truncated our democracy and imposed tyranny of the worst kind on the country. The present democracy was not given to us by the Military; it was forced out of their hands by civilians - unknown civilians who littered the streets of Lagos and other parts of the country in a sustained protest against the Military dictatorship of the evil General, Sani Abacha. The National Democratic Coalition, Afenifere, Civil Liberties Organization, Nigeria Labour Congress etc.
Of course you remember the unidentified remains of the civilians who died in "action"for democracy in the streets of Lagos and in other parts of Nigeria while protesting the unjustified and irresponsible annulment of the June 12 Presidential Election by General Ibrahim Badamasi Babangida. You, of course, remember that up till now no one has seen the corpse of the journalist, Bagauda Kaltho, who was picked up by the Strike Force during the Abacha days for publishing things the army did not like. He is believed to have been killed while some claim that he was the unidentified corpse dumped at the scene of an explosion by the Strike Force, Abacha's killer squad. The Strike Force was fond of exploding bombs in order to create a siege complex in the nation.
Who killed Kaltho and all these other civilians? The Nigerian Armed Forces, Police and security agents. Ask any of them and he would tell you that they were following orders and would add that it was nothing personal. But the first order a man should obey is his conscience.
When communists in the defunct Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR) staged a coup and Boris Yeltsin urged Muscovites to rise up and resist the coup, the USSR refused to fire on their fathers and mothers, brothers and sisters because they were not trained to kill their countrymen - they were trained to defend them with their own lives. In 1986 in the Philippines, the country's rulers sent soldiers to arrest opposition figures but millions of citizens formed human barricades around the camps to protect the opposition figures. The Filipino Army refused to fire on unarmed civilians claiming they were not trained to kill Pilipino. But when Babangida sent out soldiers into the streets of Lagos and other towns, they left the remains of thousands of unarmed and unidentified civilians - unknown civilians.
The coup by Major Chukwuma Kaduna Nzeogwu led to the death of many Northern civilian leaders. The counter coup led to the death of many military officers of Eastern origin. The civilians joined the army example, and there was a pogrom in the North in which many unknown civilians were killed and buried in a mass grave. Note that this again were unknown civilians, not soldiers.
Of course it would be wrong not to give the Nigerian Army credit for its bravery and courage - especially in other lands. At Burma, during World War 11, the Nigerian Army fought with extraordinary zeal and courage in spite of heavy losses, inclement weather and logistical deficiency, and was adjudged by allied forces to be the best fighting unit in that theatre of war. In Liberia, they fought with zeal and determination and won democracy for Liberia. In Sierra Leone, they single-handedly stopped the bloodbath there, and set the stage for other peacekeeping missions to join them.
The Nigerian Army is actually like cow dung. It is not good when heaped in one place - Nigeria. But they do a lot of good when spread around in the world. That is why I think that the unknown Nigerian Soldier is absolutely deserving of honour and should be honoured and celebrated accordingly in Burma, Liberia, Congo, Sierra Leone, and in all other places where they have gone to on peace-keeping or peace-enforcing operations. As a matter-of-fact they should be celebrated everywhere except in Nigeria. In Nigeria we should fix a day and celebrate the unknown civilian.
After all, the Afro-beat legend, Fela Anikulapo-Kuti, had already immortalised the Unknown Soldier in a song with that title "Unknown Soldier na him do am Unknown Soldier Left right left" I see you are humming the tune already. I understand. Lets give the Unknown Soldier the song, and the Unknown Civilian? Inscribe over him "Here lies in honoured glory, a Civilian known but to God."

Original_Search_Title: Small Loans Widen Horizons for the Poor
Headline: Kenya: Small Loans Widen Horizons for the Poor
Body_Text:
Does microfinance benefit economic development? When asked that question, James Mwangi, chief executive officer of Kenya's Equity Bank, responds in the affirmative. With even small loans, he told Africa Renewal, "We have seen families graduate from micro-enterprises to semi-medium enterprises."
Microfinance is a type of lending that targets low-income individuals who may find it difficult to borrow from regular banks because they lack collateral or a credit history. It is not new. Since the 1970s non-governmental groups have given small loans to poor people, especially women and those in the informal sector, to meet basic needs or to start up or expand businesses.
But an increasing number of individual and institutional investors worldwide are putting more money into the sector, reports the Consultative Group to Assist the Poor, (CGAP), a World Bank affiliate that works with governments to expand credit to poor people.
Traditionally, microfinance loans, typically between $20 and $300, were provided by non-governmental organizations (NGOs). Most such groups were not registered as financial institutions, and generally depended on donor funds for the money they lent. That made them vulnerable to changes in donor policies and to rigid rules about the types of projects they could finance.
Then the Grameen Bank in Bangladesh, the first private financial institution to extend microcredit on a large scale, established a new model. Grameen Bank demonstrated that it was possible to extend loans to millions of poor people and still make a profit. It also showed that the poor can be entrepreneurial and creditworthy.
In the last two decades microfinance has grown beyond just lending. According to CGAP, microfinance today refers to "retail banking for poor people" and includes insurance and other services and innovations such as mobile banking.
Rural women in Senegal sell processed mangos and yams: With just a little credit, poor producers can expand their businesses and incomes.
The demand has also grown tremendously, far beyond what can be provided with the available financing. A 2007 study by Germany's Deutsche Bank reported that while $4.4 bn is invested in microfinance worldwide, about $250 bn is actually needed. That demand has made microfinance an attractive option for investors seeking alternative financial instruments that are not tied up with increasingly volatile world financial markets. Such interest has not only made larger loans available, but also has made more credit and financial services available to the poor, especially women (see page 10).
Loan guarantees
Donna Katzin, of the New York-headquartered non-profit funding organization Shared Interest, says that loan guarantees have been one of the more successful innovations in the sector. Shared Interest has followed in the footsteps of the Swiss-based Recherches et Applications de Financements Alternatifs au Développement (RAFAD), which since the 1980s has guaranteed loans that commercial banks issue to microfinance institutions and projects.
By guaranteeing loans, RAFAD and Shared Interest have substantially reduced the risks that commercial banks assume when lending to individuals and groups without credit histories or collateral. The promise implied by the guarantee - that Shared Interest or RAFAD will meet part of the losses if the borrower defaults on the loan - encourages banks not only to make such loans, but also to release more money than they otherwise would. In two decades, RAFAD and its Fonds International de Garantie have issued $53 mn in guarantees on some $212 mn in credits. The funds have created 260,000 small business jobs worldwide, benefiting an estimated 1 million people.
Shared Interest works with RAFAD in South Africa. It has invested $11.4 mn in Thembani Guarantee Trust since 1994. The money earns a return of about 2 per cent in interest.
Being a non-profit, Shared Interest keeps its own returns low. But private lenders often earn more. For most poor borrowers, even interest rates as high as 20-30 per cent are still lower than the exorbitant rates usually charged by loan sharks, often the only other sources of financing where access to commercial banks is limited. Microfinance investors can therefore earn a lot more than through other traditional investments.
A growing sector
Attracted by the potential, private enterprises such as MicroVest, a US private microfinance investment fund, have poured $1 mn into Ghanaian microfinance lender Sanapi Aba Trust. Similarly, AfriCap Microfinance Fund, formed in 2001, has invested in 12 microfinance institutions, including in Ghana, Kenya, Senegal, Madagascar, Malawi, Mozambique, Nigeria and Sierra Leone. AfriCap, which has about $50 mn in capital, was the first Africa-based equity fund to be entirely focused on microfinance.
The results have been significant. The cash injection from AfriCap and Helios International, in exchange for 12 per cent and 25 per cent ownership shares, respectively, in Equity Bank of Kenya, helped turn the formerly small microfinance lender into a major commercial bank. It now serves 2.5 million lower- and middle-income Kenyans. Equity Bank was also able to buy Uganda Microfinance, the biggest microfinance institution in that country. The beneficiaries have been small and medium-scale businesses.
In 2004 Equity Bank became the first African microfinance institution to be publicly traded. By 2006 it had extended loans of more than $106 mn, much of it to women. Its investors have made a tidy profit. "We have seen a 7 per cent return on our assets and grown by 200 per cent," says Mr. Mwangi.
The growing interest and investment in Africa's microfinance sector, Mr. Mwangi believes, are largely the result of "dwindling investment opportunities elsewhere." There is also a "growing recognition that Africa has turned a corner. People are seeing the prospects in Africa, and strategically positioning themselves to take advantage of the continent's growth."
Partnerships give hope
With a dual goal of making profits and helping poor people gain access to financial services, private enterprises are increasingly partnering with donor agencies to jointly invest in microfinance. Such partnerships are in line with the 2002 Monterrey Consensus, in which heads of state worldwide agreed on priorities for financing development. Those leaders recognized the importance of microfinance and committed to promote "private-sector financial innovations and public-private partnerships." Such partnerships, they hoped, would strengthen the capacity of domestic financial institutions to cater to people who have been poorly served, such as rural residents and women. Those two markets, CGAP estimates, account for two-thirds of all microfinance borrowers globally.
One public-private partnership is the GroFin Africa Fund. Worth nearly $150 mn, GroFin is a consortium that includes the African Development Fund, the World Bank's International Finance Corporation (IFC), Deutsche Bank Foundation Americas, Skoll, Syngenta and the Shell Foundation, among others. The fund plans to invest directly in about 500 small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Rwanda, Ghana, Nigeria and South Africa.
GroFin personnel also provide technical assistance to businesses, to help them become more stable and profitable. Combining financing with business advice was a deliberate strategy, Kenneth Onyando, GroFin's East Africa regional investment manager, stated in 2007. "African SMEs too often struggle to find the capital they need because banks see them as too risky an investment," he said. "By integrating funding with business development assistance, we are offering a viable solution to this problem - giving SMEs hope and delivering returns to investors."
Business Partners International (BPI) of Kenya is a similar consortium. It includes the IFC, the European Investment Bank, the East Africa Investment Bank and the Kenyan private equity funds Tran Century and CDC group. BPI set up a $14.1 mn fund in February 2006 and provides loans ranging from $50,000 to $500,000 to its clients. The fund takes collateral when it is available in order to reduce the risk of default. However, when potential borrowers lack collateral, its lending decisions are based on "the viability of the business," BPI's chief investment officer, Sally Gitonga, told local media.
Behind schedule
Despite the growing volume of private and donor finance entering the sector in Africa, "microfinance in Africa is at least five years behind schedule, compared to South Asia or Latin America," Sasidhar Thumuluri, an analyst for MicroVest, told an investment publication. The biggest bottlenecks, he said, are "poor infrastructure, weak institutions, lack of financial and human capital."
However, he added, "recent positive changes such as establishment of democratic institutions, reverse migration of qualified professionals and improving governance in countries like Ghana are attracting greater investor interest."
Ms. Katzin notes that for initiatives such as those of Shared Interest to succeed, "there is a need for a formal banking system that has sufficient capital and is able to work with international letters of credit." Success will be difficult, she told Africa Renewal, "where regulatory environments are not conducive."
Another concern is default. Ms. Katzin argues that technical assistance is critical for clients experiencing problems, to help them perform better and reduce the risk to investors. By providing such assistance, Shared Interest has kept loan defaults to 3.2 per cent.
To further secure investor money, Shared Interest has also set up a loss reserve fund. "We are not avoiding risk," says Ms Katzin. "We are managing risk. If people fail to pay, we use our reserve fund to make up for the difference. Until now, no investor has lost a penny of principal."
Too poor for loans
Not all people are ready for credit, Ms. Katzin acknowledges. Some are so poor that taking out a loan could further mire them in debt and poverty instead of helping. Because such groups are extremely vulnerable, donors must continue to provide support in the form of grants. "There are some countries where you would not recommend a loan scheme, because poverty is so entrenched," she explains. "In such places, you need grants first, to get people on their feet, before graduating to other forms of capital."
Mr. Mwangi agrees. "You have to help households meet their livelihood costs first. Then you see children staying in school longer and the health status of the family improving. It is only then that the family can be able to save and also consume more."
Once a family is able to save, Mr. Mwangi believes, microfinance can then serve to help the family reach higher goals, such as using credit to create and expand an enterprise. "It is at that point that you start having economic growth."

Original_Search_Title: Currency in Circulation Hits N1.2 Trillion
Headline: Nigeria: Currency in Circulation Hits N1.2 Trillion
Body_Text:
Lagos — Currency in circulation reached N1.2 trillion by the end of last year, showing an increased difference of N167 billion between November and December, the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) reports.
Information obtained from CBN website yesterday in Abuja, showed that the total money in circulation stood at N988 billion in November, but by the end of the year, it had risen to N1.2 trillion.
In October, according to the apex bank's website, currency in circulation stood at N966 billion, showing an increased difference of N22 billion in November.
The report said the total circulation figure in September was N976 billion, showing a decrease of N10 billion by October.
In August, the figure stood at N948 billion, an appreciation of N28 billion between August and September.
The circulation was N937 billion in July, while in June it stood at N918 billion, indicating that between July and June, there was an increased difference of N19 billion.
In April, the figure went up to N899 billion and rose to N917 billion in May, with a difference of N18 billion.
Currency in circulation in January was N865 billion, dropped to N861 billion in February and rose to N891 billion in March.
Economic experts said the increase had taken a toll on price level of goods and services, warning that the price level would continue to rise.
National Bureau of Statistics said it was yet to compile the price level and year on year inflation rate, but its website had figures for January to November, 2008.
According to the NBS website, inflation, which started on a single digit in 2008, rose to double digit in July.
"The rise in the index was caused by marginal increase in prices of staple foods such as maize, yam, guinea corn, millet, meat, fruit and vegetables.
"The level of the composite food index was higher than the corresponding level a year ago by 18.1 per cent.
The average annual rate of rise of the index was 15.3 per cent for the 12-month period ending November 2008," the website noted, adding that the year on year average Consumer Price level as at October 2008 rose by 14.8 per cent, which was slightly higher than 14.7 per cent observed in October 2008.

Original_Search_Title: Igbinedion - NCP Condemns Plea Bargain
Headline: Nigeria: Igbinedion - NCP Condemns Plea Bargain
Body_Text:
Benin — Chairman, National Conscience Party (NCP), Dr Osagie Obayuwana, yesterday condemned the plea bargain entered into by the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) with former governor Lucky Igbinedion.
The party described it as smacks of equal application of law and a wrong signal to the anti-corruption crusade of President Umaru Yar'Adua.
Obayuwana, who spoke with THISDAY in an interview, said there was provision for the practice worldwide, adding that sometimes, prosecution looks at the fact and not the razzmatazz, where they feel that the process of prosecution may be cumbersome, a plea bargain could be entered.
He said the mood of the nation at this time did not portray the country as one committed to fighting corruption, adding that "the utilisation of plea bargain at this time sends a wrong signal on equal application of the law. Nobody enters into a plea bargain with somebody who has stolen a cow or bag of yams and sometimes you find very harsh sentence emanating from our courts against common thieves.
"It is sad that the man that steals the resource of the entire state and sent so many families into hunger and many people to their early graves is allowed to be gallivanting about freely," he said.
Obayuwana said the plea bargain took place about the same time Nuhu Ribadu was receiving knocks from the Federal Government, adding that the money allegedly stolen by Igbinedion belongs to the people of Edo State and not the Federal Government, and called on the Federal Government to refund all recovered monies from Igbinedion to the people of Edo State.

Original_Search_Title: Bujagali Dam Investor Refunds U.S.$75 Million
Headline: Uganda: Bujagali Dam Investor Refunds U.S.$75 Million
Body_Text:
Kampala — The $75m the Government lent to the Bujagali power project has been paid back.
The Government had also given the company another $17.5m for the resettlement of people living along the transmission line.
The money was lent to the firm, BEL, to enable it start the project immediately as it mobilised its own funds.
"We are going to use this $75m and other funds raised internally for the construction of a dam at Karuma falls," the President said.
"The dam had been estimated to produce 200MW, but we have found that it can produce 700MW."
He made the remarks yesterday while addressing members of the Presidential Investors Round Table at the Serena Hotel Kampala.
The group comprises business executives and was formed in 2007 to promote investments.
Museveni said in order to improve the business climate, the Government had concentrated on energy and infrastructure.
"Depending on external partners made us make a lot of mistakes, especially in the area of energy and we ended up with a shortage," he said. "Even now many factories depend on generators, but we created an energy fund to help us move the power projects quickly and among them is Karuma."
Museveni said the Bujagali Dam, with a total price tag of $770m, would generate 250 Megawatts when complete in 2011.
He also said the Government also experienced problems with donors in the construction of roads.
"Today they promise they are to work on this road and they do not implement it, so we developed the road fund of about $600m and all major roads will be done."
He said the Government was working with Kenya, Tanzania, Southern Sudan and Congo to develop a railway network.
The Government, he said, had implemented the neighbours' recommendations to stop encroachment on national parks.
In any case, he noted, tourism was more lucrative than cultivation of maize or sweet potatoes in the parks.
He said the Government was would not compromise on strategic forests.
Uganda Investment Authority board chairman Patrick Bitature said investors had complained about the high fuel and food prices and inflation.
Bitature hailed the partnership between the Government, the private sector and international entrepreneurs to improve the business climate in Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: The History of Park Yard Market
Headline: Uganda: The History of Park Yard Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE establishment of Park Yard Market is rooted in the history of St. Balikuddembe (Owino) Market. Owino Market was started in 1971 by 320 vendors who were displaced from Nakasero Market because of lack of space.
The vendors found an elderly man called Owino, who was roasting maize and sweet potatoes in the current location of the market. That is how the market was named Owino.
The initial structures were made of papyrus, which were replaced by iron sheets before permanent structures were built.
Owino, which is the biggest open market in Uganda and possibly in East Africa, is built on 7.04 hectares of land.
With time, it became overcrowded, forcing some of the vendors to move out to the expansive parking yard belonging to Nakivubo War Memorial Stadium.
The yard runs around the stadium's perimeter wall.
Park Yard, however, is not a legally gazetted market. Owino currently has 50,000 vendors, 70% of whom are women.
An estimated 200,000 people visit both Owino and Park Yard daily.
The two markets existing side by side, are not under the same management, though the public mistakes them to be one.
Park Yard stretches from where Owino Market stops at Nakivubo Mews and skirts the stadium up to Namirembe Road and stretches to Kafumbe Mukasa Road in Kisenyi.
According to the chairman of the market vendors association, Godfrey Busulwa, Park Yard is divided into 26 zones.
The yard accommodates at least 25,000 vendors dealing mainly in second-hand clothes, shoes, umbrellas, household items and polythene bags.
Busulwa, who has operated in the market for 20 years, says there were about 3,000 stalls in the market and all the traders left their merchandise on site daily.
When the market started in the 1980s, it was managed by Kampala City Council (KCC).
