Meanwhile, the opposition yesterday stayed away as Parliament debated and passed the Appropriation Bill, 2009. The Bill authorises public expenditure amounting to sh6.6 trillion for this financial year.
Led by their leader Ogenga Latigo, the opposition walked out of Parliament on Tuesday contesting the budget for patriotism promotion, Kampala International University, the presidential banana initiative and for the construction of a market in Juba, Southern Sudan. They threatened to sue the Government over the matter.
In the Bill, Parliament approved an amendment to have 70% of the budget for drugs go directly to the National Medical Stores, which is responsible for procuring drugs. In the past, only 30% of the budget went to NMS
Additional reporting by Joyce Namutebi and Cyprian Musoke

Original_Search_Title: Opinion - is Rwanda Ready for Branding?
Headline: Rwanda: Opinion - is Rwanda Ready for Branding?
Body_Text:
Kigali — Businesses all over Kigali know the importance of logo, billboards and print advertising in the media. But, we have yet to see how they use these marketing channels to define what their products and services mean to their customers.
Or is it that most companies do not know what branding is, and that in order to better reach out to their customers they have to do more than just advertise? Do they know that branding is more than just advertising?
Today many people from East Africa and abroad are flocking to Rwanda because of forward-thinking government strategies and initiatives tied to private sector. It's the new 'in' place to be.
But if Rwandan businesses and entrepreneurs don't market themselves well enough they could be overshadowed by the competition.
There are many well-known Rwanda-made products like AKABANGA hot sauce, MANUMETAL metallic furnishings, BOURBON Coffee as the first local coffee retailer, UTEXRWA for its fiber innovations in silk and banana fibre, and our very own Rwanda Television.
The question remains, how will these businesses sustain in the new Rwanda?
The new Rwanda is marked with an influx of advertising giants, business strategists and marketing professionals with a handle on what it means to carry, sell and promote branded products and services.
They indiscriminately invest heavily in brand strategies that provide consistent messaging about who they are and what they provide to their customers - with no hesitation.
Are our established local businesses readily positioned for the competition? And, how important is it for them to know how to market themselves?
Before we even ask ourselves those questions, I believe its better for us to focus our attention on whether or not we know what branding is. It has become this hot new buzzword for improving the competitiveness and innovation of business development in Africa.
Yet, most African-based business owners are not even aware of what it means and how best to utilize professionals and the tools they provide to add-value to their products and services.
Branding is not a logo, a billboard, or a print advert. Brand is an emotional connection between the customer and product generated over time.
Brand education will be a requirement for doing business in Rwanda. I will share the nature of what this means through a weekly series of articles on branding in The New Times.
Hopefully you will begin to visualize what branding means for business. The title of the column will be called Branding 101 beginning October 8th, 2009. I look forward to sharing these stories with you.
Deborah Kezaabu Kakoma is the founder and executive director of ZAABU brand studio.

Original_Search_Title: Government Screens Riot Suspects
Headline: Uganda: Government Screens Riot Suspects
Body_Text:
Kampala — SCREENING of persons arrested during the recent riots in Kampala is underway and the innocent will be released, internal affairs state minister Matia Kasaija said yesterday.
"Those found to have no connection whatsoever with the riots will be released," he told Parliament.
He was responding to a request by the Kabarole district Woman MP, Margaret Muhanga, to help trace 18-year-old Hope Stephen from Humura village in Burahya constituency.
She said Stephen is believed to have been rounded up in the riots in Kampala and his whereabouts are unknown. Muhanga said the boy's mother, a councillor, had approached her to help trace the boy.
The riots rocked Kampala, its suburbs and a few towns in Buganda for three days after the Police stopped the kingdom's prime minister from travelling to Kayunga to prepare for the Kabaka's visit, citing security reasons.
Last week, the MPs demanded a full list of the victims of the two day riots.
Kasaija, who said he has the list, added: "We know where each person is, if at all he is in the hands of the Police." Kasaija said innocent individuals who had already been charged were likely to be released. He apologised for the inconvenience which innocent people suffered.
The Minister is expected to present a statement to Parliament on the riot victims today.
Meanwhile, Parliament yesterday ended prematurely after opposition MPs walked out before debate on the Appropriation Bill of 2009.
Leader of Opposition Prof. Morris Ogenga Latigo said his side was contesting the sh6.5b budgeted for patriotism promotion, the sh10b for the presidential banana initiative, the sh4b for Kampala International University, which belongs to tycoon Hassan Basajjabalaba, and the sh1.73b for the construction of a market in Juba, Southern Sudan. It was the second walkout by the opposition for the same reasons.
Before adjourning the House, Ssekandi said: "We should teach ourselves tolerance. If you are defeated, you continue carrying out your work." Prof. Ephraim Kamuntu, the state minister for planning, condemned the walkout. He said the budget was beneficial to the whole country.
Addressing journalists later, Latigo said: "We have given the NRM the space they need. They can use the numbers they have and let the Ugandan public see."
He said the opposition wanted sh3.5b of the amount allocated to Kampala International University to be given to Mbarara University and the patriotism money be used to pay teachers.
"Patriotism money is about the 2011 elections," Latigo asserted.
Aruu MP Odonga Otto (FDC) said he had been instructed by Latigo to sue the Government over the matter.

Original_Search_Title: The Insulted of the Earth - Sedition Laws Across Africa
Headline: Africa: The Insulted of the Earth - Sedition Laws Across Africa
Body_Text:
Nairobi — I call on all ministers and assistant ministers and every other person to sing like parrots.
During Mzee Kenyatta's period, I persistently sang the Kenyatta tune.
[When] people said 'This fellow has nothing to say except sing for Kenyatta,' I said I did not have ideas of my own. Who was I to have my own ideas?
I was in Kenyatta's shoes and therefore I had to sing whatever Kenyatta wanted.
If I had sung another song, do you think Kenyatta would have left me alone?
Therefore you ought to sing the song I sing. If I put a full stop, you should put a full stop.
This is how the country will move forward. The day you become a big person, you will have the liberty to sing your own song and everybody will sing it."
Thus spoke the then Kenyan president Daniel Arap Moi, in what has to be the most eloquent exposition of the intent of the sedition law ever uttered.
Historically, sedition is the crime of speaking words against the state.
Its basic premise, that it is wrong to criticise the leadership, fundamentally flies in the face of the tenets of democracy, which demand the ability to criticise leaders as a sina qua non for informed choice.
In its modern meaning, the charge of sedition first appeared in the Elizabethan Era (c. 1590) as the "notion of inciting by words or writings disaffection towards the state or constituted authority."
According to Curtis C. Breight, author of Surveillance, Militarism and Drama in the Elizabethan Era, sedition complemented treason and martial law: While treason controlled primarily the privileged and ecclesiastical opponents, and martial law frightened commoners, sedition was meant to cow intellectuals.
Under English common law, a statement is seditious if it "brings into hatred or contempt" the Queen or her heirs, or the government and constitution, or either House of Parliament, or the administration of justice, or if it incites people to attempt to change any matter of church or state established by law (except by lawful means), or if it promotes discontent among or hostility between British subjects.
A person is only guilty of the offence if they intend any of the above outcomes and, interestingly, proving that the statement is true is not a defence. It is punishable with life imprisonment.
Thin-skinned African despots have routinely deployed sedition legislation that derives from their UK and French colonial heritage to deal with dissent and to contain bothersome journalists.
According to the World Association of Newspapers, laws on insulting leaders are in force in 48 out of 53 African countries, and are "the greatest scourge" of press freedom on the continent.
In the first five months of 2007, "insult" laws led to the harassment, arrest or imprisonment of 103 journalists in 26 African countries.
Last week's arrest of Ugandan's Radio One talk show host Kalundi Serumaga and his release on bail after being charged with six counts of sedition, is only the latest incident. Serumaga, who spent four days in police custody, was accused by the state of "intention to bring into hatred, contempt and to excite disaffection against the person of the President" during a television show on September 11.
Less than a month before that, three executives of the Ugandan bimonthly The Independent were interrogated by police for publishing an allegedly seditious cartoon of President Yoweri Museveni.
One of three, managing editor Andrew Mwenda, is already facing a sedition charge from 2005, one of 21 criminal counts that he is fighting in the courts.
In 2005, Mwenda was arrested and charged with "sedition" and his employer, KFM Radio, was briefly banned after he commented in a talk show on a possible Ugandan government role in the death of Southern Sudanese leader John Garang.
In Kenya, the Sedition laws (Section 56, 57 and 58 of the Penal Code) were repealed by the Inter-Parties Parliamentary Group reforms of 1997.
Prior to that, they were used to curtail any form of discontent, their enforcement sometimes bordering on the ridiculous.
For example, in 1990, Rev Lawford Ndege Imunde was sentenced to six years' imprisonment for "printing and possessing seditious publications exciting disaffection against the President or the Government of Kenya" after he noted in his desk diary that Robert Ouko was murdered with the connivance of the government.
Though Article 18 of the Tanzanian Constitution guarantees every Tanzanian the right to freedom of opinion and expression, the Newspaper Act of 1976 allows authorities within the government -- including the president -- the power to prohibit publications that might be deemed to not be in the nation's best interest.
It defines an act, speech or publication as seditious if it aims to bring lawful authority into hatred or contempt, or excites disaffection against the same, or promotes feelings of ill-will and hostility between different categories of the population.
Anyone printing or publishing a newspaper that contravenes these provisions is liable to face a fine or a maximum sentence of three years.
In October 2008, the radical tabloid, Mwanahalisi, was banned for three months for allegedly publishing seditious articles.
Information Minister George Mkuchika explained the rationale behind the ban thus: "The newspaper is fond of publishing articles that ridicule senior government leaders including president Jakaya Kikwete and the ruling Chama cha Mapinduzi."
In Burundi, Anaias Havyarimana and Honoré Misago were arrested in September 2008 on charges of insulting President Pierre Nkurunziza after they were overheard criticising his education policy in a private conversation. They were both remanded in custody until their acquittal in December.
In November, well-known former radio journalist and political activist Alexis Sinduhije, was charged with "insulting the president," based on a document found in his possession following an illegal police search during which a search warrant for different premises altogether was delivered two hours late, referring to a judicial file that did not yet exist.
The recovered document purportedly stated that "the responsibility for the corruption scandals and the assassinations ordered by the party CNDD-FDD lie with the man who passes his time in prayer meetings," and, according to the prosecution, referred to and insulted President Nkurunziza, a born-again Christian.
Gambian law intimidates the independent press through prison terms for reporters found guilty of sedition -- broadly defined -- or libel, and a requirement that newspaper proprietors must sign a $16,600 bond (with their houses as guarantees) to be allowed to publish.
In 2008, UK missionaries David and Fiona Fulton were sentenced to a year's hard labour over "seditious" e-mail to friends in London in which they describe the Gambian President, Yahya Jammeh, a man who believes he can cure Aids on Thursdays using herbs and bananas, as a "madman." They were also fined Â£6,250 each.
They pleaded guilty to charges of "printing, publishing or reproducing publications with intent to bring hatred or contempt or to excite disaffection against the president or the government."
In 2007, five journalists and a teacher were convicted of insulting Mali President Amadou Toumani Toure, and given suspended sentences over a school essay.
Teacher Bassirou Kassim Minta had asked his final-year secondary school class to write a humorous essay about the mistress of a fictional African leader.
He was arrested, along with Seydina Oumar Diarra, a journalist who wrote an article in the Info-Matin newspaper criticising the teacher for assignment.
Following the detentions, the article was reprinted in other newspapers, leading to the arrest of the other journalists.
Côte d'Ivoire journalist Nanankoua Gnamanteh was brought before an Abidjan court in March this year on a charge of insulting President Laurent Gbagbo in an article that appeared in Le Repère under the headline "Ali Baba and his 40 thieves," together with a photo of the president and several of his close associates and referring to Gbagbo's period as president as "nine years of political fraud... outright theft, embezzlement and kleptomania at the summit of the state."
The prosecutor requested a two-year prison sentence and asked the court to suspend Le Repère for eight months and to fine its publisher, Eddy Péhé, 10 million CFA francs (15,200 euros), despite the fact, as noted by Reporters Without Borders, that the country had decriminalised press offences.
Gnamanteh and Péhé were fined 20 millions CFA francs (30,000 euros) each, and the newspaper was suspended for eight weeks.
Though Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak has pledged to amend the 1996 Press Law and abolish prison sentences for press offences, that did not save Ibrahim Issa and Sahar Zaki, editor and journalist, respectively, of the opposition weekly Al-Dustur, who were each sentenced to one year in prison in June 2006 for "insulting the president" and "spreading false or tendentious rumours."
The charges were brought by the "ordinary people of al-Warrak," who were reportedly offended by an April 2005 article that reported on a lawsuit brought by a man from the village who accused President Mubarak of unconstitutional conduct and "wasting foreign aid" during the privatisation of state-owned companies.
In Zimbabwe, even musicians have not been spared.
Happison Mabika and Patience Takaona have been in hiding since last year when they failed to attend a court to answer charges of singing songs "too sensitive and insulting" to President Robert Mugabe.
Their lawyer Charles Kwaramba later claimed that the duo were in fear for their lives.
Although the country now has a coalition government, Dread Reckless and Sister Fearless, as they are better known by their fans, have not emerged. If convicted, the two face a two-year stint in jail.
In many jurisdictions across the globe, sedition is either formally or effectively a dead letter.
Recognising that criminal sanctions for criticism of leaders should never be imposed, the British government has not charged anyone with sedition in over 30 years and has announced its intention to repeal the law.
It is time African governments followed suit.
For too long, governments on the continent have used these laws "ruthlessly, to prevent critical appraisal of their performance and to deprive the public from information about their misdemeanours," as WAN put it.
Ugandan journalist Andrew Mwenda succinctly captured the irony of the situation, explaining that "in a country that is a democracy (or pretends to be) and not a monarchy, it is my right to cause public disaffection against the person of the president or the government so that at the next election people can vote against both."
Patrick Gathara is general secretary of Katuni, the Association of East African. Website: www.gathara.com Blog: http://gathara.blogspot.com/
Matters are not much better in the Democratic Republic of Congo, where Nsimba Embete Ponte, editor of the biweekly L'Interprète, was last year handed a 10-month prison sentence for "insulting" President Joseph Kabila by referring to rumours about Kabila's health in a series of articles.
Arrested on March 7, 2008 in Kinshasa by members of the National Intelligence Agency (ANR), Ponte was held incommunicado for three months before being transferred to the main Kinshasa penitentiary.

Original_Search_Title: AGF Wrong Dabbling Into Matters Under Investigation
Headline: Nigeria: AGF Wrong Dabbling Into Matters Under Investigation
Body_Text:
Lagos — The attention of the international community was once again focused on Nigeria recently when the sale of the major shares of one of Nigeria's leading telecommunications companies in controversial circumstances resulted in the ongoing investigation by the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission.
In two years, the nation has undergone the Wilbros, Halliburton and power probe scandals. Coming on the heels of the recent scandal rocking the banking sector, many are of the view that the V-mobile saga is one scandal too many. JUDE IGBANOI spoke with Dr. Konyinsola Ajayi, SAN at his Banana Island, Lagos offices on these controversies
The present administration came into power with a lot of expectations from Nigerian, but that enthusiasm seems to be waning by the day. For instance, most people no longer have much faith in the rule of law policy of the Yar'Adua government. How would you assess the government in this regard?
Unfortunately I would not score the administration well at all. Indeed, I think against the backdrop of their policy of rule of law and the fact that this government is supposed to have been pivoted on the rule of law, they have failed abysmally.
From the point of view of funding, I have not seen any increased funding in the judiciary. I haven't seen any serious adherence to the tenets of the rule of law. It seems that what the public sees on the other hand is that the rule of law has for the most part been used as an excuse to do what good public administration does not require.
For instance, the administration came out through the fourth estate of the realm, the press. They claimed that there were allegations against some former governors in respect of the V-mobile shares. The Attorney-General was quick to jump into the fray to make a statement ostensibly on the basis of the rule of law, only for the arm of government that had the responsibility of that matter to deny the statement of the Attorney-General. Well, the Attorney-General has since come up to say that he was misquoted; I'd like to believe that it was the case. But it's just an example of the fact that this flag that they wave all the time is a red flag that beckons a bull where there is no matador in the ring to deal with the bull. That is why we have the Nigerian crowd in the ring. So what happens is that the bull runs riot and kills people.
These point to the fact that the rule of law has been used to the disservice of the people.
A few have scored the administration above average in view of what transpired in Edo and Ondo states governorship election, that the Federal Government deliberately refused to intervene even when the courts ruled against the ruling party. The administration did not bother to interfere with the outcome of these appeals?
There are two things that we need to look at. Yes, there is the law and there is also democracy. The third I daresay is the civil society. I am sure it was very clear to the President himself that if he had intervened, we would have had a state of emergency in those states. It would have been like what we had in the 1983 elections when the Federal Government tampered with the results in a number of states including Ondo State. I am sure you know what happened in that state. There was mayhem. So, it was very clear to the Yar'Adua administration that if they had tried any of those Obasanjo tricks, they would have had a serious problem in their hands.
They were also cautioned by what happened in the case of Andy Uba and Ngige and the problems that arose from that. The government would have made itself a laughing stock. As far as I am concerned, I honestly believe they had no choice. I am not sure they did that as a matter of rule of law. That was proper exercise of the doctrine of separation powers. For me, I am not going to score them as having a pass mark on the rule of law on that basis.
The past few days have seen Nigerians being assaulted again with another scandal. This has to do with the sale of the V-mobile shares by the major share holders. The controversy that is currently ragging is the claim by the Attorney General of the Federation that the three ex-Governors were cleared, while the EFCC has maintained that it is not the case. Some see this as an embarrassment to the nation. With the magnitude of transaction involved do you share that view?
As you know the V-mobile share issue is not new. It started out with an allegation by Strive Masawiya who used to be part of the company called Econet Wireless International; this was when Ribadu was there and there were no public statements as to what happened. Now the issue has resurrected from abroad. That in itself is a bit of an embarrassment.
That is the major topic for discussion. That it took the British authorities under the Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty to bring the V-mobile scandal to our attention. What does this say about our anti-corruption crusade?
Yes, we must look at it from the alleged comments of the Federal Attorney-General. He has since denied them. Now, as the press reported that he made those statements, the point is really that it is not the responsibility of the office of the Attorney-General to comment on investigations. It is not his business! It is for the police or the customs, immigration or EFCC to carry out such investigation depending on the issues. But in this case, where the allegations bordered on finances, the agency of government with the power to investigate the allegation and come out with the findings is the EFCC. The Attorney-General ought not to have out his foot in it. If again that was done in the name of the rule of law, I say again that it is a disservice.
Now, do we exonerate the EFCC?
We don't know exactly what they have been doing. It is notionally possible that the EFCC was still carrying out its investigation. The only question any reasonable person can ask is, 'this was supposed to have been done a long time ago. We ought to know the result of that investigation.' But I would not necessarily blame the EFCC because the nature of crimes that were alleged to have been committed are so intertwined that it would require a lot of time to unravel. It took the government of the United Kingdom to say so.
The former Governor of Delta State was said to have used the state's $38 million shares in Vmobile as collateral for a personal loan. What is the legal implication of this? In spite of this the Attorney-General of the Federation allegedly went ahead to clear him.
I don't think that there can be found in any provision of our Constitution any law that would allow a state governor to pledge the shares or assets of the state for a private transaction. I doubt if such a law exists in Delta State. As a matter of fact we should look at the spirit of the provisions of the fundamental directives of state in the Constitution in relation to corruption and the ICPC Act. Against the backdrop of the decision of the Supreme Court in the case of the ATTORNEY GENERAL OF ONDO STATE v ATTORNEY GENERAL OF THE FEDERATION, interpreting the ICPC Act and its legality, you would see clearly that what the Governor is alleged to have done would be unconstitutional, unlawful and illegal!
When you pledge assets, it means you own those assets. Those assets in question belong to the good people of Delta State. They do not belong to the Governor. There can be no authority whatsoever that would enable a state Governor to charge such shares to a third party. What benefit would come to the people of the state? If the loan is not repaid the state government stands to lose $38 million which would go a long way in bringing succour to the people. This is part of what the good people of Delta State suffer today as a result of the bad administration in the state, in spite of the huge federal allocations to the state in the past 10 years. It is a shame that Delta State is not a mini-Dubai today given the enormous resources that have been allocated to the state.
It is a fraud on the people of the state to pledge their assets in favour of a third party in a private transaction. It is unfortunate that the people of the state are so impoverished that they cannot go up in arms. In some other societies that is what you would see.
Money laundering is clearly behind these allegations. How adequate and effective are our anti-money laundering laws? Do they have the necessary bite?
Our money laundering laws are embedded in three Acts basically. These are the EFCC Act, the Money Laundery Act and our Criminal Code. They go far enough to deal with the problems. The EFCC Act, for instance, empowers the body to go to court to seize the assets of accused even during investigation. That is the kind of thing that we ought to see. Our money laundering laws are robust enough to deal with the situation.
The nation has witnessed a lot of investigations in the recent past - the Halliburton saga, power probe, Wilbros and many others. The reports never come to light and nothing happens at the end of the day. What would be your recommendation to ensure that the report of this investigation doesn't go the way of the others?
It's all about good public administration. I think until we are able to get true representative democracy and the people get those who will truly represent them, we are going to keep having these kinds of problems. We have the laws, we have the institutions. It's just simply, the question of the dishonesty of our leaders. If our leaders are honest enough, if we have good public servants, there will be no problems at all. We only need men like Ribadu, we need men like Lamido Sanusi. We more of such public officers in Nigeria. The more of them we have the better this country would be.
In spite of the overwhelming public support for the CBN Governor's action in sacking the Managing Directors of five banks recently, a few people have raised eyebrows as to the true intentions of the CBN Governor. For such people, the five directors were not given a fair chance to state their case before the CBN. Do you share such an opinion?
There are two elements to this. What does our Constitution say? Some have said they have a right of fair hearing under our Constitution. Now, what does section 36 say? It says you have a right to fair hearing in a court of law or a tribunal. The CBN is not a court of law or tribunal set up by law, so section 36 does not apply to the actions of the CBN and this has been made clear by several pronouncements of the Supreme Court.
This notion that section 36 applies to master/servant relation or every civil matter is untrue. The second point is that the Supreme Court in as far back as 1966 in the case of MERCHANT BANKING CORPORATION v FEDERAL MINISTRY OF FINANCE held that banking license does not afford you a civil right under the Constitution. That the banking license is a privilege and therefore the protection that the Constitution affords does not extend to banking licenses.
Now, it not the case that one is not entitled to natural justice. That is what Justice Nnaemeka Agwu held in the case of BABA v NIGERIAN CIVIL AVAITION AUTHORITY. He said that natural justice and fair hearing under the Constitution is different from the concept of natural justice under common law. But when a statute steps in common law takes the back seat. So there were no constitutional rights infracted here.
Here, we have the Banking Act. What the Banking and Other Institutions Act (BOFIA) says in section 31 is the CBN can carry out its investigation of any bank. It also provides for Special Examination. This is where it looks at the banking system, taking into consideration the public good; it here gives the individual bank a chance to respond to the issues raised. That same Act provides in section 33 and 35 for Special Examination. It does not provide for hearing under special examination. This is what makes the difference between the rights of 140 million people and the rights of one man. The law maker did that as a matter of wisdom.
This is where the doctrine of necessity comes in. There is recently decided case by the Privy Council in England coming from the Bahamas. It decided that where banking issues come up and there is a system risk, the Governor takes steps; you cannot second guess the Governor. That when a bank is in distress, the governor must take steps in the interest of the common man. Should he wait for the situation to deteriorate before taking steps? I am afraid the consequences would be grave.
What about the insinuations that the exercise was a selective one, given the fact that only five out of all the banks were examined?
Well, the CBN Governor has made it clear that he does not have the manpower to examine all the banks at the same time. He actually examined 10 banks and the other ones are being examined, the results will be out shortly. The CBN governor has said this. The Bank of England moved against 10% of the banks there out of 300 banks in England. There over 7,000 banks in America and the authorities recently moved against only 20.
Lamido has explained that he discovered that these five banks constituted 90% of the window of inter-bank exchange. He said there was a lot of activity in the petroleum sector and these banks were the main financiers. This is because there was a rise in petroleum prices up to 147USD and all of a sudden it crashed to 47USD. There were clearly problems that he needed to deal with. Don't forget that he had noticed that these five banks were the largest users of the expanded window of inter-bank exchange. As a prudent banker, he needed to take very urgent steps as soon as he smelt a rat.
If those banks were allowed to crash, they would have pulled down most of the second or third generation banks. I think it was timely intervention for which Sanusi deserves kudos.
Many thought that family banks would go with the Soludo consolidation. But Sanusi has revealed that family banks still exist and that he is determined to put an end to the trend.
There are clear rules and the CBN and SEC should be blamed for these lapses. The laws make it clear that a bank cannot borrow money to buy shares. There are clear rules that in public offerings you do not use borrowed money. That it can only do that when its staff are involved and that what it can give is only to the tune of one year of a staff's emolument.
There is public trust in banking. Every kobo they give out is our money. That is why the question keeps coming up as to whether there should be family franchises whether in banking or other big businesses.
What we have just experienced in this banking saga is unprecedented in the Nigerian banking sector. Given the volume of funds involved and the calibre of personalities, does it have the possibility of posing any challenge to our criminal justice system?
Sure it does. This is because some of these offences are criminal in nature and it is only the Federal High Court that has jurisdiction. But the states' High Court should be able to exercise jurisdiction over the criminal offences. That would reduce the burden on the Federal High Court. There are not so many of these courts.
Given the poor showing in the 2007 elections would you say we are prepared for a better performance in 2011? The same persons and institutions are still there and government has refused to accept the recommendations of the Uwais Commission.
The President had noted that the 2007 elections were shambolistic. I hope they will do the right thing and that the will of the people will be allowed to prevail.
Ghana had its elections and the ruling political party lost. There was even a re-run and the ruling party still last. Ghana is a developing nation like Nigeria. They have similar problems. They have hamlets and villages like Nigeria, yet they were able to organise successful election.
It's all about the political will and once we exercise that we get it right.
The name of your law firm seemed to have changed recently. We now see Olaniwun Ajayi LP. What is it all about?
We must commend the foresight of Professor Yemi Osinbajo SAN who proposed a bill before the Lagos State House of Assembly that made the law on Limited Partnerships possible. The present Attorney -General has even taken it further by making possible to have Limited Liability Partnerships.
We were only privileged to have been the first law firm in Nigeria to take advantage foot that law to create a Limited Partnership in legal practice. We were determined to be one of the best in Nigeria. W desire to move with the times and be abreast with international best practices.
The whole concept is have a firm with limited liabilities of the partners, so that in the event of failure, the loses of the partners would be minimised.

Original_Search_Title: One Police-Military Clash Too Many
Headline: Nigeria: One Police-Military Clash Too Many
Body_Text:
Lagos — Six policemen on routine patrol on the coastal waterways near the Takwa Bay, Lagos jetty of the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC), recently chased a speedboat they suspected to be occupied by some pirates.
On their way, a civilian boat occupied by about five naval men allegedly blocked the policemen. According the policemen's account, they told the naval men that they were chasing some suspected pirates who had just driven past them. To use their own words, "instead of assisting us in the chase, one of the naval guys lifted his rifle and, before we knew it, shot a constable in our boat." Although, according to an eyewitness, the other naval men thereafter dropped their rifles, none of them assisted in rushing Constable Festus Akinruntan, the victim, to the military hospital, Ikoyi, where he was rushed to for treatment.
Tragic incidents of this nature have become a recurring decimal among units of the country's security forces. In October 2005 a similar tragic fracas had played out in Lagos when the Nigeria Police Force and the Nigerian Army engaged in the exchange of gunfire, causing casualties and extensive damage to vehicles and buildings. A police operative at Ojuelegba had reportedly assaulted a soldier in multi after some argument. The military officer promptly returned to his base, the Abati Barracks, Yaba, to mobilize his colleagues on a vengeance mission. There were four deaths and 60 charred vehicles in the wake of the disgraceful incident, while many Police buildings in the Area 'C' Command of the Force, at Surulere, Lagos, were razed to the ground. The arsonists in the invading force, in a temporary delirium, stormed the detention cells at the Police station, pulled down the cells' doors and iron protectors and released all suspects, among them armed robbery and homicide suspects.
They did not stop at that, the Exhibit Room of the command, housing vital documents including reports and files on investigations and court cases, was completely destroyed. The Police armoury was vandalized and combat weapons stolen.
In April 2006, there was yet another clash between policemen and men of the Nigerian Air Force at the PWD section of the Oshodi-Agege rail line in Lagos. The policemen had reportedly gone to conduct a raid on a shack suspected to be a base for criminals smoking marijuana. Whereas the men of the Air Force, in their account, alleged that the policemen assaulted them in the process, the Police claimed that the Air Force men were shielding the suspected criminals from lawful arrest. Be that as it may, what ensued was anarchy on parade, leading to several causalities, as reported. Similar incidents have virtually become a routine occurrence across the country.
We bemoan the professional and ethical aberrations of security operatives that predispose them to such lawless and wanton destruction of life and property, the very opposite of their duties to the society, for which the nation commits huge funds from its slim coffers. Granted that ours is still a very imperfect society, the truth is that Nigeria, as a 21st century sovereign state in a global world, is no longer a jungle. What really could be the explanation for the armed forces, supposedly the very embodiment of regimented discipline, descending into street brawls with sister security agencies, abusing the arms of the state in shows of shame? How can the trained security forces of a nation, bearing the country's arms for the people's defence and to maintain law and order, repeatedly descend into open beastliness? Is Nigeria, in fact, a banana republic?
The recent Takwa Bay clash between two branches of the armed services - and coming on the heels of numerous other such encounters nationwide - confirms an urgent need for the military authorities to devise more effective coordination and control of joint or overlapping operations by the security agencies, in order to prevent or minimize such ugly incidents, some of which possibly arise from genuine mishaps.
Issues of the quality of institutional discipline remain to be addressed, however. And grave indeed are these issues. From the military authorities, a lot of explanation is needed regarding what command structure it operates that makes it so easy for an aggrieved soldier to mobilize and arm colleagues for a combat operation against another security agency - often in broad daylight, with civilians, including children, watching in amazement - or how a naval rating in a civilian boat could open fire, unprovoked, at a police team on sight. It seems to us completely bizarre that a professional military operative involved in a brawl with a policeman or any other citizen while outside his base could move into the barracks and readily muster a combat force to inflict maximum havoc on their target.
The authorities must unravel the factors that render time-honoured military traditions susceptible to frequent abuse in this country, with impunity. The rampant evidence of persisting, brazen acts of indiscipline by personnel of the armed services constitutes an alarming threat to the nation's evolution of a democracy firmly built on the rule of law.
More worrisome still is the fact that the crude lawlessness that is threatening to turn the security forces into a national security risk reflects a weakness of political leadership in the country. When elected officials and top public servants openly disobey even Supreme Court rulings, then is the nation unwittingly flinging open the doors of anarchy. Such lawlessness has weakened critical institutions of state and eroded public faith in the justice system and the rule of law.
Is this the type of society we want to build and bequeath to future generations of Nigerians? The nation's Commander-in-Chief should rouse himself to his duty of ensuring that the security forces respect and obey the laws of the country, and operate under the civilised traditions of their noble professions.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni - a General On a Mission
Headline: Uganda: Museveni - a General On a Mission
Body_Text:
It was clear from the outset that we had a crisis on our hands. A cloud of anxiety hang in the corridors as lanky security operatives from the Presidential Guard Brigade patrolled the Parliamentary Building.
For the first time in a long time, and I mean a really long time, attendance of a parliamentary sitting would be excellent. There were dozens of lawmakers making first appearances in weeks. It is Tuesday September 15 and Parliament is convening for a special (read: emergency) session.
President Museveni is a man in a hurry, leading a country that has seemed to defy time. He has precipitated a rare sitting of the House. A few days earlier, the country was witness to dramatic political developments in the form of a civil uprising in various parts of central Uganda.
The uprising had demonstrated how the populace is increasingly turning militant and rebellious to the extent that they no longer fear the police or teargas and are ready to confront authority.
Mengo, the seat of Buganda Kingdom had shed off its historical cowardice in the face of gross provocation and showed that they are primed to take charge of their own destiny.
Unfortunately, 24 people had been confirmed dead courtesy of bullet-inflicted injuries, many more had been injured, while hundreds had been thrown behind bars.
The NRM regime was certainly under pressure, and its leader, President Museveni, found himself in no enviable position. It seemed things were falling apart and something had to be done immediately. Mr Museveni's mission was clear; achieve success in calming rising tensions and prevent this tribal nation from collapsing.
The government had to put its foot down and say enough is enough. For much of that morning, the chatter was about what the President would tell Parliament and what the government would do to restore sanity.
The House was scheduled to convene at 2:00pm. We strolled downstairs into the basement of the Parliament Building, to the conference hall where this special sitting of the House would take place. It is 2:15pm and MPs are still queuing through security checkpoints. I eavesdrop a conversation between two MPs and hear one ask whether President Museveni's relentlessness and address to Parliament will make a difference in calming a situation that seems resistant to resolution.
MPs trickle in casually despite the urgency of the situation but find moment to exchange pleasantries, hug colleagues and gossip. Army MP Gen. Elly Tumwine strolls to one end of the hall to meet two female MPs looking splendid in purple attire. He holds out his fancy phone and gestures to the MPs to pose for the camera.
Click, he snaps and smiles away. It is 3:33pm, an hour-and-a-half behind schedule. The Sergeant-at Arms, Mr Ahmed Kagoye, enters the conference hall and makes the announcement; "Hon members, His Excellency President Museveni and the Rt. Hon. Speaker."
In robotic fashion, we all stand up and the Police brass band plays the national anthem. Then, the first moment of controversy presents its self. Errant lawmakers from the Opposition rudely interrupt parliamentary decorum and sing the Ekitibwa kya Buganda anthem as soon as we are done with the national anthem. They were quickly halted in their tracks by heckles and boos but the symbolism was not lost.
Mr Edward Ssekandi convenes the plenary and speaks of the inspiration behind the special sitting. Aware that emotion had been riding high, the Speaker did his homework and told MPs that there would be no debate following the President's speech.
He cited rule 9 of the rules of procedure to back his ruling which says a presidential statement "shall be heard in silence and not followed by any comment or question."
Several MPs mumbled in disapproval but the protest was useless. And then the man of the moment, draped in a grey pinstriped suit, rose to the podium as his sheer force of personality took command.
You could hear a pin drop. The President opened his address by extending deep condolences to the families of the bereaved and then quickly moved to apportion blame for the uprising. He pointed fingers of accusation at the Opposition and representative MPs made a point of throwing back taunts by hissing and scoffing. But Mr Museveni would take none of their claptrap and shot back: "Those who think Uganda is a banana republic and you can change leadership by riots are wasting their time," he said to the ululation of ruling party MPs.
For the next two hours of his speech delivery, it was a case of taunts from the opposition and applause from his NRM MPs. He did not quiver or flinch as he proposed a way forward to the impasse that had somewhat brought everything in the country to a standstill.
Cultural institutions, he said, should forget about political involvement and concentrate on preserving our roots. Maintain "traditional sites like palaces and burial sites", he said. It was a moment to remind those instigating unrest just who is in charge of the State.
The Buganda cause had provided a lightning rod for all disenchanted and disaffected sections of Uganda to rally and press for regime change.
We all know the government is not without blemish for the unprecedented mayhem we witnessed but hey, reputations were on the line, authority had been threatened, and the very existence of a regime was at stake. How else would the big man have reacted?
When the curtains finally closed, NRM MPs handed the President a standing ovation while the critics gnashed their teeth. It was a defining but necessary moment, in which Mr Museveni achieved measurable success on a mission quite gruelling.

Original_Search_Title: Arusha From Different Perspectives
Headline: Tanzania: Arusha From Different Perspectives
Body_Text:
Arusha — Arusha municipality has various identities depending on perspectives.
From overseas, Arusha is an international town reminding in part that it hosts serious international institutions such as the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR). The Tribunal tries individuals who have been accused of playing roles in the genocide and human extermination in that country. Interested individuals and organized groups can request for permission to attend ICTR proceedings. Offices of the Tribunal are located at the Arusha International Conference Center (AICC) complex along the East African Community Road.
At the regional level, people in the East African Region recognize Arusha as a natural Headquarters of the East African Community (EAC). It is a natural choice for the purpose because the Headquarters of the newly constituted EAC is to be established in Arusha despite strong earlier opposition by some members of the Community particularly from Kenya. It can be recalled that the earlier version of the EAC was located in Arusha as well. That version collapsed in 1976.
Nationally, the AICC complex has stayed ahead of the pack as regards hosting of major international conferences in the country. AICC, hence Arusha have been synonymous to international conferencing over the years.
AICC management and therefore Arusha intend to stay ahead of the newly established international conference centers in Tanzania. AICC management intends to establish a Convention Center that is expected to be the best in Africa. The Center will be located at Themi along the Njiro Road near Radio Tanzania Station buildings. Adequate land is available for the purpose and designs of the buildings have already been prepared. Work will start as soon as funds become available.
Local people, view Arusha as an international town as well. They judge the status of the town by the numerous signs that are posted on walls and along streets that indicate stalls which provide services to exchange foreign currencies. It implies that established banks alone cannot adequately cater for the numerous foreign visitors in town who need to exchange currencies.
On the other hand, Arusha is also very much a local town if judged by the types of local foods served at local food outlets like restaurants and hotels. For instance, there is a choice of boiled intestine ˜soup" served with lemon slices, crushed tomato, plus onion and chili salad on the side for breakfast. Boiled green bananas or pasta known as ˜chapatti" are also served with the soup. But chapatti or bananas are optional. They are for the more hungry clients. By the way, alcohol consumers in town believe that such meal for breakfast clears hangovers from a previous night's drinking spree.
Green banana soup also called "Mtor" in Swahili is served for breakfast as an alternative to intestine soup. The soup is prepared with crushed boiled green bananas mixed with boiled beef. New clients have to consume the soup for sometime in order to appreciate the taste. The soup can be taken plain or again, with chapatti as a side order.
Most of the meat-associated dishes are served in Arusha during a ten o'clock morning break. An example of such dishes is soup made from boiled cow or goat hooves. The soup is called "Makongoro" in Swahili.
Makongoro soup partly contains boiled bone marrow that is exposed when the hooves are separated from the rest of the animal leg bones before boiling. Clients suck the bone marrow that happens to remain in the broken bones that are attached to the hooves. You see clients eyes focused at a point while sucking the marrow. It is a common facial expression of a person who is monitoring the success in extracting the substance.
The soup made from boiled hooves is tasteless and the gum contained in the hooves tends to bind the clients' upper and lower lips time and again. Tendons dominate the meat portions of the soup. But that does not bother hoof soup lovers.
Cow and goat heads as well as their tongues are also boiled and served during the ten o'clock morning break. The animal tongues are skinned before boiling. On the other hand, heads are boiled without skinning. It means that the head skin soup can comprise black, striped or grey pieces of boiled head skins depending on the color of the animals' skins from which the soup has been made. Tongues are crunchier than the head skins.
Soup made from boiled cow blood also called "Kisusio" is considered to be a delicacy in this part of the country. But it is a more urbanized dish compared to a similar recipe in rural Arusha. The recipe in rural areas is made from raw blood that is mixed with fresh milk. The blood is often extracted from live animals in this case. The recipe is never served with chapatti or any other pasta.
Arusha is also viewed as the land of milestone politics in Tanzania. It is said that independence celebrations were held in Arusha nationally in 1961. The colonial flag was lowered while the Tanzania flag was raised on the day at the now Sheikh Amri Abeid Karume Stadium.
In addition, the ruling party declared the country's mission as a sovereign state in Arusha in 1967. The mission was to create a nation which is self reliant and whose people are liberated economically and mentally. The Arusha Declaration Museum in town symbolizes the spot where the party made the declaration.
In 1971, the first President of Tanzania Mwl. Julius Nyerere erected the imposing Independence Torch Monument observed at the Declaration Museum. The monument reminds residents to monitor implementation of the country's post independence mission. Incidentally, the Independence Torch will arrive in Arusha on 25th September, 2009.
Environmentalists and others who are interested in Arusha view the town from their own perspectives. They see what they want to see.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Blames Buganda Violence on Opposition
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Blames Riot On Opposition
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has blamed the opposition for the violent riots which rocked Kampala city and some Buganda towns last week.
Addressing a special sitting of Parliament yesterday, Museveni said the chaos was orchestrated by "reactionaries who had learnt nothing from Uganda's tragic history".
He said the opposition was trying to use Mengo, the seat of the Buganda Kingdom, to attain what he called selfish interests.
The riots erupted on Thursday after the Government refused to allow the Kabaka to visit Kayunga, citing security reasons. The President said: "This is the third time these elements are causing chaos and killing Ugandans."
He noted that in 2006, Dr. Kizza Besigye, who lost the presidential elections, caused chaos in which property was destroyed.
The other riots, he said, were led by Betty Anywar (FDC) over plans to give away part of Mabira Forest for a sugar project.
Museveni said the same "opportunistic elements were responsible for last week's mayhem in which he said 15 people were killed, property destroyed and peace and business disrupted.
"Those who think Uganda is a banana republic where they can change leadership unlawfully, by rioting are wasting their time."
He wondered why Besigye would promote riots and not lawful ways of handling grievances.
While preparing for CHOGM in 2007, the President said, the opposition tried to blackmail the Government. "They trampled on us then, but we are now here for ourselves. We do not have international obligations."
Museveni appealed to the Judiciary to try the riot suspects quickly and suggested they should not get bail. He sent condolences to the bereaved families, to the injured and those who lost property.
Accordingly, the House observed a moment of silence for the victims.
Museveni promised to compensate people who lost motorvehicles at Nateete Police Station, which was burnt down in the rioting.
He said Kampala security was now guaranteed.
The President said rioters who attack civilians and security officers will be shot on the spot, to disable, not to kill them, which he said was allowed under the Police Act.
Museveni explained that this was necessary for the security people to defend themselves and protect civilians.
Elaborating the current standoff with Mengo, Museveni said the issue was not just about Kayunga, but whether Uganda should have political kings.
"This concept is totally rejected by the NRM," he said. "The king is not elected and if he wields political power, how will he account if he makes mistakes? Political leaders can be voted out, what about the king?"
He said the Government would from now involve elected leaders from Buganda to quickly resolve disputes as opposed to the unelected traditional leaders.
When the riots broke out, he said, he tried to reach the Kabaka on phone in vain. "The key for all these issues is with him and we are determined to ensure that the Constitution is observed by all, kings inclusive."
The President said traditional leaders should play roles like preventing kith and kin from inter-marrying, protecting the youth from drug abuse and homosexuality.
He said it was the reason he revived kingdoms so as to retain culture which was compatible with the modern times, "but not to revive ancient undemocratic governance."
He said kings failed to protect the sovereignty of Africa.
"Colonisation was a vote of no confidence on them. Modern Africa must transcend tribal organisation and mentality."
The President also said people's legitimate interests should not be interfered with and that one's rights must not be at the expense of other people's and condemned intimidation and manipulation.
Museveni added that the Government would soon bring a law to guard against the use of kingdoms by agencies with illegitimate motives.
Accordingly, traditional leaders will be required to declare certain gifts in order to guard Uganda's sovereignty from foreign interests that fund them. "Failure to do so will attract a penalty," he said.
Museveni also gave the background on his work with the Kabaka. He said he first met Ronald Mutebi at Prof. Yusuf Lule's house in London in 1981. They agreed to restore traditional leaders to help Africans rediscover their ancestral values, "not chauvinism".
"This is the culture I was hoping for, not self-glorification and running around. Help people of Africa know each other more," he said.
The Constitution, he added, allows all people to work towards national unity while safeguarding cultural diversity. "We want value addition - what value are you? If you are not, then what are you for? Just recycling prejudices?"
He also said the Government would implement the regional tier next financial year.
He said the current councillors would elect the regional leaders, to serve for the remaining one year before the general election. "We have waited for too long on that issue, we shall wait no longer."
Museveni also explained why Buganda kingdom's CBS radio was closed. He said it meddled in politics, spread sectarianism, lawlessness and violence with impunity, yet it is owned by a traditional institution.
He said the radio had tried to intimidate Buganda's political class "not to think freely" by branding anyone with a different view as anti-Mengo or anti-Kabaka, yet they "claim that one cannot answer the Kabaka or the Kabaka does not err".
This attitude, he added, led the Kabaka to take certain "anti-people" positions like opposing the Land Bill. This situation, he said, led to the 1961 problem with catastrophic results, yet there was no radio. "With radio, it could be worse."
The President recalled that when he hosted radio station managers, he told them: "You have been weighed and found wanting."
He said equally important matters such as unemployment, land issues, corruption and poverty needed to be addressed.
Accordingly, the President said he had appointed a special adviser on employment for the youth, and that he would secure sh30b to support the cottage industry. He said he had also called for cheap electricity, the creation of industrial estates and for food processing.
Museveni warned corrupt officials that their time had run out. "While a lot of government money has been stolen by corrupt officials, I have identified the thieves and soon you will hear of the casualties."
When the MPs shouted Temangalo, Museveni said he defended security minister Amama Mbabazi because he believed he was not guilty.
Mbabazi, the NRM secretary general, had been accused of using his position to sell his land in Temangalo to NSSF at an inflated price.
Explaining the war on corruption, Museveni said he had appointed a judicial commission of inquiry into the education sector, and a drug theft inspection unit and would soon set up another one for the road sector.
He also directed the minister for the presidency to buy bicycles for LC1 chairpersons, adding that LC3s and gombolola chiefs were equipped with motorcycles for development.
The President appealed to Parliament to pass the Land Bill, which he said would end the tenant-landlord friction.
He also said some landlords had accepted compensation so that tenants get titles.
He called on other mailo land owners who want compensation to approach the Government.
Museveni received a standing ovation from the NRM MPs as he ended his address, while the opposition remained seated.
Speaker Edward Ssekandi had warned the MPs there would be no questions or comments at the end although the members would debate the address later.

Original_Search_Title: Four Burnt to Death in Yangoji Auto Crash
Headline: Nigeria: Four Burnt to Death in Yangoji Auto Crash
Body_Text:
FOUR persons were burnt to death while five others sustained serious injuries Thursday night in a fatal accident that occurred at Yangoji village, near Kwali town along the Abuja- Lokoja highway.
An eyewitness said the accident happened at about 10; 30 pm when a J5 Peugeot bus with registration number AA 43 SMK, loaded with banana rammed into a stationary truck with registration number XA 648, killing four persons on the spot.
According to the eyewitness, the truck which had broken down on the road, did not display a caution sign to indicate that it was stationary when the J5 Peugeot bus crashed into it from behind, adding that a Volkswagen Golf car with registration number AH 505 GUS also hit the bus from behind, injuring four persons on the spot.
"The J5 bus, after hitting the back of the trailer, caught fire immediately and burnt the four occupants to death.
He said the four corpses which were burnt beyond recognition were given a mass burial at the scene through the combined efforts of Yangoji muslims community, Kwali environmental officials, the police and FRSC officials.
Confirming the ancident to our reporter, the Yangoji Unit Commander of the (FRSC), Mr. Abdullahi Umar, blamed the driver of the truck for the accident.
Similarly, one person died while eight others sustained serious injuries Friday evening in a multiple accident, before officials of the Yangoji unit command of the Federal Road Safety commission came to their rescue.
The Abaji Unit Commander of the (FRSC), Mr. Joseph Ojerinde, while confirming the accident, said it involved three vehicles, which include a Toyota Hiace bus with registration number AE 276 ZAR, a Volkswagen Golf car with registration number AE 522 KKF, and a daf truck with registration number AA 823 FTA.
According to him, the accident Occurred when the truck which was coming from Gwagwalada end overtook dangerously crashed into the two vehicles coming from Lokoja end, killing one person on the spot.
He said the corpse has been conveyed to the University of Abuja Teaching Hospital morgue, while the eight injured were rushed to Abaji General hospital for treatment.

Original_Search_Title: Residents Protest Parkview Estate Contraventions
Headline: Nigeria: Residents Protest Parkview Estate Contraventions
Body_Text:
Lagos — ALL is not well in highbrow Parkview Estate in Ikoyi in Lagos, where a spate of unwholesome physical development projects seems to be turning the supposedly exclusive residential scheme on its head.
Located close to Old Ikoyi and Banana Island, Parkview, with its numerous spatial ills, is speedily turning into a slum settlement: drainage channels (if they exist at all) are either blocked or filled up and flowing nowhere; roads are potholes-ridden and unsightly; abandoned, broken-down vehicles block access to streets; and houses as well as fences are built at variance to laid-down regulations.
Bothered about the development, the residents a couple of months ago petitioned Governor Babatunde Raji Fashola and Mr. Francisco Abosede, who is the Physical Planning and Urban Development Commissioner.
Titled "Encroachment and massive construction of permanent structures on public road setback in Parkview Estate," the correspondent sought the authority's intervention in the matter. It noted that, if not nipped in the bud, the developments could have serious consequences on the habitat of the locality.
It reads in part, "Presently, there are so many developments going on within the estate with total disregard to stipulated town planning regulations chief among which are: construction of permanent structures on road setback, blockage of drainage channels, density violation and irrational environmental abuse."
One of the residents, who pleaded anonymity, said that there was actually a response from government but that the initial enthusiasm showed by the officials had thawed.
He said, "To be fair to (Governor) Fashola, he reacts when he gets complaints, but those that are detailed to carry out his directives are slow to act and found wanting. There is a traditional ruler with a palace within this estate, but he seems either unaware or unconcerned about these happenings.
"In most cases, these government officials come and mark the contravening structures with the promise to take further action. But this is usually not done and the contraventions eventually continue.
"For instance, barely seven months after the stoppage of illegal developments sometime ago, construction work soon started again at the site with reckless abandon directly on road setback."
Nevertheless, the residents want the governor and commissioner to delegate the Task Force on Development Control "to stop these illegal developments before it is too late for remedial action."
Ikoyi is an island suburb that encompasses the eastern half of the Lagos Island and lies at the edge of the Lagos Lagoon. It is one of the communities within the Eti-Osa Local Government Area.
The area that makes up Ikoyi was originally part of Lagos Island until it was separated (from the island) by a narrow waterway that was dug by the British colonial government. This canal has now been built over or filled so that the two islands are fused together again. It is shielded from the Atlantic Ocean by Victoria Island and the broad sand spit running east down the coast.
Apart from Parkview Estate, Ikoyi includes the newer suburbs of Banana Island and Dolphin Estate.
Ikoyi has some of the most opulent residential facilities in Nigeria and is thought to have the most expensive real estate on the entire African continent, with the average new apartment selling for N120 million (around US$1 million). However, due to the limited available land, many of these are vertical apartment buildings.
The rent for a luxury three-bedroom apartment in Ikoyi is between $45,000 and $80,000 (N5.2 million and N9.3 million) annually. Five years ago, it was between $10,000 and $30,000 (N1.16 million and N3.5 million) per annum. An acre (six standard plots) in Banana Island, Parkview Estate and Osborne Road in Ikoyi now sells for between N400 million and N450 million. Five years ago, it sold for between N50 million and N150 million.
This development is a radical departure from Ikoyi's original design, which was originally composed of modest single-family residences with large gardens. Considering the lack of constant electricity, pipe-borne water, and general decay in basic infrastructure that is typical of Lagos, concerns have been raised as to whether Ikoyi has the necessary road and water infrastructure to continue to sustain this type of development.

Original_Search_Title: Kibaki Move On Ringera an Act of Impunity
Headline: Kenya: Kibaki Move On Ringera an Act of Impunity
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The illegal re-appointment of Justice Aaron Ringera by President Mwai Kibaki as the Director of the Kenya Anti-Corruption Commission was a brazen act of impunity and disdain for democracy. It was not contrary to the duelling opinions of lawyers about mere legality.
To debate the matter as though it was just a breach of the black letter of the law is to conflate process and substance. Legal formalism or the strict adherence of official conduct to law and procedure - is not an end in itself. If that were the case, then most official acts of the apartheid state in South Africa would have been fine.
Democracy is about the rule of law, but it is about more than that. Much more. Let me explain. In 1988, the Kanu state abolished the secret ballot and introduced queue voting, a procedure that forced voters to line up publicly behind their candidates. While this may have been legal, it made a mockery of free will of the electors.
It vitiated democracy. In the same year, President Daniel arap Moi amended the constitution extending the period during which a suspect could be held without judicial scrutiny from 24 hours to 14. He also abolished the constitutional guarantee of tenure for judges, completely eliminating a key vestige of an independent judiciary.
While these actions were formally legal, they were against the democratic spirit. My point is that you can hide behind legality to subvert the substance of the rule of law and emasculate democracy. Law can be an enemy of democracy and a friend of impunity.
Quiet often, cynical and hypocritical leaders point to their adherence to the law to protect kleptocracy and vested interests. Nowhere is this clearer than in one-party states, theocratic societies, or the so-called banana republics.
Take Egypt, for example, where President Hosni Mubarak is re-elected perennially in a "legal" and "democratic" election. The point really is not whether President Kibaki followed a particular section of the Anti-Corruption and Economic Crimes Act. Yes, the law is important. But that is beside the point.
The real question is the spirit of law, and what President Kibaki intended to do as the person sworn to uphold the Constitution and the laws of the Republic of Kenya. Even if the President had followed the letter of the law - which he definitely did not - we still have to look beyond the black letter of the law and see whether he acted in the spirit of the law.
Every law is a living instruction, and must be interpreted according to its spirit. Laws do not orbit in space, without an anchor, unconnected to reality or the purpose for which they were written. The President can follow the law and still violate its spirit.
In modern states, laws must buttress democracy. The "spirit" of every law must have a "democratic conscience." This means that those who apply the law - such as President Kibaki - must be guided by this democratic conscience. I submit that even if President Kibaki had strictly followed the law, he would still have violated its democratic spirit and conscience by re-appointing Justice Ringera.
The reason for this is simple. Justice Ringera is simply unfit to serve as Director of the KACC. But President Kibaki acted with the arrogance of the imperial presidency to protect a crony who has been an obstruction to the fight against corruption and impunity. Such hubris is a reserve of the absolute monarchs of yesteryear, not the democratic leaders of today's modern states.
Why is Justice Ringera unfit to serve? In his first term as KACC Director, Justice Ringera failed to do anything noteworthy. Apart from drawing a fat pay cheque, he never saw a corruption case worth pursuing. There is a long list of high level frauds that he could have investigated, the most prominent being Anglo Leasing.
But he either looked the other way, or showed no interest. In the case of Anglo Leasing, he directly told me and Mr John Githongo, the former anti-corruption czar, in London that we had better drop the matter or risk our lives.
I find it curious that Justice Ringera was re-appointed the moment Mr Francis Muthaura, the Head of the Civil Service, returned to office after a long convalescence. Is this a coincidence? I also find it curious that Minister Kiraitu Murungi, the senior politician from Meru, is acting as a godfather for both Mr Muthaura and Justice Ringera.
Mr Muthaura has been an obstructionist in the smooth running of the coalition government while Justice Ringera cannot point to a single major accomplishment as Director of the KACC. I hate to think that the Mr Murungi I knew so well has become an irredeemable ethnic chauvinist.
President Kibaki ought to move heaven and earth to banish impunity, fight corruption, and lead a national renaissance. Sadly, the President shows no interest in these pivotal struggles for Kenya's survival. In fact, his re-appointment of Justice Ringera was a demonstration of his contempt for democracy.
I have come to believe that President Kibaki is himself the embodiment of impunity. The KACC is a central tool for fighting corruption and impunity in Kenya. Justice Ringera is the wrong man at the helm.
Makau Mutua is Dean and Suny Distinguished Professor at the State University of New York at Buffalo Law School and Chair of the Kenya Human Rights Commission

Original_Search_Title: Hana Upbeat On Ghanzi Copper Deposits
Headline: Botswana: Hana Upbeat On Ghanzi Copper Deposits
Body_Text:
Canadian junior explorer Hana Mining said the Banana zone resource is proving to be the brightest spot among its Ghanzi copper-silver deposits because it has a potential to be a stand-alone operation.
During Mmegi's exclusive tour of the project, which adjacent to Kuke cordon fence and the Central Kalahari Game Reserve (CKGR) last weekend, the company's geologist said he was upbeat about demonstrating the economics of the project before year-end.Hana's leading geologist Jacques Janse van Renseburg said the Banana zone, which is the size of London, currently has a resource of 40.7 million tonnes (mt) at 1.47 percent copper and 21.6 ounce of silver.
"We have only drilled 14km of the 60km of Banana. If we update the resource, then the economics of the project will look better".
The company, which is headquartered in Vancouver, Canada, and listed on the TSX Venture Exchange and the Frankfurt Exchange, is putting all its efforts in Banana as it targets 80 to 100 MT of resource in 2011.
The latest drilling results have shown that the whole Ghanzi Cooper-Silver project consisting of five licensed blocks covering 4,370 square km has an inferred resource of 2.9 billion pounds of copper and 51.5 million ounces of silver. Janse van Resenburg and his team recently completed an independent National Instrument ("NI 43-101) compliant inferred resource estimate for the Ghanzi deposit, which looked at close to 163.5 million tonnes of resource.
This resource estimate is based largely on a 15-month drilling programme that included 98 Core holes totalling 15,000 metres and 50 RC holes totalling 6,600 metres.
The company said drill holes were typically designed to test at 150 metres vertical depth, reflecting the initial objective to initially define a surface mineable resource.Janse van Renseburg who has over 20 years experience on the field, having discovered major resources in South Africa, Angola, Mozambique and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) was excited about an innovative drilling method he introduced on site, which he said saves them time and money.
The Cyclone method which the company uses to dig Percussion holes helps it know the exact location to use RC drilling to test the vertical depth of the resource. Janse van Resenburg and his team have already exceeded the goal of their first resource estimate, whilst still having multiple target zones to drill on the property.He, however, said the global recession resulted in the exploration activities suspended for six months until June this year.
The mineralisation at the Ghanzi Copper-Silver property is classified as a sediment-hosted copper-silver deposit. Among the best known deposits of similar type is the vast Kuperschiefer deposit in Poland. Mineralisation at Kuperschiefer occurs to a vertical depth of at least 700 meters and is open.The company said this type of copper deposit can generate a resource that is both very large and of attractive grade, making for world scale mining opportunities.Hana plans to concentrate resource drilling on Banana and Zone 3 in the Phase One period (2009/10). During this period, the company will also address water access, environmental and social obligations.
In 2011, the company aims to have completed drilling at the two zones and be able to advance the resource to measured and indicated status."We hope by then we can confirm project economics with a pre-feasibility study based on the measured and indicated status," van Resenburg said.
During, Phase Three of the project which cover 2012-2013, Hana plans to have secured a mining licence, water rights, generated a bankable feasibility study, completed a non-recourse financing because it aims to start mining construction by early 2014.Janse van Resenburg said full production of the mine is targeted for the third quarter of 2015. The company plans to have open pit mining with a life span of 25 years.
Head of Hana Mining in Botswana office Kgosimang Molosiwa, who accompanied Mmegi during the site visit, said negotiations are ongoing with government to link infrastructure to the project. The company's deposit, which is 30km south of Discovery Mining's Boseto copper project, can either get power from the Maun grid or Ghanzi. It also has options to use the Trans-Kalahari Highway, which passes within 10 km of the north-end of the property.

Original_Search_Title: The Cutting Edge
Headline: Kenya: The Cutting Edge
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Recalling the terribly divisive Orange and Banana campaigns for the 2005 referendum, Maina Gathu wishes the Committee of Experts could spare fellow Kenyans the agony of having to go through it again, even though it's a legal requirement. He adds: "Another referendum might be the recipe for greater chaos in 2012, as happened in the lead-up to the 2007 poll. Can't an alternative method for endorsing the new Constitution be found?"
***
It's over four months since someone dug and left open a trench across the road near Roysambu roundabout towards Zimmerman, Nairobi, moans Wilson Otieno. "It's now so deep that it poses a grave danger to motorists and other road users. Could those responsible be asked to return and repair it and the matter investigated to establish if there was abuse of office in its authorisation?"
***
The Postal Corporation of Kenya needs to style up to remain relevant in the new competitive world, says Sammy Ng'ang'a. Three months ago, he paid for a private mailbox at Ruaraka Post Office. He was then allocated a box he can't access because they have not received the keys from headquarters. "Well, they could use our local locksmiths who cut keys while you wait."
***
What is so embarrassing about one's house-help being asked by a census enumerator about her level of education? asks Abisai Mundia. "In my view, the complaint aired in this column has no merit and just proves that we are a nation of people who like to complain about everything. The worker had a right to be counted and the truth about her recorded. And for his information, there are many domestic workers who have high school qualifications."
***
Dog lovers won't like this one, but Basilio Chabari says he won't be fooled "any more that this animal is man's best friend". Without revealing why he's personally so bitter, he adds: "Who came up with this silly idea that a dog is man's best friend? With the incidents of people being mauled, how would one dare call a dog man's best friend? This is a big joke! The only animal that qualifies for that position is a cow and the reasons are obvious!"
***
Roads in most of the country have been left to deteriorate so much that they have to be built almost afresh. Nairobi resident Martha M. was happy to be invited by her friend to attend her family's get-together at her rural home in Murang'a. The drive was smooth until they approached Maragwa Town, hitting a stretch "that is in a pathetic state with large potholes on both sides". "Is the Ministry of Roads aware of this and when are they planning to fix it?"
***
A lot of the money being used to expand roads would be saved if we did away with roundabouts, says X. N. Iraki. He adds: "Nothing wastes more time, and energy and annoys drivers like roundabouts. There is no need of expanding roads if roundabouts will still choke traffic. I don't understand why policemen man them and never respect the lights. Or is it one of those peculiar Kenyan habits?"
