Mrs. Okunsanya told journalists that the women decided to protest in the nude to reinforce their demand for democracy to be given a chance in Ekiti; this she added will be the beginning of the real re-branding process in the country. What the women want, she explained is for the true winner of the re-run election to be declared the winner. While the nation waits for the INEC to find a solution out of this debacle, controversy has continued to trail the elections in Ekiti State.
Dayo Adeyeye, who is the chairman of the Segun Oni campaign organization called on the INEC to release the result without further delay, pending the re-run in Oye local government, arguing it is an incontrovertible fact that candidates in most elections score high in their home base.
Adeyeye asked the INEC to conduct the election in Oye council area with maximum security to forestall a repeat of last Saturday's experience which saw the opposition importing thugs from all parts of the country to scare away PDP voters.
The Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) has also reacted to the impasse in Ekiti. The apex labour body in a communiqué implored the INEC to release the results of the gubernatorial re-run election as demonstrated by the electorate in Ekiti State at the polls on Saturday, April 25 without further delay and declare the true winner to forestall further crisis.
On Friday, President Umaru Yar'adua rose in defence of his party and his administration, reiterating that he is committed to resolving the election debacle in Ekiti State as quickly as possible. In a statement issued by his special adviser on media and publicity, Mr. Segun Adeniyi, the president admitted that the Ekiti debacle has over-heated the Nigerian polity and is therefore, a poignant reminder of the urgency, and imperative of reforming the country's electoral process.
Yar'adua explained that like every Nigerian, he had wanted and still wants, free and fair elections in Ekiti State, adding that the crisis thrown up by the April 25 election is not only an avoidable distraction but also a forceful reminder of the imperative of systemic electoral reforms, to which his administration is committed.
But the PDP is accusing the resident electoral commissioner, Mrs. Ayoka Adebayo of being responsible for the disquiet in Ekiti because of her actions that have exacerbated an otherwise routine announcement of election results. National publicity secretary of the PDP, Prof. Rufa'i Ahmed Alkali told Sunday Trust that Mrs. Adebayo's disappearance caused not only the PDP a lot of concern but the entire country. He said based on her untrustworthy attitude, the party asked the INEC to redeploy her to another position and appoint a new officer to conclude the Ekiti assignment.
"You know her disappearance caused a lot of trauma to the people of Ekiti State and the country as a whole. Because elections really are very sensitive, particularly when you are dealing with situations where it's volatile like what happened in Ekiti State. Our initial reaction was to keep quiet and assume that all was going well. But suddenly, she reappeared and said, all was well and she was going to continue with her work. That is not the best way to run a serious business like election.
"Given what she has done, you can see instability in her attitude, character and behaviour, and nobody will trust her with the remaining part of the assignment. That was why we called on the INEC to immediately redeploy her and send some body that is focused, organized, and who can be trusted to complete the assignment.
Prof. Alkali argued that instead of disappearing for 48 hours, the INEC commissioner should've sought the protection of the police instead of playing a hide and seek game with Nigerians. "You can't disappear for over 48 hours and only come out, and say this, and that. Resigning in this particular position is a very serious business. That's why we are very happy that the Federal Government, immediately she disappeared and issued that her so-called resignation letter, rejected it and ordered her to come out.
"The REC did not follow the normal procedure of addressing a problem, if there was a problem at all. Now you can see why the PDP is calling on INEC to go ahead and finish the remaining business of the election in Oye-Ekiti, but the AC is saying no, the result should be announced. And from our records, there are about 18,000 registered voters in Oye-Ekiti. Now how can you just throw away those 18,000 votes, for no reason?
"What AC is banking on is that, already in the Appeal Court, they nullified quite a number of votes where the PDP was in control. And now they have some votes on their favour even before the rerun elections. Now, they think that by manipulating the system, they will just have enough votes to declare their candidate as governor of Ekiti State. It doesn't work that way; because they can't take over the work of INEC. We are not in a banana republic," Prof. Alkali added.
But, national secretary of the AC, DR. Usman Bugaje disagrees with the PDP's position, disclosing that polls didn't hold in Oye because the PDP prevented election from taking place and the INEC after the stipulated time for voting had elapsed, stopped the conduct of the election, adding that "in Oye, election couldn't take place because the PDP apparently using the police, they started shooting and harassing people.
"And by the time they harassed and sent people away, they still found out that they could not do what they wanted. This is why INEC had to cancel the election in Oye local government. This is the actual beginning of the whole problem".
Similarly, Dr. Bugaje alleged that because of violence in Ido-Ofi local government, and in Ifako council area where the PDP's candidate hails from results were not collated at the designated centres by INEC, disclosing that "almost 60 per cent of the results were collated in police stations. Of course, we know the police are PDP police and therefore they inflated the numbers.
"At the end of the day, when results were collated and figures were coming we were leading, but when Odo-Osun came we said no we were not going to accept the figure and that is when the problem started. The PDP put pressure on the Resident Electoral Commissioner to accept the figures but she said no because it did not meet the standard. And she said Oye election is not postponed but cancelled because that was the regulation of the INEC".
The AC candidate, he said, won the re-run election having won the highest number of votes, saying "what the result is showing now is that the AC candidate has got the majority votes because beside this re-run he was already leading with more than 11,000 votes. And if INEC has done exactly the kind of thing they ought to do he should be declared the winner of the election. If you cancelled all these fabricated votes the total is still there".
Meanwhile, the acting Governor, Mr. Tujni Odyemi has advised the people of the state to wait for the next line of action to be taken by the INEC, adding that efforts were being made to resolve the issue amicably.
Odyemi said the people should remain calm and be law-abiding pending the conclusion of the on-going re-run election, to give peace a chance.
Though the state is peaceful for now, but the general feeling amongst the people of the state is that the state is sitting on a time bomb. To forestall a total breakdown of law and order, security has been beefed up around the state capital, with soldiers stationed at strategic locations to complement the police.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Acquires Major Stake in Schweppes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Acquires Major Stake in Schweppes
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE Stock Exchange-listed Delta Beverages has acquired a controlling stake in Schweppes Zimbabwe, businessdigest has established.
The deal was sealed two weeks ago for an as yet unascertained value.
Analysts said the deal could give Delta further comfort in the beverage market after acquiring its former competitor in soft drink manufacturing industry. Delta already controls over 80% of the soft drinks market in the country.
The acquisition of Schweppes comes at a time when Delta is reportedly concluding the sale of its 40% stake in Ariston Holding Ltd, shares the group acquired three years ago in order to tap into the horticultural concern's rich foreign currency reserves in the wake of acute foreign exchange shortages in the country.
Analysts said Delta's move on Schweppes is a reflection that the company is refocusing its business around traditional core beverages business. Schweppes will also bring the famous Mazoe Orange drink to the Delta stable, among other products.
Delta a few years ago unbundled a number of companies to unlock value for shareholders and concentrate on its beverages and beer business but an economic meltdown characterised by foreign currency shortages forced management to make a takeover bid in Ariston.
Efforts to reach Delta chief executive officer Joe Mutizwa proved fruitless, but well placed sources said the deal was concluded a fortnight ago.
"Delta acquired Schweppes two week ago. I am sure they are waiting for the right time to announce, but the deal was sealed," one of the sources said.
Zimbabwean-born South Africa businessman Mutumwa Mawere bought 100% of Schweppes Zimbabwe capital from the Coca-Cola group for US$5 million in 2004, but allegations of externalisation of foreign currency emerged against him triggering a wholesale nationalisation of his businesses.
According to the sources, Delta clinched the deal ahead of other contenders who had been eyeing the company.
Meanwhile, Ariston chief Kumbirai Katsande is reportedly heading a management consortium to buy Delta's US$3,2 million stake in the horticultural company.
Sources close to the deal said Katsande and some of the group's senior executives were on the verge of clinching a 40% Delta holds in Ariston.Delta made an offer to acquire 60% of Ariston's total issued share in 2006 but managed to snap 40%. Delta, sources said, resolved to dispose of its equity in Ariston to concentrate on its beer and carbonated drinks manufacturing.
Katsande this week could neither confirm nor deny the management move to acquire the Delta stake.
Ariston is a horticultural firm and is organised in five operating divisions: Southdown Estates, which is engaged in growing and manufacture of tea, coffee, macadamia nuts and bananas; Claremont Estates -- growing of pome and stone fruit, breeder protected field flowers and trout; Kent Estate -- growing of roses, poultry and livestock; Favco -- distribution of fresh fruits and vegetables, and the Capital Tea Company -- packaging and distribution of tea and coffee.

Original_Search_Title: Ekiti - Don Urges Yar'Adua to Intervene
Headline: Nigeria: Ekiti - Don Urges Yar'Adua to Intervene
Body_Text:
A Scholar, Professor Ladipo Adamolekun has asked President Umaru Yar' Adua to urgently intervene in the ugly drama happening in Ekiti State over the results of the re-run election so as not to make the country a laughing stock of the world.
He described the Ekiti Resident Electoral Commissioner, Chief Mrs Ayoka Adebayo as courageous and honest Nigerian who stood her ground not to go against her conscience.
Speaking exclusively with Vanguard, Prof Adamolekun said the development occasioned by the refusal of INEC to release the result was despicable and capable of portraying the country the "sick democracy of Africa."
According to him, "it is a sham that election in about 1/3 of the wards in the third smallest of the 36 states in this country is in a mess.
"This is a defining moment for President Yar'Adua. It is difficult to reconcile the electoral reform with the maintenance of the do- or-die politics which is anti-thetical to a commitment to the rule of law.
The only option open to him in Ekiti is to announce the results without delay.
Adamolekun, formerly of the Obafemi Awolowo University OAU said "Otherwise any further talk of the electoral reform or rule of law would not be taken seriously by Nigerians or the international community.
"I would like to believe that the President would not like Nigeria to become the sick democracy of Africa and the laughing stock of the world".
"What is happening in Ekiti is like turning Nigeria into a citizen of second or third class country if not a banana Republic. This is shaming all Nigerians. How would Nigerians anywhere explain what is happening in Ekiti compared to what happened in Ghana in December 2008 and in South Africa in April 2009.
"It shows clearly that Nigeria is a decade or more behind those countries. Must we tolerate this nonsense? Talk of electoral reform is a sham going by happenings in Ekiti.
"Ekiti people have indicated that they would not tolerate the leader they did not elect. Would soldiers be sent to Ekiti as the Speaker of House of Representatives Dimeji Bankole said to install a leader they did not elect?
"Unless Ekiti is to be ruled by a governor protected by several battalions of soldiers it does not appear the Ekiti people will accept somebody they did not vote for.
"Ekiti will be sentenced to electoral legitimacy deficit with all its negative consequences. If a compliant REC is sent there to do what the courageous and honest Ayoka Adebayo refused to do. It is remarkable that INEC Chairman Maurice Iwu proclaim the hale and hearty Adebayo sick less than 24 hours to her resignation.
"It is unfortunate that Iwu has no shame to resign for lying to Nigerians. What is happening in Ekiti would be sentencing the state to two or more years of underdevelopment and this is not what the Ekiti people want. They have made it loud and clear who they want youths are on the streets.
"The historical account of 1965, 1983 and 1993 is responsible to a large part for the poor performance development of the country. With the evolving electoral rascality in Ekiti, the country appears headed for more years of poor performance development.
"The Ekiti people have every right to reject an imposed leader and one hope that the manifestation of their rejection will be peaceful but continuous until they succeed in obtaining justice through the confirmation of the candidate of their choice as their governor.
"All Nigerians who believe in the right of citizen to determine who rules them must support the Ekiti people in everyway possible.

Original_Search_Title: I Can Prepare My Own Meals
Headline: Uganda: I Can Prepare My Own Meals
Body_Text:
Kampala — Engineer Badru Kiggundu, a former lecturer at Makerere University, is the Chairman of the Electoral Commission. He told Susan Muyiyi about his work, family and why he returned to Uganda, leaving a lucrative engineering career in the US
Did becoming a Haji change your life?
Yes. I became firm in my creed and it strengthened my love for people of different beliefs. After all, we were all created by the same creator. It was a fabulous experience.
Going through the cleansing process felt good. It was also interesting to meet people from different countries, all gathered for the same cause. Social ranking didn't matter; we were all dressed simply and seated down.
What recollections do you have of the colonial times?
There were a number of governors whose names I don't recall. One of them lived at State House in Entebbe. I was so young to directly interact with them. However, it was easy to tell when a governor was making his way through town. People saluted them.
They were the major cause of the wars that troubled Uganda. I was in S2 when the union jack was lowered at Kololo. I attended the parade as a student and it was quite interesting.
How have you managed to keep your two wives happy?
I love and provide for my two wives equally.
When was your turning point?
One of them was in 1981, when I attained my PhD. It was a very big step for me professionally. The other was my appointment to this job in 2002. I was vetted by the parliamentary appointments committee and I gladly embraced the job as an opportunity to serve my country.
I have no regrets whatsoever. I took up the challenge like taking on the proverbial bull by its horns. Well, there are complaints, but that is a part of life regardless of what one is involved in.
What made you leave a lucrative engineering career in America?
I love my country so much. There is no amount of money that could have made me stay. I wanted to contribute to the education of fellow Ugandans. I hadn't lived with my parents for a while and I was very eager to come back and be a part of their lives.
They are now deceased. I also wanted to groom some family members from my dad's side. Some people I told thought I wasn't serious.
They wondered why anyone would desire to come back to a dusty country. My response was: "Over 200 years ago America, must have had a lot of dust and if it hadn't been for the few Americans who stayed to make it what it is today, few people would want to stay there.
The change we want to see might not be achieved in only a day, but what I have achieved so far is fulfilling."
9 things about Kiggundu
ACHIEVEMENTS
I was appointed senior lecturer of engineering at the New Mexico engineering research institute at the University of New Mexico. One of my assignments was to unravel new approaches to the re-utilisation of aged pavement materials without causing an environmental menace.
I succeeded in this and the methods I researched on are still being used. During my time at Auburn University, together with a research team, we got a grant of $3m.
For the 12 years I worked at Makerere University, I contributed to the education of more than 400 engineers. I worked hard to develop a culture of post-graduate studies among the teaching staff.
PATIENCE
I have an exceptional degree of patience and respect for every human being, regardless of their social and religious standing.
HARD WORK
I believe so much in getting work done. I never surrender to a challenge. I work until I find a solution. I don't believe in working only eight hours a day when there is need to accomplish more.
FAMILY
I love my family, my two wives and children equally. I might not be the pampering type of parent, but I know that they were divinely given to me.
CHALLENGES
I like challenges. I am not content with the status quo. I find a simplistic approach towards life unacceptable. I believe that there is no gain without pain. Why would anyone expect output without investing? The mentality of always wanting to be on the receiving side is not right.
A MAN OF ALL SEASONS
I am nonconventional. If you gave me a knife to peel matooke, I would gladly do it. If my wife and helpers are away, I prepare my meals. I can actually cook steamed matooke in banana leaves.
I can slaughter a goat, even a cow. When my senior wife and I lived in the US, I escorted her to the labour ward and witnessed the agony a woman goes through to have children.
CHILDHOOD
I had a very humble childhood in the village. I attended rural schools and didn't wear a pair of shoes until I was in P7. I slept on a mat and used bark cloth as a blanket. I walked 10 miles every day to and from school.
LEISURE
I was an athlete. I love socialising with family and friends. I travel a lot by virtue of work. I never go to bed without reading something. I actually reserve the day's newspapers for that time. I help my children with their home work. I also love taking them to the parks.
MOTTO
I believe in always being the best I can be so that when I leave a place, it is definite that I left a mark. I don't run away from a problem. If I had a nickname, it would be Mr. Never Give up.
Life is not a straight path. I look at it as a circle. One day you are on the mountain top and another day, you hit rock-bottom. But while at the lowest, make sure that you maintain a positive attitude.

Original_Search_Title: 'APA State' On Banana Peel
Headline: Nigeria: 'APA State' On Banana Peel
Body_Text:
Since the renewed agitation for the creation of Apa State started a few months back, the leaders of that dream have been enmeshed in a series of controversy.Signed by the national president, Onche Onaji and Vice President, nNational affairs, Alex Ayegba NAISparticularly frowned at series of failed attempts to mobilise commercial motorcycle riders and several strata of youths in the area to stage recall campaigns in Otukpo against Hoourable Nelson Alapa (Otukpo/Ohimini ANPP) over his alleged opposition to Apa State creation.
The Otukpo based Joy FM radio owned by Senate President, David Mark, carried in its broadcast of February 25, news item that the People's Democratic Party (PDP) aspirant in the 2007 Benue-South senatorial election, Dr. Lawrence Onoja and Rep Alapa are opposed to Apa State creation. Since that broadcast, the chairman of Otukpo local government area, Dr. Innocent Onuh, has held several meetings with the people seeking his recall, just as text messages seeking the same punitive measure have been sent out to people including journalists.
While there has not been any known offence which Dr. Onoja is said to have committed, Alapa's sin is that he is one of the six House of Representatives members who dragged the leadership of the National Assembly to court over alleged unconstitutional methods being adopted in the process to review the constitution. His membership of that group of lawmakers seeking the court to define the role of the leadership of NASS in the constitution review process and by extension, return the process on track if found to have gone haywire, is being viewed among a certain group of leaders of Idomaland as an affront on the Senate President whose campaign for a third term in the Senate was to give the people their long-sought Apa State.
After three failed attempts, the agitation for the creation of Apa State out of Benue State to address perceived structural imbalance resurfaced last year when a group of Idoma leaders loyal to the Senate President David Mark and one Igedeman - Chief Moses Ajema, met in Makurdi and spoke in vision for the realization of the dream of almost two decades. Wholly called Idoma, the people that the area covering the proposed new state has all the requirements to carve it out from Benue State. They named some resources including petroleum and gas, coal and other natural endowments as well as population as some of the requirements for a viable Apa State. They said the area to be called Apa is far larger than six nations of the world and seven states of the federation in population and land mass.
The agitation confirms Senate President David Mark's assurance to his people during the campaigns in 2007 that his return to the senate will facilitate the creation of the long sought Apa state, although his promise was punctured by his main opponent - ANPP's Alhaji Usman Abubakar (Young Alhaji), who argued that the creation cannot be the making of one individual alone but the people of Idomaland.
Operating under the auspices of the Movement for the Creation of Apa State (MOCAS), the people who first paid a courtesy visit on Governor Gabriel Suswam to register their activities told a press conference later that the agitation has the support of all the people the area unlike in the past when they were divided along ethnic and political lines.
MOCAS Director General and Secretary General, Chief Enyi Obande and Benson Abounu respectively admitted that the first three failed attempts could not achieve the support of everyone, but disclosed that the present effort has been well planned after wide consultations with all stakeholders. They said they are working with other sections of the country in the quest as they also called on other Nigerians, irrespective of ethnicity, religion or political affiliation to support them.
The struggle for the creation of Apa State became a recurrent decimal in the history of the country few years after the creation of Benue State from the old Benue-Plateau. The struggle took centre stage during the military regime of General Ibrahim Babangida but failed when the panel on state creation threw out the demand. The causes almost succeeded during the regime of late General Sani Abacha when three new states were to be created in the North, namely Gombe, Apa and Nasarawa, if not for the population of the people which way put at 835,000, a far cry from the 1.2 million set as yardstick.
Therefore, every Idoma leader is conscious and is not waiting to see how far the agitation will go. While those loyal to David Mark rose with allegations on the radio about how Onoja and Alapa and later, General Chris Garuba (rtd), are planning to scuttle the efforts, the three who have been put on the defence rose with denials and counter accusations, alleging that Mark and some loyal politicians want to give them a bad name so that the Senate President will claim credit for Apa's creation which has long been the embryo of the two other retired generals.
The platform for name calling was formally raised with the radio broadcast which was followed by series of meetings in Otukpo by those who say they will not rest until Alapa, Onoja and Garuba are taught a bitter lesson for their perceived roles to stop Idomas from achieving Apa's creation.
The heat has been more on Alapa since he alone holds political office among the three. Like the neighbouring Tivs, the Idomas are a people who will not rest until one of their own in position leaves office. Therefore, Alapa has come under intense fire by a section of his Otukpo/Ohimini constituency who are itching for his recall. Only a week ago, the chairman of Otukpo, Dr. Innocent Onuh, among other top politicians of the area, organised youths, women and even the aged to stage a peaceful protest against Alapa whom they also accused of non-performance and betrayal as well as alleged indolence and working against the interest of his community instantly demanding his recall from the National Assembly.
Although they were instantly stopped and dispersed from the procession by armed policemen who were allegedly acting on instructions from the command headquarters in Makurdi, the protesters' placards explained their aborted mission and feelings.
But counter arguments arose with the condemnation of this action which they called "sponsored". One of these voices is the National Association of Idoma Students (NAIS) whose leadership said "we wish to warn all mischief making politicians to desist from causing strife in the land because if they do not have a development agenda, they should bury their heads in shame rather than discourage leaders like Alapa who have won the minds of the people with their populist ideas." NAIS said in a statement.

Original_Search_Title: Almost Two Years After, Steady Hands Run the Senate
Headline: Nigeria: Almost Two Years After, Steady Hands Run the Senate
Body_Text:
Lagos — Sufuyan Ojeifo writes on efforts by the Senate leadership under Sena-tor David Mark at defining a character for, and galvanizing the spirit of the Upper House to discharge its constitutional functions
Since the inauguration of the Senate on June 5, 2007, the Senate President, Senator David Mark, has made conscious efforts at providing a sharply-focused and purposeful leadership for the Upper House. From the outset of his Senate Presidency, he has resolved to surpass his predecessors' records in all the diversities and he is steadily achieving his goal.
Significantly, Mark's leadership has been able to stabilise the sixth Senate. Unlike the fourth and the fifth sessions of the Senate, which witnessed deep-seated acrimonies and a surfeit of leadership instability, Mark has deployed his legislative experience and harnessed, in positive manner, the potential of his colleagues to steady the Upper House.
The fourth session of the Senate produced three Senate Presidents - the late Senator Evans Enwerem (June 3 to November 18, 1999), the late Senator Chuba Okadigbo (November 18, 1999 to August 8, 2000) and Senator Anyim Pius Anyim (August 8, 2000 to June 3, 2003). The Senate was bogged down by intrigues and squabbles that consumed its leaders.
Although, not much of squabbles characterised the fifth session, Senator Adolphus Wabara, who was in the saddle from June 3, 2003 to April 5, 2005, lost his position to the N55 million bribe-for-budget scandal. Consequent upon his resignation from office, the Senate had elected Senator Ken Nnamani. The only tension Nnamani's leadership contended with was the ill-fated Third Term agenda.
Mark was a witness, if not a participant, in the leadership intrigues that plagued the fourth session and the politics of Third term (tenure elongation) agenda that characterized the fifth session under Nnamani. He (Mark) has sufficiently benefited from these incidents and garnered experiences from them. In fact, he knows the flashpoints in the entire configuration of the Senate Presidency.
He has the advantage of legislative experience, which Enwerem lacked and for which he paid dearly; he is also now politically wise to steer clear of the funds of the Senate, which was the banana peel that Okadigbo stepped on and slipped out of office. Okadigbo was also accused of being arrogant. But Mark, with his humility (whether real or pretentious), he has been able to disarm his colleagues.
To him, all 108 senators are bosses while he is the servant. He declares this on every occasion and the senators are enamoured by him. But this is not a mark of idleness. He has demonstrated his commitment to the wellbeing of his colleagues in sundry manners both within and outside the Senate. He has left the fifty-four standing committees intact despite pressure to reorganise, even if minimally.
He does not want to take precipitate actions that would hurt the blossoming relationship he enjoys with his colleagues. He is taking his time and, in the process, committee chairmen that have performed below expectation are availed with more time to reinvent themselves. A majority of the chairmen have been able to demonstrate their capabilities.
Although, initially, some senators who voted against him on June 5, 2007, complained quietly that he was not calling on them to contribute to debate during plenary, not any more, as all that has changed. About twenty three months in the saddle, Mark has been able to call on virtually every senator to make contributions to debates. Sometimes, he calls those who do not even indicate their intention to speak. That is what time has done.
And, unlike Enwerem who fumbled and muddled through while presiding at plenary, Mark, like Okadigbo, Anyim, Wabara and Nnamani who had some measures of experience before becoming Senate Presidents, has mastered parliamentary rules and procedures. He has read and understood the Senate Standing Rules (2007 as amended) from cover to cover.
He also taps from the experiences of his colleagues, particularly members of the Senate leadership, with whom he has worked these past twenty-two months to define, galvanise and stabilise the Senate. Consequently, the Senate has been able to consider a number of motions and bills as well as embark on sundry investigations into the activities of the Ministries, Departments and Agencies (MDAs) in the line of oversight duties.
But while Mark, as the torch-bearer, has creditably demonstrated purposeful leadership, the question in Senate circles is, how well have other principal officers bolstered his leadership? The question has elicited varied responses gleaned from different circles in the Senate.
- Ike Ekweremadu: He is the Deputy Senate President. Having been in the Senate since 2003, he has garnered legislative experience, which he is deploying to assist Mark to take decisions during debates in plenary. Ekweremadu, who studied Law up to Master's level, is articulate and brilliant, even though he lacks the leadership charisma that Mark has in excess. Mark has always called on him for interventions on constitutional issues. Historically, while he lacks the oratorical prowess and the savvy of the former Deputy Senate President, the late Senator Haruna Abubakar (June 3 to November 18, 1999), he certainly dwarfs the immediate past Deputy Senate President, Senator Ibrahim Mantu. But he is no match for Mantu in political intrigues. In the affairs and/or administration of the Senate today, Ekweremadu enjoys a lot of leeway from Mark. The Senate President deliberately and strategically allows him to pull some strings. Unlike Mark who enjoys the support of a vast majority of his colleagues, Ekweremadu enjoys the confidence of some, not all of his colleagues.
- Teslim Folarin: A Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) senator from the Northwest zone had described the emergence of Senator Teslim Kolawole Folarin from Oyo State as Senate Leader as a disaster. The senator said he did not believe that anything good could crystallise from Folarin's leadership of the Senate. The 46-year old acolyte of the late strongman of Ibadan Politics, Chief Lamidi Adedibu, had clinched the position on the basis of religious balancing. The position was ceded to the Southwest zone. Mark and Ekweremadu (Senate President and Deputy Senate President respectively) are Christians. It was argued that a Muslim should be given the position of Senate Leader; otherwise, Senator Gbenga Ogunniya, another Christian, who has been in the Senate since 1999, would have emerged. Another senator said he wished Senator Olorunnimbe Mamora were in PDP, religion might have been discountenanced to pick his as Senate Leader. Folarin, according to the senator, does not have the fecundity, profundity, eloquence and legislative experience of Mamora (former Lagos State Speaker who is now Deputy Minority Leader). Historically, Folarin, a Political science graduate, is in the shadows of prominent Nigerians who had held the position of Senate Leader from the Second Republic - Dr. Olusola Saraki (Second Republic), Professor Wande Abimbola (Third Republic), Samaila Mamman and Abdallah Wali (fourth session of the current Senate) and Dr. Dalhatu Sarki Tafida (fifth session of the Senate). Folarin has been leading debates and business of the senate from prepared texts purportedly packaged by an in-house consultant. He, unlike, some other principal officer, has not been able to hold his colleagues spell bound with his ex-tempore contributions as there has not been any. On the administrative side, he is said to wield a great deal of influence, which he is said to be deploying to his personal advantage.
- Kanti Mahmud Bello: He is the Chief Whip of the Senate. He is from Katsina State. A member of the Third Republic Senate, Bello has been in the Fourth Republic Senate since 2003. A fearless politician, he contributes robustly to debates on the floor of the Senate. Although an Engineer by training, he has always intervened in debates on the floor from the prism of the constitution. Remarkably, he does not come to the chamber with a properly bound copy of the 1999 Constitution. He always reads from loose pages of the document. He combines the strategies of deploying parliamentary rules with political sagacity in a bid to the senate to support his favoured position. Sometimes, he succeeds; sometimes, he fails. But as Chief Whip, he has not, like Senator Roland Owie (June 3 to November 18, 1999) or Senator Victor Oyofo who took over from him or even Senator Udoma Udo Udoma who took over from Oyofo, been alive to his responsibility of whipping his colleagues into line during plenary. For instance, Owie would move round the chamber to ask his colleagues to take their seat and keep silent. Sometimes, Bello forgets that he is the Whip. The presiding officer has always called on him to do his job.
-Victor Ndoma Egba (SAN): He is the Deputy Senate Leader. The Cross River Central Senator has been in the Senate since 2003. His brilliance and eloquent contributions to debates on the floor stand him out in the Senate. Well-respected for his knowledge of the law, the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, Ndoma-Egba flourishes in making constitutional interventions whenever there are clashes between the provisions of bills being considered and the constitution. He is a very engaging personality. What he lacks in physical stature, he makes up for in intellectual prowess. His contributions on the floor are always ex-tempore. He announces himself as one who knows the stuff that he is made of. He rarely raises a point of order and anytime he does, the presiding officer is wont to sustain it.
- Mohammed Mana: He is the Deputy Chief Whip. He was elected into the Senate in 2007 and he is the only new senator to have been elected into a leadership position in the Senate. Representing Adamawa North Senatorial District. The 59-year old former Military Administrator of Plateau State is taciturn, but speaks smoothly and forcefully anytime he contributes to debates on the floor.
- Maina Ma'aji Lawan: He is the Minority Leader in the Senate. Lawan was former Borno State Governor in the ill-fated Third Republic. He was in the Senate from 1999 to 2003; he was out from 2003 to 2007; and, now he is back. His legislative experience and the fact that his party, the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) has the highest number of seats in the minority caucus of the senate, clinched him the position of Minority Leader. Lawan (55), blessed with a near baritone voice, is articulate if nor rhythmical in his presentation and/or contributions to debates on the floor. A graduate of Business Administration, he is always business-like. He seconds, whenever he is in chambers, motions on Executive bills moved by the Senate Leader. He has almost always gravitated on the same wavelength with his Deputy, Olorunnimbe Mamora, during debates on the floor. Very recently, when Mamora moved a motion calling on the senate to disown the reported vote of confidence passed in the Chairman of the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) by eleven senators led by Chairman of the Senate Committee on INEC, Senator Isiaka Adeleke, Lawan seconded and supported the motion, even though the motion was defeated.
- Olorunnimbe Mamora: The 56-year old former Speaker of the Lagos State House of Assembly from 1999 to 2003 has been in the Senate since June 3, 2003. He represents Lagos east senatorial district. He was for some time between 2003 and 2007, Chairman of the Senate Committee on Ethics, Privileges and Public Petitions. In the current session of the senate, he is Deputy Minority Leader, a position he clinched on the platform of the Action Congress (AC). Mamora is a phenomenon in the Senate. He is not only experienced, but he is also very brilliant. His interventions during debates are always very mature; his presentations and/or contributions are always refreshing and illuminating. He understands the standing rules of the senate and the parliamentary procedures like he knows the back of his hands. With a rich, sweet voice, for he is more or less the Senate choir master (remember when he sang a dirge for the late Senators Yari Gandi and Badamasi Maccido who died in a plane crash in Abuja?), he passes across his message (be it constitutional points of order or straight contributions to debates on motions or bills) in plenary.
- Ahmed Sani Yarima: Former Governor of Zamfara State from 1999 to 2007 is the Minority Whip. Unlike the Chief Whip whose party (PDP) accounts for eighty-six senators out of the 109 members of the Senate, he is expected to keep under check only twenty-three members of the minority caucus. The members (of ANPP, AC, ACCORD Party) hardly cause distractions and therefore he is virtually on holiday during plenary. And, by virtue of being a member of the body of Principal Officers of the Senate, he enjoys the privilege of being recognised by the presiding officer during plenary to contribute to debates. Although he is not experienced in legislative matters, he is muddling through.
- Kabiru Gaya: He was former Governor of Kano State in the ill-fated Third Republic. He is currently Deputy Minority Whip of the Senate. Gaya (57) has not been very active in terms of contributing to debates on the floor; whereas, he has the privilege of being recognised as a principal officer before any other member of the Senate, he has not maximally availed himself of the privilege.
Nevertheless, the administrative style of Mark; his desire to perform and surpass his predecessors; his commitment to rally all senators behind him and other strategic moves by the Senate Leadership, irrespective of the individual leader's capital and deficit, are expected to produce a synergy between members and leaders that will continue to define and redefine a united Senate and underscore a collective can-do spirit in lawmaking for better governance of the nation as well as effective oversight checks on the executive arm of government and its MDAs.

Original_Search_Title: City Deep Plans to Be 'Market of the Future'
Headline: South Africa: City Deep Plans to Be 'Market of the Future'
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Fruit and vegetable mart founded in 1893 will become a 'food hub'.
WELL before 5am, boxes of tomatoes, lettuce, other vegetables and fruit are being stacked at the 63ha Johannesburg Fresh Produce Market, waiting for retailers to sift through and strike a price with the sellers, who market on behalf of 15000 producers around SA.
CEO Kgosientso Ramokgopa has plans to expand the market, which is owned by the Johannesburg City Council. He sees "the market of the future" where prices are displayed in real time like the JSE's ticker, producers can log on to the website and track their produce and consumers can shop at what will become a food hub.
"It's a city on its own," says Ramokgopa, who aims to make best use of the virgin land that the market owns, which is close to Transnet's depot. The market has its peak time at 7am, but by is empty by 1pm.
Hopefully, Ramokgopa says, the market will be able to operate beyond those hours when a retail component comes into play. There are also plans to open an organic market one day a week.
An additional 30ha will be added to the existing market, as part of plans to create a food hub as opposed to a fresh produce market. A meat and fish market will be introduced to supply small, medium and large butchers who want to buy in bulk and not on the small scale available at the moment.
A fruit and vegetable section will be introduced for household customers, who will be able to save based on the average market price.
"The driving consideration is the consumer," says Ramokgopa. About R300m will be spent to turn the market into the "market of the future". Funds will be raised on the open market.
More than 17000 buyers buy produce at the market -- 10000 of whom are regulars. Turnover for the past financial year was more than R2,8bn and the average value of sales per month is R235m. The record for a single business day is R16m.
A million tons of fruit and vegetables pass through the market each year, making it the fifth-largest market in the world. Volumes are higher than at Rungis International, in Paris, which sells 884706 tons and has been deemed to be the largest wholesale market .
The site in City Deep also hosts several other businesses on a "one-stop-shop" basis. The complex has shops where wholesale and retail meat, fish, eggs, groceries and provisions are sold.
The market, which works on a fixed commission basis, dates back to 1893, but started trading on a commission market system in 1974. It is now the largest fresh produce market in Africa. It has a diversity of buyers who come from all over SA, Namibia, Botswana, Swaziland, Mozambique and Zimbabwe.
The original market was in the centre of Johannesburg at what was known as Market Square, where trading mostly took place by barter. By 1913, the market had outgrown Market Square and moved to Newtown, where trade took place by auction, and all sorts of fresh goods were sold, including poultry, game, hay, lucerne and pheasants. The last year that the market traded in Newtown, 1974, turnover was R2,4m.
The market was established on the site at City Deep in 1974 and about 60% of the property has been developed. The three sales halls are used for vegetables, fruit and potatoes and onions and measure a total of 65000m' . It has 55 cold rooms that can accommodate 4561 pallets of fresh produce, while 50 banana ripening rooms can handle 1590 pallets of bananas at a time. Watermelons are the only product still auctioned, and have a separate under-cover trading area of about 3500m' .

Original_Search_Title: NSHR - Superlative Institution, Erroneous Personnel
Headline: Namibia: NSHR - Superlative Institution, Erroneous Personnel
Body_Text:
WE should by all means acknowledge all discourses in their different facets, which have taken place so far on the issues surrounding human rights.
Diverse as they may be, these discourses have significantly contributed to what we know today about human rights. It is not our collective approach under normal circumstances to venture into platforms of this disposition dually but on account that we subscribe to the ideas of the way forward at our university, we felt that we should collectively contribute.
One of the discourses was by our high school colleague who talked about human rights institution and then used the same platform to tell us that her father (Hon Moongo) is the best politician. Little does she know that we do watch and attend parliamentary sessions and it is only logical that a daughter will see the father as the best and this is the case with all other kids her age. Despite the fact that we dismiss her submission, we find elation in the fact that she contributed. Our work here today is to engage dear readers in a discourse in which we will argue that NSHR has shifted focus and needs young blood for the sake of Nation building.
The idea that led to the realisation of the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights in Philadelphia deserves an applause accompanied by the white-initiated Nobel Peace Prize. Human rights are not just rights as the name indicates, human rights are rights inherent to all human beings, whatever our nationality, place of residence, sex, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, language, or any other status. We are all equally entitled to our human rights without discrimination. These rights are all interrelated, interdependent and indivisible.
Thus, they are a necessity that we should all reinforce in a contemporary Namibia, Africa and the world at large. They are necessities, which we need as ingredients of a harmonious society. Human rights are not something that we can distort or make fun of, they are not that, nor are they something under which we can hibernate to accomplish our personal goals and ambitions to satisfy our egos.
Our people (black) all over the world suffered human rights abuses for centuries and they are perhaps still suffering in today's world. We the 'darkies' should be gratiful to those pioneers of black consciousness who have capitulated their lives. These are extraordinary darkies such as Martin Luther King Junior, W.E.B. Du Bois, Bob Marley, Marcus Garvey, Rosa Parks and many others who heroically contributed to the rights not only of blacks, but human rights in general. These heroes will turn in their graves if they are to learn how their African brothers in the name of Namibian Society for Human Rights (NSHR) are conducting their affairs under the banner of this noble notion (human rights) for which they fought tersely throughout their lives.
NSHR was established on December 1, 1989. At the time of its establishment, a citizen by the name Phil Ya Nangolo has been at its helm until this very second when you are reading this paper and by the time you finish reading it. From the onset without leaving you in suspense, it is our argument that NSHR is an institution that Namibia needs big time. It is an institution that can contribute significantly to the growth, upbringing and help in mutual co-existence among our nationals. NSHR as it is today, in our view, does not live up to its citizens' expectation and satisfaction. A one-man show, NSHR has emerged as a tool that its director uses to launch attacks on people that he either envies or hates for reasons only known to himself and his two lieutenants.
There is no doubt that the attacks that are launched against few individuals and a certain entity, have historical background which is not known to us in which he was perhaps aggrieved, and now he is using the time that he has to get back at whoever he feels like getting back at. There is an old saying that says 'ombedi ihayii moshiti' which can be vaguely translated to mean that blame does not go to something else than a person. What is very paradoxical to us as students of political studies is the fact that the compatriot in our discourse (Mr Ya Nangolo) used to belong at one point to the political organisation he always tarnishes. The question at the back of our minds is what poked Ya Nangolo (who regards Jonas Savimbi as a hero) to jump the historic ship and find himself as an inflexible antagonist of the body that he probably knew and still knows its anthem? Was it in search of the living, or perhaps personal ambitions that are not met, lost opportunities or maybe one of those 'lost focus and vision' rhetoric?
We have found the director to be a fuming and very angry Tate who wastes no time in creating confusion, fear, intimidation with his obscured deeds and hallucinations. Politics of personalisation always has been his favourite cup of tea. He is always telling us that a particular person did this and that in most cases it is always the same people being spoken about or those that belong to that same group. It is always those people that do wrong things. In my view, this happens when you have your eyes only set on those people 24 hours due to reasons only known to yourself.
The other year he was telling Namibia that people disappeared, clearly stating that the same people and political organisation he always accuses are behind the disappearance. He came back again the following year with his fictitious arguments in which he aspired to take the nation's most cherished man (the Founding Father) to the white court. Throughout these times the nation stood behind their leaders, united as a rock, with nationwide demonstrations by citizens from all walks of life; young and old, able and disabled, tall and short. For the first time ever since we were politically conscious have we seen the nation provoked to such extremes and to make it worse by one citizen! Their unity gives testimony to a saying of the century that goes 'the people united, striving for the common good, for all members of society, will always emerge victorious'.
Those of them that doubted the saying must have had unending 'I could've'.
In our judgment despite the pronouncement of the white court, the feelings expressed by thousands of Namibians including the Head of State left the NSHR and its buddies at The Hague with no choice but to explore other means of disturbing peace such as the one we had mid-last year of the apparent discovery of graves in Angola. The director's hallucinated scientific discovery was chanted down as misleading by the Government's Minister of Safety and Security in our upper chamber.
As matters stand now, the director of the NSHR is a victim of social denial by his own making. Citizens have collectively publicly demonstrated against his doings, songs are being composed in which he gets donations of sharp utterance; the fingers are being pointed at him as he walks in public places. We are responsible for the aftermaths of our actions. The public's reaction through social denial can be said to have proliferated the anger and frustration of the NSHR's boss thus becoming in our view the bitterest citizen consequently finding it difficult to pursue his agenda.
The Way Forward
If the NSHR could move away from a revenge and personal ambition execution institution, we could swiftly budge towards a harmonious society with a good human rights atmosphere. Citizens, us in particular, do not see the work of NSHR apart from its practices of memory politics, accusation and getting even. We need a new human rights agenda that is based on ideas and intellectually driven. In our own conviction, NSHR boss is perhaps aging and wrongly using his old age in fighting losing battles. He needs to retire and give over to someone young and energetic who is professionally guided, fueled with the ability to distinguish between work and personal drives. Youthful intellectual agenda can be modelled from NWR, NYC and many others and even the donor country (US) recently.
The director deserves a good retirement package from his donors and bosses so that he can view the exquisiteness of the land of the braves which he missed due to obsession with scientific discoveries of graves and documenting The Hague, this is the beauty of Ruacana, Etosha (safe now), Soussesvvlei and even the new Onkoshi Camp. The director always screeches out against apparently people hanging on to power.
Since NSHR's establishment Ya Nangolo has been its director for 17 years now. One always wondered where he obtained his mandate, and who elected or perhaps appointed him for all those years. People he always badmouths are elected every five years. Guess who is lynching on to power now ladies and gentlemen! Charity begins at home. This young intellectual we are advocating should come from those flamboyant youthful men and women with a postgraduate qualification on human rights or related fields, with a keen interest in human rights. The new NSHR agenda should be ideologically driven with a large component of enlightening our citizens about human rights and related issues, operating in all 13 regions.
The new agenda will hold workshops and conferences on human rights, the new agenda will give internships to university law students, the new NSHR will have educational campaigns on human rights in local media and the new agenda under youthful leadership will publish books on human rights in order to keep long-lasting knowledge. The new NSHR will establish itself as an institution unlike someone's recreational area, it should be democratic and a structural institution with positions and project personnel filled on merit and not on patronage.
However, we should state here that we hold nothing against Ya Nangolo, but we are merely separating apples from bananas for the sake of our developmental state project of which we do not compromise. We should mention that we are not discounting our compatriot's freedom of speech and expression, which he is fully conscious of (he always quote the constitution). Once retired, the director can still go and criticise, whoever he wishes to as it is his right to, but he must not do it in the name of human rights because it is wrong! In retirement, he can still do what he seems to like (getting even, hallucinating, insulting, provoking and accusing people). The Founding Father has set an example; Mandela did the same and many others.
Worldwide, we are celebrating the new agenda of the US youthful President-elect black Barack Obama. We call on the board of donors/directors of NSHR (if there is even) to set the new agenda and get the right personnel for the sake of nation building. Citizens are even asking it to be registered with the former seventh heaven of Mr Kanime (ECN) since it is no different to new projects by political entrepreneurs!
Job Shipululo Amupanda (Final year) and Hafeni Dioma Nashoonga (3rd year)
Political Studies Students, University of Namibia

Original_Search_Title: Tsvangirai Says No to Govt House
Headline: Zimbabwe: Tsvangirai Turns Down State House Residence Offer
Body_Text:
Harare — PRIME Minister Morgan Tsvangirai has turned down an offer to take up residence at State House and instead opted to live at his Strathaven home, the Zimbabwe Independent has established.
Tsvangirai as head of government was expected to take up residence at State House while President Robert Mugabe continued living at Zimbabwe House, his current place of residence.
Sources in the Tsvangirai-led MDC said the prime minster was made the offer to live at State House and he turned it down saying he had ample accommodation at his home.
Mugabe, the sources said, had extended the offer in the spirit of the all-inclusive government.
Tsvangirai's spokesperson James Maridadi could neither confirm not deny whether the offer was extended.
"The most important role for Prime Minister Tsvangirai is to work towards the rebuilding of Zimbabwe's economy and the democratisation of the country," Maridadi said. "Issues of whether he stays at State House or not are not important. What is important to him is to get the job at hand done."
He, however, was quick to say that in future Tsvangirai would consider where he would live.
In the early 1980s President Canaan Banana lived at State House while Prime Minister Mugabe was at Zimbabwe House.
Sources said Tsvangirai's decision not to move into State House was arrived at because the inclusive government was not permanent and in case he lost future presidential elections expected in two years, it would be embarrassing for him to move back to his Strathaven home.
"There were numerous factors which determined whether he took up residence at State House or not but the main reason is that the Strathaven house was more homely to Tsvangirai than moving to a new place," another source said. "The fact that the inclusive government is not permanent meant that he could be forced to move out anytime."
State House used to be Government House, the home of Southern Rhodesian governors. The last governor to occupy it was Lord Soames from December 1979 to April 1980. Prime Ministers lived across the road at what is now Zimbabwe House.

Original_Search_Title: IGG Mwondha Defies Parliament Again
Headline: Uganda: IGG Mwondha Defies Parliament Again
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE relationship between the Inspector General of Government (IGG) and MPs has been strained ever since she refused to appear before legislators to defend her office's budget. She got away with it that time by sending her deputy, but this time Faith Mwondha threw spanner in the works by refusing to be vetted for reappointment.
She has challenged the authority of the parliamentary committee on appointments to decide her fate. The legislators approved her deputy's reappointment, but said Mwondha was working illegally if she continued to snub them.
Mwondha has also been at war with the head of Public Service, John Mitala, over the signing of her re-appointment letter. The Attorney General and the Speaker of Parliament both said the IGG should appear in Parliament first. Will she survive again? She insists that only the President should decide.
Ironically, in another matter, the President has rubbished Mwondha's report, recommending that the eviction of the tenants in the low-cost Naguru and Nakawa housing estates be stayed. Mwondha fought the former local government minister over the project intended to redevelop the estates. She said the tender had been wrongly awarded to OpecPrime Properties, who then threatened to sue for damages. The tenants will have to vacate the dilapidated homes to pave way for the investor to build new homes.
A ban on fishing may be the only way to allow fish stocks in Lake Victoria to recover, given the rampant illegal fishing going on. The Government is considering stopping fishing if by December fish stocks continue their worrying decline. The three countries that share the lake hope to remove 50% of illegal gear used in catching immature fish by June as one of the measures to combat the problem. Fish stocks have dropped from 1.9m tonnes in 1999 to 383 tonnes in January this year.
The MPs of Rwanda are concerned about the remains of the victims of the 1994 Rwanda Genocide interred in Masaka, Mpigi and Rakai districts of Uganda. Hundreds of decomposing bodies floated into Lake Victoria through the Kagera River and were buried in mass graves at different sites as they came. The Rwandan parliament heard that Ugandans were desecrating the remains by exhuming the bones for witchcraft purposes. Some wanted the remains repatriated to Rwanda if they could not be treated decently by Ugandans.
Ugandans have been urged not to fret about the state of the President's finger, which has been in a plaster for weeks now. The Minister for the Presidency said Museveni's finger is getting better and has not hampered his ability to fulfill his duties. MPs were concerned after seeing the plaster and the President using his left hand for handling, eating and greeting.
On a brighter note, the President demonstrated the results of an industrial project initiated to process green bananas (matooke) into a more useful form that can be preserved. He ate a Tooke biscuit made from matooke flour. The initiative is also using the banana peels to process biogas to fuel the industry.

Original_Search_Title: The Ezeigbo Palaver
Headline: Nigeria: The Ezeigbo Palaver
Body_Text:
When I was growing up, I often heard people use the word "palaver'. It was hardly ever used in any serious environment. Rather, those who spent hours drinking at the local bar, village inn or at venues where they played the game of draughts always engaged themselves in a shouting match or pedestrian argument or altercation. Then almost as quickly as the "palaver' erupted, peace would return to the forum. It was at such a forum that the expression Igbo Enwe Eze would lead to a fight.
The word "Ezeigbo' is unique. It is a title you do not find in Igbo land, except that of the Ikemba Nnewi, Dim Chukwuemeka Odumegwu Ojukwu, who got his own during the protracted Eze Nri dispute. There can not be an Ezeigbo in Igbo land because there is no kingdom with which the Ezeigbo will be associated. In all Igbo-speaking states, there are various kingdoms. The late Sam Mbakwe, former Governor of the old Imo State, stated that the Ojukwu Ezeigbo controversy was not necessary as the only Eze he knew in Avutu was the Eze of Avutu. This is quite true as every Eze has a geographical description of his kingdom.

Original_Search_Title: Country to Benefit From $3 Million Maize Project
Headline: Uganda: Country to Benefit From $3 Million Maize Project
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA is set to benefit from a $3m (about sh6b) maize project to promote a maize variety resistant to striga weed infections.
Striga weed, which affects maize, rice and sorghum is known as Emoto in Teso, Tiger in Busia, Kayongo in Busoga, Luyonga in Lusamia, Alyeri in Lango, Odwa/Luduwa in Acholi and Ava among the Madis.

Original_Search_Title: Khenyeza Has No Respect At All for the Bafana Shirt
Headline: South Africa: Khenyeza Has No Respect At All for the Bafana Shirt
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — DEAR Mabhuti Khenyeza. You may not like the sound of this, but I bet a colleague the contents of my piggy bank that you would either not honour your call-up letter to the national team or you would typically saunter into camp way past the Sunday evening deadline you were given.
You have become such a predictable little bugger that I was supremely confident that I would not have to smash my prized piggy into smithereens to settle this bet. And predictably, you did not disappoint.

Original_Search_Title: Baganda Fight for Their Heritage
Headline: Uganda: Baganda Fight for Their Heritage
Body_Text:
Kampala — Specioza Nakabugo (63) sits on a mat under a mango tree on a well-mowed grass patch, her expression a blend of boredom and gloom.
The elderly woman stares into space as her two grandchildren noisily play a game of ball under the scorching midday sun at her son's home in the residential suburb of Lungujja, 10 minutes' drive south of the Ugandan capital. Nakabugo's not particularly happy, despite having her grandchildren around her.

Original_Search_Title: How I Was Kidnapped, Beaten - Hon. Suur
Headline: Nigeria: How I Was Kidnapped, Beaten - Hon. Suur
Body_Text:
Moses John and Tina Sawok — He was kidnapped by unknown gunmen in Makurdi, only to find himself in Nasarawa. In this chat with ONUKOGU KANAYO JUBAL, MOSES JOHN and TINA SAWOK, Hon. Suur Solomon Aboh, the councilor for Iwarnyam Ward, Konshisha local government area of Benue State, recounts his ordeal and the possible reasons for his abduction.
Can you tell us what happened to you?

Original_Search_Title: Shhh National Assembly Asleep
Headline: Nigeria: Shhh National Assembly Asleep
Body_Text:
Lagos — We are profoundly fascinated by the presence of intelligent people in government. The Germans place such people in the category of the hoch verstandig; people of high intelligence.
Shakespeare's Julius Caesar first spotted them around 1601, when for purely self-protective reasons, he showed a strong distaste for such men around him in the running of government affairs, "Let me have around me men that are fat; sleek-headed men and such that sleep all night: Yond Cassius is lean and hungry looking; he thinks too much: such men are dangerous". That was the view that Julius Caesar had for the type of intelligent men that we are talking about; the type of men that he did not want to have around him. One attribute of these intelligent people is that they are quick at seeing bad laws and taking (un)due advantage of such bad laws. People in this category are to be found everywhere, except that some of them could be unlucky to come from places where intelligence is buried.

Original_Search_Title: Farewell to 'Hope'
Headline: Nigeria: Farewell to 'Hope'
Body_Text:
"At the time the request was made, EFCC had cleared the former governors. I didn't ask the EFCC to clear the former governors after I got the request from Britain. All they have done is to embarrass the government. I can't grant any request if all the ingredients of the offences were committed here."
-- Justice Minister, Michael Aondoakaa

Original_Search_Title: Economy Weighing on Voters' Minds
Headline: Angola: Economy Weighing on Voters' Minds
Body_Text:
Luanda — Angola's economy may be booming on the back of high oil prices and strong diamond exports, but six years after a peace deal ended the 27-year civil war, unemployment stands at around 65 percent.
Take a walk down any street in Luanda and next to the shiny banks and new office buildings, there are street vendors, many just teenagers, hawking anything they can find to make a living.

Original_Search_Title: Developing Countries Brace for Tougher Fishing Regime
Headline: Africa: Developing Countries Brace for Tougher Fishing Regime
Body_Text:
A little-known European Union regulation coming into force January next year could have a major impact on the health of fishing industries in developing countries, according to a new study.
The EU is the single largest consumer of fish in the world, accounting for around 40 per cent of global imports. Such is the continent's appetite for aquatic life, the cumulative net fish exports from developing countries now exceed earnings from coffee, bananas and rubber.

Original_Search_Title: Zanu PF Chairman John Nkomo Faces Sodomy Charges
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zanu PF Chairman John Nkomo Faces Sodomy Charges
Body_Text:
A 31 year old Bulawayo man, Mncedisi Twala, has sensationally claimed that ZANU PF National Chairman, John Nkomo, molested him in April 2002.
After first fleeing to South Africa Twala says he came back after the formation of the unity government and filed a police complaint in July. The police however refused to investigate his complaint until last week Thursday when they called him to supply more information. He was arrested, allegedly for making a false report, and spent 6 days in custody. Twala also claims attempts were made at Luveve Police station to inject him with a mysterious substance.

Original_Search_Title: Why is Clinton Coming to Continent, What Are the Issues?
Headline: Africa: Why is Clinton Coming to Continent, What Are the Issues?
Body_Text:
US Secretary of State Hillary Clinton embarks on a seven-nation visit to Africa this week, anxious to show the continent is a priority for the Obama administration even as it tackles a host of other issues.
She will visit Kenya, South Africa, Angola, Democratic Republic of Congo, Nigeria, Liberia and Cape Verde.

Original_Search_Title: Saccos Creation Takes Shape
Headline: Uganda: Saccos Creation Takes Shape
Body_Text:
Kampala — The hall at Nakivubo Blue Primary school near Owino market was filled to capacity.
The over 1,200 men and women were not out to watch a musical performance, but to discuss the progress of their Savings and Credit Cooperative Union (SACCO). They were members of Kampala Park Yard Traders SACCO.

Original_Search_Title: Kidnapping - How I Spent Two Days in Kidnappers' Den - Catholic Priest
Headline: Nigeria: Kidnapping - How I Spent Two Days in Kidnappers' Den - Catholic Priest
Body_Text:
The Catholic Ecclesiastical Province of Owerri was visibly stunned beyond description when it got information that an Indian-born Catholic priest, Rev. Fr. Matthew Cherian, had been kidnapped by gun-toting gangsters at Ulakwo, Owerri North local government area of Imo State.
Our Correspondent, Chidi Nkwopara, cornered the victim and he naturally opened up narrated his experience on how it all begin and how did it end..

Original_Search_Title: Mukono Brothers Get Life for Murder
Headline: Uganda: Mukono Brothers Get Life for Murder
Body_Text:
Kampala — Three brothers have been sentenced to life imprisonment for murdering a special-hire driver and stealing his car.
David Kasada, Dauson Bamugoloze and Moses Waiswa, all residents of Wakisi sub-county in Mukono, appeared before Justice Vincent Zehurikize at Mukono High Court.

Original_Search_Title: Bond Well Received, But Uncertainty Still a Bind
Headline: South Africa: Bond Well Received, But Uncertainty Still a Bind
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — SA IS an infrequent foreign borrower, so the fact that the new $1,5bn 10-year bond issue was well received in foreign markets is excellent news.
The issue was the first non verbal commentary by foreigners on the new government -- it was the first time the lenders had actually put money on the table, as opposed to simply expressing an opinion, about the new government. Their verdict was positive, signalling the market is bullish about SA's longer-term future.

Original_Search_Title: Bandits Abduct Deputy Minister
Headline: Zambia: Minister Abducted
Body_Text:
BANDITS on Wednesday night abducted Deputy Minister in the Office of the Vice President Gaston Sichilima and later dumped him in Chudleigh area before getting away with his Toyota Fortuner Government vehicle.
Police spokesperson Bonnie Kapeso said three men armed with two AK 47 rifles ambushed Mr Sichilima outside his house on Leopards Hill Road in Kabulonga around 21:30 hours.
He said the criminals pounced on the deputy minister as he was about to drive into his yard.
The bandits who had a backup Toyota Corolla put Mr Sichilima into the back seat and drove off and only stopped in Chudleigh where they dumped him.
"Apart from the Government vehicle, the criminals got the minister's three cell phones and we have launched a manhunt," Mr Kapeso said.
In another development, two Catholic priests are feared dead after an engine-powered banana boat they were travelling in capsised on Lake Bangweulu.
Mr Kapeso said two other priests and a cox-swain on the boat were rescued by a fisherman who was on the lake in the heavy storm on Wednesday morning.
Mr Kapeso said the priests were travelling from Nsombo to Chilubi Island when they met their fate.
He said that a team of rescuers had since been dispatched to search for the missing priests and that as at 10:00 hours yesterday, the bodies had not been retrieved.
Zambia Episcopal Conference (ZEC) spokesperson Paul Samasumo said a team of priests had been sent to Chilubi Island to join the search.
Luwingu District Commissioner George Mwamba identified the missing priests as Lenatus Mutale and Simon Matafwali.
Chilubi district is an Island in Northern Province on Lake Bangweulu and is only accessible by air and water transport.
And accountant general, Mike Goma has revealed that some cashiers in Government departments have improvised stamps for banks which they use to purport that they have deposited public funds when they have not done so.
Mr Goma told the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) that his office was investigating a case in which an officer in Chipata is alleged to have pocketed K143 million using the same tactic.
Mr Goma said this when the committee chaired by Luena Member of Parliament Charles Milupi took to task Southern Province Permanent Secretary Darius Hakayobe and his delegation on why they overpaid a contractor by more than K13 million.

Original_Search_Title: Nnamani, Nwobodo And Chime's Second Term
Headline: Nigeria: Nnamani, Nwobodo And Chime's Second Term
Body_Text:
Enugu — There is no better way to describe the position Nkanuland holds in Enugu State than to say that it is the heartbeat of politics in the 18 year-old state, known for intriguing and fascinating politicking.
Either by luck or happenstance, the clan was able to produce the first executive Governor in the old Anambra State, in the person of Jim Nwobodo, who was in power between 1979 and 1983 under the defunct Nigerian Peoples Party (NPP).
Although Nwobodo was displaced by an Udi man, Christian Onoh in 1983, the latter's reign under the platform of National Party of Nigeria (NPN), was short-lived as the military truncated the second republic the same year, leading to a long era of military rule.
When another democratic dispensation beckoned in 1992, just a year after Enugu State was created, Okwesilieze Nwodo, an Nsukka man was elected governor under the platform of National Republican Convention (NRC). But, again, his reign merely lasted for about a year before the military struck again.
When democratic rule was returned to the country again in 1999, an Nkanu man, Chimaroke Nnamani was elected again, not by luck or happenstance this time but by the political shrewdness and sagacity of another Nkanu man, Nwobodo.
Nnamani, who was elected under the platform of Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), however disagreed with his political godfather, Nwobodo, shortly afterwards and deployed the entire state arsenal against his mentor.
Nwobodo came out of the political battle a humbled man while Nnamani took charge of the politics of the state effectively, with his Ebeano political family, determining who got what, how and when, during his eight-year reign.
The Ebeano Family provided a veritable platform for Chief Ken Nnamani, an Nkanu son to go to the Senate in 2003 as representative of Enugu East Senatorial zone, dominated by the homogenous Nkanu clan.
And in 2005, when the Senate President, Adolphus Nwagbara was named in a bribery scandal just like his predecessor, Chuba Okadigbo, it became the turn of the state to produce a Senate President and the lot fell on Nnamani, though his Nkanu brother (Chimaroke) wanted Senator Ike Ekweremadu.
Nnamani eventually turned about to be the only Senate President in that dispensation that survived the notorious banana peels that pulled down others before him.
Therefore, by the time that a new era came in the state on May 29, 2007 with the inauguration of Sullivan Chime, a protégé of Ebeano political family as Governor, Nkanu land was privileged to have a former Senate President and two former governors.
Even, the Udi and Nsukka people touted to be more educated could not boast of such political figures with contacts all over the state and in the country in general.
But less than two years into Chime's administration, a cry of marginalisation is emanating from the much-expressed heartbeat of Enugu politics.
This issue of Nkanu marginalisation, was made an issue recently when leaders of thought and traditional rulers in the area converged at the palace of the traditional ruler of Nike Community, Igwe Julius Nnaji.
The former Senate President and two serving members of the House of Representatives, Gilbert Nnaji and Offor Chukwuegbo were among the who is who in attendance at the meeting.
Apart from Nwobodo and ex-Governor, Nnamani who were conspicuously absent, the meeting attracted former and serving political office holders as well as royal fathers and leaders of thought of Nkanu extraction.
While the leaders did not come up with a communiqués at the end of the closed-door meeting, the steering committee was given the mandate to chart a new course for the people of the area.
It was gathered that this arrangement was made after futile attempts by some serving state government officials to dissuade the formation of a Central Nkanu body, which could be seen as opposing Chime, whose second term got endorsement of the state chapter of PDP last March.
For instance, Commissioner of Public Utilities, Emeka Ani, was quoted as saying that he would not be part of anybody opposing the government, as he was a testimony to the goodness of the Chime administration.
But Nnamani, Nnaji and Chukwuegbo were said to have made it clear to the gathering that the body was not meant to oppose the governor, though the former Senate President, pointed out that there was nothing wrong with opposition as long as it was constructive.
Daily Independent gathered that the issue of Nkanu marginalisation and Chime's second term had rekindled old animosities in Nkanu land, including the age-long rivalry between the former Senate President and Nwobodo, who hail from the same Amechi Awkunanaw community.
Nwobodo is now leading a group that is known as PDP Consensus Forum (CF) in Enugu East Senatorial zone, which has vowed to ensure that Chime secured re-election.
Sources told the reporter that Chime's second term ambition has rekindled the rivalry between the two Nkanu political giants, who were known to have remained far apart from each other politically, in the past 15 years.
A member of the Nnamani group, who spoke on grounds of anonymity, said that Nwobodo wanted to mortgage Nkanu interest for personal gains, just like he imposed Nnamani on the state without the consent of Nkanu people.
"Did he consult Nkanu people before choosing Chimaroke? It was his personal decision and at last, the whole thing backfired. We are not against Chime getting re-elected as governor. If the constitution of Nigeria allows that, who are we to say no? But all we are saying is that Nkanu people should have a better deal," the source stated.
The former Senate President was reportedly more irked by a purported agreement that Chime was supporting a speculated senatorial ambition of Nwobodo, in return for his total backing for the governor's own ambition.
Sources said that Nnamani is of the view that Nwobodo, who is seen as an elder statesman, should not go to the Senate and that a younger person could offer effective representation to the people of Enugu East Senatorial zone.
Although it was confirmed that Nnamani has no person in mind, those said to be interested include a serving member of House of Representatives, Gilbert Nnaji, former Secretary to State Government (SSG), Onyemuche Nnamani and former Accountant General, Chinyeaka Oha.
The trio alongside Nnamani attended the meeting of the NPF, where the alleged Nkanu marginalisation was made an issue.
But Nwobodo at the weekend said his support for the second term bid of Chime was borne out of the remarkable achievements of the governor in less than two years in office.
Nwobodo said that Chime was a leader, who knew what was good for the people, adding that the governor had created a peaceful atmosphere in the state that was quite different from the atmosphere of scuffles in the past administration.
