Matters were complicated when Namfisa approved these funds and the ministry was infuriated beyond description. Another aspect of which few people are aware is that the managers of these funds have already gone back to their investors telling them everything was hunky dory and there will be no Withholding Tax for the year in dispute. When this transpired not to be the case, a run on unit trusts was feared, and as a source in one of the banks so delicately put it: We simply do not had the cash on hand to refund all of the big guys at the same time, were they to withdraw their investments and take them somewhere else. This conflict had the potential to destroy a large part of the Namibian unit trust industry.
But as in any compromise, concessions have to come from both sides. If my sources are correct, the ministry will drop its nonsense of a 35% and a PAYE-related Withholding Tax and charge a flat 10% on all unit trust interest income. For their part, the legal engineering by the industry must be brushed aside, and all so-called roll-up funds must also pay the standard 10% Withholding Tax.
Exactly what the minutiae of the compromise further entail I do not know, but I am anxiously awaiting the announcement later today.
It is easy to apportion blame and point fingers at investment managers and at the ministry for the mess the unit trust industry had to face. All of this could have been avoided, were there better consultation between the ministry and the industry.
One thing that upset me is the fact that there is not a single individual in the industry that I talked to, that is not scared stiff of the ministry. One more outspoken investment manager, who still threatened to murder me if I divulge its identity, said it cannot understand this fear of the ministry. We are the reason for their existence, not the other way around, was all I got.
Which just underscores my point how glad I am the Rule of Law came out the winner.

Original_Search_Title: Credible Election Lies in Attitudinal Change, Not INEC Chairman - Ezeani
Headline: Nigeria: Credible Election Lies in Attitudinal Change, Not INEC Chairman - Ezeani
Body_Text:
For the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC), the last one month has particularly been tough, given the growing calls on its chairman, Professor Maurice Iwu, to step down in acceptance of guilt for the way the 2007 general election went.
The calls were ignited once more by the Appeal Court judgments in Ondo and Ekiti where the candidates returned as winners were given the boot.
But, is INEC the problem, as is believed by critics who want Iwu removed before his tenure lapses in 2010? Hasn't the politician with a do-or-die mindset got a big share of the blame? Andy Ezeani, chief press secretary (CPS) to the INEC boss, answers these questions in this on-line interview with Rafiu Ajakaye. Excerpts:
Calls for the sack of the INEC chair have intensified recently, bolstered by a number of court judgments. What is the thinking in the commission?
Well, I speak for myself here, but I can tell you that the commission does not think much of what you refer to as calls for its leadership to abdicate its responsibility. What for? What you have going on presently is a spin job running riot, sponsored by few elements from the political class running scared. Unfortunately, the media is being used. Labour has now been bought into and a lot more resources are going round. At least there is one good aspect of it, the money is coming out and is being distributed, albeit for a most unholy cause.
If there is sense and logic in this assault on an innocent patriot, then court judgments in two states cannot lead to call for the leadership of INEC at Abuja to abandon office when that very leadership did not conduct any election in any state. That is one. Secondly, the tribunals were established as part of the electoral process, to effect such correction as they did. So, if the tribunals cannot then effect such changes why set them up? But then, we are not dealing with men propelled by rational thinking. And by the way, we have had rerun elections in few other states based on tribunal ruling. What is now the different stuff with the case in Ondo and Ekiti that it should be made to be the end of life? Some people are probably rehearsing for a bid for some regional influence and so the nation will be at a standstill.
There is this allegation that the Prof. is trying to work his way into a second term. How do you receive such charge?
There is no allegation anywhere. You are simply allowing yourself to be used by some characters to voice out their nightmare. That's a part of what I referred to earlier when I said some folks in the political class are running scared. But first, they have to ask the man involved whether such is his ambition. You see, the man you are referring to has a solid profession and is not looking for a job. You just watch him step out of the present national duty at the end of his tenure and see how fast he will be snapped up by nations and solid institutions that appreciate his worth. You know people judge others with themselves. Most of the elements in the political scene in the country have no other job or handiwork than latching on to the state purse, season after season. Yes, we know the fears that propel those who are presently causing the present instability in the political system. We know why they are running scared, but let that be their headache. A certain governor for instance has been told by these spin doctors that he has little chance of wining re-election if this man remains on the saddle and puts the measures he is planning in place. So, the otherwise calm fellow has literally taken to the circuit with a campaign that is as unnecessary as it will do him no good.
Is INEC not disturbed that the upturning of a number of its pronouncements by courtis a testimony to what critics called the shoddy manner the 2007 poll was conducted?
Can't people be sincere and appreciative for once? These upturning of elections and successful challenge of election results in the wake of the 2007 elections happen because somebody put into place a system that makes for transparency and chance for correction of wrong. Before the 2007 elections, it was virtually impossible for any challenge of election result at the tribunal to be successful. Why? Because the system was closed and not conducive at all for any loser to obtain any factual evidence to back up a claim of wrong result. INEC under Prof. Iwu has affected the electoral process positively in many ways and instead of giving credit people with warped mind insist on seeing everything from the negative. Oh, you believe that the elections of 1999 and 2003 were better than that of 2007 because those who went to tribunals in the two previous instances came out empty handed eh? It is simply amazing how a society is being misled into not appreciating the great thing being done for it. Nigerians are intelligent people, they should open their minds and see how these self-serving politicians are using them.
Many Nigerians feel that the confidence vote by senators on the INEC boss is a mere payback, to compensate the man for 'rigging' them into office. What was the mood like when this vote came about, and how do you react to the avalanche of criticisms greeting the Senate's action?
Look, one is at a loss of what to make with some of you guys and the things you allow others to put into your head. And by the way stop saying many Nigerians feel or say... You can say some misled Nigerians feel or are...Now, all the senators at the Senate today were elected in the 2007 elections, right? Let me be very direct here, some the senators from Lagos State for instance, who by their posturing want to create the impression that their colleagues came in through the back door and they did through the front door, through what electoral process did they arrive? Was it the same election conducted under Prof. Maurice Iwu or was there a different INEC chairman that handled Lagos elections in 2007? I mean, how annoying and hypocritical can people be? This is simply unbelievable. A group of people emerged from the same election the same year with others and comfortably they hold tight to their mandate and boldly turn to accuse others of having a tainted mandate. Have some people taken leave of their senses? What gives some people the temerity to insult others this much? Ok, so for the majority of senators who were reasonable enough to know that the problem of the electoral system in Nigeria is not Prof. Maurice Iwu all they are doing is paying the INEC chairman for "rigging" them into office, but for the senator from Lagos elected the same day with others, because he believes in his wisdom that the problem of Nigeria begins and ends with Prof. Iwu that proves that his election was conducted in heaven and not by Prof. Iwu, right?
Given this doubt among some Nigerians, has INEC any chance of proving critics wrong ahead of the 2011 poll? Some critics feel the INEC chairman is not fit to conduct the rerun poll in Ekiti, given the verdict of the court which critics argue was an indictment on the commission?
Good, now you are saying some Nigerians. Try and make it few Nigerians. I have answered some of the issues you are repeating here, but let me re-emphasise, the INEC chairman does not conduct state elections. That is the direct responsibility of the resident electoral commissioner. I guess those who lose sleep because of Prof. Iwu do not want to hear that, but that is the way it is. So, conducting a repeat election in Ekiti is not an issue. But as I said earlier, INEC has successfully conducted rerun elections in Sokoto, Bayelsa and Kogi. These elections were hailed by Nigerians. So, please what is the big deal with Ekiti? But one more point, the rerun elections that have held after the 2007 elections reflected some essential reforms by INEC, which accounted for those elections being better organised. For instance, the so-called ad-hoc staff which politicians used to undermine elections have been discarded and security was improved upon. Is there anything wrong with giving credit where credit is due? Prof. Iwu has been steadily carrying out reforms of the Nigerian electoral system and he is leaving a huge legacy.
If Prof. were to say the way out of electoral malpractices, what would he say?
When you see Prof. you ask him. But if you ask me I will tell you that so much depends on the attitude and decision of the Nigerian politicians and candidates in elections. As long as they see elections as a do-or-die affair, with loads of money to subvert the system on one hand and weapons on another to destroy those who share different views, the chances are that election malpractices will continue. People just need to learn how to live with losing elections in our society.
Would he seek for power to punish erring (INEC) officers and financial autonomy?
For God's sake this whole thing is not about one man and what he will do or what he wants. Nigeria is too big for the kind of one-man show people are making out the whole thing to be. Or have we become a banana republic like that? If you are asking whether the election management body will move to punish erring staff, yes of course that is being done and more of such measures will be taken in the days ahead. This will be done side by side with the step to try all those who committed electoral offences. As for financial autonomy, that is where Nigerians are not helping themselves much. They, including Labour and others, should be in the forefront of campaign to win financial autonomy for the commission. Nigerians should strive and insist on securing and protecting the electoral commission from sundry assault and political influence.
People have sought to compare Dr. Afari-Gyan of Ghana with Prof., how do you view this comparison?
Both gentlemen are good friends. I know that. We were with Dr. Afari-Gyan few months ago when Ghana was registering voters for the last election. Well, the total number of voters in all of Ghana may be about the population of Lagos State alone, or lower, but no matter. In Ghana they don't have policemen on their street during elections. In Ghana the two main political parties are about equal in size and those who belong to the party that lost one election do not defect to the one that won. They stay and rebuild their party and prepare for the next election. That is how opposition works hard and wins in Ghana, and in serious societies. The man elected president in the last Ghanaian election, John Atta Mills, contested and lost the previous two presidential elections. The immediate past president, John Kuffor, contested and lost two presidential elections before he won the third one. I once spent some time with him in Ghana a year before he won the presidency and I remember he had his policies on his finger tips. He did not just criticise Rawlings then, he had alternative policies of his party. So talking of Afri-Gyan and Prof. Iwu as electoral commission chairman, one is lucky to be presiding over a country with a more disciplined social and political environment. The other person is working more or less in a jungle where people with money and power don't believe they should obey the law. And one more thing, Afari-Gyan has been chairman of Ghana's electoral commission for almost 15 years. Prof. Iwu has been chairman of INEC for four years and some people are mobilising labour, the media, Bar association and maybe market women to get him going before his time. That is their idea of electoral reform that will transform Nigeria overnight.

Original_Search_Title: Utexrwa to Create 6,000 Jobs in Five Years
Headline: Rwanda: Utexrwa to Create 6,000 Jobs in Five Years
Body_Text:
Kigali — The management of Rwanda's only textile company, Usine Textile du Rwanda (Utexrwa), targets to recruit about 6,000 Rwandans in the next five years.
The local employees will be only direct workers spread out in different production units.
Utexrwa's Managing Director, Raj Rajendran, said that the employment target will only be achievable after the implementation of the company's new investment five-year plan.
"Some will be enrolled in silk production, others in banana and pineapple fabrication whereas others will work in treated mosquito nets unit," he said. He added that the number will increase gradually as investments increase over a period of five years.
In the company's five-year plan, $100 million (about Rwf56.2b) will be invested as operation costs and in acquisition of more machinery to supplement on the existing ones.
"This will enable the company to produce quality silk products for exports, mosquito nets, and the proposed banana and pineapple fabric," he says.
Currently, Utexrwa employs about 750 direct workers in different departments of the factory. But this is also expected to increase to 1000 workers, according to management.
The company is about to start producing mosquito nets this year while also adding value to its silk products.
Rajendran revealed that about 4 million mosquito nets are the target for the local market by the end of this year with initial investments worth $6 million.
About 8 million nets are targeted by 2010. Without divulging into details, Rajendran disclosed that the company is negotiating with a German company for enhancement of the required technology in mosquito nets production.
"Once all is set, UTEXRWA will start ITNs production by probably by March or April this year having gotten government's support," he continued.

Original_Search_Title: Akunyili and the 'Re-Branding' of Country - The Limits of Propaganda
Headline: Nigeria: Akunyili and the 'Re-Branding' of Country - The Limits of Propaganda
Body_Text:
The recent announcement by the Minister of Information and Communication, Professor Dora Akunyili, that she has initiated a campaign to "re-brand" Nigeria has, predictably, generated intense controversy.
Press comments have generally been hostile even where they conceded that the professor of pharmacology seems to have proved her mettle as the Director-General of the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC).
A few pundits, however, have vigorously defended the campaign while some newspapers, including Thisday (February 17) and Vanguard (March 2) have offered qualified support.
Akunyili herself epitomizes how spectacular the achievements of propaganda can be - and that, not to put too fine a point to it, is what her re-branding campaign is all about. Before her appointment as NAFDAC's boss eight years ago, there was little public awareness of the regime of food and drug administration in the country. By the time she left NAFDAC last year, the acronym had become a household word.
Nothing better illustrates this phenomenal achievement by Akunyili than a little encounter I had one fine morning with one smart looking child street hawker in Abuja a couple of years ago. I was in my car driving along Michael Okpara Way in Wuse, Abuja, and waiting for the traffic light to turn green close to the T-junction with Harbert Macaulay Way, when the hawker sidled to my window with a large bunch of bananas. I thought he should be in school not on the streets at that hour of the day and wound down the glass to tell him so even though I knew it was a futile thing to say.
The kid soon proved even smarter than he looked; before I could say a word, he raised the bunch to my face and, with a broad, if somewhat mischievous grin, announced that the bananas were "NAFDAC approved". I didn't know when I burst out laughing and, instead of chiding the kid over something that was obviously not his fault, I bought the bunch without even haggling over his price.
This encounter alone was to me evidence enough that Akunyili had done an excellent job in creating public awareness about the proper usage of foods and, of course, drugs in the country. However, with her announcement that she would soon embark on a campaign to re-brand Nigeria, I fear that she is about to become a victim of her propaganda success.
The problem with propaganda is that it almost always leads to self-deception. Akunyili may have succeeded possibly well beyond her wildest imagination in turning NAFDAC into a well-known brand, but the reality of food and drug administration in the country is that her success has been more of image than substance.
The fact is that contrary to the image that NAFDAC under Akunyili has virtually eliminated the phenomena of fake drugs and drug abuse both have hardly experienced any significant decline. In spite of all her efforts, the open and illegal drug markets in the country including the three most notorious ones at Onitsha, Kano and Aba, have never really gone out of business. So also have those who openly hawk prescription drugs on our streets.
In a widely published article late last year by one, Olamide Akintayo, a former Chairman of the Lagos State Branch of the Pharmaceutical Society of Nigeria (PSN), in which he reviewed Akunyili's years as NAFDAC's boss, he showed how some of her policies negated some of the very goals for which it was established.
For example, he showed how the Lagos State PSN in 2002 warned NAFDAC against introducing NAFDAC numbers on product packs because fake drug merchants could easily print such numbers and thus make their products look genuine. NAFDAC ignored the advice. The deaths last year of scores of children that were administered My Pikin teething medicine was a direct consequence of that decision. Yet Akunyili seems to have successfully deflected blame for those avoidable deaths.
When she was first nominated for a ministerial job last year following her departure from NAFDAC, there were widespread expectations that she would get the "lucrative" health portfolio as a natural progression from her apparent success at NAFDAC. Pretty soon, however, it became obvious that it wasn't going to be a cakewalk for her; articles and even paid adverts started appearing in newspapers about which profession in the healthcare delivery system was best suited to head the ministry.
One advert in Thisday of November 21, 2008 credited to "Elders Forum of the Nigeria Medical Association, Lagos State" insinuated, without naming names, that Akunyili not being a medical doctor was not fit to head the health ministry. Predictably, a counter advert appeared two days later in the same newspaper credited to a "Coalition of Concerned Citizens for Better Health". This one argued that in appointing his health minister President Umaru YarÃ¡dua should ignore those who said he should pick only a medical doctor.
As things turned out the president acted contrary to popular expectations. He sent Akunyili to a most unlikely ministry - that of information and communication - for someone regarded as the best thing to have happened to the country's health care delivery in ages.
Akunyili's posting, however, is a paradox. At the same time that posting a pharmacist with no experience in professional media management to oversee government's information department seems a mismatch, her success in creating a positive image of herself contrary to the reality on the ground more than qualified her for her new job.
Herein, however, lies the danger that, like most creatures of media-spin, she may end up a victim of her propaganda success. Having succeeded in creating a very positive image of herself against the reality on the ground, she has obviously come to believe that, given the right price, image can always trump substance.
Otherwise, I cannot see why she would have contemplated re-branding Nigeria - never mind according it priority - at a time like this when the future has never looked so bleak for Nigerians and their country, what with the global economic recession, the widespread insecurity in the land and the almost total breakdown of the country's infrastructure, not to talk of stories of mind-boggling corruption that Nigerians see and hear.
As if to underscore the danger of her belief in image over substance, Akunyili's rebranding campaign seems to have taken off on a very false and shaky start. Late last month, she was at a book launch by UNICEF on the state of the world's children as minister of information and communication. As is usual on such occasions, UNICEF officials reeled out figures of infant and maternal mortality rates of its member-countries. Nigeria, with official maternal mortality rate of 800 per 100,000 births was, not surprisingly, at the bottom rungs.
Akunyili promptly got up to reject those figures. They did not, she said, reflect the reality on the ground. Yet those figures were produced by the National Demographic Health Survey and certified as accurate by the Federal Ministry of Health.
Chances are, far from those figures being an exaggeration of the reality, as Akunyili insisted, they were a gross understatement about how terrible the situation is; hospital based surveys by the Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Nigeria (SOGON) show that in states like Lagos and Kano where maternity visits to hospitals are high, the mortality rates are in the region of 3,500 per 100,000.
If Akunyili would reject government's own figures simply because someone else used them in a way she believed portrayed the country in poor light, it should be obvious that her re-branding campaign would end as an exercise in futility because it would certainly lack credibility.
Like human beings no country in the world is all virtue and no vice or vice-versa. Again like human beings, every country owes itself to emphasize its virtues over its vices. But in a country like ours where the reward and punishment system clearly discourages even basic honesty and hard work, it is the height of self-deception to think our mere say-so, no matter how sweet we make it sound, would convince anyone that Nigeria has become a favourite destination for visit or business.
Akunyili - and her principals - would do better than use our meagre resources in these terribly hard times in pursuit of empty, if not fraudulent, sloganeering.

Original_Search_Title: Police Recover 35 Cartridges Buried Under Banana Tree
Headline: Nigeria: Police Recover 35 Cartridges Buried Under Banana Tree
Body_Text:
Policemen from Ikeja Division, weekend, recovered 35 rounds of live cartridges buried under a banana tree in one of the government quarters in Ikeja.Vanguard, however, gathered that there had been no arrest yet as the residents claimed ignorant. But the police said investigation was on-going.
Report said the policemen who acted on a tip-off, arrived the residential quarters located on Ladoke Akintola Street at about 10 a.m. yesterday, where they swung into action by digging round the banana tree, in the process of which the live cartridges said to have been neatly concealed inside a black polythene bag was discovered.
Confirming the discovery, Police spokesman, Mr Frank Mba, said detectives were closing in on suspects connected with the offence.
In another development, two suspected pipeline vandals were, weekend, arrested by the Marine Police with the recovery of 37 25-litre jerricans filled with petroleum product suspected to have been stolen, along CMS waterfront, Lagos. The suspects, said to have conveyed the product in an outboard engine wooden boat, were arrested at about 9.45 a.m yesterday. The Lagos State Police image-maker, Mr Mba, also confirmed the arrest.

Original_Search_Title: When Railroads Lead to Pubs
Headline: Tanzania: When Railroads Lead to Pubs
Body_Text:
Arusha — With Arusha residents consuming over 2 million litres of beer every month, chances are every road in town should be leading to a drinking pub.
Recent developments however indicate that even railway lines in Arusha have started to lead into pubs; well, a large hanger which used to serve as engine repair workshop at the local locomotive terminal has been converted into an alcohol dispensing bar.
Located on the further end of the railway station near Unga-Limited, the hangar complete with a railroad leading right into it, is now a fully fledged pub and what's more, if recent observations are anything to by, the new bar boasts enough patrons to keep its beer based economy afloat.
A counter located at the garage center and manned by two girls, is fully stocked with all varieties of beers, wines and hard drinks while at one corner a beaming young man registers good business selling grilled steak (Nyama-choma) French fries (Chips) and roast bananas.
Opposite the Nyama-choma station there is a snooker table which is laid out ready for a game (or several) of pool-table while in the further end of the building a room houses another trade, soup and other boiled delicacies, to prepare patrons stomachs for much harder liquid offing at the joint.
A recent visit to the place revealed a number of vehicles parked outside indicating that customers of the new railway pub could either be coming from far-off places.
With no life existing at the station and all offices padlocked, it is still not easy to get railway officials to comment on this or any other related issue.
The Arusha Railway station has been dormant for over two decades now after trains ceased to operate on the route for some reason. Even now that the former Tanzania Railways Corporation has been privatized to the Indian firm 'Rites Limited,' there is no indication that train transport will ever resume in the northern zone.
Last year, during a parliamentary session in Dodoma, the Minister for Communication, Science and Technology, Dr. Shukuru Kawambwa stated that Cargo trains were expected to soon resume services in Arusha, possibly early this year but until now railway lines and the former station premises are still overgrown with shrubs and covered with dust and rust.
Now in the latest development, the most important component of the local railway station, which is the engine repairing hanger, has just been turned into a drinking pub.

Original_Search_Title: Poland - Wild, Wild Hell for Players
Headline: Zimbabwe: Poland - Wild, Wild Hell for Players
Body_Text:
THE Polish league - the soft landing for desperate Zimbabwe footballers is no longer so rosy for local players, who have a share of monkey chants and bananas tossed at them.
Zimbabwe international centre back, Dickson Choto gave details of how he has encountered racism in Poland, in a documentary made by the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC).
The 27-year-old has been turning out for Legia Warsaw, in the last five years, after breaking into the Polish league in 2002. Choto has previously played for the defunct Darryn T Africa, which was owned by Polish coach Wieslaw Grabowski. Several local players are joining the Polish league enmasse.
But the BBC Sports Editor, Mihr Bose said in the documentary: "Racism may never be fully eradicated from football, but what I found during an investigation into the problem in Poland was truly shocking.
"One evening, as myself, my cameraman and producer were going about central Warsaw filming our piece for Inside Sport, we were approached by a skinhead, who said he was a Legia fan and made it clear that he was a racist and keen to broadcast his view.
"As I interviewed him he told me to go back to my country, meaning India, the land of my birth. He would not look me, in the eye as we spoke and at the end of my interview, refused to shake my hand."
Bose said he was made very welcome at Legia Warsaw. But there were areas of the ground he was told he could not go into because of the colour of his skin.
He added that this was confirmed, when he met Dickson Choto, the Zimbabwean international, who plays for Legia Warsaw.
Before the meeting Choto had spent some time watching his teammates training. This could have been a training session anywhere -- people of different colours united by football.
"But, as we walked round the stadium, Choto pointed to a stand where his friends and family were not welcome because of the colour of their skin."
Choto advised Bose that he should not go there as he would not be safe.
Then Choto said he also had his share of monkey chants and bananas being tossed at him. But these days this usually happened more at away matches.
Worse still, Choto said when he reacted to such racism on the field of play, Polish football authorities called him to a disciplinary hearing, where the opposition manager would declare he did not hear the chants, despite these being heard loud enough and clear to be heard by Dickson's wife, who would be at home watching their matches on television.
Bose went to a Legia Warsaw home match at the Polish Army Stadium, where the team fielded black players without any visible problems, although he was not able to go anywhere near the stand, which takes up a whole side of the ground, where the "ultras" gather.
Choto said that Poland was not ready to host Euro 2012.
Although Polish competition organisers accept there is a problem at league level, they say national team supporters do not display racist behaviour, despite photographic evidence suggesting the contrary.
The organisers, however, are confident that 2012 will pass without incident. Legia Warsaw is currently the home of former Monomotapa striker Takesure Chinyama.
Two Zimbabwe internationals, Method Mwanjali and the reigning Soccer Star of the Year Evans Chikwaikwai are undergoing trials at the same club.
Clemence Matawu, the 2006 Soccer King, who is trying his luck at Legia had a change of heart and decided to join TSPBB, in deal brokered by Grabowski.
Herbert Dick had a stint at Legia Warsaw while Leo Kurauzvione and Patrick Daka played in the Polish league. It is however one of the easiest leagues for the local players to break into European football.

Original_Search_Title: Killed for an Apple and a Banana
Headline: South Africa: Killed for an Apple and a Banana
Body_Text:
Two foreign shopkeepers have been killed in their store - for an apple and a banana.
The robbers took the fruit and fled when they could not open the cash register.
The killings bring the number of immigrants murdered in the Western Cape over the past two weeks to 10.
Milnerton police confirmed on Wednesday that the two Ethiopians had been shot dead on Tuesday night while working in their shop on Mnandi Street in Du Noon.
Station spokesperson Daphne Dell said a banana and an apple had been stolen.
Dell ruled out the possibility that the murders had been xenophobic. She said one of the suspects had been armed and had fired several shots at Yonatan Gebreneskel, 22, hitting him in the head and chest.
Awake Ababa, believed to be in his early 20s, had been shot in the back. Both had died at the scene.
Both suspects are still at large.
A reliable source told the Cape Argus the murders were about "business".
"They were hitmen sent to take them out. It's business," he said, alleging that Somalis in the area had been ordered by taxi operators to pay a R14 000 "protection fee".
Last year, after the xenophobic attacks in the Western Cape, Du Noon taxi operators admitted to demanding a protection fee from foreign nationals who ran shops.
The killings follows at least three others involving foreign nationals:
When the Cape Argus visited Du Noon on Wednesday, an eye-witness said: "I heard two shots. When I came over to the shop, I saw a pool of blood.
"One of the bodies was lying close to the fridge with a shot in the head. The other was close to the door with a shot in his back.
"It's all about jealousy. The shop was always neat and people loved to come and buy from them," she said.
She said the victims had re-opened for business only three days earlier, after having closed because of the recent taxi violence, which had resulted in some Somali shops in the area being looted.
"It was not xenophobic nor a robbery. They were not hungry," said Bazil Congo, the victims' landlord.
He said a third man had survived the attack by running away.
"It is just senseless and merciless killing," Congo said.
Another shop owner said: "They were deeply religious, very kind and gentle. They loved to be part of this community, helping where they could."
A tearful Ethiopian shop owner in the area said: "I don't know why they killed my brothers and no one is taking responsibility."

Original_Search_Title: Rebirth of Socialism
Headline: Namibia: Rebirth of Socialism
Body_Text:
"BLIND policies and blind cadres can result in the ruin and desecration of our beautiful natural and human resources. But enlightened debate on national goals and objectives firmly sets us on a highway to a viable and glorious future." Reads a preface to the book 'Towards A Socialist Ethos' by the late Canaan S. Banana, once President of the Republic of Zimbabwe.
Well Banana may be no more but this preface still rings very much true today for his native Zimbabwe then and now, and as much for Namibia now. His words are even instructive now when capitalism is standing before perhaps its sternest test in modern times when the financial markets are tumbling, unleashing a similar chain reaction in the capitalist economies worldwide with the public expected to quietly prop it up without much debate that goes to the very essence of the system how it may have gotten itself in the conundrum in which it find itself today.
The screaming headline of The Namibian column, 'Eye on Africa' last Friday, could not have come at a better time than now when Namibia seems to be gearing up to jump onto this bandwagon of stoking up capitalism, for what we have in Namibia is nothing else than own version of capitalism. Whether for better or worse, it has never been an issue in the Land of the Brave, and for a long time from the look of things never would be an issue, for those at the helm are comfortable in their decadence. It is this very decadence that the public is now being asked to prop up and preserve by coming to the rescue of the ailing capitalists system of which the first signs of its dying throes we may be seeing in the tumbling of the financial markets in the mother/father capitalist nations.
'The Other Face of Capitalism', the Column's headline screamed raising some excitement in some, perhaps prematurely, that eventually capitalism is in the witness box - not that capitalism has not already been indicted. However, the verdict on such indictment has not been proclaimed and for some reasons seems to have been postponed for more than a 100 years. Come 2008, what thinkers like Karl Marx and others had predicted seemed to be coming true, with the collapse of the financial sector in the biggest capitalist metropolises like New York, London, Bonn and Paris. Sooner or later other contraband metropolises, and Windhoek is no exception, are to follow and the signs are already there.
Thus, seeing the screaming headline in question I thought the ideologue that he is Alex Kaure, the writer of the column, would finally tear asunder capitalism and declare its death. Can there be better telling signs than what we started to see at the end of last year? I thought this is what Kaure would delve into but somehow he seemed to be stopping shy of this, only telling us what we all along may have known, the terminal illness of capitalism.
Nevertheless, it is gratifying to see in the 21st Century such rare and endangered socialist inclined species who are not prepared to acquiesce to the old age capitalist propaganda but put matters in their proper perspective. This is what Kaure is exactly doing amid paralysing and conspiratorial quietness that seems to accompany the bid to prop up a system that is meant to die its natural death, as had already been predicted over the decades. Prior to reading "Eye on Africa" last Friday, I had received an interesting quote through the e-mail from a friend in Canada. As much as he has been a close friend in the heydays of the democratic revolution, and thereafter more a socio-cultural friend, actually a brother, I must say I was surprised to see the citation of such a quotation coming from him. Is the old leftist school regrouping that seems to have been comfortably quiet sharing in the goodies of the capitalist order while the workers and their comrades continue to bear the brunt of the decadence of the ruling class?
"Owners of capital will stimulate the working class to buy more and more expensive goods, houses and technology, pushing them to take more and more expensive credits, until debt becomes unbearable. The unpaid debt will lead to the bankruptcy of banks, which will have to be nationalised, and the State will have to take the road which will eventually lead to communism." My friend drew my attention to this all-telling and incisive quote from Karl Marx. This is exactly what I was expecting Kaure to take head-on by telling us how wrong Karl Marx may have been in his analysis in view of what we are seeing today 100 years after, in the collapse of the world financial markets. There is no denying that the Namibian economy is a replicate of these collapsing financial markets. Indeed she has already started to rehearse and imitate some solutions and responses.
However, Namibia does not have any other option but fall in line with her bigger and more important capitalist nations in the true vogue of the quote by Marx above. Yes, the significant capitalists have sneezed and the rest of us must naturally feel the effect thereof. And therein lies the reflective words on Banana's book's preface about lack of enlightened debates. But worse, lack of ideological reflection.
"Ideology charts the path of our revolution. It is as much its forerunner as it is its contemporary, nourishing it all the time and the whole way through so it can have succor and meaning," I dare to quote from President Robert Mugabe yesteryears.
As Namibia feels the vibration of the world economic crisis, one sees socialism being introduced through the back door as the State positions itself to fall in line and provide the necessary stimulus. Towards what end it is as yet undefined and perhaps this may become clear following the indaba the Minister of Trade and Industry convened with the market and other capitalist stakeholders this week. Perhaps the socialism that we have been shying away from for years and that seems to have become taboo to avowed socialists of yesteryears, may at last come back on the agenda? Because capitalism, proving after all what it has been made out to be, is eventually only paving the way to a bigger stake of the people in the economy. Is this not what socialism is all about?

Original_Search_Title: Guber Tussles - After Abia, All Eyes On Imo
Headline: Nigeria: Guber Tussles - After Abia, All Eyes On Imo
Body_Text:
Lagos — With the final verdict delivered by the Court of Appeal, Port Harcourt on the contentious Abia State governorship election, all attentions have now shifted to its sister state, Imo where legal fireworks are still raging over who is the validly elected governor of the state between three contenders for the seat. The litigants are the incumbent governor, Dr. Ikedi Ohakim of the Progressive Peoples Alliance (PPA), Chief Martin Agbaso of the All Progressives Grand Alliance (APGA) and Senator Ifeanyi Araraume of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP).
The dimension the Imo battle has taken seems to indicate that even after ten years of uninterrupted democracy Nigerian politicians are still learning the ropes. Many times they deliberately fail to do many things properly, but instead of learning from past mistakes, they turn around to cajole and intimidate the judiciary in a bid to obtain what they failed to get from the electorate.
It is a settled norm in democracy that the courts are the final arbiter in electoral disputes but politicians often abuse the judiciary by placing before the court, what cannot stand the test of time, even in banana republics.
Two years after the conclusion of the 2007 general elections in Imo State , various defeated candidates have sought refuge in the courts with various claims over who won or who did not win the election. They have succeeded in putting judiciary under so much pressure in their bid to advance their arguments before the learned men of the bench.
This has been the phenomenon across the country, but the case of Imo State appears to be the most interesting. On Wednesday, November 27, 2008, the Court of Appeal sitting in
Port Harcourt adjourned the case between Governor Ikedi Ohakim (PPA) and Senator Ifeanyi Araraume (PDP) indefinitely.
This, according to the court, was to allow a similar appeal by another governorship contender, Martins Agbaso at the Appeal Court in Abuja to be determined.
Agbaso, who contested both the April 14, and 28, 2007 governorship elections in Imo State under the umbrella of APGA is vigorously contesting the cancellation of the former by the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC). This development has since shifted attention of political observers and indeed discerning electorate in the country to the state.
The main issue to be determined by the court is whether having participated in the re-run April 28, 2007 governorship election, the APGA candidate has the right to claim victory in the April 14 election which was cancelled by INEC.
In challenging the cancellation, Agbaso is claiming that he won the April 14 election.
But his opponents are querying him on why he took part in the re-scheduled election, if he was convinced he won the previous one fairly.
Agbaso has however told his opponents that his participation in the second election does not legitimize the cancellation of the first election.
Obviously, the situation will remain so until the matter is finally resolved by the court. This
scenario has however heightened political tension in Imo State and this will remain so until the issues are laid to rest by the final court. However, before the new twist, the election tribunal that sat in Owerri had dismissed Agbaso's petition against INEC and declared Governor Ikedi Ohakim as the winner of the April 28, 2007 governorship election in Imo State .
Another dimension brought to the governorship issue in the state is the action filed by the candidate of the PDP, Ifeanyi Araraume challenging the appeal. At the resumed hearing of the appeal, Agbaso had through his Counsel, Wole Olanipekun, SAN, and Patrick Ikwueto, SAN, sought leave of the court to amend his brief to bring in additional evidence.
However, Counsel for INEC, Adetunji Oyeyipo, SAN, Counsel for Ohakim, Bon Nwakanma, SAN, who led Chris Uche, SAN and Lateef Fagbemi, SAN [who stood for Araraume] had all opposed the application for leave to amend and reminded the court that it had at the last adjourned date ordered that the business of the court at its next sitting was to hear the preliminary objections.
According to Oyeyipo "we believe that the objections ought to have been taken first before the leave to amend because it is not intended to cure the various objections that we have raised; it is necessary the court determines the competence of the appeal first. The position was adopted by Nwakanma and Fagbemi and the Court presided over by Justice Rabiu Danlamin Muhammed upheld their submissions and decided to hear the objections first.
Arguing the objections, Oyeyipo for INEC told the court that "it is common ground that the appellant [Agbaso] had filed an appeal asking the Court to compel INEC to announce the results of the governorship elections conducted in Imo State on April 14th 2007. It is also common ground that he contested the elections of April 28th 2007; he not only contested that elections he went ahead to challenge the results on the same grounds [he contested April 14th] and he lost at the Tribunal and went on to the Court of Appeal Port Harcourt Division and also lost.
"The Court of Appeal Port Harcourt Division discovered that the elections of April 14th was inconclusive, the appellant having exhausted the lines of challenge unsuccessfully came back to revive the appeal which he had abandoned for over one year. The grounds of his appeal are the same with his petition before the tribunal and Court of Appeal Port Harcourt Division and the only way it could be described is as an abuse of court process, the appeal ought to be dismissed because the consequence is disastrous and I urge the court to dismiss the appeal as being incompetent and an abuse of process" he added.
Fagbemi in his submission averred that "the Court of Appeal Port Harcourt Division had in an earlier case involving the appellant made a pronouncement on page 46 of the judgment where it held that the elections of April 14th upon which the appeal was based was inconclusive and not concluded as nobody was announced as the winner.
"If the results were complete, the results would have been announced, the scores of contestants were not announced having been cancelled. His coming to this court was an afterthought as he came out of time. I submit that even if he had come on time, having pursued his appeal at the Court of Appeal Port Harcourt and was defeated, I submit that that defeat is for all times and I urge the court to dismiss the appeal" he added.
Nwankanma for Ohakim adopted the arguments of INEC and Araraume's counsels on the incompetence of the suit and further contended that because the originating summons was filed without compliance with Section 48 of the Court of Appeal rules, that the appeal cannot be heard. He asked the court to dismiss the appeal for being incompetent.
Responding, counsel for Agbaso, Olanipekun told the court that "we are here because of the judgment of Chikere J. of Federal High Court, Abuja of April 30, 2007 and contrary to what was said, appeal was lodged on May 14, 2007.We urge the court to look at the judgment of the Court of Appeal Port Harcourt at page 46 to 59 as referred to by Fagbemi, SAN.
"We want to submit that the judgment held that there has not been a decision on the merit of the case and so it cannot be res judicata. What we are saying without conceding is that this is an abuse of process, it is the latter that can be described as an abuse of the former. I urge the court to dismiss the objections and hear the appeal," he added.
It would be recalled that the Court had on December 11, 2008, granted an application brought by Imo State Governor Ohakim and Ararume which sought to join them as parties in the appeal brought by Agbaso the governorship candidate of the APGA in the state.
Specifically, Agbaso is challenging the decision of an Abuja Federal High Court that struck out his suit challenging the decision of INEC to cancel the April 14, 2007 gubernatorial election results in the state on the grounds that it was fraught with violence and on further grounds that the elections of April 28, 2007 had superseded it.
Also, Agbaso through his counsel, Wole Olanipekun, SAN, and Patrick Ikwueto, SAN, had gone to the lower court asking for a judicial review of the action of INEC which he accused of wrongly cancelling the entire governorship election in Imo State held on the 14th of April 2007.
He is asking the court to determine whether given the facts of the case, the lower court was right in holding that the appellant's action as constituted, inured to election conducted by INEC and the Resident Electoral Commissioner, Imo State cited as 1st and 2nd respondents respectively, on April 28th ,2007 while the appellant's action was still pending.
Agbaso is praying the court for an order allowing the appeal, setting aside the decision of the lower court and for the Court of Appeal to invoke its jurisdiction under section 15 of the Court of Appeal Act 2004 and exercise full jurisdiction over the case and deal with it in the same manner as the trial court would have done.
Or in the alternative, the court was urged to invoke the general powers of the court and remit the suit for hearing before another judge of the Federal High Court with a direction that on the basis of the results already cancelled in 24 out of the 27 Local Government Areas in Imo State, the result of the governorship election held on April 14th 2007 be declared and announced.
Some commentators, lawyers and politicians alike have wondered concerning the merits or demerits of the appeal with some people seeing the present appeal as 'forum shopping' by a defeated politician who does not want to accept defeat and wait for another day.
But the national Chairman of APGA, Chief Victor Umeh, in an interview believes that Agbaso's appeal does not amount to forum shopping but a legitimate attempt to get justice as according to him "Chief Agbaso's appeal pending at the Court of Appeal Abuja should not confuse anybody; we have a lot of newspaper articles that were aimed at distorting the facts and the discerning public. You will recall that in 2007 after the 14th April general elections, the APGA executives converged at our National Secretariat, Abuja and issued a statement condemning the cancellation of the governorship elections in Imo State .
As the nation awaits the verdict of Court of Appeal Abuja on the competence of the suit, there are two things that the court is expected to pronounce on and they are whether a candidate whose matter has already been heard and decided by the Governorship Election petitions tribunal and the Court of Appeal can re-open such a case and whether such a case constitutes an abuse of court process as the issue that were decided on by the Tribunal and Court of Appeal are one and the same.
Either way, whatever is the decision of the Court of Appeal on the competence or otherwise of the suit, it will represent a major precedence in the nation's jurisprudence. For now, all eyes remain focussed on Imo for the long awaited ruling on perhaps, the nation's most complicated case arising from the April 14, 2007 governorship election.

Original_Search_Title: Native Doctor Held Over Collecting Human Blood
Headline: Uganda: Native Doctor Held Over Collecting Human Blood
Body_Text:
Kampala — A man claiming to be a traditional medicine man was on Wednesday arrested for allegedly scooping blood from a scene where two suspected cattle thieves had been lynched.
Moses Kawagga alias Uncle Mooze from Maganjo in Kawempe, was arrested in Matugga with the blood, which he was carrying in fresh banana leaves.
The officer in-charge of Matugga Police Station, Bob Kagarura, said the Police were investigating Kawagga's motive.
Kawagga, in the company of a man only identified as Herbert, told the Police that the blood was for ritual purposes.
Several cases of ritual murders are being investigated by the Police, with the most prominent one being that of Kato Kajubi, a Kampala businessman, accused of paying for the murder of a 12-year-old boy.
The boda boda cyclist, Gonzaga Ssali, who was carrying the two men, was also detained. Herbert escaped from the Police.
The thieves were Douglas Kikomeko, a witchdoctor from Kisowera zone in Kawempe and Dan Mudosi.
Sam Mafabi was rescued by the Police and rushed to Mulago Hospital in a critical condition.
Kagarura said villagers caught Mafabi and Mudosi with two cows suspected to have been stolen from a resident, Jane Kiwanuka, at Kkungu on Tuesday.
The two men later led the villagers to their accomplice, Kikomeko, who was taken from his shrine to Kkungu and beaten to death.
The Police said Kikomeko was wanted by the Police over several cases of theft.
Kawagga denied any connection to the suspects. The bodies were taken to the city mortuary.

Original_Search_Title: Fun Lovers, Event Centres On Valentine Heat
Headline: Nigeria: Fun Lovers, Event Centres On Valentine Heat
Body_Text:
Lagos — Fun lovers are eventually in for the much awaited Valentine Day tomorrow. Just as couples, lovers and relations are upbeat in heralding the day, event stations and entertainment centres are not spared the valentine fever. Significantly most of the outfits spice their menu with varying offers. In Lagos, for instance, Rhythm 93.7FM is offering lovers a mystery valentine. Mystery in the sense that guests don't get to know the venue of the event until the ticket is purchased. This valentine session entitled Hush 4 Lovers will be hosted by veteran comedian, Basketmouth, and compeered by I.K (wild child) while the DJs are Jimmy Jatt and Humility. This event is billed to start by 8p.m.
Also in the city, an unforgettable night of romance awaits crawlers at The Civic Centre, Ozumba Mbadiwe Street, Victoria Island. The audience will be thrown into a night of enchantment in a romantic setting which will include dinner, drinks, live entertainment plus automatic entry into a raffle draw that will ensure a variety of well chosen take home gifts.
As part of its contribution to the socio-economic wellbeing of the nation, the organisers are donating 50 percent of the proceeds generated in the course of this event to selected orphanages. The dress code is formal, Black and White with a touch of red.
For those who crave a little adventure, Tunde and Wunmi Obe's (T.W.O.) Kopykats Entertainment is taking the romantic mood offshore, and inviting them to come and experience a three-hour sleek, sassy sail on the Love Boat as it presents to them Cupid's Ball III 2009. The cruise will be featuring the best of entertainment such as a live music from the Baddest band in da land, Dej vu with guest artistes. Also included is a fashion show with exciting lingerie.
It promises to be a three-hour romantic, fun and exciting evening cruise with dinner, dance and live entertainment on board the prestigious Nire Prest Cruise Liner. The show comes up at 12E Admiralty Way, first roundabout, Lekki Phase 1, Lagos. The red carpet will start from 5p.m. while the boat will set sail at 6p.m. prompt.
Outside the shores of Nigeria, in neighbouring Cotonou, the capital of Benin Republic, former movie maker and director of many Yoruba movies such as ja Ominira, Ajani Ogun, Orun Mooru, Yemoja's Daughter, Black Goddess and a host of others, Dr. Ola Balogun, will be hosting socialites and celebrities to a valentine weekend treat at both the country's former Sheraton Hotel, now Marina Hotel and Du Lac Hotel, where the guests are expected to lodge. The valentine bash, which is strictly for couples, kicks off today, with Ola Balogun's Iroko band, popularly known for its ingenuity and afro-centric music, spoiling the couples to frenzy. Beyond that, the couples will be expected to visit several places of interest in the country.
It will be Love Tones on St. Valentine Day at Gemstone Cafe located at 8, Isaac John Street, GRA, Ikeja, as they invite guests to step into an evening of sensual inspiration, of love and beautiful renditions, music that lifts the soul to amazing elevations, great food in a great atmosphere.
The outfit implores guests to dress in a smart casual to the show, while assuring them of a live music, free styling, mellow atmosphere, recital/spoken word and all they can eat buffet.
For couples, there will be an opportunity to relax and unwind in a romantic atmosphere as Black Butterfly Events holds its first edition of Diamond Ball.
According to the MD, Black Butterfly Events, Glory Adeyemi, the company decided to organise the event to allow couples rekindle the love in their marriage, which because of their busy nature may have grown cold.
The event, which will hold at Welcome Centre, Muritala Mohammed International Airport Road, Lagos, will afford couples opportunity to enjoy themselves without the pressure of the kids.
It is also to pull down the walls that have been built around some marriages in a dinner atmosphere of fun, laughter, music and comedy with Mallam Africa, Emeka Smith, and Chris Eluwa.
As part of the event, couples will partake in the raffle draw and win prizes like, Dana Air tickets, a suit at Welcome Centre Hotels, Chaise Lounge Sofa, and a day at the spa and gym.
The financial institutions are not left out of the valentine heat as Bank PHB in conjunction with Arise, The Global Style and Culture Magazine Out of Africa, will also be renewing their commitment and business relationship to their potential and current pink account holders by presenting to them, The Pink Valentine Ball with Toni Braxton here in Nigeria. The show, which will be holding today and tomorrow, will be staged first at THISDAY Dome, Abuja, while the Lagos leg will be at the MUSON Centre tomorrow, Saturday February 14.
The organisers of the event said all those who opened a pink account before Thursday February12, were automatically entitled to a free ticket into The Pink Valentine Ball show while a former pink account holder who was able to increase his already existing pink account deposit by N20, 000 is also entitled to a chance of winning a free ticket into the show. The dress code for the ball is a touch of pink to this event.
Service personnel are not left out of the mood of the moment. At the Air Mess Club of Sam Ethnan Airforce Base, Ikeja, Lagos, for instance, Red Page Entertainment Media will be staging a Lovers Night Concert tomorrow from 4p.m. till dawn.
The organisers said the concert is meant to leave a lasting impression and as well bring back life to the Nigeria Airforce Base, Ikeja. For them, Valentine is not just about people you love, it is their own way of saying they care, as the key objective of the show is to create an atmosphere of love, leisure and relaxation devoid of seriousness synonymous with the military. It will also be an opportunity to educate Airforce men and their relatives on the scourge of HIV, using the instrumentality of music and art to preach consciousness and unity among the youth.
Part of activities to make the event highly scintillating are a lover's customised stage, 5,000 watts sound system, unique lighting effect, red carpet treatment and many more. Artistes billed to add glamour to the show are D'Ages, Echo, Xlesto Banty, Opstill, J.One, Tonywne, Black Pope while 'I no go die' will be treating the audience to a laugh razzmatazz. For guest appearance, you have Klever J. and Nobert Young. This concert is free, and open to both military personnel and civilians.
Not wanting to be left behind, the management of Lagos Sheraton Hotel is beckoning on its esteemed customers to come and experience joy, companionship, romance, dream, and excitement this Valentine season as it offers them an exquisite extended buffet of delicious appetisers, cold and warm dishes accompanied with live music.
This is in line with the firm's avowed commitment to offer its customers the best that is available in entertainment, refreshments and relaxation in this part of the world.
To ensure their valentine experience lingers for long, they are also making a special offer for couples, which include: special welcome drink, Gift of lover's Rose, Box of special lover's cake, Free glass of Red/White wine at lover's dinner, Free diner for two, Free breakfast for two, and Free entry to the Night Club for complimentary weekend for two couples. Offer is exclusive of all beverages, flat rate, no hidden charges. Best lover's deal in town. Highlighting on the special offer, Fasuyi Funmi, Public Relations Coordinator for Sheraton Lagos Hotel said: "The executive Chef and his team have created a fantastic menu and a welcome drink, which will be served from 6p.m. in the entire restaurant with a candlelight and attentive service crew. The event is another opportunity for guests to enjoy the very best gourmet and treats available from across the world."
Elsewhere, Kelvin Spice, located at 37 Ojodu Berger-Akute Road, Alagbole, Lagos, will be showcasing music and comedy talents on Sunday, February 15, at 4p.m, as it presents to its customers Val Continues Comedy and Musik Groove. For Kelvin Spice even though Saturday, February 14, is the Valentine Day, the groove still continues beyond Saturday.
So, they are inviting their customers, to come celebrate love in its full dosage with Gbenga Adeyinka, as he takes them to an unprecedented level of jokes and laughter alongside some music acts and DJs, Eteye, Eazytee, K-Light, DJ Chris and lots more.
The same Sunday, February 15, the 2009 edition of the annual valentine rendezvous Love Bananas will be staged at the LTV Main Hall, Agidingbi, Ikeja. The show, which will be featuring Buchi and Gordons, the two major acts that performed during its maiden edition in 2007, will also be having a collection of other musicians, comedians and dance groups performing alongside. They include Koffi, Jedi, World to God, Xplicit, Charlo Edge, G-House and the BNG group.
Speaking on the event, Olisaemeka Obi, one of the organisers said that the assemblage of entertainment stars for one event is for the purpose of giving the public maximum satisfaction. "Love Bananas offers families, the young, old, single and married, the opportunity to revitalise relationships, while enjoying exciting comedy, rhythm, and dance. Since the show falls within the weekend, it is focused to make significant contributions, especially for families, singles and young couples who desire a positive and inspiring fun. The show will begin by 4 p.m.

Original_Search_Title: Utexrwa to Invest Rwf56 Billion On Expansion
Headline: Rwanda: Utexrwa to Invest Rwf56 Billion On Expansion
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwanda's sole textile company - UTEXRWA is planning to invest US$100m (Approx Rwf56.2b) in its expansion plan that is expected to span over the next five years.
According to the Managing Director of the company Raj-Rajendran Munyaneza, this amount will be invested in the production of mosquito nets, silk and banana fibre textiles.
"Our current plan is to invest over US$80m (Approx. Rwf45.2b) in mosquito nets, US$5m (Approx. Rwf 2.8b) and US$ 5 (Approx. Rwf 2.8b) in banana fibre textile manufacturing," said Munyaneza.
Utexrwa plans to produce about four million mosquito nets for the local market by the end of this year and to also produce about 8 million mosquito nets in 2010.
Munyaneza stressed that his company has already acquired the technology and that the machines have already been shipped and are expected in Rwanda in about three months.
Detailing the production and market strategy for silk, Munyaneza pointed out that so far Rwanda is leading in the whole region in silk production and the quality of this silk is competitive on the global market.
"Because of the quality of the silk Rwanda produces, we are targeting a big market in the United States, United Kingdom and Canada; we also intend to start our silk exportation towards the end of this year," said Munyaneza.
Utexrwa's silk production process is said to be the first of its kind to be done under one roof.
The silk processing unit incorporates, mulberry cultivation, silk worm rearing, cocoon production, silk reeling, re-reeling, twisting, yarn dyeing, warping, weaving, processing and also produces silk apparels and other products like neck ties and scarves.
These products are sold under the brand name 'Silk Hills'
According to Munyaneza, these silk products are typically 100 percent made in Rwanda and are manufactured from imported semi-automatic reeling, twisting machines, warping and weaving machines, for which the company has invested US $1 million (Approx. Rwf566m) so far.
When asked details of Banana Fibres textile manufacturing, Munyaneza said that so far his company is in partnership with Kigali Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) and Rwanda development Board (RDB) to ensure the production of the Banana fibres.
In a separate development, officials from the Ministry of Trade Commerce and Industry together with a group of consultants from the Policy and Strategy Unit in the President's Office are moving around the country assessing the progress and challenges faced by several industries.
The team which had visited Utexrwa on Friday is expected to come up with an advisory policy document that would guide the government in improving the performance of industries.

Original_Search_Title: How Will Gadaffi Fare At the Helm of the AU?
Headline: Africa: How Will Gadaffi Fare At the Helm of the AU?
Body_Text:
Kampala — Fellow Africans, Another ritual for the African Union ended in Addis Ababa early this week. As expected, outgoing Chairman, Jakaya Kikwete handed over the baton to the incoming Chair, Muammar Gadaffi of Libya.
As usual, Gadaffi's flamboyance and showmanship almost overshadowed the main event; what with traditional rulers from a few countries in the continent hanging on his coattails chanting, "King of Kings! Hail the Chief! Hail Gadaffi"!
Like him or hate him; Gaddafi makes more sense in Africa than a myriad of despots who hardly manage to run the affairs of their countries in the interest of their nationals. At least in Gadaffi's empire; there are no rampant reports of corruption and oil is actually benefitting Libyan nationals even if Gaddafi may be the main beneficiary.
Compared to countries like Nigeria, Sudan and other oil- producing countries in Africa, Gadaffi can be considered a lesser devil when it comes to distribution of national resources. And yes, his generosity transcends Libyan territories. If you are in doubt, ask his disciples in 22 African countries together with his new-found friends in traditional rulers. They have benefitted financially from Gadaffi's humanitarian good neighbourliness.
However, now that Kikwete is out of the AU hot seat, how will Africa judge his performance? Has he moved the continent an inch from where John Kufuor left? Or was his, merely a play-safe-be-good-to-everybody then leave the office? It must be remembered that Kikwete took over the AU chairmanship at a very trying moment in Eastern Africa.
Kenya was immersed in unprecedented political crisis. Darfur in Sudan was still burning despite the AU and UN interventions. Somalia was volatile as usual with a besieged president unable even to use a handful of Ugandan and Ethiopian soldiers to sustain him in power.
Down South, the kleptocratic Bob Mugabe was still clinging to power despite losing the elections. Attempts to force him to share power failed for the better part of Kikwete's chairmanship despite prodding by the African Union, the European Union and the United Nations General Assembly not to mention the United States.
To tell you the truth, there is not much that Africa will remember Jakaya Kikwete for apart from being at the Peace Accord-signing ceremony in Nairobi in April last year following the election fiasco that Kenya went through. Even then, he came more or less as a messenger of super powers to coerce belligerent opponents into striking a deal because time was running out.
Kikwete has left Africa largely as he found it. He ruffled no feathers in Zimbabwe, Sudan or Mauritania. He turned a blind eye to the two coups that took place during his chairmanship. In other words, he was a perfect diplomat at the continental chair.
But perhaps the reason Kikwete behaved the way he did was because he had too many distractions back home in Tanzania. With spiraling corruption scandals popping up now and then; he became a besieged president at home trying to salvage his discredited regime. In so doing, he lost the entire cabinet, his prime minister and central bank governor to corruption.
For this reason, he spent the better part of 2008 not in Dar-es-Salaam running the government but in the regions mending fences with Tanzania's rural communities.
Now that Gadaffi has taken over, what should Africa expect in the next 12 months? I think Africa has reason to be optimistic and expect a lot. If for no other reason except that Gadaffi comes to the office with a clearly stated mission and vision for the continent. He has the vision to obtain a union government for the continent. His dream is to see Africa competing on equal footing with the European Union, the United States , India and China; the four super economic powers when it comes to domestic market self-sustainability.Gadaffi, like all of us, knows that of the 53 states in Africa the majority of them are not self-sustaining. These banana republics need to merge together to share resources and responsibilities to compete on the global arena.
Gadaffi knows that for 53 African states to sustain 53 missions in major world capitals is a waste of scarce resources they hardly have. Yes, one year is too short for Gadaffi to realise his goals but we know in that one year, he will push a defined agendum that he has unveiled to us.

Original_Search_Title: Buganda Khadis Back Mubajje
Headline: Uganda: Buganda Khadis Back Mubajje
Body_Text:
Kampala — SIX Muslim district khadis from Buganda and another from western Uganda yesterday pledged loyalty to the Mufti, Sheik Shaban Ramathan Mubajje.
The Muslims leader also denounced the breakaway group headed by Sheik Zubair Kayongo.
The sheiks vowed to support the Uganda Muslim Supreme Council (UMSC) leadership, arguing that the Kibuli-based rivals were treacherous and had contravened the council constitution.
The khadis pledged their allegiance during a meeting with Mubajje at his office at the UMSC headquarters in Old Kampala.
The khadis were sheiks Ahmad Kiyaga (Mukono), Afane Kawooya (Mubende), Huzairu Kiruta (Masaka) and Habib Mande (Toro).
Others were sheiks Swaibu Kaweesi (Rakai), Bulaim Nkangi (West Buganda covering Mpigi and Wakiso) and Abdul Kinene Mukiibi (Luweero).
Mukiibi, who also doubles as the deputy Mufti, likened the rival group to dry banana leaves that are dead and useless.
Mukiibi boasted that his leadership in Luweero would not allow the rival elements to divide Muslims in the district.
Kaweesi urged Mubajje to stay clam despite continuous provocation by the rival group.
"They have fitina (envy). They have on several occasions prayed for your death. They even went to Mecca to pray that you die. They took you to court but they have not succeeded in any of their endeavours. Please, do not get annoyed," Kaweesi advised.
The tough-talking Mubajje, buoyed by the support from the khadis, warned the rival group against interfering with UMSC work.
Mubajje said he had also received messages from khadis from eastern and northern Uganda pledging their allegiance.
"Muslims in the country are not happy with the Kibuli group's treacherous behaviour and they have their own ways of dealing with such people," Mubajje warned.
Last month, the rival group installed Kayongo as Mufti and Sheikh Abdul-Hakim Ssekimpi as his deputy.
On Tuesday, the Kampala district khadi, Sheikh Suleiman Kasule Ndirangwa and eight Twale (division) leaders in Kampala, disowned Mubajje and defected to the Kibuli breakaway faction.
"He (Ndirangwa) was one of the 30 khadis who attended the General Assembly on January 25 and appended his signature on the resolution that said the group that took me to court had acted against the UMSC rules and regulations," Mubajje disclosed.
Mubajje gave Ndirangwa five days to hand over all UMSC assets and facilities under his care.
During the same function, Musa Kamagu refuted reports that the Katwe Twale leader, Sheikh Ahmed Matovu, had also defected to the Kibuli faction.
Another Imam, Abdur-Salam Mbogo from Ggaba, also distanced himself from the Kibuli group.
UMSC general secretary Eng. Zaid Kavuma, spokesperson Hajji Nsereko Mutumba and former Kampala khadi Sheikh Abdul-Kadir Mbogo attended the meeting at Old Kampala yesterday.
Earlier, Mutumba said Mubajje would replace Ndirangwa, the Kampala district khadi.
Before becoming the district kadhi, Ndirangwa was the imam of Kibuli mosque.
Mutumba explained that the eight Twale leaders who defected to Kibuli were all appointees of Ndirangwa and that they would be replaced once a new district kadhi is appointed.
"The procedure is that a Muslim district council nominates three names (for appointment as district khadi) for the Mufti to pick from. But the Mufti has the right to reject all of them. He also has powers to name an acting district khadi before the district council nominates," Mutumba explained.
He also warned the Kibuli group for threatening to storm the Old Kampala mosque, the UMSC seat. He advised the group not to mistake Mubajje's diplomatic approach for a weakness. "We are ready to defend the Muslim constitution at any cost and we have the mandate of all Muslims of Uganda, not just one tribe. We shall not allow anybody to overturn the leadership of UMSC through a coup," he warned.
If the "rebels' want the leadership of UMSC, he added, they should seek the mandate of Muslims through elections as the constitution says.
"Anything outside of that will not be accepted and shall be resisted with all means possible," Mutumba stressed.
Additional reporting by Cyprian Musoke

Original_Search_Title: Demand for Property Drops in Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Demand for Property Drops in Lagos
Body_Text:
This is not the best of time for owners of properties in highbrow areas of Lagos as prices of houses and land have dropped, while demand for property in the areas also fell in the last three months.
Investigations by Daily Independent revealed that places like Lekki, Banana Island, Ajah, Ikoyi, Ikeja GRA, Ogudu GRA, Victoria Island are worst hit by the development and this is not in the best interest of those who built or bought houses in these areas for commercial purposes.
This, Daily Independent learnt, seems to be the impact of the global financial melt down on the building industry in the country.
It was gathered that the trend was first noticed in Banana Island, Osborne Road, Park View and Ikoyi in October last year has spread to other parts of Lagos.
A standard plot of land, which was between N200 million and N250 million, crashed by 10 percent in October and further dropped to almost 30 percent by the end of the year.
A plot of land in Banana Island, according to Chief Kola Akomolede, a Lagos-based estate surveyor and valuer, is now about N140 million.
He added that a plot of land in Ikoyi, which was valued between N80 million and N100 million during the same period is now N70 million.
Estate surveyor and valuers who spoke to Daily Independent on Tuesday said they are daily receiving requests from interested buyers in these areas but the price they are offering are far beyond the asking price of these properties.
Mr. Segun Madegun, an estate manager and developer, said he was surprised to see that places in Mainland like Ikeja GRA, Magodo, Shangisha and Ogudu GRA are now experiencing the trend that has taking over property transactions in places like Lekki, Ajah and Ikoyi.
"When it was noticed before the end of last year in Lekki and Ikoyi some of us thought it may not get to places in the mainland as fast at it was in these places because of the peculiarities of the property market in these places but events have proved us wrong and the worst is now here for property owners," Madegun stated.
He added that while genuine interests in properties are low compared with what happened this time last year the number of houses and land up for sale in the market are increasing on a daily basis.
Another estate surveyor and valuer, Mr. Adeola Olufon, said the trend will continue because of the fact that houses in these areas are for the high net worth people who are already accommodated but only needed to acquire houses for commercial purposes.
Olufon, who is the principal partner of Adeola Olufon and Company, a Lagos-based firm of estate surveyors and valuers, added that while these areas are experiencing drop in prices of land and houses, the reverse is the case in other parts of Lagos because of the high demand for accommodation by a large number of people who are desirous of decent shelter.
"While the rich will be careful in buying houses in places like Lekki and Ikoyi for commercial purposes, a large number of people who needed accommodation in other parts of Lagos will be scrambling for small accommodation because the global financial situation will not encourage building of more houses," Olufon stressed.

Original_Search_Title: Finance Ministry Predicts Drop in Revenue
Headline: Cameroon: Finance Ministry Predicts Drop in Revenue
Body_Text:
The Ministry of Finance, MINEF, has predicted a drop in revenue this year.The Minister Delegate in charge of the Budget, Pierre Titi, was speaking at the conference of the External and Internal Services of MINEF at the Yaounde Conference Centre, January 29-31.
He said the sharp drop in oil revenue on the international market from US $147 a barrel in July 2008 to US $40 in January 2009, witnessing a 73-percent reduction within a period of five months has been identified as a major challenge.
Besides, prices of prominent Cameroonian export products in the world market such as bananas, aluminium, cocoa, wood, rubber and cotton are equally showing tendencies of decreasing.
"If these tendencies are confirmed, foreign exchange rates in 2009 would deteriorate by 25 percent as against 18 percent in 2008", the Minister said.He said the budgetary difficulties are a consequence of the current global financial crisis with Cameroon not protected.
To Lazare Bela, Director of Economic Affairs, in line with the downward projections of the IMF, Cameroonian exports would sell at 42.5 dollars instead of 60.5 retained in the budget.
He said with the rate of the dollar dropping, oil revenue would record enormous losses of about FCFA 128,000 million compared to the budget voted.
The non-oil revenue is projected at 3.6 against 5.4 in the budget orientation. The revenue projected at FCFA 1535.0 million is revised at FCFA 1480.0 million showing a deficit of FCFA 55 million.
