According to the Director of the Budget, Gilbert Didier Edoa, practical dispositions include quarterly notification of engagement quotas to ministries, freeze votes for the purchase of goods and services, save for exceptions, respect procedures, systematic control of authorising officers, and computerisation of stores.
The Director emphasised limiting the transfer of votes, prohibiting the constitution of budgetary provisions, assets and guarantees, prohibiting the provision of cash and prohibiting the payment of non-assigned expenditure.

Original_Search_Title: Homes Prices Are Falling - Akomolede
Headline: Nigeria: Homes Prices Are Falling - Akomolede
Body_Text:
Lagos-based estate valuer, Kola Akomolede, speaks to CORRESPONDENT, Bamidele Ogunwusi, on the effects of the global financial meltdown on the housing industry in the country. Excerpts:
Some people are of the impression that the global economic meltdown may not affect us here, especially the housing sector. What is your take on this?
There is no way anything that is happening in the global economy will not affect the housing industry in the country. This is so because of the dwindling oil revenue accruing to the country. As a result of this, there will be less money in circulation. You and I know that Nigeria's economy is principally based on government revenue and expenditure. The property market is going to be affected, most especially the high net worth property.
Can you explain this further?
I mean properties in places like Victoria Island, Ikoyi, Osborne Road, Park View, Banana Island and Lekki. Prices of properties in these areas will fall. Before now prices that people were asking for properties in these areas are artificial. For instance, last year a duplex in Lekki was being sold for N140 million, which is about $1 million. But a property in United States for one million dollar will be more superb than the one being sold here in Nigeria. Last year, duplexes were being sold for N500 million and N1 billion. This may not be so this year.
What informed your stand?
This is because the kind of money that was used to buy those kinds of houses are free money and the development we are having now will reduce the circulation of free money that politicians, who have access to it in government used in buying houses in these areas.
While prices of houses in these areas will fall, those in places like the Mainland may not fall. In most cases, prices of houses in the Mainland may increase.
Where are the areas you are talking about in the Mainland? Do they include Apapa GRA and Ikeja GRA?
No. Houses in Apapa GRA and Ikeja GRA are on the same level with houses in Victoria Island, Ikoyi and Lekki. But other paces in the Mainland and other parts of the country may experience increase in prices. The increase may be marginal in most of these areas.
Will the development lead to a drop in construction activities?
There may not be a drop in construction activities. People will spend more in building their houses. In the highbrow areas, increase in cost of construction may not affect the cost of house because there is artificial pricing. People will only pay for location. The fall in prices will be higher than the increase in construction cost. I think we have begun to see a fall in prices of houses in Ikoyi, Victoria Island and Lekki. Just two days ago a client whose land in Lekki was valued at N80 million last year was surprise to see that the land is now N70 million. This trend will continue as long as free money is no longer available. Let me predict that prices of land and houses in these areas will continue to drop.
Why is it that prices of property are falling in United Kingdom and United States on daily basis and we are not witnessing this in Nigeria?
In United Kingdom and United States prices of houses are falling because there are so many houses in the market. Where there are many houses in the market prices are likely going to crash. You know that more than 80 per cent of houses in these countries are through mortgage and because people can no longer pay their mortgage these houses are in the market and there is daily drop in prices.
Since we import major building materials, don't you think their prices will increase due to the development?
Yes. It will greatly affect housing delivery.
While you were mentioning places where prices of house will fall you did not include Abuja and Port Harcourt. Are these areas not going to be affected?
Whatever affects property in Lekki, Victoria Island and Ikoyi will certainly affect Abuja. The only difference there is that artificial pricing of houses in Abuja is not as high as we have in Lagos. Monetisation policy of government has reduced artificial pricing of properties in Abuja. Properties in Abuja are government demand-based. The former Minster of the Federal Capital Territory, Mallam Nasir El-Rufai, sold houses in Abuja excluding the value of land. This was why value of properties in Abuja had already fallen before now.
What of Port Harcourt?
Militants in Niger Delta have helped in reducing the prices of houses in Port Harcourt. House in Port Harcourt had already had its recession. Oil workers no longer live in Port Harcourt. They now live in Lagos and go to work by helicopter and return to Lagos after work. Prices of houses in the area may not fall further than we already have.

Original_Search_Title: Mambokadzi Still Intact, Mashusha
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mambokadzi Still Intact, Mashusha
Body_Text:
TWO Mambokadzi dancers who last year sent tongues wagging in entertainment circles when they fell pregnant have since given birth and are back in action with the dance ensemble.
Mbalitha Ndlovu and Monica Nyapfere were absent from the group for the last half of 2008 and had quit the outfit for good.
But it emerged last week the duo was officially on maternity leave and has since returned to the stage after they were blessed with baby girls.
Mbalitha's daughter is now two months old while Monica gave birth last month.
Mambokadzi director Enisia Mashusha confirmed the duo had rejoined the other dancing queens after completing their "sabbaticals".
"They had not quit the group as some people suggested," she said, "but we had given them leave because they were pregnant.
"We are glad they both successfully gave birth to healthy baby girls and have resumed their duties.
"We are also pleased that their husbands have let them continue dancing unlike in previous cases when some got married and quit the group."
Mambokadzi lost four dancers in 2007 after they got married and abandoned their dancing shoes.
Mashusha said their husbands had discouraged them from continuing with the art forcing the group to recruit new blood.
The group has, however, managed to retain its quality through expert nurturing and hard work, she said.
All but one group member -- Viola Sibanda -- are married and have children.
The other members are Precious Makwame, Hlengiwe Dube, Caroline Moyo and Angie Dube.
In separate interviews last week, the dancers revealed that they were committed to the group, the art and the general development of the industry.
Monica and Mbalitha said they were happy to be back at the group after a long time out of action.
"It feels good to be back with the group," Mbalitha said. "I like my profession so much and it was hard to watch other girls dancing while I sat in the crowd.
"I am glad I am now fit to go down those dancing paces again."
Since its formation in 2004, Mambokadzi has been one of the most popular dance outfits locally and it has also toured the region, leaving lasting impressions in countries like South Africa, Mozambique, Zambia and Botswana.
The group has also been outstanding with its popular Ndotamba video in addition to featuring on Joseph Garakara's two videos, Idya Banana and Simbabwe.

Original_Search_Title: Finance Workers Urged to Eschew Wastagee
Headline: Cameroon: Finance Workers Urged to Eschew Wastagee
Body_Text:
Cameroon will not resist the financial tornado ignited from the United States which is fast transforming into the World economic crisis. The Minister Delegate in Charge of the Budget at the Ministry of Finance sounded the warning bell yesterday in Yaounde while opening the 2009 Annual Conference of officials of the Central and External Services of MINFI.
From every indication, the boss in charge of budget feels disturbed, very disturbed indeed especially as the nefarious effects of the crisis are already at the door steps and require the tightening of the belt in budgetary execution. "Several indices observed already attest to the fact that the effects of the crisis are already felt in Cameroon", he said, pointing to two main indicators.
These, he said, include: the steady drop of oil prices in the world market from 147 dollars in July, 2008 to an average of 40 dollars in January, 2009 or 73 per cent fall in five months and the reduction in the prices and demand for some major commodities such as, timber, banana, cotton, aluminium and rubber. "If the present trends continue for long our balance of trade in 2009 will deteriorate by at least 25 per cent against the 18 per cent appreciation experienced in 2008", Titi said.
These are certainly very bad signs coming in at the time Cameroon has just attained the completion point of the Heavily Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC) Initiative. But this may not be a calamity should Cameroon take pragmatic measures to contain the shocks. It is therefore not by accident that the annual Conference of officials of the Central and External Services of MINFI decided to choose as theme, " Budget Management in the Face of the Crisis".
The risk in the present state of affairs, as stated by Minister Delegate, Pierre Titit, is that public revenue might be affected. "Our role is to put in place all that is necessary to contain the difficulties therein and ensure the normal functioning of the State", he said.
How then will this be done, is the question on many lips? Concrete measures must be taken to ensure a realistic and ambitious management of the budget. Realistic management of State budget in this sense means taking into account available resources, avoiding wastage and adopting productive expenditure. It is a budget that is executed without corruption and embezzlement.
For two days running, participants at the conference will tackle issues such as public expenditure in 2009, expectations of the private sector within the context of global crisis, the strategy of the fiscal administration in the face of the crisis, new modes of financing the economy and communication and partnership as instruments of fighting crisis.

Original_Search_Title: A Strategy for Change - Maina Kiai speaks to Kwamchetsi Makokha
Headline: Kenya: A Strategy for Change - Maina Kiai speaks to Kwamchetsi Makokha
Body_Text:
Maina Kiai, a former chair of the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights, was a driving force behind Kenyans for Peace, Truth and Justice (KPTJ), formed in the aftermath of the 2007 election debacle. In an interview with Kwamchetsi Makokha, he takes stock of the year gone by.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: Are you happy with what Kenya got in setting up the coalition government last year?
Maina Kiai: We wanted a limited coalition based on an understanding of the players. We wanted a limited time span for the transitional coalition government. The idea was very clear in our mind that if it was open-ended; you would lose the energy to reform, because people would begin playing politics with everything else. We wanted a maximum of two years - to put these guys in a room and say to them: 'You must reform, and you only have two years.'
So it was a huge disappointment when the coalition period became open-ended. It was the beginning of the end for reform. We all saw it when the cabinet was formed. The [negotiating] team stopped meeting at Serena [Hotel, venue of the mediation process]. There was no more interest. They got what they wanted and it was a huge setback. We had no control over it.
The second indicator was when [President Mwai] Kibaki and [Prime Minister] Raila [Odinga] rejected a small lean Cabinet despite the fact that everybody, including their own supporters, wanted one... Again, the message was very clear: they are here to eat, and it is about replenishing stocks for the next competition, which is in 2012. So it is not surprising to hear about the scandals in government. In fact, I believe that if we could ask an internationally reputable investigator to come and look at the books of every single ministry in three months, you would find more scams than we are seeing now - and it cuts across both sides.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: What do you think KPTJ achieved last year?
Maina Kiai: There is nothing as effective as organic movements, and KPTJ was completely organic. Nobody planned it. We all gathered to try to figure out what to do. Even at the first meetings, it was clear we were all for peace but we were also not naïve to think that calm is the same as peace. Calm without truth and justice would only lead to redoubling of the issues that caused the fighting in the first place. Yes, we got a lot of flak from people saying, 'Why aren't you focusing on peace?' We focused on peace but we understood that if we wanted to have real peace, then we could only have it through truth and justice.
Everybody came out and made a big difference because we were clearly leading the only group that seemed to address every facet of the crisis. Some groups were trying to meet for peace or calm - that was driven, in my view, by a desire for money from the donors. Even the donor community was so interested in calm that they forgot that you cannot have calm without peace. Unless we address these things, our agenda is not finished.
KPTJ reenergised civil society in the country. It had been struggling to find its feet but now, civil society is reawakening in a way that most of us are happy with. The comments about civil society being dead or collapsing are not heard any more. From the taxation of members of parliament to fuel prices, all the activities that have happened since have more ordinary people speaking up and coming out. It rejuvenated the sense of empowerment in the country. One of the things this country needs is for Kenyans to stand up, speak to power, and not be intimidated by it. I think KPTJ showed that very well and it is an important contribution. You cannot measure it in milestones and indicators, but its impact has been apparent over the course of the year.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: What strengths did KPTJ bring to the crisis last year?
Maina Kiai: KPTJ was good in analysing issues and presenting fact-based evidence. When KPTJ released its election data, it made a huge impact because it became clear that something was wrong. It was KPTJ's work that has led to Kriegler's work being questioned: you cannot convince anybody that you do not have evidence of rigging at [the tallying centre]. He did not bother to look for evidence. Of course, you will not have evidence if you do not bother to look for it.
KPTJ put issues in a manner that made people understand what was at stake. Of course, those clinging to power saw KPTJ as working for the other side, but I can tell you for a fact that even ODM was not happy because we were not saying they should take over the government either.
KPTJ was very clear - it was not about ODM [Orange Democratic Movement] or PNU [Party of National Unity], it was about us Kenyans and so the solutions we put forward were in terms of a 50-50 deal. So you end up in a place where you have to take a principled stand and you are accused because someone's interests are harmed by a principled stand. It is like apartheid. You cannot say 'I don't like apartheid but I am not against it either.' I am sorry; you have to be against it.
Civil society must be very clear that never should it be in a position where it actually feels that it needs to be neutral. You cannot be neutral about evil or wrongdoing. The consequences of that are that once you are firm against wrongdoing, those who are doing wrong will see you as an enemy.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: The American ambassador endorsed the results at first. What do you think happened to change his view?
Maina Kiai: I think the way it blew up shocked a lot of people. For whatever reasons, not even the international human rights organisations had anticipated what came to happen. I think in a sense the country and the world had been lulled to sleep by the 2002 elections and the referendum in 2005. We did those fairly and peacefully but fell asleep and imagined that we would do the 2007 elections. But the signs were clear.
I was particularly pleased to see [American ambassador Michael] Ranneberger turning around because what he did was clearly unconscionable and wrong - more so because he was in possession of the exit poll results. He could at least have been equivocal, but in his case, he was very categorical. I am not sure if that was an agenda from him or from the US government. I think it still needs to be interrogated. The fact that as Kenyans we stood up to the American ambassador and said it does not matter - we will take you on - on principle, on what is right and wrong, that was very important.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: What were KPTJ's shortcomings in the past year?
Maina Kiai: We are best in a crisis, for sure, but we are not as well organised as we would want to be. We do not fit together adequately and I think there are things that hopefully between now and the next few years we can reform. Maybe we can reorganise properly. It is a lot of hard, dirty work. It is unsung; it is unheralded and it is quiet, but we have to get going. There are many disparate movements today that must be brought together, otherwise our impact will never be felt the way it is supposed to be. Right now, if you look at the past year, in a sense it has opened everybody's eyes about the real nature of the political class. There is anger and frustration out there. If it is not channelled into a social movement that people believe in and can see will lead to something, then the alternative is more of the same - if not worse.
Our work has barely started. The work we did in 2008 was barely the beginning of the hard work that lies ahead. We have to be organised; it is a difficult and enormous task and you have to have the time, the skills, the courage and the funds to organise.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: Are there any lessons from last year's engagements?
Maina Kiai: I think the international linkages that KPTJ engaged were critical. The backdoor channels KPTJ opened with the Kofi Annan group were critical too. But the lesson is that not just one approach is sufficient. It is not just press conferences; it is also the back channels no one hears about. It is about keeping doors open, opening up to ideas. One critical lesson is that if you keep to this work, you open yourself to receiving information you would otherwise not get. That is a wonderful thing.
The only thing we did not plan for was the security situation - that people could go to jail, the frustration that the state would find KPTJ a threat. I have done human rights work since the 1990s throughout [Daniel arap] Moi's years and you always felt at any time, you could be either be shot or arrested. After the 2002 elections, I felt that that era had gone. One of the things I said about Kibaki's tenure was that finally I could work in peace without having to watch my back or worrying about security, being shot or arrested.
The year 2008 brought back the same feelings of Moi, of worry about what is going to happen. You had to be careful and because of the stakes there, I think the level of threat was way higher. One of the lessons we have to take at a broader level is we have to spend more time understanding the personalities of those we are stacked up against - the values that we push because a lot of the issues in this country, whether security or politics, are really about economics and are personality driven.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: What has been the cost?
Maina Kiai: Among civil society, differences have emerged based on ethnicity, which I think is good. I think it is important that we know who we are. For many people who were speaking out, it shook a lot of our relationships with each other, and with our relatives and friends. That is part of the price you pay when you stand up in very heated circumstances.
But of all the costs, the security cost was the highest. Even at a monetary level. We added extra security and began to do things we never did before. So there is a financial price to pay and it also limits your independence. You start limiting your time. You cannot hold meetings in the evenings because you do not know what is going to happen after that.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: You were a target of numerous death threats. What was going through your mind?
Maina Kiai: You know, you do not do this work to be a martyr. Anybody doing this work wants to live as long as possible. I cannot pretend there was no fear. Fear is a good emotion. You take fewer risks and you are not reckless. But what is going through your mind is, 'Have things come to this, to the extent to want to kill me? What is it? What is holding them?'
When they target people like you, that it reveals the extent of the problem. It must be very huge, and you have touched a raw nerve.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: Why you?
Maina Kiai: Clearly, I was being targeted for three reasons. For a while, I was the face of KPTJ. Secondly, I am a Kikuyu and there is a sense that Kikuyus must stick together because we have to. Thirdly, I was a public servant and there is a sense among the political class that public service means sycophancy.
I also knew that I had irritated them for a long time and clearly that is an important thing to do because those irritations were having an impact. It felt good that our work was having an impact. I am clear about my work though. I do not want to be taken out. This is not about dying, it is not about heroism either. You need to be alive to fight another day. I believe that you are more effective when you are strategic rather than reckless.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: How do you assess the implementation of the long-term issues of the mediation process?
Maina Kiai: The politicians got what they wanted. Part of the weakness of this is that the structure brought up by Annan gave no opportunity for non-state actors to carry the people's agenda to the table. It was clearly between two protagonists - it is the way it works when you have a conflict. The structures he built? When he left, the balloon deflated. It is a pity that the people he chose to take over from him have not been able to hold the attention of the political class. There was no timeline and no threat for failing to adhere to it.
It would have been good to have a timeline, so that if it does not work, something else happens. Once they formed the coalition, that was it. The good thing for us is, looking at the report of the Commission of Inquiry into the post-election violence, Judge Waki wrote his report in a way that forces action. Because of him, even the Kriegler report had to be taken seriously.
If he had done a nondescript report, we would have forgotten it and moved on. He also has the benefit of history. Had this been before the International Criminal Court, there would have been nowhere to go. It helps to have someplace larger than the country. It took control away from political hands. A lot of the anger is that you can't influence, you can't bribe them. It was interesting... lots of wananchi would like to skip all these and go to The Hague. What it tells you is the utter lack of faith in institutions. For me, 2008 said we have no institutions in this country. ECK collapsed, the judiciary collapsed, the police, name it. We had paper institutions and 2008 was a good slap in the face to remind us that we are banana republic - we speak good English and have a good coast - but we are a banana republic.
What needs to happen?
A number of things have to happen for us to approach the next elections with confidence. One is [implementing the recommendations of] the Waki Report. Impunity must end and the political class must understand finally that there is a price to pay and it is a serious price. We are not looking for a thousand people to prosecute; we are looking for the key players to understand that this is it. The 'good' thing about the Waki Report is that it touches people across the political divide, which is great for us because there is no victim, and there is no vanquished. So if we do not do the recommendations in the Waki Report and do them right, we are inviting disaster in 2012.
Secondly, the electoral boundaries commission and the independent electoral commission and how they are formed or work will be critical. It is not enough to bring names into the electoral commission that have served in places. This country does not have confidence that there are people who are strong, independent and competent. The big trigger for our violence was the elections, so you must make sure that process does not invite any doubt whatsoever. The process and the people responsible must have competence, integrity, independence and strength of character. I am not sure if we have these people yet.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: What do you think should happen to the dichotomy of vision in civil society?
Maina Kiai: The beauty about KPTJ was its organic structure. The people and the organisations came together on their own and agreed on the agenda. I think that in a sense a similar effort has to come up where people say why are we doing these one-agenda things. Where can we go with it and what is going to be the result? We also need to look at some people proactively going out to these movements to bring them together. Some things happen organically, others have to be initiated.
The people to do so must have the time, space, momentum and energy, come from across the ethnic divide, and show a national face. This group needs to be composed of completely new people, people who have never been politicians saying, 'We are here and this is the face of alternative leadership in Kenya.' We can then raise the standards and raise the stakes of what we get.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: What obstacles or opportunities do you see ahead?
Maina Kiai: The openings are stark and the country is ready for change. Obama's impact is unbelievable in this country. What he did was to demonstrate that anything is possible. He has made a huge impact on the world.
There is anger with the political class. I am no longer at the level of reform. I am at the level of change, which is much more radical than reform. We want to get rid of what is there and put in new things.
The biggest obstacle is ourselves, the limitation of our own possibilities. If we can release and open that up, we will be fine. Given the people, and the time, focus, energy, resources and the organisation, this country will change.
It is about having a long-term agenda. We have to stop this business of making compromises and choosing the lesser evil. Let's have a complete change.
Kwamchetsi Makokha: How should civil society navigate the difficult relationship with politicians?
Maina Kiai: You need to have relationships with the political class because it is a reality. But you have to always be on guard and to understand. It is easy to push for alliances. We can have a coming together of interests, but we are not allies.
* Maina Kiai is the former chair of the Kenya National Commission on Human Rights.
* A progressive writer, journalist and communications consultant, Kwamchetsi Makokha is the founder of Form and Content Consultancy, and a member of KPTJ's steering group. He can be reached at kwamchetsi@nbnet.co.ke.
* Please send comments to editor@pambazuka.org or comment online at http://www.pambazuka.org/.

Original_Search_Title: Numbness As a Blank Cheque
Headline: Nigeria: Numbness As a Blank Cheque
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a recent interview with THISDAY, former United Nations Secretary-General, Kofi Annan, expressed concern about the passive attitude of Africans to leadership. He said, "There have been too much complacency and the people have been too patient- they accept all sorts of things they should not accept from governments and institutions."
In Nigeria, it is not difficult to appreciate that we are indeed living in trying times. While the country sickens for lack of purposeful leadership at many levels, the people seem to have lost their humanity. That may account for the general passivity and numbness in the country at the moment. The old image of a country where people stand up for what is right is gone. In the market place, in the street, at home and in public offices ethics have been thrown overboard as many indulge in cheating and many unfair deals to make money at all cost.
Gradually, oddities, immorality, mediocrity and shameful public behaviour have been arrogated as statecraft in Nigeria. Being in political power is now generally conceived as an avenue to loot the treasury. And that is why any political office holder who quits power without embezzling public funds is regarded by his compatriots as a big failure. We applaud mediocrity and despise meritocracy. The nouveau riche and upstarts in our midst, who acquire wealth through questionable means, are publicly applauded and given titles. Nothing is hateful or repugnant to us anymore. We live in a dirty environment without any qualms. We habitually litter our streets with orange and banana peels and pure water sachets without any feeling of guilt.
We have equally lost our sense of shame. We fight, urinate and defecate in the streets and public squares without any shame. Our primary and secondary health care systems have collapsed and nobody gives a hoot. Last year alone, many sick Nigerians fled to India in search of a better medical treatment. Most of the country's roads are no longer motorable. The transcendent perception of our humanity has certainly waned. It is probably only in Nigeria that a decomposed dead human body could be lying in the middle of the street and no passer-by expresses any human sympathy.
Faith in the country seems to be on the decline, particularly on the part of poor and unemployed who feel a sense of disappointment by the country. Because we have lost faith in our public schools, we have allowed many of them to deteriorate irredeemably. As alternative, most parents now compete to enrol their children in very expensive private schools.
In the past we celebrated real excellence and achievement of a responsible and morally-upright government. Today we celebrate the under-achievement of morally-bankrupt politicians. We have become so used to non-performance, systematic collapse and failure in government that any small thing done by any government institution is applauded. For example, when electric supply is restored after many days of blackout, people in the neighbourhood roar, "Up NEPA" as if they are being done a favour. Their right to regular electric supply has been long forgotten.
These tragic situations cry out for immediate remedy. In an age when most people in the world are coming together to tackle pressing problems, we cannot afford to remain in our cocoons, in our passivity and loss of faith in Nigeria. Agreed, this attitude is engendered by bad leadership and corruption that have been ravaging Nigeria for long, but taking it as if it were one's fate to suffer the inefficiency of the system is not a solution.
Nothing would be gained by losing our humanity. The revival of Nigeria must begin with the regaining of our humanity. Therefore, let corruption be exposed no matter whose ox is gored. Let us stand up for what is right. Let shameful public behaviour be condemned. Let us shun immorality. Let us stop celebrating under-achievement. That is the only way to move Nigeria forward.

Original_Search_Title: Junior Lions Bundles Out Flying Eagles
Headline: Nigeria: Junior Lions Bundles Out Flying Eagles
Body_Text:
Cameroon yesterday defeated Nigeria 2-0 in one of the semi-final matches at the ongoing African Youth Championship in Kigali to book a place in the final against Ghana on Saturday.
Earlier in the first match of the day, Ghana defeated South Africa 4-3 to reach the final of the tournament.
It was a sad day for the Flying Eagles, whose skipper Lukman Haruna also floundered a penalty kick awarded to his side by Zambina referee Kaoma Wellington that would have reduced the goal margin.
Zoua Daogari Jacques tapped in a cross from Ekeng Ekeng Patrick Claude in the 31st minute to put his country in the lead, before a mix-up between goalkeeper Dele Ajiboye and Abdulkarim Lukman from Tiko Messina resulted in Cameroon's second goal.
The match played at the Rwanda's National Stadium in Kigali, started on even note with Nigeria and Cameroon making frantic effort to take the lead.
The opener did not come until in the 31st minute; when Zoua Daogari Jacques, combining beautifully well with Ekeng Patrick Claude found the back of the net for his fatherland.
At the closing stages of the first half, Rabiu Ibrahim and Haruna Abdulkarim lost opportunities to draw level for Nigeria, shooting wide from the sixteenth yard box.
On resumption, Origpya Edile, Chrisantus Macauley and Rabiu Ibrahim made spirited efforts to level scores, but the Cameroonian defence manned by Yaya Banana, Enow Juvette and Fomw Charley stood firm to ward off attacking forays from the Flying Eagles.
As the clock was ticking away, Tiko Mesina broke lose on the right wing and slotted a piercing pass in the Nigerian box that was kicked in by Abdulkarin in an effort to pass the ball to Ajiboye in goal for Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Police Destroy Illicit Drugs Worth Over Rwf5 Million
Headline: Rwanda: Police Destroy Illicit Drugs Worth Over Rwf5 Million
Body_Text:
Kigali — Police in Nyarugenge yesterday destroyed illegal hard drugs and alcohol worth over five million Francs that had been seized in recent operations as they were being sneaked into the country through neighbouring countries; the DR Congo and Uganda.
"We are yet to talk to our neighbours to tighten the law on drug trafficking. If it is legal to consume drugs in those countries, it is illegal here and any time we get them they will be destroyed," Chief Supt Emmanuel Butera, director of Community policing said.
The drugs which include 50,000 sticks of marijuana, quantities of local toxic alcoholic beverage popularly known as Kanyanga and over one thousand sachets of Chief Waragi, were confiscated through separate operations by the department of community policing and local authorities.
They were set ablaze in an effort to curb crime which in most cases is motivated by drug abuse by idlers most of whom are youth.
"RNP calls upon all Rwandans to come together in fighting against dealers and consumers of drugs. Their effect is so dangerous that it affects and damages the brain," said Butera.
He emphasized the role of the Community Policing Committees (CPC) and the local population especially people living at the boarders in ensuring reduction of crime in an effort to create a crime-free Rwanda for all.
"People mostly those living near border towns of Gisenyi (Western Province) and Rusumo (in the Eastern Province) should spearhead the struggle because there are the main routes where the drugs get into the country," Butera appealed.
Police spokesperson, Willy Marcel Higiro, called for continued collective efforts between the people and the police as a public institution.
"Talking about drugs does not sound new to Rwandans especially Kigali city residents and other major towns of the country but together this will soon be history," he said.
According to Butera, 147 people were arrested in connection with drug trafficking last year.
In a related development, today police in Remera is scheduled to destroy a truckload of locally brewed beer from bananas locally known as urwarwa.

Original_Search_Title: Obama - Change of Horses, Not Hearts
Headline: Uganda: Obama - Change of Horses, Not Hearts
Body_Text:
In an overcast pitch-dark night, the flash of ray of a glow-worm radiates like it was an incandescent sunlight.
There was always a great trepidation amongst us the youngest lads if we were required to step out from the light of the kerosene wick in our home-hut into gloomy moonless night, unless we were escorted by some older person. By then, we had no recourse to cell torches to beam our way. We had to depend entirely on the sensations of our feet to grope about the village paths where the ever vigilant toads and frogs croaked malevolently amongst themselves in what appeared to be a conspiracy against us.
It was once when we were only too happy to troop out from the main living room during a dark evening hour. Our grandmother had prepared a mouth-watering goat-head dish in a cooking pot, sealed with a thatch of fresh banana leaves, which we were eager to eat for supper. So, we were full of gusto as we collected the supper items from the kitchen for the much awaited meal to be served.
In our final round of ferrying things, we were surprised to find that the pot which was to star in our meal was conspicuously not in place from where it had been resting as it cooled by the hearth. We detected, by the flicker of the dying embers, a silhouetted pot that was miraculously drawing away from us into the darkness beyond the open patch of the crumbling mud wall.
We found ourselves freed of our fear of darkness as we quickly spread out to grab the prized pot which was writhing and swinging away from us. We stumbled and fell into the surrounding potato mounds, blindly tracing the direction of the queer disappearance of the pot.
Thanks to a fluster of flying glow-worms, the reason for the strange apparition soon loomed to us. A village dog had tucked its head into the pot of our goat- head dish, and in its yelp to slip out from the steaming stuff, it had frantically rolled and whirled itself, succeeding to crack open the bottom of the cooking pot. This saved its own head from being further scalded, enabling it to flee, while pilloried by the fragment of the pot that remained embedded around its neck. We were left forlorn and down-cast for missing our hungrily expected feasting. This amazing slick of events is not limited to village occurrences alone. Even in the world of politics, the fondest dreams often go sour, especially if they are not founded on sensible premises.
For a long time now, the world people have been nursing a growing yearning for a new order which would usher in the bliss of eternal peace and universal prosperity to all mankind. In Africa, years of despair from its failed regimes have combined with the ravishing control by the rich countries to wreck continuing hopelessness and helplessness of the continent.
Out of this bleak situation has emerged President Barrack Obama, glowing with the promise of a new way of doing things. The USA, hitherto a bastion of racism against the black people, became transfixed into accepting the son of a Kenyan Luo to occupy the highest office of the land. This is extolled as historic turning point of US politics.
Across our continent, there has been a frenzy of celebrations. In Kenya, in particular, Obama's ascendancy has been welcomed as heralding a power change, marked by officially declaring public holidays in the country. No one had time to remember that the last time Kenyans went to the polls to elect their own president, it simmered into a blistering violent acrimony nationwide. Throughout Africa, it is the black presidents themselves who are keeping our countries in painful tears.
It is easily forgotten that, whatever is the political symbolism and rhetoric, Obama has been sworn in to be the President of the United States of America and to pursue its agenda. The election of Obama was intended only as a change of horses, not a change of heart.
The belief that out there is an Obama Presidency, whose job is to provide prospects to satisfy the needs of black Africa is a mirage of a lake in the desert, as devious as the cooking pot that was stolen by the village dog from our grandmother's kitchen.
Indeed, Obama's rise to power boosts the psychological confidence of the black people, and other deprived groups, about the legitimacy of the desire to build a democratic sheen to change power relations.
But, as we savour the implications of this psychological victory, there should be no illusions: our fate as Africans will not be determined from Washington, but the internal organization and strengths that we can marshal for ourselves in building the independence and dignity of our people. It is the attitude that we instill for our well-being and democracy that will give Africa its prosperity and glory.
A glow worm, however useful it might be during dark period, cannot claim the place of the omnipotence of the sun's day-light.
James Magode Ikuya The author is a member of NEC (NRM) representing historicals

Original_Search_Title: An Agenda for New Aviation Minister
Headline: Nigeria: An Agenda for New Aviation Minister
Body_Text:
The recent overhaul of the Federal cabinet by President Umaru Yar'Adua is commendable. The president said he did this to reposition the government for better articulation and execution of his seven -point agenda.
This is a welcome development as this is indicative that Mr. President expected better handling of the nation's affairs from the old team but met with disappointment. The president did not say they had totally failed but he perhaps delighted to see more successes recorded by the team.
The step taken by the president is to strengthen his grip on the affairs of the nation hence the calibre of the people he preferred this time around.
The Ministry of Aviation is very key to the economic development of the nation. In many ways the Aviation Ministry can make or mar the country's economic emancipation.
In this era of the global village, when investors are looking every day for new grounds to break, the Aviation Ministry can be a catalyst to move the nation forward. This is why the president needs to be commended for his choice of Mr. Babatunde Omotoba as the new minster for Aviation. Although those close to the ministry and the type of politics going on therein may want to say that someone like the new minister may not be able to cope, but I hasten to say that the age of Methuselah has nothing to do with the wisdom of Solomon.
It is usual in the sector to condemn anybody that is chosen to head this ministry each time such appointment is made. The common refrain is that such a choice is not a professional or better still not an aviator. For those who may not know, the new minister is a qualified civil engineer and at the same time a qualified chartered accountant. A first class candidate and a cool-headed young man. Morning, they say, shows the day.
It is sure by now he must have heard a lot, seen a lot, felt a lot and even experienced a lot concerning this all-important ministry. But I have been dazzled by the native intelligence he has shown so far. No leaping before you look. No putting the cart before the horse. No rash comments about the nature of things as they are.
No sweeping condemnation of his predecessors in office. No policy statements that will turn logic on its head. No fearful appearances to cow subordinates into submission. No threats of bringing down the roof over the heads of everybody in the ministry especially the agencies in the ministry. First things first.
What we have seen are slow but sure steps towards reviving an industry that has gone comatose courtesy of a myrad of man-made problems. We do not need to remind ourselves of the tragedies of the past as far as Aviation is concerned. The agony of such opening and the effect will be too hard to bear. We can't be talking of the open sores we call airports that have been crippled by negligence and a desperate effort to sell them off as scraps.What we can do is to help the Honourable Minister in prayers and in altruistic actions and admonitions that will bring smile to the faces of the traveling public.
To succeed and to gain the confidence of Nigerians and foreigners alike, the Minister must be above board. He must not give in to the whims and caprices of the politicians in government whose only interest is swelling their bank accounts at the detriment of the infratructure and facilities at the nation's airports. We have seen enough anguish caused by what the aviators themselves called 'cutting corners'. The minister must stand for the truth in all things. This I know is in short supply in this clime and especially in the Aviation ministry, but the Minister must eschew tendentious statements and actions. He will see them aplenty but he must get ready to be able to read between the lines.
It is good thinking that he has called for the books of the agencies in the ministry. Not that he can see it all but at least he may see 'something'. Those he will be working with are what we call in common parlance 'ogbologbo'. Shrewd, clever and daring civil servants. He must be careful of the notorious banana peel. It is not only in the hallowed chambers of the legislature that it exists! The easiest way to get the praises of Nigerians is performance. The airports must work again. I mean the facilities at the airports must be overhauled, total overhaul. Runways, toilets, conveyor belts, fire tenders, water hydrants, power or energy, car parks, everything must get new and proper attention. The system by which airports do not get money to maintain these facilities for six seven months should be stopped. Paying contractors should not be more important than fixing these gateways to our country. The Honourable Minster will be doing us great good if he can be different. At least, if he can sustain the no- accident regime, the crave for new and airworthy aircraft and giving the 21 airports across the country a facelift, his name will be etched in gold.
Olododo wrote in from Unity Estate, Iyesi Ota, Ogun State

Original_Search_Title: Our Negative Value System is Breeding Misfits
Headline: Nigeria: Our Negative Value System is Breeding Misfits
Body_Text:
Poor education is about the most outstanding factor militating against the emergence of a sane Nigerian society capable of sustaining itself. Our failure at maintaining the virile educational system that the colonialists left behind is one of the major failures of leadership. Education can be said to be the cultivation of moral and intellectual faculties. It is the acquisition of skills and abilities, as well as sound values and virtuous moral principles.
An organised school is a necessity for the survival and development of any nation or group. Any country that lacks the functional capacity to educate its young is a failed state. Our school system, if it can be called that, has packed up completely. What is functioning now does not deserve to be called a system. Schools, to the best of my knowledge, are designed to produce functional human beings in multi-disciplines relevant to society. It is supposed to confer on the recipients the ability to use knowledge so acquired for creative endeavours and as a vehicle for further acquisition of knowledge, ability and capacity. Today what do you have? You have primary school leavers who cannot read, secondary school leavers who cannot write, and university graduates who have little or no understanding of the disciplines in which they hold degrees not to talk of knowledge of areas besides theirs.
Education, though, does not start and end with mere literacy. An illiterate man, by this understanding, may be educated while the literate one can be said to be uneducated. Do not forget that literacy merely is the ability to read and write. So far as a person is capable of learning, whether through the spoken word or the written script, that person is capable of being educated. The literate therefore have no monopoly on education, but they do monopolize the ability to read and write. However, that intelligent but illiterate person will be more functional if he acquires the ability to read and write, since this will open up the world's knowledge to him. The desirability of literacy is that it is a vehicle of greater education, emancipation and ability.
Having defined education as the cultivation of intellectual ability and moral value, to what extent then can the sort peddled in our country today be said to be achieving these dual goals? How well is the Nigerian school set-up conferring even mere literacy on our kids? Today, the entire educational set-up in our country is gone bananas. The formal educational structure has decayed rather badly, not to talk of the informal learning and parenting that goes on in homes! To worsen matters, most Nigerian kids are brought up by absentee parents.
The school teachers are hardly paid on time. Real books (not half-baked hand-outs) are not available and, when obtainable, are not affordable. Even the teachers themselves are in dire need of professional training so as to improve their capacity at service delivery. Our schools have lost pretensions at being schools. There are simply no equipment: no labs, no libraries, nothing at all with which to facilitate the process of teaching and learning. The students are something else. But what can we expect? They show complete unwillingness to learn. Hard work is strange to them and obedience to their teachers is stranger still. They are sick children of a sick nation. Only miracles can spin sound products from a sick society. Even worse is the formulation and implementation of whatever philosophy it is that governs the entire system. Everybody realizes that today's world is governed by science and technology, yet our education 'experts', in characteristic Nigerian style, concentrate on creating new universities rather than focus on equipping and upgrading the existing ones.
One wonders whether it is better to build new universities or beef up the science faculties of existing ones. However, allowing for the new universities of science and technology, what real efforts are being made (now that the cart has been put before the horse) to train science students at the secondary level to feed the new universities? A cursory look at the science program of primary and secondary schools today will discourage any parent. A comparison with what obtains in other countries will cause mass despair. Japanese high school mathematics has been compared to American college mathematics. However, American primary school mathematics can be favourably compared to our senior secondary school efforts.
A common Japanese high school science quiz will find our university science students out of depth. Little wonder that such countries continue to be our superiors!
Yet, every year, the authorities pay lip service to science education. As a small-scale employer of labour, I can say that my personal experience of recent school graduates leave much to be desired. There was once this slogan: 'Literacy for all by the year 2000'. Of course, our country failed to achieve that! Now, government is talking about millennium goals, yet no serious effort is being made to even achieve mass literacy even by the year 3000. It is calculated that, in 2000, Nigeria had no less than 70% illiteracy rate. How can it not be so? We Nigerians are noted for intellectual laziness. No student these days reads more than prescribed school text, that is, if he reads at all. After all, Nigerian students are known to pass exams without any knowledge!
Let it be known that the world today is powered by knowledge. Unless this drift into ever deeper ignorance is corrected and arrested, our slide into poverty and debt will acquire more speed rather than abate. Let it be known that whether rich or poor, to be educated is better than to be uneducated. Education must be compulsory, if our country must emerge from this doldrums. Since the people seem not to appreciate the import, government must move from mere lip service to concrete actions. Efforts must be made to properly integrate education into the overall developmental policy framework. Tuition must be functional so as to produce persons skilled in disciplines necessary for the growth of this country. We must look towards Japan for solid examples of the integration of education with society and training with industry.
The products of the formal education system must either be employed or be encouraged to engage in productive self-employment. It is better than the situation where many university graduates degrade into petty trading. That the educated have been known to sometimes fare worse than the illiterate is not enough excuse for the educated to put their brains on hold. Education must reclaim its pride of place. Today, education appears to be superfluous in society. The educated seem to have been pounded into submission by the poor reward system in operation. Imagine that an orthopaedic surgeon may earn less than N2m a year while an illiterate trader who does not contribute one iota to productivity may earn the same amount in one month. Moreover, in a materialistic society such as ours, the trader has more honour than the doctor. No wonder then that more of our youths prefer trading to otherwise respectable professions. This unhealthy attitude has just got to change. We cannot afford to have a country of rich morons. A country in which school enrolment is falling, despite our ever-exploding population, is headed the wrong way. We cannot sit back and watch this happen. The quality and spread of our education system must be improved upon or we will see worse days ahead.
UP NIGERIA!

Original_Search_Title: As Traditional Festivals Begin to Vanish
Headline: Nigeria: As Traditional Festivals Begin to Vanish
Body_Text:
Culture is a way of life and the African society is woven around its various types. The people's beliefs, traditions, norms, values and their general ways of life reflect their cultural existence. A manifestation of this is mirrored inside many festivals that are part and parcel of the African people. Festivals are an integral part of the cycle of life of a traditional African man or woman. From the birth of a bouncing baby girl to the adolescence of a young black teenager in the bush, right to the elderly recline of a chief in front of his house, there is a strong inclination towards festivals.
Festivals are common to all human societies from Asia, Europe, down to Africa. It is one of the ways through which man expresses his awareness of transcendental beings outside himself. Masquerade festivals are pertinent among African tribes. They are usually used to display cultural values and aesthetics. The masquerades are very fundamental to African society. In some places, they are believed to be the dead ancestors and messengers of traditional deities from the supernatural world.
For instance, the people of Ogidi-Ijumu local government area in Kogi State observe the Agbo masquerade festival which takes place annually between the tenth and eleventh months of the year in order to mark the end of one and the beginning of another year. A ritual procession takes place for a period of six days during which an oracle is consulted, materials for the costumes are gathered, costumes made, food gathered, every necessary preparation is made. On the performance day, a battalion of Agbo masquerades will dance down to the palace of the traditional ruler, who then addresses them, telling the masquerades not to beat visitors and school children with their whips. Afterwards, the masquerades start displaying their acrobatic skills, blended with rigorous dance steps. They then dance around town in their different shapes and sizes in beautiful costumes made from different leaves such as Iko (a rare sort of elephant grass), banana leaves and brooms, at times with baskets. Out of the battalion is the king's masquerade which is the largest and whose costume is usually a lot different from the others because he usually wears beads, carries a talking drum and holds a horsewhip.
During the event, people are seen in their best attires and natives of the town that are based in other parts of Nigeria come home to participate in the experience. Tourists also come from far and wide to witness the performance. Little children are seen carrying canes, mimicking and running after the beautifully crafted masquerades with heightened excitement. There is an ancient belief that the masquerade possesses supernatural healing powers. It is believed that any sick person who touches the masquerade will be healed no matter the character, young or old. During the festival, people are seen struggling to touch the Agbo masquerade. Some queue to take snapshots.
But compared with the pre-colonial period, African festivals are not what they used to be anymore. Colonialism gave birth to modernism and modernism saw African festivals as being barbaric. The introduction of new religions also brought about festivals like Christmas and Easter for Christians, Eid-El-Fitr and Eid-El Kabir for Muslims, Diwali (festival of lights) for Hindus or Vesak (in honor of the Buddha) for Buddhists.
In Ogidi-Ijumu, for instance, majority of the people are Christians, others Muslims and an estimated 10% still follow the old ways. Due to all this, the people have taken a total departure from the performance of the festival. Last year, for instance, the festival did not take place.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Expose Poor Sanitation
Headline: Malawi: Rains Expose Poor Sanitation
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Zimbabwe - where cholera has claimed more than 2,700 lives so far according to the Red Cross - is not the only southern African country facing increased disease as rains set in across the region. Malawi is also battling a cholera outbreak which has killed 19 people since the onset of the rainy season, an unusually high death toll.
Up to 485 cases of the epidemic have since been registered and treated. World Health Organisation records from the 2007/2008 rainy season indicate not even a single cholera case was registered in the country's capital, Lilongwe, last year, although up to 20 deaths and 1,022 cases were documented in nine of Malawi's 27 districts.
Apart from the current outbreak in Lilongwe, one other cholera case was treated in the country's commercial capital, Blantyre, two weeks ago, but this was imported from Zimbabwe, according to Malawi's principal secretary for health Chris Kang'ombe.
"Queen Elizabeth Central Hospital in Blantyre treated a Zimbabwean truck driver who had cholera. He recovered and has since returned to Zimbabwe," said Kang'ombe.
There is a lot of cross-border trade and movement between Malawi and neighbouring Zimbabwe and Malawi health authorities have been on alert and intensifying civic education on cholera to ensure that the serious Zimbabwe cholera situation does not spread into the country, according to Kang'ombe.
But cholera is not primarily spread directly from person to person. The country's health experts have attributed the problem to lack of safe water combined with poor sanitation and poor hygiene.
The outbreak has hit Lilongwe, and surrounding communities hardest. Kang'ombe said all the people that have died were resident in Malawi's fastest-growing city which has large populations living in slums with little access to safe water. Cholera is transmitted through contaminated water or food.
"We encounter cholera outbreaks almost every rainy season when people who have little or no access to safe water resort to using untreated water from swamps," Kang'ombe told IPS.
The Banda clan, living on the outskirts of Lilongwe city, has lost two family members to the disease within a period of two weeks. Another member of the family was also infected but has recovered after treatment.
A clan member, Jabu Banda, said his aunt got ill with cholera two weeks ago and was admitted to one of the tents erected in Likuni, one of Lilongwe's high density areas, by the ministry of health specially to care for cholera victims. "She died two days after being taken to the health centre," said Banda.
He said his niece also started showing signs of cholera a week after the death in the family. "We took her to the health centre but she also died a day later," Banda said.
Banda said his cousin who played the role of guardian for the two victims was also diagnosed with cholera last week.
"She has just been discharged from the clinic but she is yet to recover fully. She is very weak," Banda told IPS.
In managing the outbreak, Malawi's Ministry of Health has erected special tents near local hospitals and within areas that have been highly affected by the cholera outbreak.
"The idea is to avoid mixing cholera patients with others admitted to hospitals for other less contagious illnesses," said Kang'ombe.
He said the outbreak would have been quickly contained if people had improved on their hygiene. Kang'ombe said a lot of people in townships and surrounding areas eat fresh foods such as fruits without washing them. Fruits such as mangos, bananas and pineapples are in abundance during the rainy season in Malawi.
"We are providing chlorine to households for them to be able treat their water. We are also stopping communities from preparing food at gatherings such as funerals and to avoid buying cooked food from streets to avoid contamination," he said.
The ministry has also cautioned people who handle corpses of cholera cases to be extra careful. Culturally, most communities in Malawi administer a bath to the dead just before burial.
Meanwhile, there are more fears of cholera outbreaks in other parts of Malawi - health officials are vigilant in the flood-prone areas of the country which include southern districts of Chikwawa and Nsanje, the lowest-lying areas of Malawi, which experience floods annually and where cholera epidemics are most common during the rainy season.
Floods have already affected 2,100 households in 21 villages in Nsanje district and 1,573 other families in Chikwawa district since the beginning of the New Year, according to government statistics from district commissioners' offices.
A task force comprising the Ministry of Health, United Nations Children's Fund - (UNICEF), World Health Organisation (WHO) and United Kingdom's Department For International Development (DFID) is currently working to promote civic education on hygiene and chlorination of water sources in the country to control further cholera outbreaks.
Malawi's rainy season runs from November to May and the country still has five more months to contend with cholera.

Original_Search_Title: Avalanche of Debt Barrels Down On Banks
Headline: Namibia: Avalanche of Debt Barrels Down On Banks
Body_Text:
Windhoek — I find it amazing that some analysts still cling to the idea of a rebound somewhere in the second half of this year. What I want to get into everybody's head is that there is a barrage of debt avalanching down on America and Europe, and no matter what any government does, it wouldn't go away by itself.
Even worse, once we are stuck in structural deflation, then that debt becomes heavier by the day.
Government leaders in the West need to come to terms with the reality: the American consumer is bankrupt, the European consumer is near bankrupt and the banking systems in both these territories are not strong enough to carry the tremendous losses on their balance sheets. As long as they keep trying to re-inflate an insolvent system they are actually adding to the insolvency, not removing it. You do not solve a debt trap by borrowing yourself out of it. You either work yourself out of it, or you default.
At some point sanity will have to prevail and the bailouts will have to stop. What was so different with
Lehman Brothers than say, Northern Rock?
The longer the G7 nations try to patch a sieve, the longer they are delaying a return to normal trading conditions. I know that their efforts are motivated by political outcomes, but this helps nobody, least of all us on the poorer side of the spectrum. We need the world economy to stabilise and we need the East's manufacturing countries to keep buying commodities from us. We also need the West to realise "whatever it takes" may not be enough.
Take a very practical example - diamond sales. I expect gem diamond sales to shrink by as much as 40% this year, compared to 2007 (not 2008). That constitutes an enormous chunk out of our fiscus as we are uncomfortably reliant on a myriad of royalties, taxes and levies all based on the well-being of the diamond mining sector. And as a Namdeb shareholder, the government is also directly dependent on diamond sales.
A few rough calculations I did during the week indicate that if we keep the Medium Term Expenditure Framework as published last year, as a benchmark, then we are going to rake up a budget deficit of around 4,8% of GDP. This is not a train smash - granted - but it certainly takes us back to 1995 when budget deficits became a very dangerous threat to our financial well-being.
I also expect, for the purposes of window dressing, to receive a fairly modest budget in March, with the big whammer only slammed on us later in the year with the very popular "Additional Expenditure Appropriation Bill." Those big words always mean only one thing. Sneaking in a second budget in the same financial year to draw attention away from the political damage one can expect were the full expenditure published at the tabling of the main budget.
But these are all side issues. I do not think our budget will balance but I do not regard it as an overly serious issue. This is an election year and one must expect some juggling with the figures to achieve political objectives. And above all, we can afford it.
Let us compare out indebtedness to other so-called developed countries. Our public debt is in the order of 30% of GDP. The government's stated intention, under normal conditions, is to gradually reduce it. The USA's public debt is approaching 80% of GDP while their total national debt, public and private combined, has passed the 355% mark. (That is not a typo - over 350%).
Italy, which I view as a banana republic, has a public debt exceeding 110%. Their total national debt is also measured in the hundreds of percent. Zimbabwe does not make the list. Indeed, this frightening list has almost two hundred candidates higher up than us in the negative, or debt, rankings. So, yes, we can afford a deficit, and going by our history and our proven ability to manage our financial affairs, we can afford a deficit for many years.
I am cautioning against the culture of budget deficits, not against the need for them. If we really have to run up a deficit, that is fine. If we have to repeat it for a number of years, that is fine. There is not a single country that can poke a finger at us for debt. But we must avoid the trap that we were in the middle nineties - fostering a culture of accepting budget deficits as normal.
Somewhere in a report this week I read that if the USA does not incur a single cent in additional debt as from tomorrow, they will still pay back their current debt for the next fifty years. And that report gave itself out as conservative.
We need to ask ourselves how much are we prepared to burden our children and grandchildren through the excesses we now defend as absolutely necessary.

Original_Search_Title: Kyambogo University Exhibits Beautiful Art
Headline: Uganda: Kyambogo University Exhibits Beautiful Art
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOR many years, it stood as a centre of practical skills education. Even before assuming university status, it produced some of Uganda's most creative visual arts brains.
And yet Kyambogo University has somehow lurked in the shadows of this country's artistic glory.
The university's school of industrial art and design has a huge collection of artwork. It is the kind of work that might on the surface appear ordinary but herein lies its uniqueness - the young artists most of whom use local ordinary material to produce a variety of artwork.
From banana fibres to ordinary clay and grass to local dyes, the students and their teachers take advantage of their environment to produce beautiful work.
Muturi Kimani, the head of the weaving department is working with the students to develop different samples of fabrics made from banana fibres.
Even though still in their raw form, these fabrics present firm texture and various natural patterns. Kimani says the fabric would be presented in different colours.
The sculpture department too presents a variety of beautiful artifacts made from clay and prepared into different products from the university kiln.
Pots, flower vases, bowls and other such functional artifacts turn out with a smooth brown glazed finishing that lends a glittery effect to the work.
The department has also produced multimedia crafts including necklaces, bangles, bracelets and other ornamental artifacts.
Many of these have been crafted using metal scraps and refuse. John Mugisha, the head of the sculpture department, says the university realises the need for making the best of what is available to us.
I realise, however, that there is not much concentration on painting. In general, the works on exhibit at the university depict an adaptation to nature and a more creative use of ordinary material around them.

Original_Search_Title: About Daughters Who Split Firewood
Headline: Cameroon: About Daughters Who Split Firewood
Body_Text:
If you want to listen to some feminine gists, then visit a hair dressing saloon. Most women have transformed these beauty spots into their gossip rooms. During such chit-chats, everything goes; from the poor dressing habits of the next-door-neighbour, the flirtatious attitude of a colleague, the wretchedness of a friend, to even the source of wealth of this or that person.
And talking about wealth, two ladies almost tore themselves apart the other day in one of these senior service beauty saloons in Yaounde. What began like a joke with the women enumerating their wealth soon went out of hand. And why? One of the ladies went bananas as she couldn't condone a remark on the source of her wealth. This is the question that made the woman see red.
"We all know the source of your wealth. If your daughters were not out there in Europe splitting firewood, would they be able to send you so much money to build houses"?
A simplistic analysis of the question makes it difficult to understand why the lady was so furious. After all, even our old mothers in the village still split wood for their fire. Or, can it be that there is something more and repulsive to this splitting firewood in Europe? I need your help to understand.

Original_Search_Title: Nnamani Vs Iwu - the Hypocrisy Element
Headline: Nigeria: Nnamani Vs Iwu - the Hypocrisy Element
Body_Text:
In the quiet but politically significant town of Akpugo in Nkanu West Local Government of Enugu State, something serious happened in the expansive house of a popular political stalwart. The date was April 12, 2003. What happened was nothing new to the Nigerian political terrain - rigging! While the electorate queued, ostensibly to elect the president and their representatives at the National Assembly, political "movers" and "shakers" were busy manufacturing (not even doctoring) election results. At the end of the day, the kind of results the former Senate president, Ken Nnamani always refers to as fictitious were announced in favour of all Ebeano candidates, including Senator Ken Nnamani himself.
It is nothing new, not even to a Nigerian toddler, because from time immemorial Nigerian elections have been rigging-prone and only the master riggers take them by force. This particular incident is no news to Enugu people who know how the Ebeano political machinery was deployed to root out all political dissidents, including the erstwhile godfather of former governor, Senator Chimaroke Nnamani, Chief Jim Ifeanyichukwu Nwobodo. It is also no news in any way that the former Senate President, Chief Ken Nnamani (who is the kinsman of Senator Jim Nwobodo) was the greatest beneficiary of that roundly rigged and stage-managed 2003 polls in the area, having deployed his financial resources to achieve this in conjunction with the awesome Ebeano political machinery.
By a stroke of luck, Adolphus Wabara allegedly ate the forbidden fruit in 2005 and was swept away by the proverbial banana peels, paving way for Senator Ken Nnamani to become Senate President. By another stroke of luck, Obasanjo allegedly dabbled into tenure elongation project which, if it had succeeded, Senator Nnamani (whom Obasanjo never wanted or trusted) could have become an interim president. Senator Nnamani played safe, even after allegedly collecting double portion of the Third Term largesse, incident Obasanjo's loyalists allege, pitched both men as mortal enemies.
Perhaps, the more important question today is: why has 'Sinful' Prof. Iwu remained under the constant attack of 'Saint' Ken Nnamani? Not a few believe Ken Nnamani has several more questions to answer on oath in order to get to the root of the matter. One, is it true that the U-turn the former Senate President made at a point Nigerians expected him to show the highest level of moral rectitude by religiously following through on the PTDF Investigation had something to do with the alleged settlement? Is it true that in that deal, he was assured he would be handed over the PDP machinery in Enugu to produce the successor of his sworn enemy, Chimaroke Nnamani at the 2007 governorship polls on the platform of Accord Party? Is it true that he was even fooled to as far as name a new and preferred commissioner of police to replace the then incumbent commissioner and a long list of names to replace strategic electoral officers all deemed to be loyal to the then Governor Chimaroke Nnamani in order to smoothen the 'operation take-over'?
Is it true that on being given this juicy offer of humiliating Ebeano, he melted in the hands of OBJ like a chocolate in a warm moisturised mouth, abruptly ordered the doctoring of the Senate Order Paper which stopped the PTDF Ad Hoc Probe Committee from presenting their report for Senate's consideration?
It could be recalled that the allegation of expunging the laying of the Report was then made by no less a person than Chairman of the Senate Committee on Rules and Business (which produces that important document) and who doubled as the Chairman of that Ad Hoc Committee. The obvious is that it took the mass resignation of the Chairman and Members of the Committee to get him to rescind his unpopular decision. It is also a fact that by the time the Committee and majority of Senators who cried blue murder pressured him and the Report was laid the next legislative day for consideration, Ken Nnamani hurriedly sent the Senate on a highly opprobrious election recess.
Today, while Baba continues to bear his incalculable losses on the woefully failed Third Term Agenda with statesmanship and equanimity towards Ken Nnamani, the former Senate President went wild and amuck against Iwu and INEC. Both were caught in the cross fire as they came under one of the Nnamani's Senate's inconclusive and vendetta-driven probes.
Yet even over one and half years after leaving office as Senate President, Nnamani has never spared even the slightest opportunity to curse and throw punches at Professor Maurice Iwu and his INEC that not only denied him the opportunity to humiliate his rival Chimaroke, but instead even added salt to his injury by returning him as his successor at the Senate because both of them hail from the same Senatorial District. Iwu and INEC thus became his punch bag, whether inside the Senate Chambers, before a Nigerian audience or the international community.
Were the 2007 elections perfect? Not at all! No system is without its imperfections, let alone an emerging democracy like ours. Most nations strive towards it perfection. With time, Obama, a black man, finally became a President-elect in the same country where he could have had no right to sit in a bus with a white man or cast vote decades ago. Even a fool would know that Iwu was caught in a very tight corner between OBJ and his many enemies. President Yar'Adua acknowledged the imperfections of our electoral system and set up the Uwais Committee which has come up with interim, but wonderful recommendations (including legislative issues that would have been conveniently addressed by Ken Nnamani as Chairman of the National Assembly). It is also heart warming that the Courts have been making redresses as appropriate. However, despite some noticeable defects of the April 2007 polls, the recent ratification of President Umar Musa Yar' Adua's election by the Supreme Court of Nigeria has carved a niche for Prof. Iwu in the nation's history books as the man who conducted the election that led to a civilian-to-civilian administration in Nigeria for the first time. If for nothing else, he deserves some credits to that extent.
The former Senate President's continuous pummelling of Iwu and INEC almost two years after the election have obviously gone beyond the patriotic and constructive criticism a citizen could offer. Now, could Senator Ken Nnamani have continued with his non-constructive criticisms if his choice had emerged governor in Enugu in 2007? Never! His name-callings and holier-than-thou attitude equates the highest audacity of hypocrisy.
'Saint' Ken Nnamani even admitted during an International Republican Institute programme on electoral reforms this 2008 that the National Assembly under his leadership failed to make some fundamental amendments to the Electoral Act. Senator Nnamani has to take the greater part of the blame for his glaring shortcomings in his national assignment because most of the Electoral Tribunal verdicts and commentators have emphasised that a lot of the problems that trailed the elections were as a result of loopholes in the electoral laws. Inversely, the Ghana elections he was crying loud about is the product of a tidy job by that country's National Assembly. Their electoral laws empower the press, the civil society, and the Electoral Commission of Ghana (ECG) and he, Nnamani, saw the difference.
•Dr Ukachukwu, is the Director incharge of media, Coalition for Electoral Reform in Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: We are Addressing Challenges of a Mega City - Banire
Headline: Nigeria: We are Addressing Challenges of a Mega City - Banire
Body_Text:
Muiz Banire is the Lagos State Commissioner for the Environment.
In this interview with Head, Covers and Investigation, Isioma Madike, and Reporter, Emeka Umejei, he said the people and markets affected by the realignment of the state into a mega city are being relocated to more befitting places as the beautification of the city, which is one way of tackling the changes confronting his Ministry, is a continuous one.
The beautification of Lagos State is ongoing. What would you say is the philosophy behind the project?
The motive behind the beautification of Lagos State is the restoration of its beauty. We are basically doing this to restore the lost beauty and make it more gorgeous to its inhabitants.
Are you saying the scheme has nothing to do with the mega city project?
No. Lagos is a mega city by all standards and has nothing to do with beautifying the city. Mega cities are measured by population. Once you are over 10 million, then you are a mega city. But beyond that, what we are doing is addressing the challenges of a mega city that we are.
What is the Mega city project costing the government?
